topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT523 - DATA PRIVACY AND SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Security Goals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 1: Security Goals — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What Are Security Goals?

In the discipline of **Data Privacy and Security**, *security goals* (often called **security objectives** or **security services**) are the foundational properties that an information system must guarantee in order to protect data and computing assets from unauthorized access, modification, disruption, or destruction. They form the **blueprint for designing, evaluating, and auditing** any secure system.

Formally, security goals are a *tripartite (and extended) set of invariants* — properties that the system's state must preserve across all executions against a defined adversary model.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECDT523 – Module 1):**
> *Security goals are the high-level protection objectives that a system is designed to achieve. They are typically expressed as the **CIA Triad** (Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability), often extended to include **Authentication, Authorization, Non-Repudiation, and Accountability**.*

---

## 1.2 The CIA Triad — The Classical Triad

The **CIA Triad** is the cornerstone of information security. It was formalised in the 1970s and remains the most widely accepted framework in academic literature and in KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus.

### Conceptual Analogy: The Bank Vault

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy — A bank's safety deposit vault:**
> 1. **Confidentiality** → Only the account holder (and authorized staff) can *see* what is inside the locker. The box has a key and an ID check — strangers are kept out.
> 2. **Integrity** → Once you store a document, the bank guarantees it has *not been altered, swapped, or replaced* with a fake. If a thief opens it and touches anything, the tamper seal breaks.
> 3. **Availability** → When *you* (the legitimate owner) arrive during business hours, the locker *must open immediately*. Even if a thousand customers come at once, the system must serve them without crashing.
>
> The three sides of the triangle must be in **equilibrium** — over-investing in confidentiality at the cost of availability is just as bad as neglecting integrity.

```
         CONFIDENTIALITY
              /\
             /  \
            /    \
           / CIA  \
          / TRIAD  \
         /__________\
INTEGRITY <--------> AVAILABILITY
```

### 1.2.1 Confidentiality

**Definition:** Confidentiality ensures that information is **disclosed only to authorized parties**. It protects data from unauthorized *read* access, ensuring that sensitive information is not exposed to eavesdroppers, snoopers, or malicious insiders.

**Standard metrics / constants:**
- **AES key sizes:** **128 bits, 192 bits, 256 bits**
- **RSA minimum recommended key:** **2048 bits** (as of 2024 NIST guidance)
- **Information-theoretic secrecy:** Shannon's perfect secrecy condition: $P(M \mid C) = P(M)$

**Threats to confidentiality:** Eavesdropping, man-in-the-middle attacks, packet sniffing, shoulder surfing, data leakage, social engineering.

**Countermeasures:** Encryption (AES, RSA, ECC), access control lists (ACLs), data classification, steganography, secure channels (TLS, VPN).

---

### 1.2.2 Integrity

**Definition:** Integrity ensures that data is **not modified or destroyed in an unauthorized or undetected manner**. It protects the *correctness*, *completeness*, and *trustworthiness* of information throughout its lifecycle.

**Standard metrics:**
- **Cryptographic hash output sizes:** **SHA-256 → 256 bits**, SHA-512 → 512 bits, MD5 → 128 bits (deprecated).
- **HMAC key length:** recommended **≥ 256 bits**.
- **Bit Error Rate (BER)** acceptable thresholds in storage media.

**Threats to integrity:** Tampering, man-in-the-middle modifications, malware, bit rot, unauthorized write access, replay attacks.

**Countermeasures:** Hash functions (SHA family), Message Authentication Codes (MAC/HMAC), digital signatures, version control systems, write-once storage, checksums, CRCs.

---

### 1.2.3 Availability

**Definition:** Availability ensures that systems, data, and services are **accessible to authorized users when needed**. It guarantees reliable and timely access to information resources.

**Standard metrics:**
- **Uptime SLAs:** **99.9% ("three nines")**, **99.99% ("four nines")**, **99.999% ("five nines")**.
- **Mean Time To Failure (MTTF)** and **Mean Time To Repair (MTTR)**.
- **RTO (Recovery Time Objective)** and **RPO (Recovery Point Objective)** in disaster recovery.

**Threats to availability:** Denial-of-Service (DoS) attacks, Distributed DoS (DDoS), ransomware, hardware failures, natural disasters, misconfigurations, network outages.

**Countermeasures:** Redundancy (RAID, clustering), load balancing, failover mechanisms, backups, disaster recovery plans, DDoS mitigation services, UPS systems.

---

## 1.3 Extended Security Goals (Beyond the CIA Triad)

While CIA captures the essence, modern systems require **four additional goals** to handle identity, accountability, and trust.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 Scheme explicitly lists these extended goals within Module 1 of PECDT523.

### 1.3.1 Authentication

**Definition:** The process of **verifying the identity** of a user, system, or entity that requests access. It answers the question: *"Are you really who you claim to be?"*

**Methods:**
- *Something you know* — password, PIN, security question.
- *Something you have* — smart card, OTP token, mobile device.
- *Something you are* — biometrics (fingerprint, iris, face).
- *Somewhere you are / context* — IP-based geolocation, device fingerprinting.

### 1.3.2 Authorization

**Definition:** After authentication, authorization determines **what an authenticated entity is permitted to do**. It enforces access policies: *"You are who you say you are, but may you do what you're trying to do?"*

**Models:** Role-Based Access Control (RBAC), Attribute-Based Access Control (ABAC), Mandatory Access Control (MAC), Discretionary Access Control (DAC).

### 1.3.3 Non-Repudiation

**Definition:** Guarantees that a party **cannot deny** having performed an action (sending a message, signing a document, performing a transaction). It provides *undeniable proof* of origin and receipt.

**Mechanisms:** Digital signatures (RSA, ECDSA, Ed25519), digital certificates, audit logs, trusted timestamping, blockchain receipts.

### 1.3.4 Accountability

**Definition:** Ensures that every action on a system can be **traced back to a uniquely identified user or process**. It supports auditing, forensics, and post-incident analysis.

**Mechanisms:** Audit logs, security information and event management (SIEM), user activity monitoring, immutable log storage, chain-of-custody procedures.

---

## 1.4 Summary Table — The Seven Security Goals

| # | Goal | Question Answered | Primary Threat |
|---|------|-------------------|----------------|
| 1 | **Confidentiality** | Who can *see* this? | Eavesdropping |
| 2 | **Integrity** | Has it been *modified*? | Tampering |
| 3 | **Availability** | Is it *reachable* now? | DoS / failure |
| 4 | **Authentication** | Are you *who you claim*? | Impersonation |
| 5 | **Authorization** | Are you *allowed* to do this? | Privilege escalation |
| 6 | **Non-Repudiation** | Can you *deny* doing it? | Repudiation attacks |
| 7 | **Accountability** | Who *did* this? | Anonymity abuse |

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick mnemonic:** *"CIA + 4 A's"* — Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability, Authentication, Authorization, Accountability, Anti-repudiation (Non-Repudiation).

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Adversary Model — Who Are We Defending Against?

A *security goal* is meaningless without a clearly defined **adversary (threat model)**. KTU 2024 expects students to map each goal to the adversary capabilities it counters.

| Adversary Type | Capability | Goals Defeated |
|----------------|-----------|----------------|
| **Passive eavesdropper** | Can read transmitted data | Confidentiality |
| **Active interceptor** | Can read + modify data | Confidentiality + Integrity |
| **Impersonator** | Can forge identity | Authentication |
| **Insider** | Has partial legitimate access | Confidentiality, Integrity, Authorization |
| **Resource monopolizer** | Floods the system | Availability |
| **Repudiator** | Denies having sent something | Non-Repudiation |

---

## 2.2 Why Three Goals? — Theoretical Justification

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The three properties are **mutually dependent yet independently necessary**:
> - A system that is *confidential* but cannot be *accessed* is useless (low availability).
> - A system that is *always available* but reveals *all data* to everyone is dangerous (no confidentiality).
> - A system that is *available and confidential* but allows silent *modification* cannot be trusted (no integrity).

Mathematically, if $S$ is the system state space and $T$ is the set of authorized transformations, then:
- **Confidentiality** demands that the *projection* of $S$ onto a non-authorized observer be statistically independent of the secret.
- **Integrity** demands that $S$ can only move along trajectories in $T$ (no out-of-band transitions).
- **Availability** demands that the *response time* of $S$ to authorized queries remain below a threshold $\tau$.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet (Confidentiality & Integrity Metrics)

The following table summarises the **most-tested formulas and quantitative properties** for the Module-1 Security Goals topic in KTU 2024 ESE.

| Goal | Metric / Property | Formula or Constant | Typical Value | Notation Note |
|------|-------------------|---------------------|---------------|---------------|
| Confidentiality | Shannon perfect secrecy | $P(M \mid C) = P(M)$ | — | $M$ = plaintext, $C$ = ciphertext |
| Confidentiality | AES key entropy | $H(K) = \log_2 \vert \mathcal{K} \vert$ | 128, 192, 256 bits | $\mathcal{K}$ = key space |
| Confidentiality | RSA modulus bit length | $n = p \cdot q$, $\vert n \vert \geq 2048$ | 2048–4096 bits | $p, q$ large primes |
| Integrity | Hash output collision resistance | $\mathcal{O}(2^{n/2})$ for $n$-bit hash | $2^{128}$ for SHA-256 | Birthday paradox |
| Integrity | Preimage resistance | $\mathcal{O}(2^{n})$ | $2^{256}$ for SHA-256 | — |
| Integrity | HMAC security bound | $\leq \mathcal{O}(2^{n}/\ell)$ | — | $\ell$ = query count |
| Availability | Uptime percentage | $U = \frac{\text{uptime}}{\text{total time}} \times 100\%$ | 99.9 % – 99.999 % | — |
| Availability | Annual downtime | $D = (1 - U) \times 365 \times 24 \times 60$ min | — | Three nines $\approx 525.6$ min/yr |
| Availability | MTBF / MTTR | $\text{MTBF} = \text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}$ | — | Reliability engineering |
| Authentication | Password entropy | $H = L \cdot \log_2(N)$ | — | $L$ = length, $N$ = alphabet size |
| Non-Repudiation | Digital signature size | $\approx 256$ bytes (RSA-2048) | — | ECDSA: 64 bytes |
| Authorization | ACL size complexity | $\mathcal{O}(n \cdot m)$ | — | $n$ users, $m$ resources |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU note on table syntax:** When writing $\vert \mathcal{K} \vert$ for "cardinality of the key space" inside any table, the vertical bar is rendered as `\vert` (not raw `\vert`) so it does not collide with the markdown table delimiter. Always do this when expressing absolute value or set cardinality inside a table cell.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Which Security Goals Are Critical | Real System Example |
|--------|----------------------------------|---------------------|
| **Banking / FinTech** | All seven — especially non-repudiation, integrity, authentication | SWIFT, UPI, NEFT |
| **Healthcare** | Confidentiality, integrity, accountability | EHR systems, HL7 |
| **E-Commerce** | Availability, integrity, authentication | Amazon, Flipkart |
| **Defence / Military** | Confidentiality, integrity, non-repudiation | Defence messaging, secure boot |
| **IoT / Smart Cities** | Availability, authentication | Smart meters, traffic control |
| **Cloud Computing** | All seven; multi-tenancy amplifies confidentiality & availability | AWS, Azure, GCP |

---

## 2.5 Attack ↔ Goal ↔ Countermeasure Mapping

This is the **most-frequently tested mapping** in KTU 2024 ESE. Memorise it.

| Attack | Goal Violated | Countermeasure |
|--------|--------------|----------------|
| Sniffing / packet capture | Confidentiality | Encryption (TLS, IPsec) |
| Replay attack | Integrity + Authentication | Nonces, timestamps, sequence numbers |
| SQL injection | Integrity + Confidentiality | Parameterised queries, input validation |
| Phishing | Authentication | MFA, digital certificates |
| DDoS | Availability | Rate limiting, scrubbing centres, CDN |
| Insider data theft | Confidentiality + Accountability | DLP, audit logs, least privilege |
| Forging a digital receipt | Non-Repudiation | Digital signatures + PKI |
| Privilege escalation | Authorization | RBAC, principle of least privilege |
| Tampering with firmware | Integrity | Code signing, secure boot, TPM |
| Ransomware | Availability + Integrity | Backups, immutable storage |

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section derives the **mathematical formulations** that underpin each security goal. The aim is to give KTU 2024 students the *rigorous* definitions examiners love to see in 14-mark answers.

---

## 3.1 Confidentiality — Information-Theoretic Derivation

### 3.1.1 Shannon's Definition of Perfect Secrecy

A cryptosystem achieves **perfect secrecy** if for every plaintext $M$ and every ciphertext $C$ with non-zero probability, the a posteriori probability of $M$ given $C$ equals the a priori probability of $M$.

$$
P(M = m \mid C = c) \;=\; P(M = m) \quad \forall\, m, c
$$

**Derivation step 1 — Start with Bayes' theorem:**

$$
P(M = m \mid C = c) \;=\; \frac{P(C = c \mid M = m)\,P(M = m)}{P(C = c)}
$$

**Derivation step 2 — Apply the perfect-secrecy condition:**

$$
\frac{P(C = c \mid M = m)\,P(M = m)}{P(C = c)} \;=\; P(M = m)
$$

**Derivation step 3 — Cancel $P(M = m)$ from both sides (assuming $P(M=m) > 0$):**

$$
P(C = c \mid M = m) \;=\; P(C = c)
$$

**Interpretation:** The ciphertext distribution is *independent* of the plaintext. **Shannon's theorem** then states that for perfect secrecy, the key space $\mathcal{K}$ must satisfy $\vert \mathcal{K} \vert \geq \vert \mathcal{M} \vert$ (key entropy ≥ plaintext entropy). Hence the *one-time pad* is the only provably perfect cipher.

---

### 3.1.2 Entropy of a Cryptographic Key

The **Shannon entropy** of a key $K$ drawn uniformly from a space $\mathcal{K}$ is:

$$
H(K) \;=\; -\sum_{k \in \mathcal{K}} P(K = k) \cdot \log_2 P(K = k)
$$

**Derivation step 1 — Substitute uniform probability $P(K = k) = 1/\vert \mathcal{K} \vert$:**

$$
H(K) \;=\; -\sum_{k \in \mathcal{K}} \frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{K} \vert} \cdot \log_2 \frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{K} \vert}
$$

**Derivation step 2 — Factor out the constant:**

$$
H(K) \;=\; -\frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{K} \vert} \cdot \log_2 \frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{K} \vert} \cdot \sum_{k \in \mathcal{K}} 1
$$

**Derivation step 3 — Sum has $\vert \mathcal{K} \vert$ terms, so they cancel:**

$$
H(K) \;=\; -\log_2 \frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{K} \vert} \;=\; \log_2 \vert \mathcal{K} \vert
$$

**For AES-256:** $\vert \mathcal{K} \vert = 2^{256}$, so $H(K) = 256$ bits. This means an attacker must brute-force $2^{256}$ keys on average — infeasible with current technology.

---

## 3.2 Integrity — Hash Function Security

### 3.2.1 Collision Resistance (Birthday Bound)

For an ideal $n$-bit hash function, the expected number of trials to find a collision follows the **birthday paradox**.

**Derivation step 1 — Probability that $q$ random samples are *all distinct*:**

$$
P_{\text{no collision}}(q) \;=\; \prod_{i=1}^{q-1} \left(1 - \frac{i}{2^n}\right)
$$

**Derivation step 2 — Approximate the logarithm for large $n$ and small $q/2^n$:**

$$
\ln P_{\text{no collision}}(q) \;\approx\; -\sum_{i=1}^{q-1} \frac{i}{2^n} \;=\; -\frac{q(q-1)}{2 \cdot 2^n}
$$

**Derivation step 3 — Set probability of *finding a collision* to $1/2$:**

$$
1 - e^{-q(q-1)/(2^{n+1})} \;\approx\; \frac{1}{2}
$$

**Derivation step 4 — Solve for $q$ approximately:**

$$
q \;\approx\; \sqrt{2^{n+1} \cdot \ln 2} \;\approx\; 1.18 \cdot 2^{n/2}
$$

**Conclusion:** An $n$-bit hash requires $\mathcal{O}(2^{n/2})$ trials to find a collision. Hence **SHA-256** offers $\approx 2^{128}$ collision resistance — the gold standard.

---

### 3.2.2 HMAC Security Bound

For an $n$-bit hash, the security bound against forgery (assuming the underlying hash is a pseudo-random function) is:

$$
\text{Adv}^{\text{HMAC}}_{\text{forgery}}(t, q) \;\leq\; \frac{t}{2^{n-1}} + \frac{q^2}{2^{n+1}}
$$

where $t$ is the time and $q$ is the number of queries. **Interpretation:** HMAC inherits the strength of the underlying hash and remains secure even if the inner hash's collision resistance is partially broken (a property called *keyed-hash security*).

---

## 3.3 Availability — Quantitative Metrics

### 3.3.1 Annual Downtime from Uptime SLA

Given a target uptime $U$ (e.g., 99.9 %), the **maximum allowed annual downtime** $D$ (in minutes) is:

$$
D \;=\; (1 - U) \times 365 \times 24 \times 60
$$

**Derivation step 1 — Substitute $U = 0.999$ (three nines):**

$$
D \;=\; (1 - 0.999) \times 365 \times 24 \times 60
$$

**Derivation step 2 — Simplify $(1 - 0.999) = 0.001$:**

$$
D \;=\; 0.001 \times 365 \times 24 \times 60
$$

**Derivation step 3 — Multiply constants:**

$$
D \;=\; 0.001 \times 525600 \;\approx\; 525.6 \text{ minutes/year}
$$

**Comparative table:**

| SLA Class | Uptime $U$ | Annual Downtime $D$ | Monthly $D$ |
|-----------|-----------|----------------------|-------------|
| Two nines | 99 % | 5256 min $\approx$ 87.6 hr | 7.3 hr |
| Three nines | 99.9 % | 525.6 min $\approx$ 8.76 hr | 43.8 min |
| Four nines | 99.99 % | 52.56 min | 4.38 min |
| Five nines | 99.999 % | 5.26 min | 26.3 sec |

---

### 3.3.2 Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)

For a system with $N$ redundant components, each with failure rate $\lambda$ (failures per hour):

$$
\text{MTBF}_{\text{system}} \;=\; \frac{1}{N \cdot \lambda} \quad \text{(parallel, no common-mode failure)}
$$

**Derivation step 1 — Single-component MTBF:** $\text{MTBF}_{\text{single}} = 1/\lambda$.

**Derivation step 2 — For two independent components in parallel, system fails only when *both* fail:**

$$
P(\text{system fail}) \;=\; P(\text{comp1 fail}) \cdot P(\text{comp2 fail}) \;=\; \lambda_1 \lambda_2 \cdot t^2
$$

**Derivation step 3 — Effective system failure rate:**

$$
\lambda_{\text{sys}} \;\approx\; \lambda_1 \lambda_2 \cdot t \quad \text{(for small } \lambda t\text{)}
$$

**Derivation step 4 — System MTBF:**

$$
\text{MTBF}_{\text{sys}} \;=\; \frac{1}{\lambda_{\text{sys}}} \;\gg\; \text{MTBF}_{\text{single}}
$$

**Real-world utility:** This is why cloud providers (AWS, Azure) can offer four-nines or five-nines SLAs — through *massive* redundancy, the effective $\lambda_{\text{sys}}$ shrinks drastically.

---

## 3.4 Non-Repudiation — Digital Signature Property

A digital signature scheme $(\text{KeyGen}, \text{Sign}, \text{Verify})$ is **existentially unforgeable under chosen-message attack (EUF-CMA)** if the adversary's advantage:

$$
\text{Adv}^{\text{EUF-CMA}}_{\text{sig}}(\mathcal{A}) \;=\; \Pr\left[\text{Verify}(pk, m^*, \sigma^*) = \text{accept} \;:\; (m^*, \sigma^*) \notin \text{Queries}(\mathcal{A})\right]
$$

is **negligible** in the security parameter $\lambda$. This *negligibility* is what makes non-repudiation binding — the signer cannot deny because forging a valid signature pair $(m^*, \sigma^*)$ is computationally infeasible.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Cross-Goal Trade-offs

| Trade-off | Conflict | Resolution Strategy |
|-----------|----------|---------------------|
| Confidentiality vs Availability | Heavy encryption slows servers | Hardware acceleration (AES-NI), session caching |
| Integrity vs Performance | HMAC on every packet adds latency | Use lightweight MACs (e.g., GMAC, Poly1305) |
| Authentication vs Usability | Strong MFA is annoying | Adaptive / risk-based authentication |
| Non-Repudiation vs Privacy | Strong identity trails reduce anonymity | Pseudonymous accounts + selective disclosure |

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

The following **Mermaid diagrams** visualise the relationships, mappings, and flow of security goals as expected in KTU 2024 ESE answers.

---

## 4.1 The CIA Triad and Extended Goals — Master Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Security Goals PECDT523] --> B[Classical CIA Triad]
    A --> C[Extended Goals]
    B --> B1[Confidentiality]
    B --> B2[Integrity]
    B --> B3[Availability]
    C --> C1[Authentication]
    C --> C2[Authorization]
    C --> C3[Non-Repudiation]
    C --> C4[Accountability]
    B1 --> D[Countermeasures]
    B2 --> D
    B3 --> D
    C1 --> D
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    D --> D1[Encryption AES RSA ECC]
    D --> D2[Hashing SHA-256 HMAC]
    D --> D3[Redundancy RAID Failover]
    D --> D4[MFA Certificates Biometrics]
    D --> D5[RBAC ABAC ACL]
    D --> D6[Digital Signatures PKI]
    D --> D7[Audit Logs SIEM]
```

**Reading guide:** The diagram shows that the seven security goals (classical CIA + four extended) all map to a *shared countermeasures layer* containing seven families of technical controls.

---

## 4.2 Attack → Violated Goal → Countermeasure Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Eavesdropping] --> G1[Confidentiality Violated]
    A2[Tampering] --> G2[Integrity Violated]
    A3[DoS Attack] --> G3[Availability Violated]
    A4[Impersonation] --> G4[Authentication Violated]
    A5[Privilege Escalation] --> G5[Authorization Violated]
    A6[Repudiation] --> G6[Non-Repudiation Violated]
    A7[Identity Concealment] --> G7[Accountability Violated]
    G1 --> C1[Apply Encryption]
    G2 --> C2[Apply Hashing MAC]
    G3 --> C3[Apply Redundancy]
    G4 --> C4[Apply MFA]
    G5 --> C5[Apply RBAC]
    G6 --> C6[Apply Digital Signatures]
    G7 --> C7[Apply Audit Logging]
```

**Reading guide:** Each *attack* violates *one specific security goal*; the *countermeasure* on the right is the canonical defence. This mapping is the most testable artefact in KTU ESE Module 1.

---

## 4.3 Layered Security Architecture (Defence in Depth)

```mermaid
graph TB
    L0[Application Layer] --> L1[Authentication Authorization]
    L1 --> L2[Session Layer]
    L2 --> L3[TLS IPsec Encryption Confidentiality]
    L3 --> L4[Network Layer Firewalls IDS IPS]
    L4 --> L5[System Layer OS Hardening]
    L5 --> L6[Storage Layer Encryption Hashing Integrity]
    L6 --> L7[Physical Layer Locks Biometrics]
    L7 --> L8[Backup Redundancy Availability]
    L8 --> L9[Audit Logging Accountability]
```

**Reading guide:** The layered model (often called *defence in depth*) places each security goal in the layer where it is most effectively enforced. Confidentiality lives in layers 3 and 6; integrity in 6; availability in 8; non-repudiation in 9.

---

## 4.4 Authentication → Authorization Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User
    participant S as Server
    participant DB as Credential Store
    U ->> S: Step 1 Submit Credentials
    S ->> DB: Step 2 Lookup Hash
    DB -->> S: Step 3 Return Stored Hash
    S ->> S: Step 4 Verify Authentication
    alt Authentication Successful
        S ->> S: Step 5 Check Authorization Policy
        alt Authorization Granted
            S -->> U: Step 6 Access Allowed
        else Authorization Denied
            S -->> U: Step 7 Access Denied Forbidden
        end
    else Authentication Failed
        S -->> U: Step 8 Access Denied Unauthorized
    end
```

**Reading guide:** Authentication and authorization are *sequential* and *distinct* — authentication establishes *who you are*; authorization determines *what you may do*. KTU examiners test this distinction rigorously.

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

The following questions mirror **actual KTU 2024 ESE pattern** for PECDT523 Module 1. Marks are split as per KTU norms: **Part A = 3 marks each**, **Part B = 14 marks each** (with internal choice).

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PECDT523]`

**Define the CIA Triad. Explain why it is considered the foundation of information security.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **CIA Triad** is a foundational model of information security consisting of three core goals:
1. **Confidentiality** — ensuring that information is accessible only to those authorized to view it. *(1 mark)*
2. **Integrity** — ensuring that information is accurate and has not been modified by unauthorized parties. *(1 mark)*
3. **Availability** — ensuring that information and systems are accessible to authorized users when needed. *(1 mark)*

It is the foundation because **every security policy, mechanism, or control** can be traced back to protecting one or more of these three properties. Modern extensions (authentication, non-repudiation, accountability) build *on top* of the CIA framework rather than replacing it.

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PECDT523]`

**List and briefly explain any four extended security goals beyond the CIA Triad.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Authentication** — verifies the identity of a user or system claiming access. *(0.75 marks)*
2. **Authorization** — determines what an authenticated user is permitted to do. *(0.75 marks)*
3. **Non-Repudiation** — prevents a party from denying they performed an action; achieved via digital signatures. *(0.75 marks)*
4. **Accountability** — ensures every action can be traced to an identified user, typically via audit logs. *(0.75 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, PECDT523]`

**(a) Explain the three classical security goals of the CIA Triad in detail. For each goal, state two threats and two countermeasures.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**1. Confidentiality (≈ 2.5 marks)**

Confidentiality ensures that sensitive information is disclosed *only* to parties who are authorized to receive it. It protects against unauthorized *read* access.

*Threats:*
- Passive eavesdropping on an unencrypted Wi-Fi network.
- Insider exfiltration of customer records from a database.

*Countermeasures:*
- Strong encryption algorithms (AES-256 for data at rest, TLS 1.3 for data in transit).
- Access control lists and data classification policies.

**2. Integrity (≈ 2.5 marks)**

Integrity ensures that data remains *unaltered* during storage, processing, or transmission. Any unauthorized modification must be detected.

*Threats:*
- A man-in-the-middle modifies a bank transaction's destination account.
- A malicious script tampers with software update packages.

*Countermeasures:*
- Cryptographic hash functions (SHA-256) and HMACs to detect modifications.
- Digital signatures (RSA, ECDSA) for end-to-end integrity verification.

**3. Availability (≈ 2 marks)**

Availability ensures that systems and data are *accessible* to authorized users whenever required, within acceptable response-time bounds.

*Threats:*
- A volumetric DDoS attack that floods a web server with bogus requests.
- A ransomware attack that encrypts critical business data.

*Countermeasures:*
- Redundant infrastructure (load balancers, multi-region deployments) and DDoS scrubbing services.
- Regular off-site backups and a tested disaster-recovery plan with defined RTO and RPO.

---

**(b) Discuss the four extended security goals (Authentication, Authorization, Non-Repudiation, Accountability). Explain how digital signatures achieve non-repudiation.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**1. Authentication (≈ 1.5 marks)**

Authentication verifies identity. *Multi-factor authentication (MFA)* combines *something you know* (password), *something you have* (OTP token), and *something you are* (fingerprint) to raise the assurance level.

**2. Authorization (≈ 1.5 marks)**

Authorization enforces *what* an authenticated user may do. The **Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)** model assigns permissions to roles (e.g., "doctor", "nurse") and users to roles — simplifying administration in large systems. The **Principle of Least Privilege** dictates that each user is granted only the minimum rights necessary.

**3. Non-Repudiation (≈ 2 marks)**

Non-repudiation provides *undeniable cryptographic proof* that a specific party performed a specific action. It relies on **digital signatures**:
- The signer uses their *private key* $SK$ to sign a hash of the message: $\sigma = \text{Sign}(SK, H(m))$.
- Anyone with the signer's *public key* $PK$ can verify: $\text{Verify}(PK, m, \sigma) = \text{true}$.
- Since the private key is known *only* to the signer, the signature is unforgeable, and the signer cannot later deny signing the message.

**4. Accountability (≈ 2 marks)**

Accountability traces actions to identified users through *immutable audit logs* and *Security Information and Event Management (SIEM)* systems. Logs must include *who*, *what*, *when*, *where*, and *how* — and be protected from tampering using write-once storage or hash chaining.

---

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, PECDT523]` *(Internal Choice Alternative)*

**(a) With neat diagrams, explain the CIA Triad. How does it differ from the Parkerian Hexad?** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**The CIA Triad (≈ 3 marks)**

The CIA Triad is a triangle with **Confidentiality**, **Integrity**, and **Availability** at its three vertices. Each vertex represents a *security property* the system must uphold. The triangle is often drawn as:

```
              CONFIDENTIALITY
                    /\
                   /  \
                  /    \
                 /      \
                /________\
        INTEGRITY        AVAILABILITY
```

- *Confidentiality* — protection from unauthorized disclosure.
- *Integrity* — protection from unauthorized modification.
- *Availability* — protection from disruption of access.

**The Parkerian Hexad (≈ 4 marks)**

Donn Parker proposed the **Parkerian Hexad** in 1998, which adds three more properties to the CIA:

1. **Confidentiality** — same as CIA.
2. **Integrity** — same as CIA.
3. **Availability** — same as CIA.
4. **Possession** (or *Control*) — the loss of control over data to an unauthorized party (e.g., a stolen laptop still containing encrypted data — the *possession* is lost even if confidentiality is preserved).
5. **Authenticity** — the property that data genuinely originates from the claimed source.
6. **Utility** — the data is *useful* in its current form (e.g., encrypted data after the key is lost has *no utility* even if it remains confidential).

**Key Differences:**

| Aspect | CIA Triad | Parkerian Hexad |
|--------|-----------|-----------------|
| Number of goals | 3 | 6 |
| Authors | Saltzer-Schroeder / industry consensus | Donn B. Parker (1998) |
| Additional properties | None | Possession, Authenticity, Utility |
| Focus | Confidentiality-centric | Broader operational view |
| Adoption in curriculum | Universal (KTU, NIST) | Used in advanced risk modelling |

**Why the Hexad matters:** It captures *scenarios* the CIA Triad misses — e.g., a stolen but encrypted USB (possession lost, confidentiality preserved) or a corrupted database (utility lost, integrity violated differently).

---

**(b) Define Non-Repudiation and Accountability. Compare and contrast them with a real-world banking example.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Non-Repudiation (≈ 3 marks)**

Non-repudiation guarantees that a *party* involved in a communication or transaction **cannot later deny** their participation. It is a property of the *transaction* and is typically enforced through digital signatures, secure audit trails, and trusted timestamping.

**Accountability (≈ 2 marks)**

Accountability ensures that *every action* on a system is **traceable to a uniquely identified user or process**, enabling audit, forensic investigation, and post-incident response. It is a system-wide property enforced through identity management, logging, and SIEM.

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | Non-Repudiation | Accountability |
|--------|-----------------|----------------|
| **Primary focus** | The *specific transaction* | The *system and its actors* |
| **Trigger** | A signed / acknowledged event | Any logged user action |
| **Mechanism** | Digital signatures, certificates | Audit logs, SIEM, identity tracking |
| **What is provable?** | That *this message* was sent by *this party* | That *this user* did *this action* at *this time* |
| **Failure scenario** | Repudiation of a signed contract | Anonymous malicious insider |

**Real-world Banking Example (≈ 2 marks):**

When a customer transfers ₹50,000 via net-banking:
- **Non-Repudiation** is achieved by digitally signing the transaction with the customer's private key (using a hardware token or mobile app) — the bank stores the signature as irrefutable proof of authorization. The customer *cannot* later claim they did not initiate the transfer.
- **Accountability** is achieved by logging the transaction with a unique user ID, IP address, device fingerprint, timestamp, and geo-location. Even if the customer's credentials were stolen, the audit trail (correlating login time, location, and device) can flag anomalies and support forensic investigation.

The two are **complementary**: non-repudiation binds the *specific event* to the *specific party*; accountability binds the *user* to *their entire activity history*.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes students make in KTU 2024 ESE answers on this topic:**
> 1. **Confusing authentication with authorization** — the most common single error. *Authentication = "Who are you?"*; *Authorization = "What may you do?"*. Examiners explicitly dock **1–2 marks** for this conflation.
> 2. **Omitting examples** — definitions without *real-world scenarios* lose marks. Always pair each goal with at least one example (e.g., banking, healthcare, e-commerce).
> 3. **Ignoring extended goals** — many students answer *only* the CIA Triad and forget authentication / non-repudiation / accountability, losing up to **3–4 marks**.
> 4. **No diagrams in 14-mark answers** — KTU's marking scheme reserves **1–2 marks** for neat labelled diagrams. A Mermaid-style or hand-drawn CIA Triad is *expected*.
> 5. **Skipping the "why"** — writing *what* confidentiality is, but not *why* it matters, leads to partial credit only. Always include the *threat* and the *countermeasure*.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-revision checklist — Security Goals (Module 1, PECDT523)**

- **The 7 security goals:** Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability, Authentication, Authorization, Non-Repudiation, Accountability.
- **CIA Triad** is the **non-negotiable foundation**; the other four are *extensions* built on top.
- **Confidentiality** uses **encryption** (AES, RSA, ECC); quantified by **Shannon entropy** $H(K) = \log_2 \vert \mathcal{K} \vert$.
- **Integrity** uses **hashing & MACs** (SHA-256, HMAC); collision resistance follows the **birthday bound** $\mathcal{O}(2^{n/2})$.
- **Availability** is measured in **uptime SLA nines** (99.9 % ≈ 525.6 min/yr downtime).
- **Authentication** factors: knowledge, possession, inherence, location, behaviour.
- **Authorization** models: DAC, MAC, RBAC, ABAC. Use **principle of least privilege**.
- **Non-Repudiation** is enforced by **digital signatures** (RSA, ECDSA, Ed25519).
- **Accountability** is enforced by **immutable audit logs** and **SIEM**.
- **Trade-offs exist** between goals — perfect confidentiality may hurt availability; strong authentication may hurt usability.
- **Defence in depth**: each goal is enforced in a *specific layer* of the architecture.
- **Parkerian Hexad** extends CIA with **Possession, Authenticity, Utility** — useful in advanced risk modelling.
- For any **14-mark answer**, always include: (i) definitions, (ii) threats, (iii) countermeasures, (iv) real-world example, (v) diagram.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cryptographic Attacks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECDT523 - DATA PRIVACY AND SECURITY > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Cryptographic Attacks ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Cryptographic Attack** is any systematic attempt by an adversary (cryptanalyst) to subvert the security guarantees of a cryptosystem. A cryptographic attack exploits weaknesses in the **algorithm**, the **protocol**, the **key management infrastructure**, or the **physical implementation** to recover plaintext, deduce the secret key, forge signatures, or otherwise violate the **confidentiality**, **integrity**, **authentication**, or **non-repudiation** properties guaranteed by the cryptographic primitive. According to **Kerckhoffs's principle (1883)**, the security of a cryptosystem must reside entirely in the secrecy of the key — *not* in the secrecy of the algorithm. Therefore, all attack models below assume the attacker has **complete knowledge of the algorithm** and seeks to defeat it using varying degrees of access to plaintext, ciphertext, and side-channel information. ### The Four Classical Cryptanalytic Attack Models | Attack Model | What the Attacker Knows | Attacker Capability | |---|---|---| | **Ciphertext-Only Attack (COA)** | Only encrypted messages | Eavesdropping on insecure channel | | **Known-Plaintext Attack (KPA)** | Several (plaintext, ciphertext) pairs | Recovered from archives, leaks | | **Chosen-Plaintext Attack (CPA)** | Ciphertexts for plaintexts of his choice | Encryption oracle access | | **Chosen-Ciphertext Attack (CCA)** | Plaintexts for ciphertexts of his choice | Decryption oracle access (adaptive/non-adaptive) | ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The Five Difficulty Locks** > Imagine a bank vault protected by progressively stronger locks. A **Ciphertext-Only Attack** is like a thief who has only heard the *clicking sounds* of the lock — they must guess the combination from audio alone. A **Known-Plaintext Attack** is like a thief who once saw a bank manager dial the combination, but only from a distance (they know the digits that *usually* get used). A **Chosen-Plaintext Attack** is like a corrupt insider who can walk up to the vault, dial any combination they wish, and listen to the *click* each time. A **Chosen-Ciphertext Attack** is like a locksmith who, given any locked box, can legally open it (perhaps because it's their job) — they exploit this to find the master key. **Brute force** is simply trying every possible key combination until one unlocks the vault. ### Two Fundamental Threat Categories > [!IMPORTANT] > **Active Attack** — Adversary **modifies, inserts, deletes, or replays** messages to deceive the system (e.g., man-in-the-middle, replay, injection). > **Passive Attack** — Adversary only **observes** traffic to learn information (e.g., ciphertext-only, traffic analysis, timing analysis). ### Strongest Possible Adversary: The "Adaptive Chosen-Ciphertext Attack" (CCA2) > [!NOTE] > The **gold standard of modern cryptographic security** is indistinguishability under **IND-CCA2**, where the attacker can submit *any* ciphertext (except the actual challenge) to a decryption oracle, and even adaptively choose queries based on previous answers. Any practical cryptosystem deployed today (TLS 1.3, AES-GCM, RSA-OAEP) is designed to resist this level of adversary. ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Taxonomy of Cryptographic Attacks — Master Classification ``` CRYPTOGRAPHIC ATTACKS ├── A. MATHEMATICAL / ANALYTICAL ATTACKS │ ├── 1. Ciphertext-Only Attack (COA) │ ├── 2. Known-Plaintext Attack (KPA) │ ├── 3. Chosen-Plaintext Attack (CPA) │ ├── 4. Chosen-Ciphertext Attack (CCA / CCA1 / CCA2) │ ├── 5. Adaptive Chosen-Plaintext / Ciphertext │ ├── 6. Brute Force (Exhaustive Key Search) │ ├── 7. Algebraic Attack │ ├── 8. Linear Cryptanalysis (Matsui, 1993) │ ├── 9. Differential Cryptanalysis (Biham–Shamir, 1990) │ └── 10. Integral / Square Attack │ ├── B. PROTOCOL-LEVEL ATTACKS │ ├── 11. Man-in-the-Middle (MITM) │ ├── 12. Replay Attack │ ├── 13. Reflection Attack │ ├── 14. Downgrade Attack (e.g., Logjam, FREAK) │ ├── 15. Bleichenbacher's Attack (RSA-PKCS#1 v1.5 padding oracle) │ └── 16. POODLE / Padding Oracle Attack │ ├── C. HASH-SPECIFIC ATTACKS │ ├── 17. Birthday Attack / Collision Attack │ ├── 18. Preimage Attack (1st & 2nd) │ ├── 19. Length-Extension Attack │ ├── 20. Rainbow Table Attack │ └── 21. Hash Collision (e.g., SHAttered, 2017) │ ├── D. IMPLEMENTATION / SIDE-CHANNEL ATTACKS │ ├── 22. Timing Attack (Kocher, 1996) │ ├── 23. Power Analysis (SPA / DPA) │ ├── 24. Electromagnetic (EM) Analysis │ ├── 25. Cache / Flush+Reload Attack │ ├── 26. Acoustic Cryptanalysis │ └── 27. Fault Injection (Glitching, Voltage) │ └── E. SOCIAL / SYSTEMIC ATTACKS ├── 28. Dictionary Attack ├── 29. Rubber Hose / Bribery ├── 30. Insider Threat / Key Compromise └── 31. Malware / Cold Boot Attack ``` ### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Major Attack Types #### 2.2.1 Brute Force (Exhaustive Key Search) 1. Attacker intercepts **one** valid (plaintext, ciphertext) pair. 2. Systematically iterates through every possible key $k \in \mathcal{K}$. 3. For each candidate $k_i$, computes $C_i = E_{k_i}(P)$. 4. Accepts $k_i$ as the correct key if $C_i = C$. **Work factor** of a perfectly secure cipher equals the **key space size**: $W = 2^k$ operations for a $k$-bit key. #### 2.2.2 Differential Cryptanalysis (Biham–Shamir) 1. Attacker chooses two plaintexts $P$ and $P'$ with a *specific* XOR difference $\Delta P = P \oplus P'$. 2. Requests encryption of both and observes the resulting ciphertext difference $\Delta C = C \oplus C'$. 3. With many such pairs, the attacker deduces statistical biases in how $\Delta P$ propagates through the cipher's S-boxes and rounds. 4. By analyzing these biases across rounds, the round keys are recovered. #### 2.2.3 Linear Cryptanalysis (Matsui) 1. Attacker finds *linear approximations* linking plaintext bits, ciphertext bits, and key bits with probability $p \neq \frac{1}{2}$. 2. Using **Matsui's Piling-Up Lemma**, the bias is amplified across multiple rounds. 3. After collecting $\mathcal{O}(1/\epsilon^2)$ known plaintexts (where $\epsilon$ is the linear bias), the secret key bits are recovered. #### 2.2.4 Birthday Attack on Hash Functions 1. Attacker generates approximately $\sqrt{N}$ random messages (where $N = 2^n$ is the hash output space for $n$-bit hash). 2. Stores their hashes in a sorted table. 3. Looks for any two messages $M_1, M_2$ producing identical hashes: $H(M_1) = H(M_2)$. 4. Such a **collision** is found after roughly $1.25 \cdot 2^{n/2}$ evaluations (vs. $2^n$ for preimage). #### 2.2.5 Man-in-the-Middle (MITM) Attack 1. **Alice → Mallory ← Bob**: Mallory sits transparently between Alice and Bob. 2. Mallory intercepts Alice's public key $PK_A$ and replaces it with *her own* public key $PK_M$. 3. Bob encrypts his session key with $PK_M$ (thinking it is $PK_A$). 4. Mallory decrypts, recovers the session key, re-encrypts with the real $PK_A$, and forwards to Alice. 5. **Defeated by digital certificates** (PKI / X.509) that bind identity to public key. ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Description | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Brute force work** | $W = 2^{k}$ | Operations to break a $k$-bit key | | 2 | **Average brute force** | $W_{avg} = 2^{k-1}$ | On average, half the keyspace | | 3 | **Time to break** | $T = \dfrac{2^{k-1}}{R}$ | $R$ = keys tested per second | | 4 | **Birthday probability (collision)** | $P(n) \approx 1 - e^{-n^2 / (2N)}$ | $n$ items, $N$ possible outputs | | 5 | **Birthday threshold** | $n \approx 1.177\sqrt{N}$ | For $P = 0.5$ collision | | 6 | **Hash security (collision)** | $2^{n/2}$ operations | For $n$-bit hash (birthday bound) | | 7 | **Hash security (preimage)** | $2^{n}$ operations | For $n$-bit hash | | 8 | **Matsui's Piling-Up Lemma** | $\epsilon = 2^{n-1}\prod_{i=1}^{n}(2\epsilon_i)$ | Combined linear bias | | 9 | **Avalanche effect bound** | Hamming distance $\approx \dfrac{n}{2}$ | Each bit flips with prob. 0.5 | | 10 | **Information-theoretic security (Shannon)** | $H(K) \geq H(M)$ | Key entropy ≥ message entropy | | 11 | **Effective key length vs. attack** | $k_{eff} = k - \log_2(N_{attempts})$ | Reduces key bits by attack samples | | 12 | **Generic birthday complexity** | $\mathcal{O}(2^{n/2})$ time, $\mathcal{O}(2^{n/2})$ space | Collision search | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Memorization Hint for KTU Board Exams**: The **birthday paradox reduces the effective security of an $n$-bit hash from $2^n$ to $2^{n/2}$** for collision resistance. This is *why* SHA-1 (160 bits → 80-bit collision security) was deprecated after the SHAttered attack in 2017 found a practical collision in $2^{63}$ operations (well below $2^{80}$). ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility | Domain | Attack Studied | Why It Matters | |---|---|---| | **TLS/HTTPS (Web Security)** | MITM, Bleichenbacher, POODLE, DROWN | All browsers must defend against downgrade and padding-oracle attacks | | **Blockchain / Bitcoin** | Birthday attack on addresses, 51% attack on mining | SHA-256 collision resistance is foundational to PoW security | | **Smart Cards / IoT** | Power analysis, fault injection, timing | Hardware-constrained devices leak physical information | | **PKI / Digital Signatures** | Chosen-message attack on RSA-PSS, Bleichenbacher | Certificate forgery breaks the entire trust chain | | **Password Storage** | Dictionary, rainbow table, brute force | Determines salting, stretching (bcrypt, Argon2) parameters | | **Cloud Storage** | Side-channel, cache timing | Shared hardware enables cross-VM attacks like Flush+Reload | ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Brute Force Average Work Factor > [!NOTE] > **Problem**: A system uses a 56-bit DES key. An attacker can test $10^{9}$ keys per second. How long, on average, will it take to recover the key? **Step 1 — Identify the key space.** $$\text{Key space} = 2^{56} \approx 7.2 \times 10^{16}$$ This is the total number of candidate keys that must be searched in the worst case. **Step 2 — Apply average-case assumption.** By symmetry, the correct key is equally likely to be in any position of the search. On average, the attacker tests **half** the keyspace before success. $$W_{avg} = \frac{2^{56}}{2} = 2^{55} \approx 3.6 \times 10^{16}$$ **Step 3 — Convert to wall-clock time.** Divide the average work by the testing rate $R = 10^{9}$ keys/sec. $$T_{avg} = \frac{2^{55}}{10^{9}} = \frac{3.6 \times 10^{16}}{10^{9}} \approx 3.6 \times 10^{7} \text{ seconds}$$ **Step 4 — Convert to days.** $$T_{avg} = \frac{3.6 \times 10^{7}}{86400} \approx 416 \text{ days} \approx 1.14 \text{ years}$$ **Conclusion**: With a 1 billion keys/sec engine, brute-forcing a single DES key takes ~1.14 years on average — which is why 3DES and DES have been retired, while AES-128 ($2^{127}$ operations) is computationally infeasible at any current hardware speed. --- ### 3.2 Derivation: Birthday Attack Complexity > [!NOTE] > **Problem**: A 128-bit hash function $H$ is used for digital signatures. What is the minimum number of messages an attacker must hash to find a collision with probability ≥ 0.5? **Step 1 — Set up the birthday probability formula.** For $n$ randomly chosen hash outputs in a space of size $N = 2^{128}$, the probability of *at least one* collision is: $$P(\text{collision}) \approx 1 - e^{-n^2 / (2N)}$$ **Step 2 — Solve for $n$ when $P = 0.5$.** $$1 - e^{-n^2 / (2N)} = 0.5$$ $$e^{-n^2 / (2N)} = 0.5$$ Take the natural logarithm of both sides: $$-\frac{n^2}{2N} = \ln(0.5) = -0.6931$$ $$n^2 = 2N \cdot \ln(2) = 1.3863 \cdot N$$ $$n = \sqrt{1.3863 \cdot N} = 1.1774\sqrt{N}$$ **Step 3 — Substitute $N = 2^{128}$.** $$n = 1.1774 \cdot \sqrt{2^{128}} = 1.1774 \cdot 2^{64}$$ **Step 4 — Approximate.** $$n \approx 1.18 \times 2^{64} \approx 2.17 \times 10^{19} \text{ messages}$$ **Conclusion**: The 128-bit hash provides only **64-bit collision security** under the birthday attack — a critical reason why hash functions must use ≥ 256-bit outputs to provide 128-bit collision resistance in modern designs (e.g., SHA-256, SHA-3-256). --- ### 3.3 Derivation: Matsui's Piling-Up Lemma > [!NOTE] > **Problem**: A 4-round block cipher is approximated by 4 independent linear approximations with biases $\epsilon_1, \epsilon_2, \epsilon_3, \epsilon_4$. Compute the combined bias using Matsui's Piling-Up Lemma. **Step 1 — Recall the lemma.** For $n$ independent linear approximations, each with bias $\epsilon_i$ (where $\epsilon_i = p_i - 1/2$ and $p_i$ is the probability the approximation holds), the **combined bias** is: $$\epsilon_{total} = 2^{n-1} \prod_{i=1}^{n} \epsilon_i$$ **Step 2 — Example computation.** Let $\epsilon_1 = \epsilon_2 = \epsilon_3 = \epsilon_4 = 2^{-5}$ (i.e., bias of $1/32$). For $n = 4$: $$\epsilon_{total} = 2^{4-1} \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{4} 2^{-5} = 2^{3} \cdot 2^{-20} = 2^{-17}$$ **Step 3 — Required plaintexts (Matsui's bound).** The number of known plaintexts needed to exploit this bias is approximately: $$N_{KP} \approx \frac{1}{\epsilon_{total}^{2}} = 2^{34}$$ **Step 4 — Interpretation.** Even though each individual round has a weak bias, the piling-up lemma amplifies it into a usable cryptanalytic advantage with manageable data complexity. This is the foundational principle behind Matsui's 1993 attack on FEAL and DES. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Attack Feasibility vs. Key Size > [!IMPORTANT] > **The table below is a HIGH-YIELD KTU board exam reference.** Examiners frequently ask students to justify why AES-256 is "secure for the next 50+ years." | Key/Hash Size | Brute Force Work | Birthday Work | Practical Break Status (2024) | |---|---|---|---| | **40-bit** (export-grade RC4) | $2^{39} \approx 5.5 \times 10^{11}$ | N/A | Trivially broken in hours | | **56-bit** (DES) | $2^{55} \approx 3.6 \times 10^{16}$ | N/A | Broken in days (EFF Deep Crack, 1998) | | **80-bit** (Skipjack, SHA-1 collision) | $2^{79}$ | $2^{40}$ | SHA-1 broken in 2017 (SHAttered) | | **112-bit** (3DES, two-key) | $2^{111}$ (Meet-in-middle: $2^{80}$) | N/A | Deprecated by NIST (2023) | | **128-bit** (AES-128, SHA-256 collision) | $2^{127}$ | $2^{64}$ | Secure; collision impractical | | **192-bit** (AES-192) | $2^{191}$ | N/A | Secure | | **256-bit** (AES-256, SHA-512 collision) | $2^{255}$ | $2^{128}$ | Quantum-vulnerable (Grover halves it) | ### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Attack-Model Hierarchy (Strength Ordering) > [!NOTE] > **Stronger ↓ attacks subsume weaker attacks.** If a cipher resists CCA2, it automatically resists CPA, KPA, and COA. | Strength Level | Attack Model | Defended By | |---|---|---| | **Weakest** | Ciphertext-Only (COA) | All classical ciphers | | ↓ | Known-Plaintext (KPA) | All modern ciphers | | ↓ | Chosen-Plaintext (CPA) | IND-CPA secure ciphers (AES-CTR, RSA-OAEP) | | ↓ | Non-adaptive CCA (CCA1) | RSA-OAEP, AES-GCM | | **Strongest** | Adaptive CCA (CCA2) | RSA-OAEP, AES-GCM, ChaCha20-Poly1305 | ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Attack-Model Hierarchy Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TB Start([Adversary Goal: Break Cryptosystem]) --> Knowledge{What does
attacker know?} Knowledge -->|Only Ciphertext| COA[Ciphertext-Only Attack
COA
Weakest] Knowledge -->|Known Pairs| KPA[Known-Plaintext Attack
KPA] Knowledge -->|Encryption Oracle| CPA[Chosen-Plaintext Attack
CPA] Knowledge -->|Decryption Oracle| CCA[Chosen-Ciphertext Attack
CCA] CCA --> CCA1[CCA1
Lunchtime] CCA --> CCA2[CCA2
Adaptive
Strongest] COA --> Method1[Method:
Brute Force
Frequency Analysis] KPA --> Method2[Method:
Differential
Linear Cryptanalysis] CPA --> Method3[Method:
Adaptive Plaintext
Querying] CCA1 --> Method4[Method:
Padding Oracle
Bleichenbacher] CCA2 --> Method5[Method:
Adaptive Decryption
Queries] Method1 --> Defense1[Defense: Strong Key Size
128 bits minimum] Method2 --> Defense2[Defense: AES, SPN Networks
Proven IND-CPA] Method3 --> Defense3[Defense: IND-CPA Schemes
Randomized Encryption] Method4 --> Defense4[Defense: Authenticated Encryption
RSA-OAEP, AES-GCM] Method5 --> Defense5[Defense: IND-CCA2 Schemes
Modern TLS 1.3 AEAD] Defense1 --> End([Cryptosystem Secure]) Defense2 --> End Defense3 --> End Defense4 --> End Defense5 --> End style Start fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#000,color:#fff style COA fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#000 style KPA fill:#fde68a,stroke:#000 style CPA fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#000 style CCA fill:#f59e0b,stroke:#000 style CCA1 fill:#ea580c,stroke:#000,color:#fff style CCA2 fill:#b91c1c,stroke:#000,color:#fff style End fill:#16a34a,stroke:#000,color:#fff ``` ### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Man-in-the-Middle Attack ```mermaid flowchart LR Alice[Alice
Legitimate Sender] -->|1. Request PK_Bob| Net1((Public Network)) Net1 -->|Intercepted| Mallory[Mallory
MITM Attacker] Mallory -->|2. Forward Real Request| Bob[Bob
Legitimate Receiver] Bob -->|3. Send PK_Bob| Mallory Mallory -->|4. Send PK_Mallory| Net1 Net1 -->|5. Forged PK delivered| Alice Alice -->|6. Encrypt SK with PK_Mallory| Net1 Net1 -->|Intercepted| Mallory Mallory -->|7. Decrypt with SK_Mallory| Mallory Mallory -->|8. Re-encrypt SK with PK_Bob| Net1 Net1 -->|9. Real encrypted SK| Bob Bob -->|10. Decrypt with SK_Bob| Bob Bob -->|11. Session established| Mallory Mallory -->|12. Proxy relay traffic| Alice style Alice fill:#3b82f6,stroke:#000,color:#fff style Bob fill:#3b82f6,stroke:#000,color:#fff style Mallory fill:#dc2626,stroke:#000,color:#fff style Net1 fill:#f3f4f6,stroke:#000 ``` ### 4.3 Side-Channel Attack Functional Flow ```mermaid flowchart TB Device[Target Cryptographic
Device - Smart Card] --> SideChannel[Physical Side Channels] SideChannel --> Timing[Timing
Variation] SideChannel --> Power[Power
Consumption] SideChannel --> EM[Electromagnetic
Emission] SideChannel --> Acoustic[Acoustic
Signals] SideChannel --> Cache[Cache
Access Pattern] Timing --> Process1[Statistical Analysis
Kocher 1996] Power --> Process2[Simple Power Analysis
SPA / Differential Power
Analysis DPA] EM --> Process3[Signal Processing
EM emanation capture] Acoustic --> Process4[FFT / Spectral
Analysis] Cache --> Process5[Flush+Reload
Prime+Probe
Spectre / Meltdown] Process1 --> Key[Recover Secret Key Bits] Process2 --> Key Process3 --> Key Process4 --> Key Process5 --> Key Key --> Mitigation[Countermeasures
Constant-time code
Masking / Blinding
Noise injection
Cache flushing] style Device fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#000,color:#fff style Key fill:#b91c1c,stroke:#000,color:#fff style Mitigation fill:#15803d,stroke:#000,color:#fff ``` ### 4.4 Birthday Attack Process Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB Goal[Goal: Find Collision
H M1 = H M2] --> Phase1[Phase 1: Generate Set] Phase1 --> Gen1[Pick M_1, compute H M_1] Gen1 --> Gen2[Pick M_2, compute H M_2] Gen2 --> GenN[Continue until n messages
where n approx 1.177 sqrt N] GenN --> Phase2[Phase 2: Sort and Search] Phase2 --> Sort[Sort hashes in O n log n] Sort --> Search[Look for duplicates] Search --> Found{Collision
Found?} Found -->|No| More[Generate more messages
Double n] More --> Phase1 Found -->|Yes| Forge[Forge signature /
Malicious duplicate] style Goal fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#000,color:#fff style Forge fill:#b91c1c,stroke:#000,color:#fff style Found fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#000 ``` ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### PART A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] Define the four classical attack models on a cryptosystem. Which is the strongest, and why?** **Model Answer:** A cryptographic attack model describes the capability of an adversary attempting to break a cryptosystem. The four classical models are: 1. **Ciphertext-Only Attack (COA)**: The attacker has access only to one or more encrypted messages (ciphertexts) and must recover the corresponding plaintexts or the key. 2. **Known-Plaintext Attack (KPA)**: The attacker possesses several pairs of plaintext and their corresponding ciphertexts, and uses this knowledge to deduce the encryption key or an algorithm to decrypt new ciphertexts. 3. **Chosen-Plaintext Attack (CPA)**: The attacker can choose arbitrary plaintexts and obtain their corresponding ciphertexts (i.e., has access to an encryption oracle), enabling adaptive analysis. 4. **Chosen-Ciphertext Attack (CCA)**: The attacker can submit chosen ciphertexts to a decryption oracle and receive the decrypted plaintexts. In the **adaptive variant (CCA2)**, queries may depend on previously seen answers. > **The strongest model is the Adaptive Chosen-Ciphertext Attack (CCA2)**, because it grants the adversary maximum oracle access (both encryption and adaptive decryption). Any cryptosystem proven secure under IND-CCA2 is automatically secure under all weaker models (CPA, KPA, COA) — this is the standard notion of security for TLS 1.3, RSA-OAEP, and AES-GCM. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024] Explain the "Birthday Attack" on hash functions. Why does it reduce the effective security of an n-bit hash to n/2 bits?** **Model Answer:** The **Birthday Attack** exploits the *Birthday Paradox* — the counter-intuitive statistical fact that among $\sqrt{N}$ randomly chosen items from a space of size $N$, the probability of a collision (two items being identical) reaches ~50%. **Procedure**: 1. The adversary generates approximately $1.177\sqrt{N}$ distinct messages. 2. Computes the hash of each using an $n$-bit hash function (so $N = 2^n$). 3. Sorts the hash outputs and looks for any two that are equal: $H(M_1) = H(M_2)$. 4. Such a **collision** is found with probability > 0.5 in $\mathcal{O}(2^{n/2})$ time. **Why n/2?**: Substituting $n = 2^{n/2}$ candidate messages into the birthday probability formula: $$P(\text{collision}) \approx 1 - e^{-(2^{n/2})^2 / (2 \cdot 2^n)} = 1 - e^{-1/2} \approx 0.39$$ Increasing the number of probes to $1.177 \cdot 2^{n/2}$ raises the probability to exactly 0.5. Therefore, finding a *collision* requires only $2^{n/2}$ operations, halving the effective bit security from $n$ bits to $n/2$ bits. This is why **SHA-256 (collision resistance ≈ $2^{128}$)** is preferred over **SHA-1 (collision resistance ≈ $2^{80}$, practically broken in 2017)**. --- ### PART B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) --- #### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]** **(a)** Explain the **Man-in-the-Middle (MITM) attack** with a clear block diagram. Discuss the role of **digital certificates and PKI** in preventing this attack. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With reference to **Differential Cryptanalysis**, explain how Biham and Shamir's attack on DES works. Compare it with **Linear Cryptanalysis (Matsui)**. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for Question A (a):** The **Man-in-the-Middle (MITM) attack** is an active cryptanalytic attack in which the adversary, *Mallory*, secretly intercepts and possibly alters the communication between two parties, *Alice* and *Bob*, who believe they are communicating directly with each other. **Block Diagram** (see Section 4.2 above for the full Mermaid diagram): **Step-by-Step Attack Sequence:** 1. **Setup**: Alice and Bob wish to exchange a secret session key $SK$ over an insecure network. Bob publishes his public key $PK_{Bob}$ in a directory. 2. **Public Key Substitution**: Mallory intercepts Alice's request for Bob's public key. Mallory sends Alice **her own public key $PK_{Mallory}$** in place of $PK_{Bob}$. 3. **Session Key Encryption by Alice**: Unsuspecting, Alice generates a fresh session key $SK$, encrypts it with $PK_{Mallory}$ (thinking it is $PK_{Bob}$), and sends $E_{PK_{Mallory}}(SK)$ to Bob. 4. **Decryption by Mallory**: Mallory intercepts the encrypted session key, decrypts it using her corresponding private key $SK_{Mallory}$, and recovers the session key $SK$. 5. **Re-encryption to Bob**: Mallory re-encrypts $SK$ using the *real* $PK_{Bob}$ and forwards the message to Bob. 6. **Successful Deception**: Both Alice and Bob now share the same session key $SK$, but Mallory also knows it. She can passively eavesdrop on, or actively modify, every subsequent encrypted message. **Role of Digital Certificates and PKI in Prevention:** A **Digital Certificate** is a cryptographically signed document (typically following the **X.509 v3** standard) issued by a trusted third party called a **Certificate Authority (CA)**. The certificate binds an entity's identity (e.g., `bob.example.com`) to its public key. When Alice requests Bob's public key, the response is a certificate: $$\text{Cert}_{Bob} = \text{Sign}_{SK_{CA}}(\text{Bob's ID}, PK_{Bob}, \text{Validity Period}, \text{Serial No.})$$ Alice verifies this certificate using the CA's **public key** (which she already trusts, being pre-installed in her browser/OS). If the signature is valid, the certificate has not expired, and the CA has not revoked it (checked via **CRL** or **OCSP**), then Alice is assured that $PK_{Bob}$ genuinely belongs to Bob. **Key Benefit**: Mallory cannot forge Bob's certificate because she does not possess the CA's private signing key. Thus, the entire MITM attack collapses at the certificate-verification step. This is the foundation of **HTTPS (TLS)** security on the modern web. --- #### **Model Solution for Question A (b):** **Differential Cryptanalysis (Biham–Shamir, 1990):** Differential cryptanalysis is a chosen-plaintext attack that analyzes how specific differences (XORs) in plaintext pairs propagate through the rounds of a block cipher. **Procedure:** 1. The attacker selects two plaintexts $P$ and $P'$ with a *carefully chosen* XOR difference $\Delta P = P \oplus P'$. 2. The attacker requests encryption of both, obtaining ciphertexts $C$ and $C'$ (with difference $\Delta C = C \oplus C'$). 3. For each round $i$ of the cipher, the attacker studies the *differential propagation* — the XOR difference entering and leaving each round. The substitution (S-box) layer introduces a *non-uniform* distribution: certain output differences are more probable than others for a given input difference. 4. By collecting $\mathcal{O}(1/p)$ chosen plaintext pairs (where $p$ is the highest-probability differential), the attacker can statistically recover the *round keys* and hence the master key. 5. The attack was shown to break DES with $2^{47}$ chosen plaintexts (theoretical) — close to brute force — but with reduced-round variants of DES, it was devastating. **Linear Cryptanalysis (Matsui, 1993):** Linear cryptanalysis is a known-plaintext attack that finds *linear approximations* to the non-linear operations of a cipher, allowing the attacker to express the secret key bits as a linear combination of plaintext and ciphertext bits. **Procedure:** 1. The attacker identifies linear expressions such as: $$\bigoplus_{i \in \mathcal{P}} P_i \oplus \bigoplus_{j \in \mathcal{C}} C_j \oplus \bigoplus_{k \in \mathcal{K}} K_k = 0$$ that hold with probability $p \neq 0.5$ (bias $\epsilon = p - 0.5$). 2. The **Matsui Piling-Up Lemma** combines $n$ independent linear approximations to amplify the total bias: $$\epsilon_{total} = 2^{n-1} \prod_{i=1}^{n} \epsilon_i$$ 3. After collecting $\mathcal{O}(1/\epsilon_{total}^2)$ known plaintexts, the attacker recovers the key bits via statistical hypothesis testing. Matsui used this to break **DES with $2^{43}$ known plaintexts** — the most efficient known-plaintext attack on full DES. **Comparison Table:** | Feature | Differential Cryptanalysis | Linear Cryptanalysis | |---|---|---| | **Year / Authors** | 1990, Biham & Shamir | 1993, Matsui | | **Attack Type** | Chosen-Plaintext | Known-Plaintext | | **Core Idea** | XOR differences of pairs | Linear approximations of bits | | **Mathematical Tool** | Difference Distribution Table of S-boxes | Piling-Up Lemma, bias statistics | | **DES Complexity** | $2^{47}$ chosen plaintexts | $2^{43}$ known plaintexts | | **Effect on Modern Ciphers** | Resistance built into AES S-box design | Resistance built into AES MixColumns design | > **Conclusion**: Both attacks revolutionized modern cipher design. Every block cipher standardized today (AES, Camellia, ChaCha20) is mathematically proven or tested against both attack paradigms, giving rise to the **Wide-Trail Strategy** in AES. --- #### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2023]** **(a)** Explain in detail **Brute Force Attack** and **Dictionary Attack** with suitable examples. For a 64-bit key cipher, calculate the **average time to break** if the attacker can test $10^{12}$ keys per second. **(7 marks)** **(b)** What is a **Side-Channel Attack**? Explain **Timing Attack** and **Power Analysis Attack** (SPA and DPA). How are they mitigated? **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for Question B (a):** **Brute Force Attack (Exhaustive Key Search):** A brute force attack is the most fundamental cryptanalytic technique in which the attacker systematically tries every possible key in the keyspace until the correct one is found. **Procedure:** 1. The attacker intercepts a ciphertext $C$ corresponding to a known plaintext $P$ (e.g., HTTP headers, file magic numbers). 2. The attacker iterates over the keyspace $\mathcal{K} = \{k_1, k_2, \ldots, k_{2^k}\}$ for a $k$-bit key. 3. For each candidate $k_i$, the attacker computes $C_i = E_{k_i}(P)$. 4. If $C_i = C$, the attacker has found the key $k_i$ and can decrypt all subsequent messages. **Example:** DES uses a 56-bit key, giving $2^{56} \approx 7.2 \times 10^{16}$ possible keys. The EFF's "Deep Crack" machine in 1998 broke DES in 56 hours by brute force at $9.2 \times 10^{10}$ keys/sec. **Dictionary Attack:** A dictionary attack exploits the human tendency to choose weak, predictable passwords. The attacker uses a precompiled list of common passwords, words, names, and their variations. **Procedure:** 1. The attacker obtains a password hash database (e.g., from a leaked database). 2. The attacker hashes every word in the dictionary using the same hash function. 3. A match between a dictionary hash and a database hash reveals the plaintext password. **Example:** The 2009 **RockYou breach** exposed 32 million plaintext passwords — `123456`, `password`, and `qwerty` were in the top 5, easily defeated by a dictionary of <1 MB. **Defenses:** Salt (random per-user nonce), key stretching (bcrypt, Argon2, scrypt), and minimum complexity policies. **Calculation for 64-bit Key:** $$W_{avg} = 2^{64-1} = 2^{63}$$ $$T_{avg} = \frac{2^{63}}{10^{12}} = \frac{9.22 \times 10^{18}}{10^{12}} = 9.22 \times 10^{6} \text{ seconds}$$ $$T_{avg} = \frac{9.22 \times 10^{6}}{86400} \approx 106.7 \text{ days}$$ **Conclusion**: A 64-bit key is breakable in ~3.5 months with a fast engine, which is why modern standards require ≥ 128-bit keys. --- #### **Model Solution for Question B (b):** **Side-Channel Attack:** > [!IMPORTANT] > A **side-channel attack** is a cryptanalytic technique that exploits *physical characteristics* of the cryptographic implementation rather than algorithmic weaknesses. The attacker measures unintended information leakage — timing, power, electromagnetic radiation, sound, or cache behavior — to extract the secret key. **Timing Attack (Kocher, 1996):** In a **timing attack**, the attacker precisely measures the *time* taken for a cryptographic operation (e.g., modular exponentiation in RSA) to execute. Different key bits cause slight variations in execution time due to conditional branches, cache hits, or multiplication latency. **Procedure:** 1. The attacker triggers many cryptographic operations with the same input. 2. Statistically correlates small timing differences with individual key bits. 3. Over thousands of samples, the secret key bits are recovered bit-by-bit. **Example:** RSA modular exponentiation performs a square-and-multiply loop; when a key bit is 1, an extra multiplication occurs, slightly increasing the time. Kocher's 1996 paper demonstrated full RSA private key recovery from timing data over an SSL connection. **Power Analysis Attack:** **Simple Power Analysis (SPA):** The attacker directly observes the power consumption trace of a smart card or hardware security module during a single cryptographic operation. Visual inspection of the trace reveals distinct patterns corresponding to different operations (e.g., squaring vs. multiplying in modular exponentiation), directly leaking the key. **Differential Power Analysis (DPA):** A more powerful statistical technique. The attacker: 1. Collects thousands of power traces $P_i(t)$ for different inputs $x_i$. 2. Partitions the traces into two sets based on a guessed *key bit* (or *Hamming weight of intermediate value*). 3. Computes the average power of each set: $\bar{P}_0(t)$ and $\bar{P}_1(t)$. 4. Examines the *difference of means* $\Delta(t) = \bar{P}_0(t) - \bar{P}_1(t)$. 5. A significant spike at a particular $t$ confirms the key guess. DPA was published by Kocher, Jaffe, and Jun in 1999 and broke virtually all unprotected smart-card implementations. **Mitigation Strategies:** | Attack | Mitigation | |---|---| | Timing | **Constant-time code** (no data-dependent branches), uniform cache access | | SPA | **Balanced operations** (always square and multiply), dummy instruction insertion | | DPA | **Masking** (random intermediate values), **blinding** (randomize inputs), noise injection, random clock jitter | | Cache | **Cache flushing**, memory access pattern obfuscation (e.g., ScatterAdd, ORAM) | | EM | **Faraday cage**, signal shielding, randomized signal patterns | > **Engineering Note**: Modern hardware security modules (HSMs) and TPM chips integrate these defenses at the silicon level. AES-NI instructions in x86 CPUs are designed to be *constant-time* to resist timing attacks automatically. --- ### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in Cryptographic Attacks Questions:** > > 1. **Confusing CPA and CCA**: Many students write "chosen-ciphertext" when they mean "chosen-plaintext". Remember: **P**laintext → encryption oracle (CPA); **C**iphertext → decryption oracle (CCA). > > 2. **Forgetting the "Adaptive" in CCA2**: In CCA1 (lunchtime attack), the attacker can only query the decryption oracle *before* seeing the challenge ciphertext. In CCA2, queries can continue *adaptively* after seeing the challenge. This distinction is worth 1-2 marks. > > 3. **Birthday Math Error**: Students often write $2^{n/2}$ for hash output length $n$, but forget to multiply by $\sqrt{\ln 4} \approx 1.177$. Examiners *will* deduct marks if you give $2^{64}$ instead of $1.177 \times 2^{64}$ for a 128-bit hash. > > 4. **Brute Force Average vs. Worst Case**: The *average* time is $2^{k-1}$ operations; the *worst case* is $2^k$. KTU board exams expect you to use the **average** unless explicitly asked otherwise. > > 5. **MITM without Certificates**: Many students describe MITM but forget the **defense**. Always include a discussion of PKI/X.509 certificates — this is where easy marks are awarded. > > 6. **Side-Channel vs. Mathematical Attacks**: Side-channel attacks are *implementation* attacks; they work even on theoretically perfect ciphers. Don't confuse them with mathematical attacks on the algorithm itself. --- ### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!NOTE] > **HIGH-DENSITY KTU REVISION SUMMARY — Cryptographic Attacks** - **Kerckhoffs's Principle** — Security lies in the key, not the algorithm. - **Four Attack Models** (in order of strength): **COA < KPA < CPA < CCA**. The strongest is **CCA2** (Adaptive Chosen-Ciphertext). - **Brute Force Work** = $2^k$ (worst) or $2^{k-1}$ (average) for $k$-bit key. - **Birthday Paradox** reduces collision security of $n$-bit hash from $2^n$ → $2^{n/2}$ operations. - **Birthday threshold** for 50% collision: $n = 1.177 \cdot 2^{n/2}$. - **Matsui's Piling-Up Lemma**: $\epsilon_{total} = 2^{n-1} \prod \epsilon_i$. - **Differential Cryptanalysis** (Biham–Shamir, 1990) is a **chosen-plaintext** attack using **XOR differences**. - **Linear Cryptanalysis** (Matsui, 1993) is a **known-plaintext** attack using **linear approximations**. - **DES broken** by differential in $2^{47}$ chosen plaintexts, by linear in $2^{43}$ known plaintexts. - **Man-in-the-Middle (MITM)** is defeated by **digital certificates + PKI (X.509)**. - **Replay Attack** is defeated by **nonces, timestamps, and sequence numbers**. - **Padding Oracle Attacks** (Bleichenbacher, POODLE) target **unauthenticated RSA-PKCS#1 v1.5 / CBC mode** — defeated by **authenticated encryption (AES-GCM, ChaCha20-Poly1305)**. - **Side-Channel Attacks** exploit **timing, power, EM, cache, or acoustic** leakage — mitigated by **constant-time code, masking, blinding, noise**. - **Dictionary Attack** defeated by **salting + key stretching (bcrypt, Argon2)**. - **Rainbow Tables** defeated by **per-user random salts**. - **Hash collisions** in MD5 (2004, Wang et al.) and SHA-1 (2017, SHAttered) prove that weak hashes are practically broken — always use **SHA-256 or SHA-3-256** for collision resistance ≥ $2^{128}$. - **Replay Attack** sequence: (1) Sniff valid signed message (2) Re-send later (3) System re-validates original signature. - **Quantum Vulnerability**: Shor's algorithm breaks RSA/ECC in polynomial time; Grover's algorithm halves symmetric key strength (so use AES-256 for 128-bit post-quantum security). - **AES-256** is the de-facto standard for symmetric security beyond 2030. - **For 14-mark KTU answers**, always: (1) Define → (2) Diagram → (3) Step-by-step procedure → (4) Real-world example → (5) Defense/countermeasure. - **Memorize**: $2^{10} \approx 10^3$, $2^{20} \approx 10^6$, $2^{30} \approx 10^9$, $2^{40} \approx 10^{12}$, $2^{50} \approx 10^{15}$ — these shortcuts save time in numerical problems. - **NIST Recommendation (2024)**: AES-128, AES-192, AES-256 for symmetric; SHA-256/384/512 for hashing; RSA ≥ 2048-bit, ECDSA ≥ 256-bit for asymmetric; HMAC-SHA-256 for MAC; ChaCha20-Poly1305 for AEAD.

topics.title: Services and Mechanisms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT523 - DATA PRIVACY AND SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Services and Mechanisms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The OSI Security Architecture (X.800)

The foundation of modern data privacy and security terminology is laid down by the **ITU-T Recommendation X.800 (Security Architecture for Open Systems Interconnection)**. This standard defines a unified vocabulary that every security engineer, cryptographer, and network designer uses worldwide. The two pillars of this architecture are:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Security Services** = *What* the system does to protect data (the goal).
> **Security Mechanisms** = *How* the system achieves that protection (the tool).

| Term | Formal KTU Definition (X.800) |
|---|---|
| **Security Service** | A processing or communication service that enhances the security of the data processing systems and the information transfers of an organization, provided by the OSI layers. |
| **Security Mechanism** | A specific technique, algorithm, or procedure designed to detect, prevent, or recover from a security attack. |

## 1.2 Intuition — The "Bank Vault vs. Security Guard" Analogy

Imagine a bank that protects its valuables:

- The **promise** the bank makes to its customers is: *"Your money is safe, your identity is verified, and your transactions are confidential."* These promises are the **Security Services** — they describe *what* the customer gets.
- The **tools** the bank uses to deliver those promises are: *steel vault doors (encipherment), CCTV cameras (audit/data integrity), ID checks at the door (authentication), and alarm systems (access control)*. These tools are the **Security Mechanisms** — they describe *how* the promise is kept.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** A single promise (service) can be delivered by several tools (mechanisms), and one tool (mechanism) can help deliver several promises (services). This is called a **Many-to-Many (M:N) relationship**.

## 1.3 The Three Core Categories of X.800

X.800 organizes security into three universal categories:

1. **Security Attacks** — Any action that compromises the security of information.
2. **Security Mechanisms** — The countermeasures (the tools).
3. **Security Services** — The protective deliverables (the outcomes).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU 2024 Exams:** Always frame your answer in this triadic structure. Examiners reward students who explicitly classify a concept as an *Attack*, a *Mechanism*, or a *Service*.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in this Topic

- **Confidentiality level**: Measured in *bits of entropy* (e.g., **128-bit**, **256-bit** key strength).
- **Integrity level**: Measured by *collision resistance probability* in hash functions.
- **Authentication strength**: Measured by *false acceptance rate (FAR)* and *false rejection rate (FRR)*.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Primary Security Services (X.800)

### 1. Data Confidentiality
- **Definition:** Protects data from *unauthorized disclosure* during storage, processing, and transit.
- **Scope:** Connection confidentiality, connectionless confidentiality, traffic-flow confidentiality, selective-field confidentiality.
- **KTU Keyword Mapping:** Privacy, secrecy, non-disclosure.

### 2. Data Integrity
- **Definition:** Ensures that data is modified *only by authorized entities* in an authorized manner.
- **Two sub-services:**
  - *Connection Integrity* (with or without recovery)
  - *Connectionless Integrity* (single datagram)

### 3. Authentication
- **Definition:** Provides assurance about the *identity of the communicating peer* or the *origin of data*.
- **Two flavors:**
  - *Peer Entity Authentication* — used during connection establishment.
  - *Data Origin Authentication* — used in connectionless transfers.

### 4. Non-Repudiation
- **Definition:** Provides proof that an entity performed a specific action so that it *cannot later falsely deny* it.
- **Two flavors:**
  - *Non-repudiation of Origin* (proof of sending)
  - *Non-repudiation of Receipt* (proof of receiving)

### 5. Access Control
- **Definition:** Protects against *unauthorized use* of resources (read, write, execute, delete).

### 6. Availability (Often Added)
- **Definition:** Ensures that system resources are accessible when needed by authorized users (resilience against DoS attacks).

## 2.2 The Eight Specific Security Mechanisms (X.800)

| # | Mechanism | What it does | Real-world analogy |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Encipherment (Encryption)** | Scrambles data using mathematical algorithms and keys. | Putting a letter in a locked safe. |
