| Padding length | $k = b - (\ell + 1 + s) \bmod b$ | bits | $s$ = size of length field (64 or 128) |
| Final hash size | $n$ bits (fixed) | bits | Independent of $\ell$ |
| Compression function size | $f: \{0,1\}^{n} \times \{0,1\}^{b} \to \{0,1\}^{n}$ | bits | Domain/codomain explicit |
| Output of round $j$ (SHA-256) | $T_j = h + \Sigma_1(e) + \text{Ch}(e,f,g) + K_j + W_j$ | mod $2^{32}$ | Used inside $f$ |
| Word extension | $W_j = \sigma_1(W_{j-2}) + W_{j-7} + \sigma_0(W_{j-15}) + W_{j-16}$ | mod $2^{32}$ | Message schedule |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation rule:** When writing $f$ mapping in exam scripts, students frequently lose marks by writing the wrong domain order. The correct order is **(previous hash, current block)**, not the other way around.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **TLS / HTTPS handshakes** use iterated hashes to derive session keys and verify certificate fingerprints.
- **Git** uses SHA-1 to identify every commit uniquely.
- **Blockchain (Bitcoin)** uses double-SHA-256 (an iterated function applied twice) for proof-of-work and transaction integrity.
- **Digital signatures (RSA-PSS, ECDSA)** hash a possibly huge document with an iterated hash function before signing — signing the hash is exponentially faster than signing the document.
- **File integrity tools** (sha256sum, md5sum) are direct end-user applications of iterated hashing.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Trace

## 3.1 Derivation: Why Iterating the Compression Function Equals Hashing the Whole Message

We want to show that $H_L$ depends on every bit of $M = M_1 \parallel M_2 \parallel \dots \parallel M_L$.

**Step 1 — Base case.** Define $H_0 := IV$. This is independent of $M$ but is a publicly defined constant.

**Step 2 — First iteration.** Apply $f$ with $H_0$ and $M_1$:
$$H_1 = f(H_0, M_1)$$

**Step 3 — Induction hypothesis.** Assume that $H_{i-1}$ depends on $M_1, M_2, \dots, M_{i-1}$.

**Step 4 — Inductive step.** Compute:
$$H_i = f(H_{i-1}, M_i)$$

Since $f$ is a fixed, deterministic, public function with strong diffusion, *any single bit flipped* in $H_{i-1}$ or $M_i$ causes (with overwhelming probability) a substantial change in $H_i$. Hence $H_i$ depends on $M_1, M_2, \dots, M_i$.

**Step 5 — Conclusion.** By induction, $H_L$ depends on $M_1, M_2, \dots, M_L$, i.e., on every bit of the padded message. $\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Worked Trace — Iterated Hashing of a Toy 2-Block Message (SHA-256 Style)

Let us trace the **chaining process** on a fictitious 2-block message using a simplified 64-bit block size and 32-bit hash. This isolates the *iteration logic* without the algebraic clutter of full SHA-256.

**Initial Value (given):**
$$H_0 = \text{0x6A09E667}$$

**Message blocks (given):**
$$M_1 = \text{0xAAAAAAAA},\quad M_2 = \text{0x55555555}$$

**Compression function (simplified illustrative form):**
$$f(H, M) = \text{ROTR}^{17}(H) \oplus M \oplus \text{ROTR}^{19}(M)$$

> *Note:* This is an illustration only. Real SHA-256 uses a much richer round function with six logical functions and 64 rounds.

### Iteration 1 — Process $M_1$

**Step 1.** Compute the right-rotate of $H_0$ by 17 positions:
$$\text{ROTR}^{17}(H_0) = \text{0x6A09E667} \ggg 17 = \text{0x3350B5048}$$

Wait — for a 32-bit value, the rotation is cyclic within 32 bits. Let us re-express in 32-bit form:
$$H_0 = \text{0x6A09E667} = \text{0110\,1010\,0000\,1001\,1110\,0110\,0110\,0111}_2$$

Rotating right by 17 positions gives:
$$\text{ROTR}^{17}(H_0) = \text{0011\,0011\,0100\,1011\,0101\,0000\,1011\,0100}_2 = \text{0x334B50B4}$$

**Step 2.** Compute the right-rotate of $M_1$ by 19 positions:
$$M_1 = \text{0xAAAAAAAA} = \text{1010\,1010\,1010\,1010\,1010\,1010\,1010\,1010}_2$$
$$\text{ROTR}^{19}(M_1) = \text{0101\,0101\,0101\,0101\,0101\,0101\,0101\,0101}_2 = \text{0x55555555}$$

**Step 3.** XOR the three terms:
$$H_1 = \text{0x334B50B4} \oplus \text{0xAAAAAAAA} \oplus \text{0x55555555}$$

Compute the two XORs sequentially:
$$\text{0x334B50B4} \oplus \text{0xAAAAAAAA} = \text{0x99E1FA1E}$$
$$\text{0x99E1FA1E} \oplus \text{0x55555555} = \text{0xCCB4AF4B}$$

Therefore:
$$H_1 = \text{0xCCB4AF4B}$$

### Iteration 2 — Process $M_2$

**Step 1.** Compute $\text{ROTR}^{17}(H_1)$:
$$H_1 = \text{0xCCB4AF4B} = \text{1100\,1100\,1011\,0100\,1010\,1111\,0100\,1011}_2$$
$$\text{ROTR}^{17}(H_1) = \text{0100\,1011\,0110\,0110\,0101\,0110\,0101\,0110}_2 = \text{0x4B665656}$$

**Step 2.** Compute $\text{ROTR}^{19}(M_2)$:
$$M_2 = \text{0x55555555}$$
$$\text{ROTR}^{19}(M_2) = \text{0xAAAAAAAA}$$

**Step 3.** XOR all three:
$$H_2 = \text{0x4B665656} \oplus \text{0x55555555} \oplus \text{0xAAAAAAAA}$$
$$= \text{0x1E330303} \oplus \text{0xAAAAAAAA} = \text{0xB491A9A9}$$

**Final hash:**
$$H(M) = H_2 = \text{0xB491A9A9}$$

This is the **fixed-size output** of the iterated hash function. Notice how a different $H_1$ (caused by a single changed bit in $M_1$) would propagate through Iteration 2 to produce a *completely different* $H_2$ — this is the avalanche effect in action.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Sensitivity Analysis

| Change made | Resulting effect on $H_1$ | Resulting effect on $H_2$ | Avalanche satisfied? |
|---|---|---|---|
| Flip 1 bit in $M_1$ | $\sim 50\%$ of bits change | $\sim 50\%$ of bits change | Yes |
| Swap $M_1 \leftrightarrow M_2$ | $H_1$ changes drastically | $H_2$ also changes | Yes |
| Append a block | New $H_3$ computed | All later blocks differ | Yes |
| Truncate last block | Hash changes (length field differs) | Yes (MD-Strengthening) | Yes |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Iterated Hash Function Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M["Input Message M of arbitrary length"] --> PAD["Padding Phase Append 1 then zeros then 64 bit length"]
    PAD --> PARSE["Parse into L blocks of b bits each"]
    PARSE --> B1["Block M1"]
    PARSE --> B2["Block M2"]
    PARSE --> B3["Block M3"]
    PARSE --> Bdot["Block ML"]
    B1 --> CF1["Compression Function f"]
    IV["Initial Value H0 IV"] --> CF1
    CF1 --> H1["H1 intermediate hash"]
    H1 --> CF2["Compression Function f"]
    B2 --> CF2
    CF2 --> H2["H2 intermediate hash"]
    H2 --> CF3["Compression Function f"]
    B3 --> CF3
    CF3 --> H3["H3 intermediate hash"]
    H3 --> CFN["Compression Function f"]
    Bdot --> CFN
    CFN --> HL["HL final hash value"]
    HL --> OUT["Output H of n fixed bits"]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Decomposition of the Compression Function

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Input A previous chaining variable H sub i minus 1"] --> ADD1["Add round constant K0"]
    ADD1 --> RND1["Round 1 with logical functions Ch Maj Sigma0 Sigma1"]
    RND1 --> RND2["Round 2"]
    RND2 --> RNDN["Round N total 64 for SHA 256"]
    RNDN --> B["Output B new chaining variable H sub i"]
    M["Input M current message block"] --> MSG["Message Schedule W sub j generator"]
    MSG --> RND1
    MSG --> RND2
    MSG --> RNDN
```

## 4.3 Data Flow Across the Full Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SP1["Stage 1 Preprocessing"]
        D1["Receive M"] --> D2["Pad to multiple of b bits"] --> D3["Append 64 bit length MD Strengthening"]
    end
    subgraph SP2["Stage 2 Iteration Loop"]
        D3 --> D4["Load block Mi"]
        D4 --> D5["Compute Hi equals f Hi minus 1 Mi"]
        D5 --> D6{"More blocks remain?"}
        D6 -- yes --> D4
        D6 -- no --> D7["Emit HL as hash"]
    end
    SP1 --> SP2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Diagram Tip:** In the End-Semester Exam, you may replace the Mermaid diagram with a hand-drawn block schematic showing $IV \to f \to H_1 \to f \to H_2 \dots \to H_L$. Marks are awarded for clear labels, correct arrow direction, and stating the block size $b$ alongside the arrows.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define an Iterated Hash Function. List any two standard hash algorithms that use this construction.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An Iterated Hash Function is a cryptographic hash function that processes a variable-length input message $M$ by splitting it into fixed-size blocks $M_1, M_2, \dots, M_L$ and repeatedly applying a fixed compression function $f$ to each block, carrying forward a chaining variable $H_i = f(H_{i-1}, M_i)$, where $H_0 = IV$ is a publicly known initial value. The final hash is the last chaining variable $H_L$, producing a fixed-length output independent of input length. **(2 marks)**

Two standard algorithms that use this construction:
1. **SHA-256** (block size 512 bits, hash size 256 bits). **(0.5 mark)**
2. **MD5** (block size 512 bits, hash size 128 bits). **(0.5 mark)**

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is the role of MD-Strengthening in an iterated hash function? Mention its exact placement.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
MD-Strengthering appends a **64-bit representation of the original message length** at the end of the padded message, *before* the message is parsed into blocks. **(1 mark)**

**Role:**
- It prevents two different messages (such as a message and a prefix-extended version) from producing the same padded pattern, thereby defeating **length-extension attacks**. **(1 mark)**
- It ensures that the hash output depends not only on the message contents but also on its exact length in bits, increasing collision resistance. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> Each Part B question carries **14 marks** and is internally optional (students answer either Q(A) or Q(B)). Sub-parts are typically **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**.

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the Merkle–Damgård construction of an iterated hash function with a neat block diagram. List the four phases and the role of each.

**(b) [7 marks]** For an iterated hash function with block size $b = 512$ bits and hash size $n = 256$ bits, compute the number of padding zero bits $k$ required when the original message length is $\ell = 4470$ bits. Also compute the total number of blocks $L$ after padding.

#### Model Solution

**(a) Merkle–Damgård Construction [7 marks]**

The Merkle–Damgård construction is the standard method for building an iterated hash function. It has four phases:

1. **Padding** — The original message is padded with a `1` bit followed by zero bits, then a 64-bit (or 128-bit) length field is appended so that the total length is a multiple of the block size $b$. **(1.5 marks)**
2. **Parsing** — The padded message is divided into $L$ blocks of $b$ bits each: $M_1, M_2, \dots, M_L$. **(1 mark)**
3. **Iterative Compression** — Starting from the public initial value $H_0 = IV$, the compression function $f$ is applied iteratively: $H_i = f(H_{i-1}, M_i)$ for $i = 1, \dots, L$. **(2 marks)**
4. **Output** — The final hash value is $H(M) = H_L$, a fixed $n$-bit digest independent of input length. **(1 mark)**

**Block Diagram [1.5 marks]:**
$$M \to [\text{Pad}] \to [\text{Parse into } M_1 \dots M_L] \to \big(H_0 \xrightarrow{f, M_1} H_1 \xrightarrow{f, M_2} H_2 \dots \xrightarrow{f, M_L} H_L\big) \to H(M)$$

**(b) Padding Calculation [7 marks]**

**Given:** $b = 512$, $n = 256$, $\ell = 4470$, length-field size $s = 64$.

**Step 1 — Total padding size before length field.** We need:
$$\ell + 1 + k + s \equiv 0 \pmod{b}$$
$$4470 + 1 + k + 64 \equiv 0 \pmod{512}$$
$$4535 + k \equiv 0 \pmod{512}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $k$.** Compute $4535 \bmod 512$:
$$4535 = 8 \times 512 + 439 = 4096 + 439 \implies 4535 \bmod 512 = 439$$

So we need $k$ such that $439 + k \equiv 0 \pmod{512}$:
$$k = 512 - 439 = 73 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 3 — Total padded length.**
$$\ell_p = 4470 + 1 + 73 + 64 = 4608 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 4 — Number of blocks $L$.**
$$L = \frac{\ell_p}{b} = \frac{4608}{512} = 9 \text{ blocks}$$

**Answers:** $k = 73$ zero bits, $L = 9$ blocks. **(7 marks awarded as: Step 1 = 2, Step 2 = 2, Step 3 = 1, Step 4 = 2)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Compare the iteration structure of MD5, SHA-1 and SHA-256. Tabulate the differences in block size, word size, hash size, and number of rounds.

**(b) [7 marks]** Why is the compression function $f$ iterated and not applied once to the whole message? Explain with reference to the constraints on input length and the practical engineering trade-offs.

#### Model Solution

**(a) Comparison Table [7 marks]**

| Parameter | MD5 | SHA-1 | SHA-256 |
|---|---|---|---|
| Block size $b$ | **512 bits** | **512 bits** | **512 bits** |
| Word size $w$ | **32 bits** | **32 bits** | **32 bits** |
| Hash size $n$ | **128 bits** | **160 bits** | **256 bits** |
| Number of rounds | 64 (4 groups of 16) | 80 (4 groups of 20) | 64 |
| Initial hash $H_0$ size | 4 words (128 bits) | 5 words (160 bits) | 8 words (256 bits) |
| Logical functions used | 4 | 4 | 6 |
| Chaining equation | $H_i = f(H_{i-1}, M_i)$ | Same | Same |
| Construction | Merkle–Damgård | Merkle–Damgård | Merkle–Damgård |
| Year introduced | 1992 | 1995 | 2001 |

(Each row: 0.5 mark → 8 rows × 0.5 = 4 marks. Tabular neatness and correct values = 3 marks. Total = 7 marks.)

**(b) Why Iterate the Compression Function? [7 marks]**

1. **Arbitrary input length vs. fixed hardware input.** Real messages can be megabytes or gigabytes, but the compression function $f$ can only accept a fixed $b$ bits of input per call (e.g., 512 bits). Iterating is the only way to scale to unbounded inputs without redesigning $f$. **(2 marks)**
2. **Reusability and engineering economy.** A single, well-vetted compression function is implemented once in hardware/software. The same function is reused $L$ times — saving silicon area, code size, and verification effort. **(1.5 marks)**
3. **Sequential diffusion of the avalanche effect.** Each iteration mixes one more block into the chaining variable. By the final block, every input bit has influenced every output bit. A single-shot application to the whole message would require a function that handles variable-size input, which is far harder to analyze for collision resistance. **(2 marks)**
4. **Provable security reduction (Merkle–Damgård theorem).** If the compression function $f$ is collision-resistant, then the iterated hash function built from it is *provably* collision-resistant. This reduction is what makes the iteration mathematically sound and is the cornerstone of the construction. **(1.5 marks)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks on this topic:**
> 1. **Wrong domain order** in $f(H_{i-1}, M_i)$ — examiners award 0 marks for swapped arguments because the chaining semantics break.
> 2. **Omitting MD-Strengthening** in the padding step — costs 1 to 2 marks.
> 3. **Confusing iteration with recursion** — iterated hash functions use a loop, not a recursive call; the compression function is *not* called on itself.
> 4. **Forgetting to state the IV** — every chain of $H_i$'s must begin with the public $H_0 = IV$. Skipping this is a 1-mark penalty.
> 5. **Writing a Python-style loop in non-CS answers** — strictly prohibited under KTU 2024 norms for engineering subjects; use mathematical indexing $i = 1, \dots, L$ instead.
> 6. **In padding problems**, students often write $k = b - (\ell + 1) \bmod b$ forgetting the length field $s$ — always include $s$ explicitly.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Iterated Hash Function** = a fixed-size compression function $f$ applied in a loop over fixed-size message blocks, carrying a chaining variable forward. **(core definition)**
- **Construction name:** Merkle–Damgård. **(must-state in any 14-mark answer)**
- **Iteration equation:** $H_i = f(H_{i-1}, M_i),\; H_0 = IV$. **(formula sheet anchor)**
- **Final output:** $H(M) = H_L$, always exactly $n$ bits. **(size invariant)**
- **Block size $b$** is fixed by the algorithm (e.g., 512 bits for SHA-1, SHA-256, MD5). **(constant)**
- **Padding** must include a `1` bit, then zeros, then the 64-bit length (MD-Strengthening). **(3 mandatory parts)**
- **Avalanche effect:** flipping one bit of input should change roughly half the output bits. **(security property)**
- **Three security properties:** preimage resistance, second preimage resistance, collision resistance. **(must mention when asked about security)**
- **SHA-256** uses 64 rounds, 8 working variables, 6 logical functions. **(specific instance)**
- **Real-world users:** Git, TLS, Bitcoin, digital signatures, OS package managers. **(application context)**
- **Common exam trap:** confusing $f$'s domain order, omitting MD-Strengthening, forgetting the IV. **(valuation pitfall)**
- **Merkle–Damgård vulnerability:** length-extension attacks — addressed in SHA-3 / Keccak by using a *sponge construction* instead of iteration. **(advanced note, may be asked for 2 bonus marks)**
- **Engineering rationale:** iteration allows reuse of one well-audited compression circuit, provable security reduction, and bounded hardware cost. **(conceptual takeaway)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: SHA-512
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT523 - DATA PRIVACY AND SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** SHA-512

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**SHA-512 (Secure Hash Algorithm 512)** is a cryptographic hash function belonging to the **SHA-2 (Secure Hash Algorithm 2)** family, standardized by the **National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST)** under **FIPS Publication 180-4**. It is a deterministic, one-way mathematical transformation that accepts an input message of any arbitrary length (up to $2^{128} - 1$ bits) and produces a fixed-size **512-bit (64-byte) message digest**, commonly referred to as a hash value, hash code, or digital fingerprint.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECDT523 - Module 3):**
> *SHA-512 is a member of the SHA-2 family of hash functions that processes input data in **1024-bit (128-byte) blocks** and employs **80 rounds of computation** using a **Merkle–Damgård iterative compression structure** based on the **Davies–Meyor** compression function. The output is a 512-bit hash value designed to provide **collision resistance**, **preimage resistance**, and **second-preimage resistance** properties essential to modern cryptographic security protocols.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Digital Blender"

Imagine a high-powered industrial blender in a forensic laboratory. You can throw in documents of any size — a single receipt, a 1000-page book, or even a library — and the machine always produces **exactly the same size of "DNA sample"** (the 512-bit fingerprint).

Key properties of this "Digital Blender":

- **Determinism** — The same document always produces the exact same DNA sample.
- **Avalanche Effect** — Change a single comma in the document, and the entire DNA sample becomes completely different.
- **One-Way Function** — Given the DNA sample, you can never reconstruct the original document.
- **Collision Resistance** — It is computationally infeasible to find two different documents that produce the same DNA sample.
- **Fixed Output Size** — Whether the input is 1 byte or 1 terabyte, the output is always 512 bits.

## 1.3 Key Technical Parameters at a Glance

> [!NOTE]
> **SHA-512 Standard Parameters (FIPS 180-4)**
>
> | Parameter | Value |
> |---|---|
> | Maximum Message Length | $2^{128} - 1$ bits |
> | Block Size | **1024 bits (128 bytes)** |
> | Hash Output Size | **512 bits (64 bytes)** |
> | Word Size | **64 bits** |
> | Number of Rounds | **80** |
> | Round Constants Used | 80 (the first 64 primes) |
> | Initial Hash Values ($H^{(0)}$) | 8 × 64-bit words |

## 1.4 Position of SHA-512 in the SHA Family Tree

SHA-512 is part of the **SHA-2** family, which was published by NIST in **2001** as a successor to the now-broken SHA-1 algorithm. The family includes SHA-224, SHA-256, SHA-384, SHA-512, SHA-512/224, and SHA-512/256. SHA-512 is essentially the same algorithm as SHA-256 but with **larger word size (64-bit vs 32-bit)**, **larger block size (1024-bit vs 512-bit)**, and **more rounds (80 vs 64)**, providing a higher security margin and better performance on 64-bit processors.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture — Six Logical Phases

The SHA-512 computation proceeds through six structured phases:

### Phase 1 — Padding the Message
The original message $M$ is extended so that its length becomes congruent to **896 modulo 1024**. This is achieved by:
1. Appending a `1` bit.
2. Appending $k$ `0` bits, where $k$ is the smallest non-negative integer such that $L + 1 + k \equiv 896 \pmod{1024}$.
3. Appending the **128-bit big-endian representation of the original message length**.

The final padded message $M'$ has length that is an exact multiple of **1024 bits**.

### Phase 2 — Parsing into Message Blocks
The padded message $M'$ is divided into $N$ blocks, each of length **1024 bits**:
$$M' = M^{(1)} \parallel M^{(2)} \parallel \cdots \parallel M^{(N)}$$

### Phase 3 — Setting the Initial Hash Value (IV)
The hash computation uses **8 initial 64-bit hash values** ($H_0^{(0)}$ through $H_7^{(0)}$). These are the first 64 bits of the fractional parts of the square roots of the first 8 prime numbers (2, 3, 5, 7, 11, 13, 17, 19).

### Phase 4 — Message Schedule Generation
Each 1024-bit message block $M^{(i)}$ is expanded into **80 × 64-bit words** ($W_0, W_1, \ldots, W_{79}$). The first 16 words are taken directly from the block, and the remaining 64 are generated by a recurrence relation.

### Phase 5 — Compression Function (Main Loop)
The algorithm maintains **8 working variables** ($a, b, c, d, e, f, g, h$) and runs **80 rounds** per block, using six logical functions ($\Sigma_0, \Sigma_1, \sigma_0, \sigma_1, Ch, Maj$) and 80 round constants ($K_t$).

### Phase 6 — Final Hash Output
After processing all $N$ blocks, the concatenated final values $H_0^{(N)} \parallel H_1^{(N)} \parallel \cdots \parallel H_7^{(N)}$ form the **512-bit message digest**.

## 2.2 The Six Logical Functions of SHA-512

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Functions — Memorize These for KTU Exams**
>
> | Function | Formula | Description |
> |---|---|---|
> | $Ch(x,y,z)$ | $(x \land y) \oplus (\neg x \land z)$ | **Choice function** — selects bits of $y$ or $z$ based on $x$ |
> | $Maj(x,y,z)$ | $(x \land y) \oplus (x \land z) \oplus (y \land z)$ | **Majority function** — bit is 1 if majority of inputs are 1 |
> | $\Sigma_0(x)$ | $ROTR^{28}(x) \oplus ROTR^{34}(x) \oplus ROTR^{39}(x)$ | Uppercase Sigma 0 (used in main loop) |
> | $\Sigma_1(x)$ | $ROTR^{14}(x) \oplus ROTR^{18}(x) \oplus ROTR^{41}(x)$ | Uppercase Sigma 1 (used in main loop) |
> | $\sigma_0(x)$ | $ROTR^{1}(x) \oplus ROTR^{8}(x) \oplus SHR^{7}(x)$ | Lowercase sigma 0 (used in message schedule) |
> | $\sigma_1(x)$ | $ROTR^{19}(x) \oplus ROTR^{61}(x) \oplus SHR^{6}(x)$ | Lowercase sigma 1 (used in message schedule) |

Where:
- $ROTR^n(x)$ — right rotation of the 64-bit word $x$ by $n$ positions.
- $SHR^n(x)$ — right shift of the 64-bit word $x$ by $n$ positions.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Complete Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All Equations Required for KTU Board Examination (PECDT523 Module 3)**
>
> | # | Concept | Equation | Units / Range |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | 1 | Padded length | $L' \equiv 0 \pmod{1024}$ | bits |
> | 2 | Padding bits formula | $L + 1 + k \equiv 896 \pmod{1024}$ | $k \geq 0$ |
> | 3 | Message schedule (t < 16) | $W_t = M_t^{(i)}$ | $t = 0, 1, \ldots, 15$ |
> | 4 | Message schedule (t ≥ 16) | $W_t = \sigma_1(W_{t-2}) + W_{t-7} + \sigma_0(W_{t-15}) + W_{t-16}$ | $t = 16, \ldots, 79$ |
> | 5 | Round constant | $K_t = $ 64-bit fractional part of $\sqrt[3]{p_t}$ for $p_t$ = $t$-th prime | $t = 0, \ldots, 79$ |
> | 6 | $T_1$ computation | $T_1 = h + \Sigma_1(e) + Ch(e,f,g) + K_t + W_t$ | 64-bit |
> | 7 | $T_2$ computation | $T_2 = \Sigma_0(a) + Maj(a,b,c)$ | 64-bit |
> | 8 | Working variable update | $h = g$; $g = f$; $f = e$; $e = d + T_1$; $d = c$; $c = b$; $b = a$; $a = T_1 + T_2$ | — |
> | 9 | Block hash update | $H_j^{(i)} = H_j^{(i-1)} + $ corresponding working variable | — |
> | 10 | Final digest | $H = H_0^{(N)} \parallel H_1^{(N)} \parallel \cdots \parallel H_7^{(N)}$ | 512 bits |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Exam Pitfall:** Do NOT confuse the **uppercase** $\Sigma$ (used in the main compression loop) with the **lowercase** $\sigma$ (used in the message schedule). They use **different rotation amounts**. Writing $\sigma_0$ in the main loop is an automatic 2-mark deduction.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility of SHA-512

SHA-512 is widely deployed in mission-critical security infrastructures:

- **Digital signatures (RSA, ECDSA, Ed25519)** — The message is hashed with SHA-512 before signing for efficiency and security.
- **TLS/SSL certificates** — Used in certificate fingerprints and certificate authority chains.
- **Blockchain and cryptocurrency** — Forms the backbone of **Bitcoin mining** (double SHA-256 of headers, though SHA-256, not SHA-512) and is used in **Mimblewimble** and several altcoins.
- **File integrity verification** — Linux distributions (e.g., Ubuntu, Fedora) publish SHA-512 checksums for their ISO images.
- **Password hashing (with salt)** — Modern systems use PBKDF2-HMAC-SHA-512, bcrypt, or scrypt.
- **DNSSEC, IPsec, SSH** — Protocol-level message authentication.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Padding Length Calculation

Let the original message $M$ have a bit-length $L$. We want the final padded message $M'$ to have a length that is an exact multiple of 1024 bits. The padding consists of:
- One mandatory `1` bit.
- A variable number of `0` bits (say $k$).
- A mandatory 128-bit length field.

Therefore, the constraint is:

$$L + 1 + k + 128 \equiv 0 \pmod{1024}$$

Rearranging to solve for $k$:

$$L + 129 + k \equiv 0 \pmod{1024}$$

$$k \equiv -L - 129 \pmod{1024}$$

$$k \equiv 1024 - (L + 129) \pmod{1024} \quad \text{(taking positive residue)}$$

$$k \equiv 895 - L \pmod{1024}$$

**Worked Numerical Example:**

Let $L = 2000$ bits.

$$k \equiv 895 - 2000 \pmod{1024}$$
$$k \equiv -1105 \pmod{1024}$$
$$k \equiv 1024 - 1105 + 1024 \pmod{1024}$$
$$k = 943 \text{ zero bits}$$

Verify: $L + 1 + k + 128 = 2000 + 1 + 943 + 128 = 3072$ bits, and $3072 / 1024 = 3$ blocks. ✓

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Message Schedule Expansion for $t \geq 16$

Each message block $M^{(i)}$ is first partitioned into 16 words of 64 bits each, giving $W_0, W_1, \ldots, W_{15}$. The remaining 64 words are generated by a non-linear recurrence that mixes prior words using the $\sigma$ functions.

For $16 \leq t \leq 79$, the recurrence is:

$$W_t = \sigma_1(W_{t-2}) + W_{t-7} + \sigma_0(W_{t-15}) + W_{t-16} \pmod{2^{64}}$$

Where the $\sigma$ functions introduce non-linearity through rotation and shift operations:

$$\sigma_0(x) = ROTR^1(x) \oplus ROTR^8(x) \oplus SHR^7(x)$$

$$\sigma_1(x) = ROTR^{19}(x) \oplus ROTR^{61}(x) \oplus SHR^6(x)$$

**Conceptual Reasoning for the Recurrence:**

- The index gaps of 2, 7, 15, 16 are carefully chosen by NIST to ensure that the resulting words $W_t$ depend on all bits of the previous block, providing strong **diffusion**.
- All additions are performed modulo $2^{64}$ (i.e., the word size), with overflow discarded.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Round Function Computation

Each of the 80 rounds computes two 64-bit values $T_1$ and $T_2$ and updates the 8 working variables.

**Step 1: Compute $T_1$.**

$$T_1 = h + \Sigma_1(e) + Ch(e,f,g) + K_t + W_t \pmod{2^{64}}$$

**Step 2: Compute $T_2$.**

$$T_2 = \Sigma_0(a) + Maj(a,b,c) \pmod{2^{64}}$$

**Step 3: Update the working variables** (simultaneous assignment):

$$\begin{aligned}
h &\leftarrow g \\
g &\leftarrow f \\
f &\leftarrow e \\
e &\leftarrow d + T_1 \pmod{2^{64}} \\
d &\leftarrow c \\
c &\leftarrow b \\
b &\leftarrow a \\
a &\leftarrow T_1 + T_2 \pmod{2^{64}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Analytical Insight:**

- The **Choice function $Ch(e,f,g)$** acts as a multiplexer: if $e$ is 1, it outputs $f$; if $e$ is 0, it outputs $g$. This introduces **non-linearity**.
- The **Majority function $Maj(a,b,c)$** outputs the majority bit, providing **diffusion**.
- The rotations in $\Sigma_0$ and $\Sigma_1$ provide **bit-level mixing** across the entire 64-bit word.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Iterated Hash Value Update

After all 80 rounds of a single block are processed, the intermediate hash values are updated by adding the final working variables:

$$\begin{aligned}
H_0^{(i)} &\leftarrow H_0^{(i-1)} + a \pmod{2^{64}} \\
H_1^{(i)} &\leftarrow H_1^{(i-1)} + b \pmod{2^{64}} \\
H_2^{(i)} &\leftarrow H_2^{(i-1)} + c \pmod{2^{64}} \\
H_3^{(i)} &\leftarrow H_3^{(i-1)} + d \pmod{2^{64}} \\
H_4^{(i)} &\leftarrow H_4^{(i-1)} + e \pmod{2^{64}} \\
H_5^{(i)} &\leftarrow H_5^{(i-1)} + f \pmod{2^{64}} \\
H_6^{(i)} &\leftarrow H_6^{(i-1)} + g \pmod{2^{64}} \\
H_7^{(i)} &\leftarrow H_7^{(i-1)} + h \pmod{2^{64}}
\end{aligned}$$

## 3.5 The 8 Initial Hash Values (Memorize for KTU)

The initial hash values $H_0^{(0)}$ through $H_7^{(0)}$ are derived from the fractional parts of the square roots of the first 8 primes.

$$\begin{aligned}
H_0^{(0)} &= \texttt{6a09e667f3bcc908} \\
H_1^{(0)} &= \texttt{bb67ae8584caa73b} \\
H_2^{(0)} &= \texttt{3c6ef372fe94f82b} \\
H_3^{(0)} &= \texttt{a54ff53a5f1d36f1} \\
H_4^{(0)} &= \texttt{510e527fade682d1} \\
H_5^{(0)} &= \texttt{9b05688c2b3e6c1f} \\
H_6^{(0)} &= \texttt{1f83d9abfb41bd6b} \\
H_7^{(0)} &= \texttt{5be0cd19137e2179}
\end{aligned}$$

## 3.6 The 80 Round Constants $K_t$

Each $K_t$ is the 64-bit fractional part of the cube root of the $t$-th prime number $p_t$. The first few are:

$$\begin{aligned}
K_0 &= \texttt{428a2f98d728ae22} \\
K_1 &= \texttt{7137449123ef65cd} \\
K_2 &= \texttt{b5c0fbcfec4d3b2f} \\
K_3 &= \texttt{e9b5dba58189dbbc} \\
K_4 &= \texttt{3956c25bf348b538} \\
K_5 &= \texttt{59f111f1b605d019} \\
K_6 &= \texttt{923f82a4af194f9b} \\
K_7 &= \texttt{ab1c5ed5da6d8118} \\
& \vdots \\
K_{79} &= \texttt{c67178f2e372532b}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU exams, you are **not** required to memorize all 80 constants. The examiner typically provides a small table of $K_t$ values when needed, or tests only the **structure** of the round computation.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level SHA-512 Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Input Message M (any length)"] --> B["Phase 1: Padding"]
    B --> C["Padded Message M' (multiple of 1024 bits)"]
    C --> D["Phase 2: Parse into N Blocks"]
    D --> E["Block M_1, M_2, ..., M_N (1024 bits each)"]
    E --> F["Phase 3: Initialize 8 hash values H_0 to H_7"]
    F --> G["Phase 4: Message Schedule (W_0 to W_79)"]
    G --> H["Phase 5: 80-Round Compression Function"]
    H --> I["Phase 6: Update intermediate hash values"]
    I --> J{"More blocks?"}
    J -- Yes --> G
    J -- No --> K["Concatenate H_0 to H_7"]
    K --> L["512-bit Message Digest"]
```

## 4.2 Internal Block-Level Architecture of a Single Round

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT["Input Working Variables (64-bit each)"]
        A_var["a"]
        B_var["b"]
        C_var["c"]
        D_var["d"]
        E_var["e"]
        F_var["f"]
        G_var["g"]
        H_var["h"]
    end

    subgraph ROUND["Single Round Computation"]
        ChFunc["Ch(e, f, g) = (e AND f) XOR (NOT e AND g)"]
        MajFunc["Maj(a, b, c) = (a AND b) XOR (a AND c) XOR (b AND c)"]
        Sigma1["Sigma_1(e) = ROTR^14 XOR ROTR^18 XOR ROTR^41"]
        Sigma0["Sigma_0(a) = ROTR^28 XOR ROTR^34 XOR ROTR^39"]
        Add1["T1 = h + Sigma_1 + Ch + K_t + W_t"]
        Add2["T2 = Sigma_0 + Maj"]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT["Output Working Variables"]
        A_new["a_new = T1 + T2"]
        B_new["b_new = a"]
        C_new["c_new = b"]
        D_new["d_new = c"]
        E_new["e_new = d + T1"]
        F_new["f_new = e"]
        G_new["g_new = f"]
        H_new["h_new = g"]
    end

    E_var --> Sigma1
    F_var --> ChFunc
    G_var --> ChFunc
    A_var --> Sigma0
    B_var --> MajFunc
    C_var --> MajFunc
    H_var --> Add1
    K_t_in["K_t (round constant)"] --> Add1
    W_t_in["W_t (message word)"] --> Add1
    Sigma1 --> Add1
    ChFunc --> Add1
    Sigma0 --> Add2
    MajFunc --> Add2
    Add1 --> A_new
    Add1 --> E_new
    Add2 --> A_new
    A_var --> B_new
    B_var --> C_new
    C_var --> D_new
    D_var --> E_new
    E_var --> F_new
    F_var --> G_new
    G_var --> H_new
```

## 4.3 Message Schedule Expansion Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["1024-bit Block M_i"] --> Split["Split into 16 words: W_0, W_1, ..., W_15 (64-bit each)"]
    Split --> Decision{"t in 0 to 15?"}
    Decision -- Yes --> Direct["W_t = M_t (direct from block)"]
    Decision -- No --> Recurrence["t in 16 to 79: W_t = sigma_1(W_t-2) + W_t-7 + sigma_0(W_t-15) + W_t-16"]
    Direct --> Schedule["Complete Schedule: W_0 to W_79"]
    Recurrence --> Schedule
    Schedule --> NextRound["Feed W_t into 80 rounds of compression"]
```

## 4.4 Merkle–Damgård Iterative Structure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M1["M_1 (1024 bits)"] --> CF1["Compression Function f"]
    IV0["IV: H_0 to H_7 (initial hash values)"] --> CF1
    CF1 --> H1["H^(1): Updated hash after block 1"]

    M2["M_2 (1024 bits)"] --> CF2["Compression Function f"]
    H1 --> CF2
    CF2 --> H2["H^(2): Updated hash after block 2"]

    MN["M_N (1024 bits)"] --> CFN["Compression Function f"]
    Hprev["H^(N-1)"] --> CFN
    CFN --> HN["H^(N): Final 512-bit digest"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Diagram Interpretation Tip:** The above Merkle–Damgård structure illustrates how SHA-512 inherits its **collision-resistance property** from the underlying compression function. If the compression function is collision-resistant, then the iterated hash function is also collision-resistant (as proven by Merkle and Damgård independently in 1989).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**List any six parameters of the SHA-512 algorithm with their standard values.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Six Key Parameters of SHA-512:**
> 1. **Maximum message length:** $2^{128} - 1$ bits
> 2. **Block size:** 1024 bits (128 bytes)
> 3. **Hash output size:** 512 bits (64 bytes)
> 4. **Word size:** 64 bits
> 5. **Number of rounds:** 80
> 6. **Number of round constants:** 80 (the first 64 primes, used twice with some repetition pattern in FIPS-180)
> 7. **Number of initial hash values:** 8 × 64-bit words
> 8. **Message schedule length:** 80 words of 64 bits each

*(Any six correct parameters with values — full 3 marks.)*

---

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between the Choice function $Ch(x, y, z)$ and the Majority function $Maj(x, y, z)$ used in SHA-512.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> | Feature | $Ch(x, y, z)$ | $Maj(x, y, z)$ |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Formula** | $(x \land y) \oplus (\neg x \land z)$ | $(x \land y) \oplus (x \land z) \oplus (y \land z)$ |
> | **Number of input bits considered** | 2 (selects between $y$ and $z$ based on $x$) | 3 (takes majority of all three) |
> | **Role** | Acts as a **multiplexer / selector** | Acts as a **voting / majority gate** |
> | **Output bit value** | Equals $y$ if $x=1$, else equals $z$ | Equals the bit that appears at least twice among $x, y, z$ |
> | **Usage in SHA-512** | Computed as part of $T_1$ using $e, f, g$ | Computed as part of $T_2$ using $a, b, c$ |
> | **Property** | Introduces conditional non-linearity | Introduces unconditional non-linearity |

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Using SHA-256's rotation constants (like $ROTR^2$, $ROTR^{13}$, $ROTR^{22}$ for $\Sigma_0$) instead of SHA-512's ($ROTR^{28}, ROTR^{34}, ROTR^{39}$). This is a **2-mark deduction** per occurrence.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Confusing the uppercase $\Sigma$ (main loop) with lowercase $\sigma$ (message schedule). They use **different rotation/shift amounts**.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Forgetting to perform addition modulo $2^{64}$ — always write "$\pmod{2^{64}}$" or "modulo $2^{64}$" after additions.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Drawing the message schedule as 16 words instead of **80 words** — full 7 marks lost on the schedule part.
> - **Common Mistake 5:** Not labeling the Initial Hash Values (IV) correctly as coming from fractional square roots of the first 8 primes.

---

### Question A (14 Marks): **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 — Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the complete step-by-step process of padding a message in SHA-512. Calculate the number of padding bits required for a message of length **3000 bits**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **Merkle–Damgård iterative compression structure** of SHA-512. List the eight initial hash values. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a):

**Step 1: State the padding rule.**
In SHA-512, the message is padded so that its length becomes congruent to **0 modulo 1024**. The padding consists of three components:
1. A single `1` bit appended to the original message.
2. A variable number of `0` bits (call it $k$) to satisfy the length constraint.
3. The **128-bit big-endian binary representation** of the original message length $L$.

**Step 2: Write the constraint equation.**
The total length after padding is:
$$L + 1 + k + 128 = L' \text{ (where } L' \text{ is a multiple of 1024)}$$

Therefore:
$$L + 1 + k + 128 \equiv 0 \pmod{1024}$$

Equivalently:
$$L + 1 + k \equiv 896 \pmod{1024}$$

**Step 3: Solve for $k$ when $L = 3000$.**
$$3000 + 1 + k \equiv 896 \pmod{1024}$$
$$3001 + k \equiv 896 \pmod{1024}$$
$$k \equiv 896 - 3001 \pmod{1024}$$
$$k \equiv -2105 \pmod{1024}$$

Take the positive residue:
$$k = 1024 - (2105 \mod 1024) = 1024 - 57 = 967 \text{ zero bits}$$

**Verification:** $3000 + 1 + 967 + 128 = 4096$ bits $= 4 \times 1024$ bits. ✓

**Step 4: Summarize the padded structure.**

| Component | Length |
|---|---|
| Original message | 3000 bits |
| `1` bit | 1 bit |
| `0` padding | 967 bits |
| Length field (128 bits) | 128 bits |
| **Total padded length** | **4096 bits (4 blocks)** |

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b):

**Step 1: Describe the Merkle–Damgård construction.**
The Merkle–Damgård construction is a method of building a **collision-resistant hash function** from a **collision-resistant compression function**. SHA-512 uses this construction as follows:

1. The padded message is divided into $N$ blocks of 1024 bits each: $M^{(1)}, M^{(2)}, \ldots, M^{(N)}$.
2. An initial hash value $H^{(0)}$ of 512 bits is loaded (8 × 64-bit words).
3. For each block $M^{(i)}$, the compression function $f$ takes the current hash $H^{(i-1)}$ and the message block $M^{(i)}$ and produces the next hash $H^{(i)}$:
$$H^{(i)} = f(H^{(i-1)}, M^{(i)})$$
4. After processing all $N$ blocks, the final hash $H^{(N)}$ is the 512-bit message digest.

**Step 2: Present the block diagram (textual version).**

> $IV = H^{(0)}$ → [Block $M^{(1)}$, Compression $f$] → $H^{(1)}$ → [Block $M^{(2)}$, Compression $f$] → $H^{(2)}$ → ... → $H^{(N)}$ = **512-bit Digest**

**Step 3: List the eight initial hash values.**

The 8 initial hash values are the first 64 bits of the fractional parts of the square roots of the first 8 prime numbers (2, 3, 5, 7, 11, 13, 17, 19):

| Word | Value (hex) |
|---|---|
| $H_0^{(0)}$ | `6a09e667f3bcc908` |
| $H_1^{(0)}$ | `bb67ae8584caa73b` |
| $H_2^{(0)}$ | `3c6ef372fe94f82b` |
| $H_3^{(0)}$ | `a54ff53a5f1d36f1` |
| $H_4^{(0)}$ | `510e527fade682d1` |
| $H_5^{(0)}$ | `9b05688c2b3e6c1f` |
| $H_6^{(0)}$ | `1f83d9abfb41bd6b` |
| $H_7^{(0)}$ | `5be0cd19137e2179` |

---

### Question B (14 Marks): **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 — Supplementary]**

**(a)** Explain the **message schedule** of SHA-512 with the help of the recurrence relation. Why are the index gaps 2, 7, 15, and 16 chosen? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **80-round compression function** of SHA-512. Clearly state the formulas for $T_1$ and $T_2$ and the working variable updates. List the six logical functions. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a):

**Step 1: Introduction to the message schedule.**
Each 1024-bit message block $M^{(i)}$ is expanded into a sequence of **80 words** $W_0, W_1, \ldots, W_{79}$, each of 64 bits. This expanded schedule is then fed into the 80 rounds of the compression function.

**Step 2: Direct loading for the first 16 words.**
For $t = 0, 1, \ldots, 15$, the words are loaded directly from the 1024-bit message block:
$$W_t = M_t^{(i)}$$

**Step 3: Recurrence relation for $t \geq 16$.**
For $t = 16, 17, \ldots, 79$, the words are generated by the non-linear recurrence:
$$W_t = \sigma_1(W_{t-2}) + W_{t-7} + \sigma_0(W_{t-15}) + W_{t-16} \pmod{2^{64}}$$

Where the lowercase sigma functions are:
$$\sigma_0(x) = ROTR^1(x) \oplus ROTR^8(x) \oplus SHR^7(x)$$
$$\sigma_1(x) = ROTR^{19}(x) \oplus ROTR^{61}(x) \oplus SHR^6(x)$$

**Step 4: Justification of index gaps 2, 7, 15, and 16.**

The choice of these specific index gaps is **not arbitrary** — it is the result of extensive cryptanalysis by NIST to ensure:

- **Diffusion:** Each new word $W_t$ depends on words spanning nearly the entire previous portion of the schedule, ensuring rapid mixing of all message bits.
- **Resistance to differential cryptanalysis:** The gaps 2, 7, 15, 16 create overlapping but non-trivial dependencies that thwart attempts to forge collisions.
- **Computational efficiency:** The chosen gaps allow all required words to be computed sequentially using only previously computed values, without backward references.
- **Avalanche effect:** Even flipping a single bit in $M^{(i)}$ causes widespread, unpredictable changes in all subsequent $W_t$ values.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b):

**Step 1: Overview of the compression loop.**
The compression function maintains 8 working variables $a, b, c, d, e, f, g, h$ (initially set to the current 8 hash values $H_0^{(i-1)}, H_1^{(i-1)}, \ldots, H_7^{(i-1)}$) and runs **80 identical rounds**, with each round using a different round constant $K_t$ and message schedule word $W_t$.

**Step 2: The six logical functions.**

| Function | Formula | Type |
|---|---|---|
| $Ch(x, y, z)$ | $(x \land y) \oplus (\neg x \land z)$ | Selector |
| $Maj(x, y, z)$ | $(x \land y) \oplus (x \land z) \oplus (y \land z)$ | Voter |
| $\Sigma_0(x)$ | $ROTR^{28}(x) \oplus ROTR^{34}(x) \oplus ROTR^{39}(x)$ | Main loop |
| $\Sigma_1(x)$ | $ROTR^{14}(x) \oplus ROTR^{18}(x) \oplus ROTR^{41}(x)$ | Main loop |
| $\sigma_0(x)$ | $ROTR^{1}(x) \oplus ROTR^{8}(x) \oplus SHR^{7}(x)$ | Message schedule |
| $\sigma_1(x)$ | $ROTR^{19}(x) \oplus ROTR^{61}(x) \oplus SHR^{6}(x)$ | Message schedule |

**Step 3: Formulas for $T_1$ and $T_2$.**

$$T_1 = h + \Sigma_1(e) + Ch(e, f, g) + K_t + W_t \pmod{2^{64}}$$

$$T_2 = \Sigma_0(a) + Maj(a, b, c) \pmod{2^{64}}$$

**Step 4: Working variable updates (round $t$).**

$$\begin{aligned}
h &\leftarrow g \\
g &\leftarrow f \\
f &\leftarrow e \\
e &\leftarrow d + T_1 \pmod{2^{64}} \\
d &\leftarrow c \\
c &\leftarrow b \\
b &\leftarrow a \\
a &\leftarrow T_1 + T_2 \pmod{2^{64}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5: Final hash update after all 80 rounds.**
$$H_j^{(i)} = H_j^{(i-1)} + \text{(corresponding working variable)} \pmod{2^{64}}, \quad j = 0, 1, \ldots, 7$$

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for SHA-512 (KTU 2024 — PECDT523 Module 3)**
>
> - [ ] SHA-512 produces a **512-bit (64-byte)** hash from any input up to $2^{128} - 1$ bits.
> - [ ] Block size is **1024 bits** (not 512 bits like SHA-256).
> - [ ] Word size is **64 bits**; **80 rounds** are executed per block.
> - [ ] Padding rule: append `1` + $k$ zeros + **128-bit length field**, with $L + 1 + k \equiv 896 \pmod{1024}$.
> - [ ] Initial Hash Values (IV) come from the **first 64 bits of the fractional part of $\sqrt{p}$** for the first 8 primes.
> - [ ] Round constants $K_t$ come from the **first 64 bits of the fractional part of $\sqrt[3]{p}$** for the first 80 primes.
> - [ ] **Message schedule:** 80 words of 64 bits. First 16 are direct; remaining 64 use the recurrence with index gaps **2, 7, 15, 16**.
> - [ ] **Uppercase $\Sigma$** is for the main loop (different rotation amounts than lowercase).
> - [ ] **Lowercase $\sigma$** is for the message schedule.
> - [ ] $T_1 = h + \Sigma_1(e) + Ch(e,f,g) + K_t + W_t \pmod{2^{64}}$.
> - [ ] $T_2 = \Sigma_0(a) + Maj(a,b,c) \pmod{2^{64}}$.
> - [ ] Working variable update follows a **shift-register pattern**: $h \leftarrow g, g \leftarrow f, \ldots, a \leftarrow T_1 + T_2$.
> - [ ] SHA-512 uses the **Merkle–Damgård iterative construction**.
> - [ ] All additions are performed **modulo $2^{64}$** — never forget this in the KTU answer sheet.
> - [ ] Standardized by NIST under **FIPS Publication 180-4** (year 2012 revision).
> - [ ] SHA-512 has **truncation variants**: SHA-512/256 and SHA-512/224 (defined in FIPS 180-4).
> - [ ] Primary security properties: **preimage resistance, second-preimage resistance, and collision resistance**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# WHIRLPOOL — Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **WHIRLPOOL** is a cryptographic **hash function** designed by **Vincent Rijmen** and **Paulo S. M. L. M. Barreto** in the year **2000**, and was later revised in **2001** and **2003**. It produces a **message digest of 512 bits** from an input message of arbitrary length (strictly less than $2^{256}$ bits). It is recognized as the international standard **ISO/IEC 10118-3:2004** and is a NESSIE-recommended algorithm.

Whirlpool processes data through an iterated, Merkle–Damgård-style construction, where the core compression function is built upon a **dedicated 512-bit block cipher** that is structurally inspired by the **Advanced Encryption Standard (AES / Rijndael)**, but uses an **8 × 8 byte state matrix** instead of AES's 4 × 4 matrix.

### 1.1.1 Specification Summary

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Block size | **512 bits** |
| Digest size | **512 bits** |
| Internal state | **8 × 8 matrix of bytes (64 bytes)** |
| Number of rounds | **10 rounds** |
| Word size | **8 bits (byte-oriented)** |
| Underlying primitive | Modified AES-style block cipher |
| Designers | Vincent Rijmen, Paulo S. L. M. Barreto |
| Year | 2000 (rev. 2001, 2003) |
| Standard | ISO/IEC 10118-3:2004 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** Whirlpool is the **only ISO-standardized 512-bit hash function** based directly on an AES-style structure. It is a key candidate topic for KTU 2024 Scheme questions relating to "secure modern hash algorithms" and "AES-derived ciphers."

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Fingerprint Factory

Imagine a **fingerprint factory** that accepts any document — whether it is a single word, a photo, a 1000-page book, or an entire library — and produces a **fixed-length unique fingerprint** of exactly 512 bits (64 characters) at the exit door.

- **The factory line** is the compression function $W$.
- **Each delivery truck (message block)** arrives at the factory in a 512-bit parcel.
- **The factory's "memory" (chaining variable)** holds the state of the previous batch.
- **Every truck is processed, mixed, shaken, and stamped** for 10 rounds.
- The **final fingerprint is sealed** before leaving the factory.

**The Golden Rules of the Fingerprint Factory:**
1. **Same document → Same fingerprint** (Deterministic).
2. **Tiny change in document → Completely different fingerprint** (Avalanche effect).
3. **You cannot reverse-engineer the document from the fingerprint** (One-way / Pre-image resistance).
4. **Two different documents should never share the same fingerprint** (Collision resistance).

> [!NOTE]
> This analogy captures the **deterministic, fixed-output, irreversible, and highly sensitive** nature of cryptographic hash functions that KTU examiners emphasize.

---

## 1.3 Why Was Whirlpool Designed?

While SHA-1 and MD5 were under growing cryptanalytic pressure, the cryptographic community needed a **new generation of hash functions**. The designers of Whirlpool chose to:

1. Reuse the **well-vetted AES design philosophy** (substitution–permutation network).
2. Extend the state from **128 bits (AES)** to **512 bits** for higher security margins.
3. Provide a **512-bit digest** to resist **birthday-paradox collisions** for decades.
4. Avoid the SHA-2 family for **algorithmic diversity and independence**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The 512-bit digest offers a security level of **$2^{256}$** against birthday attacks, compared to **$2^{80}$** for SHA-1.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# WHIRLPOOL — Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The High-Level Algorithmic Architecture

The Whirlpool hash function follows a **Merkle–Damgård strengthening** structure and consists of the following pipeline:

### Stage 1 — Preprocessing
1. **Padding**: Append a single **'1' bit** to the message, followed by the minimum number of **'0' bits** to make the length congruent to **896 mod 1024**.
2. **Length Append**: Append the **256-bit original message length** (so that the total length becomes a multiple of 1024 bits).
3. **Parsing**: Break the padded message into $N$ blocks of **512 bits each**: $M_1, M_2, \ldots, M_N$.

### Stage 2 — Iterative Hashing
4. **Initial Hash Value**: 
$$H_0 = \underbrace{\texttt{00 00 00 00} \cdots \texttt{00 00 00 00}}_{512 \text{ bits}}$$
5. **Compression Loop**: For $i = 1, 2, \ldots, N$:
$$H_i = W(H_{i-1}, M_i)$$
where $W$ is the **compression function** taking the 512-bit chaining value $H_{i-1}$ and the 512-bit message block $M_i$.

### Stage 3 — Output
6. **Final Hash**: Output $H_N$ as the 512-bit message digest.

> [!NOTE]
> The compression function $W$ is the **algorithmic heart** of Whirlpool and is built from a dedicated 512-bit block cipher $E$ (a modified AES).

---

## 2.2 The Compression Function $W$ — AES-Style Block Cipher

The compression function is based on a **dedicated block cipher $E$** with:
- **Block size**: 512 bits
- **Key size**: 512 bits
- **Rounds**: $R = 10$

The round function $E$ applies four transformations in sequence, **10 times**, followed by an **output transformation** (partial last round):

### 2.2.1 The Four Round Transformations

| Step | Transformation | Operation | Analogy |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **SubBytes (SB)** | Apply 8-bit non-linear S-box to each byte of the 8×8 state | "Confusing" — non-linearity |
| 2 | **ShiftColumns (SC)** | Cyclically shift each column of the 8×8 state downward by $c$ rows | "Spreading" — column diffusion |
| 3 | **MixRows (MR)** | Multiply each row by a $8 \times 8$ MDS matrix over $\text{GF}(2^8)$ | "Mixing" — row diffusion |
| 4 | **AddRoundKey (ARK)** | XOR the state with the round key $K_r$ | "Combining" — key injection |

### 2.2.2 ShiftColumns Pattern

In the 8×8 state matrix, each column $c$ (numbered $0$ to $7$) is shifted down by **$c$ positions**:

$$\text{SC}: \text{State}[i][j] \leftarrow \text{State}[(i - j) \bmod 8][j]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Notice that the shift offset **varies per column** in Whirlpool, whereas in AES, all columns use the same shift offset within a given row. This provides **stronger inter-column mixing**.

### 2.2.3 Key Schedule

The 512-bit round keys $K_1, K_2, \ldots, K_{11}$ are generated from the 512-bit input key $K_0 = M_i$ using a **dedicated key schedule**, which itself is built on the same SubBytes + ShiftColumns + MixRows + AddRoundKey structure (with a different round constant schedule).

---

## 2.3 The S-box (SubBytes Layer)

The S-box of Whirlpool is generated by a **recursive construction** using a smaller **"mini" S-box**:

- **Mini S-box** $S'[x]$: A non-linear 4-bit substitution table applied bit-wise.
- **Full S-box** $S[x]$: Each output bit is computed as $S'[j]$ of a 4-bit slice of the input, then bits are permuted.

This results in an 8-bit S-box that is **non-linear, balanced, and has no fixed points**.

The S-box provides **non-linearity**, the cornerstone of cryptographic security against linear and differential cryptanalysis.

---

## 2.4 The MDS Matrix (MixRows Layer)

The MixRows transformation uses a **$8 \times 8$ Maximum Distance Separable (MDS) matrix** $M$ over the Galois Field $\text{GF}(2^8)$ with the irreducible polynomial:
$$p(x) = x^8 + x^4 + x^3 + x^2 + 1$$

For each row $r$ of the state matrix, the transformation is:
$$\text{State}'[r] = M \times \text{State}[r] \pmod{p(x)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The MDS property guarantees the **branch number** is maximized at $9$, meaning any non-zero input difference to a row produces at least **9 active bytes** at the output — providing **optimal diffusion**.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Concept | Formula / Definition | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Message length limit | $\vert M \vert < 2^{256}$ bits | Prevents length-extension ambiguity |
| Block size | $b = 512$ bits | Block cipher block length |
| Digest size | $n = 512$ bits | Hash output length |
| Rounds | $R = 10$ | Security margin |
| Compression function | $H_i = W(H_{i-1}, M_i)$ | Merkle–Damgård iteration |
| Initial state $H_0$ | All zeros (512 bits) | IV — Initialization Vector |
| Galois Field | $\text{GF}(2^8)$, $p(x) = x^8 + x^4 + x^3 + x^2 + 1$ | Byte arithmetic basis |
| Branch number (MDS) | $\mathcal{B} = 9$ | Optimal diffusion |
| Birthday security | $2^{n/2} = 2^{256}$ | Collision resistance |
| Pre-image security | $2^{n} = 2^{512}$ | One-wayness |
| Round transformation | $\text{SB} \rightarrow \text{SC} \rightarrow \text{MR} \rightarrow \text{ARK}$ | AES-style round |
| Padding rule | Pad to $\equiv 896 \pmod{1024}$ bits | Reserve 256 bits for length |
| S-box type | Recursive mini S-box + bit permutation | Non-linearity source |
| Shift offset column $c$ | $c$ positions downward | Inter-column mixing |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU**: The **512-bit block size and 512-bit key size** are **double** the AES values. This is **not** AES itself; Whirlpool is a **new cipher** sharing AES's **design philosophy** (SPN structure) but with **different parameters, S-box, MDS matrix, and round constants**.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Use of Whirlpool |
|---|---|
| **Digital Signatures** | Message digest of long documents before RSA/ECDSA signing |
| **File Integrity** | Checksums for archival systems and OS images |
| **Blockchain** | Hash of transactions in some altcoin implementations |
| **Password Hashing** | Base primitive in PBKDF variants |
| **HMAC** | Inside HMAC-Whirlpool keyed MAC schemes |
| **ISO Compliance Systems** | Government, banking, and financial IT infrastructure |
| **Forensic / Cyber Forensics** | Tamper detection of evidence files |
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# WHIRLPOOL — Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivation of the Hash Computation

## 3.1 Detailed Step-by-Step Hash Computation

### Step 1 — Message Preprocessing

Given an input message $M$ of length $L$ bits (with $L < 2^{256}$), construct the **padded message** $M'$:

1. Append a single '1' bit.
2. Append $k$ '0' bits, where $k$ is the smallest non-negative integer such that:
$$L + 1 + k \equiv 896 \pmod{1024}$$
3. Append the 256-bit representation of $L$.

The total length of $M'$ is now an integer multiple of **1024 bits** (i.e., an integer multiple of $2 \times 512$-bit blocks). This is because:
$$896 + 256 = 1152 \equiv 128 \pmod{1024} \text{ and } 128 + 896 = 1024 \checkmark$$

> [!NOTE]
> The 256-bit length field **cures the classical Merkle–Damgård ambiguity** — two messages of different lengths will hash differently even if they share the same content pattern.

### Step 2 — Parsing Into Blocks

Split $M'$ into $N$ blocks:
$$M' = M_1 \Vert M_2 \Vert \cdots \Vert M_N$$
where each $M_i$ is exactly **512 bits**.

### Step 3 — Initialize the Chaining Value

$$H_0 = \underbrace{\texttt{0x00 00 00 00} \cdots \texttt{00 00 00 00}}_{64 \text{ bytes}}$$

### Step 4 — Iterative Compression (Core Loop)

For each block index $i$ from $1$ to $N$, perform:

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_0 &= H_{i-1} \oplus M_i \quad \text{(initial round key)} \\
\text{State}_0 &= M_i \\
\text{For } r &= 1, 2, \ldots, 10: \\
\text{State}_r &= \text{ARK}(\text{MR}(\text{SC}(\text{SB}(\text{State}_{r-1}))), K_r) \\
K_{r+1} &= \text{KeySchedule}(K_r, r) \\
\text{State}_{10}^{\text{final}} &= \text{ARK}(\text{MR}(\text{SC}(\text{SB}(\text{State}_{10}))), K_{11}) \\
H_i &= \text{State}_{10}^{\text{final}} \oplus H_{i-1} \oplus M_i
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **XOR feedback at the end** ($H_{i-1} \oplus M_i$) is a **Davies–Meyer style** hardening that turns the block cipher into a one-way compression function, preventing trivial inversion.

### Step 5 — Output the Digest

The final message digest is:
$$\text{Whirlpool}(M) = H_N$$

This is the **512-bit hash value** of the original message $M$.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Trace — Single Block Scenario

Suppose we have a 1-block message ($N = 1$, $L < 512$, with appropriate padding):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Pad } M &\Rightarrow M_1 = \text{1-bit} \Vert \text{0-bits} \Vert \text{256-bit length} \\
\text{Step 2: } H_0 &= 0^{512} \\
\text{Step 3: } K_0 &= H_0 \oplus M_1 = M_1 \\
\text{Step 4: Apply 10 rounds of } & \text{SB} \rightarrow \text{SC} \rightarrow \text{MR} \rightarrow \text{ARK} \\
\text{with derived round keys } & K_1, K_2, \ldots, K_{11} \\
\text{Step 5: Compute } H_1 &= \text{FinalState} \oplus H_0 \oplus M_1 \\
\text{Step 6: Output } & \text{Whirlpool}(M) = H_1
\end{aligned}
$$

The 512-bit value $H_1$ is the **Whirlpool digest** for that single-block input.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the S-box Construction

The Whirlpool S-box is **recursively constructed**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Input byte } x &= (b_7 b_6 b_5 b_4 b_3 b_2 b_1 b_0)_2 \\
\text{Split into 4-bit nibbles: } & (b_7 b_6 b_5 b_4) \text{ and } (b_3 b_2 b_1 b_0) \\
\text{Apply mini S-box } S' &: \text{to each nibble} \\
\text{Apply bit permutation } \pi &: \text{to the resulting 8 bits} \\
\text{Output: } S[x] &= \pi(S'[b_7 b_6 b_5 b_4] \, \Vert \, S'[b_3 b_2 b_1 b_0])
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The mini S-box $S'$ is itself a non-linear 4-bit table, derived to provide optimal **differential uniformity** (differentially 4-uniform) and **non-linearity score**.

---

## 3.4 The Round Constant Schedule (Key Schedule)

For each round $r$, a round constant $C_r$ is XORed into the top-left byte of the key state. The constants are:

$$C_r = (S'[8(r-1) + j])_{j=0,1,\ldots,7} \text{ for each column } j$$

This ensures that **all round keys are distinct**, even for identical input keys (an avalanche prerequisite).

---

## 3.5 Complete Security Properties Summary

| Property | Whirlpool's Achievement | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Pre-image resistance** | $\geq 2^{512}$ operations | Direct inversion infeasible |
| **Second pre-image resistance** | $\geq 2^{512}$ operations | Cannot find $M' \neq M$ with same hash |
| **Collision resistance** | $\geq 2^{256}$ operations | Birthday paradox bound |
| **Avalanche effect** | Single-bit flip $\rightarrow \approx 50\%$ output bit change | High diffusion |
| **Indifferentiability** | Provably secure random oracle in ideal-cipher model | Strong theoretical foundation |
| **Resistance to length extension** | Yes (length field included) | Safe for naïve MAC usage |
| **Standardized** | ISO/IEC 10118-3:2004 | Globally recognized |
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# WHIRLPOOL — Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Whirlpool Hash Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inputMsg["Input Message M (less than 2^256 bits)"] --> pad1["Append 1 bit"]
    pad1 --> pad2["Append k zero bits to reach 896 mod 1024"]
    pad2 --> pad3["Append 256-bit length field"]
    pad3 --> parse["Parse into 512-bit blocks M1, M2, ... MN"]
    parse --> init["H0 = 0 (512-bit zero vector)"]
    init --> loopStart{"For i = 1 to N"}
    loopStart --> xorKey["K0 = H(i-1) XOR Mi"]
    xorKey --> roundLoop["Apply 10 AES-style rounds: SB -> SC -> MR -> ARK"]
    roundLoop --> finalTrans["Output Transformation (partial round)"]
    finalTrans --> feedback["Hi = FinalState XOR H(i-1) XOR Mi (Davies-Meyer feedback)"]
    feedback --> loopCheck{"i less than N?"}
    loopCheck -->|Yes| loopStart
    loopCheck -->|No| outputHash["Output: 512-bit Whirlpool digest = HN"]
    outputHash --> finalDigest["Whirlpool M = 512-bit hash"]

    style inputMsg fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd
    style finalDigest fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32
    style roundLoop fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f9a825
    style feedback fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#d84315
```

---

## 4.2 Internal Round Structure of the Block Cipher

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inState["Input State (8x8 byte matrix)"] --> sb["SubBytes SB: Apply 8-bit S-box to each byte"]
    sb --> sc["ShiftColumns SC: Cyclically shift each column by its index"]
    sc --> mr["MixRows MR: Multiply by 8x8 MDS matrix over GF 2^8"]
    mr --> ark["AddRoundKey ARK: XOR with 512-bit round key Kr"]
    ark --> outState["Output State -> next round or final transformation"]

    ks["Key Schedule (from Ki)"] --> ark

    style sb fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#1565c0
    style sc fill:#c5e1a5,stroke:#558b2f
    style mr fill:#ffcc80,stroke:#ef6c00
    style ark fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#ad1457
    style ks fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#5e35b1
```

---

## 4.3 Compression Function $W$ — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    HiPrev["H(i-1): 512-bit Chaining Input"] --> xorA["XOR with Mi"]
    Mi["Mi: 512-bit Message Block"] --> xorA
    xorA --> K0["K0: Initial 512-bit Round Key"]
    K0 --> round10["10 Rounds of Modified AES-Style Encryption"]
    round10 --> finalSt["State after Round 10"]
    finalSt --> xorB["XOR with H(i-1) and Mi"]
    HiPrev --> xorB
    Mi --> xorB
    xorB --> HiOut["Hi: Next 512-bit Chaining Value"]

    style HiPrev fill:#b3e5fc,stroke:#01579b
    style Mi fill:#b3e5fc,stroke:#01579b
    style K0 fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100
    style round10 fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17
    style HiOut fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#1b5e20
```

---

## 4.4 The 8x8 State Matrix — Whirlpool vs AES Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    aes["AES 4x4 State: 128 bits / 16 bytes"] -->|--different--> wp["Whirlpool 8x8 State: 512 bits / 64 bytes"]
    aes -->|--different--> aesSbox["S-box: inversion in GF 2^8 with poly x^8+x^4+x^3+x+1"]
    wp --> wpSbox["S-box: recursive mini S-box with bit permutation"]
    aes --> aesShift["ShiftRows: row-based cyclic shifts"]
    wp --> wpShift["ShiftColumns: column-based cyclic shifts"]
    aes --> aesRounds["Rounds: 10, 12, or 14"]
    wp --> wpRounds["Rounds: fixed 10"]
    aes --> aesKey["Key sizes: 128, 192, 256 bits"]
    wp --> wpKey["Key size: 512 bits (single)"]
    aes --> aesPoly["GF 2^8 polynomial: x^8+x^4+x^3+x+1"]
    wp --> wpPoly["GF 2^8 polynomial: x^8+x^4+x^3+x^2+1"]
    aes --> aesMDS["MixColumns: 4x4 MDS matrix"]
    wp --> wpMDS["MixRows: 8x8 MDS matrix"]
```
