# WHIRLPOOL — Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: List any **six** key parameters of the Whirlpool hash function.**

**Model Answer:**

| S.No. | Parameter | Value |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Block size | 512 bits |
| 2 | Digest size | 512 bits |
| 3 | Number of rounds | 10 |
| 4 | Internal state | 8 × 8 byte matrix |
| 5 | Maximum input length | less than $2^{256}$ bits |
| 6 | Standard | ISO/IEC 10118-3:2004 |
| 7 | Designers | Vincent Rijmen, Paulo S. M. L. M. Barreto |
| 8 | Year of release | 2000 (revised 2001, 2003) |

*(Any six correctly stated parameters carry 3 marks.)*

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Differentiate between the S-box of Whirlpool and the S-box of AES in two points.**

**Model Answer:**

| S.No. | Aspect | AES S-box | Whirlpool S-box |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Construction | Computed via multiplicative inversion in $\text{GF}(2^8)$ followed by an affine transformation | Generated recursively using a 4-bit mini S-box followed by a bit permutation |
| 2 | Underlying polynomial | $x^8 + x^4 + x^3 + x + 1$ | $x^8 + x^4 + x^3 + x^2 + 1$ |
| 3 | Design philosophy | Direct algebraic inversion | Composite, layered substitution |

*(Any two valid points carrying 1.5 marks each.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### Question B1 — Option A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Explain the complete structure of the Whirlpool hash function in detail. Describe its compression function, round transformations, and key schedule.**

#### Part (a) — Compression Function and Algorithm Pipeline **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The Whirlpool hash algorithm consists of three main stages:

**1. Message Preprocessing (Padding):**
The message $M$ of length $L$ bits is padded as follows:
- Append a '1' bit.
- Append the minimum number of '0' bits to make $L + 1 + k \equiv 896 \pmod{1024}$.
- Append the 256-bit representation of $L$.

This ensures the padded message length is a multiple of 1024 bits.

**2. Parsing:**
The padded message is split into $N$ 512-bit blocks: $M_1, M_2, \ldots, M_N$.

**3. Hash Computation:**
The hash is computed iteratively:
$$H_0 = 0^{512}$$
$$H_i = W(H_{i-1}, M_i), \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, N$$
where $W$ is the compression function and the final output is $H_N$.

**4. The Compression Function $W$:**
The compression function is based on a **dedicated 512-bit block cipher** $E$, structured as an **AES-style SPN** with 10 rounds. It accepts:
- **Plaintext**: $M_i$ (512 bits)
- **Key**: $H_{i-1}$ (512 bits)

The function executes 10 rounds and applies an output transformation, then computes:
$$H_i = E_{H_{i-1}}(M_i) \oplus H_{i-1} \oplus M_i$$

This **Davies–Meyer feedback** ensures one-wayness.

#### Part (b) — Round Transformations and Key Schedule **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**The Four Round Transformations** (applied 10 times):

1. **SubBytes (SB):** Each of the 64 bytes of the 8 × 8 state matrix is substituted using the **Whirlpool S-box** (recursive mini S-box construction). This provides **non-linearity**.

2. **ShiftColumns (SC):** The bytes in column $c$ are cyclically shifted down by $c$ positions. This ensures that columns are **re-mixed** across rows.

3. **MixRows (MR):** Each row is multiplied by an 8 × 8 **MDS matrix** over $\text{GF}(2^8)$. This provides optimal **diffusion** with branch number 9.

4. **AddRoundKey (ARK):** The state is XORed with the 512-bit round key $K_r$.

**Key Schedule:**
- The 512-bit round keys $K_0, K_1, \ldots, K_{11}$ are generated from the input key $K_0 = H_{i-1}$.
- The same round function is reused with **different round constants**.
- The round constant $C_r$ is generated from the mini S-box: $C_r[j] = S'[8(r-1) + j]$.
- This guarantees that all round keys are distinct, providing **avalanche**.

**Final Output Transformation:**
A partial round (SB → SC → MR → ARK) using the 11th round key is applied, and the result is XORed with $H_{i-1}$ and $M_i$ to produce $H_i$.

---

### Question B1 — Option B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: With a neat diagram, explain the working of the Whirlpool hash function. Compare its structure with the AES algorithm.**

#### Part (a) — Whirlpool Algorithm with Diagram **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Whirlpool Working Steps:**

1. **Input Padding:** Append '1' bit + zeros + 256-bit length field to make total length a multiple of 1024 bits.

2. **Block Division:** Split padded message into 512-bit blocks $M_1, M_2, \ldots, M_N$.

3. **Initialization:** Set $H_0 = 0^{512}$.

4. **Compression Function Application:** For each block, compute:
$$H_i = E_{H_{i-1}}(M_i) \oplus H_{i-1} \oplus M_i$$

5. **Block Cipher $E$ Internals:**
   - 10 rounds of SubBytes → ShiftColumns → MixRows → AddRoundKey.
   - Final partial round + Davies–Meyer XOR feedback.

6. **Output:** The final $H_N$ is the 512-bit Whirlpool hash.

**ASCII Pipeline Diagram (Marks credited):**

```
   M (input) → [Pad + Length] → M1, M2, ... MN
                                       ↓
   H0 = 0 → [XOR] → K0 → [10 rounds of E] → Final State
                                       ↓
                          [XOR H(i-1), Mi] → Hi
                                       ↓
                                  (loop for N)
                                       ↓
                              HN (512-bit digest)
```

#### Part (b) — Comparison of Whirlpool with AES **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| S.No. | Feature | AES | Whirlpool |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Primary purpose | Block cipher (encryption) | Hash function |
| 2 | Block size | 128 bits | 512 bits |
| 3 | Key size | 128 / 192 / 256 bits | 512 bits (single) |
| 4 | State matrix | 4 × 4 bytes (16 bytes) | 8 × 8 bytes (64 bytes) |
| 5 | Number of rounds | 10 / 12 / 14 | 10 (fixed) |
| 6 | Shift operation | ShiftRows (rows shifted) | ShiftColumns (columns shifted) |
| 7 | Mix operation | MixColumns (4 × 4 MDS) | MixRows (8 × 8 MDS) |
| 8 | S-box | Algebraic inversion + affine | Recursive mini S-box + permutation |
| 9 | GF(2^8) polynomial | $x^8 + x^4 + x^3 + x + 1$ | $x^8 + x^4 + x^3 + x^2 + 1$ |
| 10 | Standard | FIPS 197 | ISO/IEC 10118-3:2004 |
| 11 | Key schedule | Independent | Reuses block cipher rounds |
| 12 | Design origin | Rijndael (Daemen, Rijmen) | Rijmen, Barreto (2000) |

**Concluding Remark:** Whirlpool is **inspired by** but **distinct from** AES. It uses a **larger state (512 bits)**, **different S-box**, **different Galois field polynomial**, and a **column-based shift** to achieve cryptographic properties suited for **hashing**, not encryption.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 
> **Common Mistakes KTU Students Make (Lose 2–4 Marks):**
> 1. **Confusing Whirlpool with AES itself** — Whirlpool is **NOT AES**. It is a *separate* hash function that *borrows design philosophy* from AES but uses different parameters. Saying "Whirlpool uses AES-256" is **incorrect**.
> 2. **Wrong block size** — Many students write 128 bits. The correct block size is **512 bits**.
> 3. **Wrong polynomial** — The Whirlpool GF($2^8$) polynomial is $x^8 + x^4 + x^3 + x^2 + 1$ — **NOT** the AES polynomial $x^8 + x^4 + x^3 + x + 1$.
> 4. **Confusing rounds** — Whirlpool has **10 fixed rounds** — not 10/12/14 like AES.
> 5. **Forgetting the Davies–Meyer XOR feedback** — The final XOR with $H_{i-1} \oplus M_i$ is essential for one-wayness; skipping this loses 2 marks.
> 6. **Confusing initial value** — $H_0$ is **all zeros**, not the message digest.
> 7. **Padding confusion** — Padding rule is $\equiv 896 \pmod{1024}$, **not** 448 mod 512.
> 8. **Writing "ShiftRows" instead of "ShiftColumns"** — KTU specifically uses the term "ShiftColumns" in Whirlpool.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final High-Density Revision Block — Read 3 Times Before Exam**

- ✅ Whirlpool = **Hash function**, designed by **Rijmen & Barreto (2000)**, standardized as **ISO/IEC 10118-3:2004**.
- ✅ **Block size = 512 bits**, **Digest = 512 bits**, **Rounds = 10**.
- ✅ Internal state = **8 × 8 matrix of bytes (64 bytes)**.
- ✅ Based on a **dedicated 512-bit block cipher** with AES-style SPN structure.
- ✅ **Four round operations**: **S**ubBytes → **S**hiftColumns → **M**ixRows → **A**ddRoundKey.
- ✅ **S-box**: Recursive construction using a 4-bit mini S-box + bit permutation.
- ✅ **MDS matrix** for MixRows is **8 × 8** over $\text{GF}(2^8)$ with polynomial $x^8 + x^4 + x^3 + x^2 + 1$.
- ✅ **ShiftColumns**: Column $c$ shifted down by $c$ positions (not uniform like AES's ShiftRows).
- ✅ **Merkle–Damgård construction** with **256-bit length field** to prevent length-extension.
- ✅ **Davies–Meyer feedback**: $H_i = E_{H_{i-1}}(M_i) \oplus H_{i-1} \oplus M_i$.
- ✅ **Initial hash value $H_0$ = 512 zero bits**.
- ✅ **Maximum message length**: less than $2^{256}$ bits.
- ✅ **Padding rule**: Append '1', then zeros, to reach length $\equiv 896 \pmod{1024}$, then 256-bit length.
- ✅ **Collision resistance**: $2^{256}$ (birthday bound), **Pre-image**: $2^{512}$.
- ✅ **NOT AES** — distinct S-box, distinct polynomial, distinct block size, distinct shift direction.
- ✅ **Real-world uses**: Digital signatures, file integrity, HMAC, blockchain, ISO-compliant systems.
- ✅ **Branch number** of MDS = **9** (optimal).
- ✅ **Standardized by NESSIE** project (New European Schemes for Signatures, Integrity, and Encryption).
- ✅ Remember: **ShiftColumns** (not ShiftRows), **MixRows** (not MixColumns), **8 × 8** (not 4 × 4).
- ✅ Final KTU mantra: *"Whirlpool is to hashing what AES is to encryption — both are SPN-based, both 10+ rounds, both field-based — but Whirlpool is **wider, deeper, and built for one-wayness**, not confidentiality."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Digital Signature: Comparison
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT523 - DATA PRIVACY AND SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Digital Signature: Comparison

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a Digital Signature?

A **Digital Signature** is a cryptographic mechanism that provides **authenticity**, **integrity**, and **non-repudiation** for digital messages or documents. It is the mathematical equivalent of a handwritten signature or a stamped seal, but far more secure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:** A digital signature is a mathematical scheme that uses asymmetric cryptography to produce a unique alphanumeric string (signature) bound to a message and the signer's private key, verifiable by anyone possessing the corresponding public key, the message, and the signature.

The three foundational security properties guaranteed by any standard digital signature are:

1. **Authenticity** — Confirms the identity of the signer.
2. **Integrity** — Detects any tampering of the message after signing.
3. **Non-Repudiation** — The signer cannot later falsely deny having signed the message.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Wax Seal of Medieval Royalty**
> Imagine a king who wants to send a sealed letter to a general. The king has a unique **signet ring** (his **private key**). When he presses the ring into hot wax on the letter, it leaves a unique imprint (the **digital signature**). Anyone in the kingdom who knows what the king's seal looks like (the **public key**) can verify that the letter truly came from the king. If a thief tampers with the letter, the seal breaks, revealing the forgery. Critically, the king cannot later claim he never sent the letter — the wax seal is his non-repudiable proof.

In modern cryptography, the wax seal is replaced by **complex mathematical functions** (modular exponentiation, elliptic curve operations, discrete logarithms), and the "imprint" is a numeric signature that is computationally infeasible to forge.

### 1.3 Why Comparison Matters in KTU Examinations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The 2024 KTU scheme specifically requires students to critically compare digital signature schemes across multiple parameters — key size, signature size, security strength, computational overhead, and standardization. Examiners frequently ask comparison-based questions worth 7–14 marks.

The major digital signature schemes studied in PECDT523 are:

- **RSA Digital Signature Scheme**
- **Digital Signature Algorithm (DSA)**
- **Elliptic Curve Digital Signature Algorithm (ECDSA)**
- **ElGamal Digital Signature Scheme**
- **Schnorr Digital Signature Scheme**
- **Rabin Digital Signature Scheme**

### 1.4 Generic Digital Signature Workflow

Every digital signature scheme operates on a **three-stage pipeline**:

1. **Key Generation** — Produces a private key ($K_{pr}$) and a public key ($K_{pu}$).
2. **Signing** — Computes signature $S = \text{Sign}(K_{pr}, M)$ over message $M$.
3. **Verification** — Confirms validity by checking $\text{Verify}(K_{pu}, M, S) = \text{Valid} \vert \text{Invalid}$.

This uniformity allows fair cross-scheme comparison.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Major Digital Signature Schemes — Technical Deep Dive

#### 2.1.1 RSA Digital Signature Scheme

**Mathematical Basis:** Difficulty of factoring the product of two large primes.

**Key Generation:**
- Select two large primes $p$ and $q$.
- Compute modulus $n = p \times q$.
- Compute Euler's totient $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$.
- Choose public exponent $e$ such that $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$.
- Compute private exponent $d \equiv e^{-1} \pmod{\phi(n)}$.

**Signing:** Compute $S = M^d \pmod{n}$.

**Verification:** Compute $M' = S^e \pmod{n}$. Accept if $M' = M$.

#### 2.1.2 Digital Signature Algorithm (DSA)

**Mathematical Basis:** Discrete logarithm problem in a prime-order subgroup of $\mathbb{Z}_p^*$.

**Parameters:** Prime $p$, prime divisor $q$ of $p-1$, generator $g$, per-user private key $x$, per-user public key $y = g^x \pmod{p}$, secret random $k$ per signature.

**Signing:**
- Compute $r = (g^k \pmod p) \pmod q$.
- Compute $s = k^{-1}(H(M) + x \cdot r) \pmod q$.
- Signature is the pair $(r, s)$.

**Verification:**
- Compute $w = s^{-1} \pmod q$.
- Compute $u_1 = H(M) \cdot w \pmod q$.
- Compute $u_2 = r \cdot w \pmod q$.
- Compute $v = (g^{u_1} \cdot y^{u_2} \pmod p) \pmod q$.
- Accept if $v = r$.

#### 2.1.3 Elliptic Curve Digital Signature Algorithm (ECDSA)

**Mathematical Basis:** Elliptic Curve Discrete Logarithm Problem (ECDLP).

**Parameters:** Elliptic curve $E$ over finite field $\mathbb{F}_p$, base point $G$ of order $n$, private key $d$, public key $Q = dG$.

**Signing:**
- Select random $k \in [1, n-1]$.
- Compute point $(x_1, y_1) = kG$.
- Set $r = x_1 \pmod n$. If $r = 0$, choose new $k$.
- Compute $s = k^{-1}(H(M) + d \cdot r) \pmod n$.
- Signature is $(r, s)$.

**Verification:**
- Verify $r, s \in [1, n-1]$.
- Compute $w = s^{-1} \pmod n$.
- Compute $u_1 = H(M) \cdot w \pmod n$ and $u_2 = r \cdot w \pmod n$.
- Compute $(x_1, y_1) = u_1 G + u_2 Q$.
- Accept if $r \equiv x_1 \pmod n$.

#### 2.1.4 ElGamal Digital Signature Scheme

**Mathematical Basis:** Discrete logarithm problem in $\mathbb{Z}_p^*$.

**Parameters:** Prime $p$, generator $g$, private key $x$, public key $y = g^x \pmod p$.

**Signing:**
- Choose random $k$ with $\gcd(k, p-1) = 1$.
- Compute $r = g^k \pmod p$.
- Compute $s = k^{-1}(H(M) - x \cdot r) \pmod{p-1}$.
- Signature is $(r, s)$.

**Verification:**
- Verify $1 \leq r \leq p-1$.
- Compute $v_1 = g^{H(M)} \pmod p$.
- Compute $v_2 = y^r \cdot r^s \pmod p$.
- Accept if $v_1 = v_2 \pmod p$.

#### 2.1.5 Schnorr Digital Signature Scheme

**Mathematical Basis:** Discrete logarithm problem with shorter signature length than DSA.

**Parameters:** Prime $p$, prime $q \mid (p-1)$, generator $g$ of order $q$, private key $x$, public key $y = g^x \pmod p$.

**Signing:**
- Choose random $k$.
- Compute $r = g^k \pmod p$.
- Compute $e = H(M \vert r)$.
- Compute $s = k - x \cdot e \pmod q$.
- Signature is $(e, s)$.

**Verification:**
- Compute $r' = g^s \cdot y^e \pmod p$.
- Compute $e' = H(M \vert r')$.
- Accept if $e' = e$.

#### 2.1.6 Rabin Digital Signature Scheme

**Mathematical Basis:** Difficulty of computing square roots modulo $n = pq$.

**Key Generation:** Choose primes $p, q \equiv 3 \pmod 4$. Set $n = pq$. Public key is $n$; private key is $(p, q)$.

**Signing:** Compute $S = M^{1/2} \pmod n$ (square root).

**Verification:** Check $S^2 \equiv M \pmod n$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the "Why" matters:** Each scheme leverages a different **hard mathematical problem**. RSA relies on integer factorization, DSA/Schnorr/ElGamal rely on discrete logarithm, ECDSA relies on elliptic curve discrete logarithm, and Rabin relies on square root extraction modulo a composite. The security of a digital signature is only as strong as the assumed hardness of its underlying problem.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Parameter** | **RSA** | **DSA** | **ECDSA** | **ElGamal** | **Schnorr** | **Rabin** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Hard Problem** | Integer Factorization | Discrete Logarithm (DLP) | Elliptic Curve DLP | Discrete Logarithm | Discrete Logarithm | Square Root Modulo $n$ |
| **Public Key Size (128-bit security)** | **3072 bits** | **3072 bits** | **256 bits** | **3072 bits** | **3072 bits** | **3072 bits** |
| **Private Key Size** | 3072 bits | 256 bits | 256 bits | 256 bits | 256 bits | $p, q$ each 1536 bits |
| **Signature Size** | 3072 bits (1 value) | 320–512 bits (2 values) | 512 bits (2 values) | 1024–2048 bits (2 values) | 320 bits (2 values) | 1024 bits |
| **Signing Speed** | Slow (large exponentiation) | Moderate | **Fast** | Slow | Moderate | Moderate |
| **Verification Speed** | **Fast** (small public exponent) | Moderate | Moderate | Slow | **Fast** | **Fast** |
| **Key Generation Speed** | Very Slow | Fast | **Very Fast** | Fast | Fast | Fast |
| **Standard** | PKCS#1, X.509 | FIPS 186-4, DSS | FIPS 186-4, ANSI X9.62 | None (academic) | IEEE P1363 | ISO/IEC 9796 |
| **Patent Status** | Expired (2000) | **Unpatented** | **Unpatented** | **Unpatented** | **Patented (expired)** | **Unpatented** |
| **Hash Required** | Optional | **Mandatory** | **Mandatory** | **Mandatory** | **Mandatory** | Optional |
| **Signature Form** | Single integer | Pair $(r, s)$ | Pair $(r, s)$ | Pair $(r, s)$ | Pair $(e, s)$ | Single integer |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** At **128-bit security level** (the KTU 2024 benchmark), ECDSA achieves the same cryptographic strength as RSA/DSA with a key that is approximately **12× smaller**. This is why ECDSA is preferred in mobile and IoT applications.

### 2.3 Engineering Real-World Utility

| **Industry** | **Preferred Scheme** | **Reason** |
|---|---|---|
| Web/TLS certificates | RSA, ECDSA | Wide compatibility |
| Government/Defense (USA) | ECDSA, RSA | FIPS 186-4 compliance |
| Blockchain/Bitcoin | **ECDSA (secp256k1)** | Compact signatures, fast verification |
| Banking/Finance | RSA, DSA | Long-term archival, regulatory acceptance |
| IoT/Embedded Systems | **ECDSA** | Small keys, low memory footprint |
| Smart Cards | RSA, ECDSA | Hardware acceleration available |
| Academic/Research | ElGamal, Schnorr | Mathematical simplicity |

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example — RSA Digital Signature

**Problem:** Alice wants to sign the message $M = 7$ using RSA. The system parameters are $p = 11$, $q = 13$, $e = 7$. Compute the signature and verify it.

**Step 1: Compute the modulus.**

$$n = p \times q = 11 \times 13 = 143$$

**Step 2: Compute Euler's totient.**

$$\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1) = 10 \times 12 = 120$$

**Step 3: Verify the public exponent is valid.**

$$\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = \gcd(7, 120) = 1 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 4: Compute the private exponent using the Extended Euclidean Algorithm.**

We need $d$ such that $7d \equiv 1 \pmod{120}$.

Using the extended Euclidean algorithm:

$$120 = 17 \times 7 + 1$$
$$1 = 120 - 17 \times 7$$

Therefore $d \equiv -17 \equiv 103 \pmod{120}$.

**Step 5: Compute the digital signature.**

$$S = M^d \pmod n = 7^{103} \pmod{143}$$

We apply the square-and-multiply method for modular exponentiation. First, express 103 in binary:

$$103 = 64 + 32 + 4 + 2 + 1 = 1100111_2$$

Compute successive squares modulo 143:

$$7^1 \equiv 7 \pmod{143}$$
$$7^2 \equiv 49 \pmod{143}$$
$$7^4 \equiv 49^2 = 2401 \equiv 2401 - 16 \times 143 = 2401 - 2288 = 113 \pmod{143}$$
$$7^8 \equiv 113^2 = 12769 \equiv 12769 - 89 \times 143 = 12769 - 12727 = 42 \pmod{143}$$
$$7^{16} \equiv 42^2 = 1764 \equiv 1764 - 12 \times 143 = 1764 - 1716 = 48 \pmod{143}$$
$$7^{32} \equiv 48^2 = 2304 \equiv 2304 - 16 \times 143 = 2304 - 2288 = 16 \pmod{143}$$
$$7^{64} \equiv 16^2 = 256 \equiv 256 - 1 \times 143 = 113 \pmod{143}$$

Multiply the required powers (positions 64, 32, 4, 2, 1):

$$7^{103} = 7^{64} \times 7^{32} \times 7^{4} \times 7^{2} \times 7^{1}$$

Compute step by step:

$$113 \times 16 = 1808 \equiv 1808 - 12 \times 143 = 1808 - 1716 = 92 \pmod{143}$$
$$92 \times 113 = 10396 \equiv 10396 - 72 \times 143 = 10396 - 10296 = 100 \pmod{143}$$
$$100 \times 49 = 4900 \equiv 4900 - 34 \times 143 = 4900 - 4862 = 38 \pmod{143}$$
$$38 \times 7 = 266 \equiv 266 - 1 \times 143 = 123 \pmod{143}$$

Therefore, the digital signature is:

$$S = 123$$

**Step 6: Verification by Bob.**

Bob computes:

$$M' = S^e \pmod n = 123^7 \pmod{143}$$

Using binary expansion of 7: $7 = 4 + 2 + 1$.

$$123^1 \equiv 123 \pmod{143}$$
$$123^2 \equiv 123^2 = 15129 \equiv 15129 - 105 \times 143 = 15129 - 15015 = 114 \pmod{143}$$
$$123^4 \equiv 114^2 = 12996 \equiv 12996 - 90 \times 143 = 12996 - 12870 = 126 \pmod{143}$$

Multiply:

$$126 \times 114 = 14364 \equiv 14364 - 100 \times 143 = 14364 - 14300 = 64 \pmod{143}$$
$$64 \times 123 = 7872 \equiv 7872 - 55 \times 143 = 7872 - 7865 = 7 \pmod{143}$$

Thus $M' = 7 = M$, confirming the signature is **valid**. $\blacksquare$

### 3.2 Worked Example — ElGamal Digital Signature

**Problem:** Sign the message $M = 5$ using ElGamal with prime $p = 23$, generator $g = 5$, private key $x = 7$. Use random $k = 11$ and assume $\text{Hash}(M) = 5$ for simplicity.

**Step 1: Compute the public key.**

$$y = g^x \pmod p = 5^7 \pmod{23}$$

Compute $5^2 = 25 \equiv 2$, $5^4 \equiv 2^2 = 4$, $5^7 = 5^4 \cdot 5^2 \cdot 5^1 \equiv 4 \cdot 2 \cdot 5 = 40 \equiv 17 \pmod{23}$.

Public key: $y = 17$.

**Step 2: Compute the first signature component.**

$$r = g^k \pmod p = 5^{11} \pmod{23}$$

We have $5^2 \equiv 2$, $5^4 \equiv 4$, $5^8 \equiv 16$, and $5^{11} = 5^8 \cdot 5^2 \cdot 5^1 \equiv 16 \cdot 2 \cdot 5 = 160 \equiv 160 - 6 \times 23 = 160 - 138 = 22 \pmod{23}$.

Therefore $r = 22$.

**Step 3: Compute the modular inverse $k^{-1} \pmod{p-1}$.**

We need $11^{-1} \pmod{22}$. Since $\gcd(11, 22) = 11 \neq 1$, this $k$ is invalid. We must choose a $k$ with $\gcd(k, p-1) = 1$.

**Corrected:** Let $k = 3$ (since $\gcd(3, 22) = 1$).

$$r = 5^3 = 125 \equiv 125 - 5 \times 23 = 125 - 115 = 10 \pmod{23}$$

**Step 4: Compute $k^{-1} \pmod{p-1}$.**

$3 \times 15 = 45 \equiv 45 - 2 \times 22 = 1 \pmod{22}$. So $k^{-1} = 15$.

**Step 5: Compute the second signature component.**

$$s = k^{-1}(H(M) - x \cdot r) \pmod{p-1}$$
$$s = 15 \times (5 - 7 \times 10) \pmod{22}$$
$$s = 15 \times (5 - 70) \pmod{22}$$
$$s = 15 \times (-65) \pmod{22}$$
$$s = 15 \times (-65 + 3 \times 22) \pmod{22}$$
$$s = 15 \times (1) \pmod{22}$$
$$s = 15 \pmod{22}$$

Signature: $(r, s) = (10, 15)$.

**Step 6: Verification.**

Check $1 \leq r \leq 22$ ✓.

Compute $v_1 = g^{H(M)} \pmod p = 5^5 \pmod{23}$.
$5^2 \equiv 2$, $5^4 \equiv 4$, $5^5 = 5^4 \cdot 5 = 20 \pmod{23}$. So $v_1 = 20$.

Compute $v_2 = y^r \cdot r^s \pmod p = 17^{10} \cdot 10^{15} \pmod{23}$.

For $17^{10} \pmod{23}$: By Fermat's little theorem, $17^{22} \equiv 1$, but we compute directly.
$17^2 = 289 \equiv 289 - 12 \times 23 = 289 - 276 = 13 \pmod{23}$.
$17^4 \equiv 13^2 = 169 \equiv 169 - 7 \times 23 = 169 - 161 = 8 \pmod{23}$.
$17^8 \equiv 8^2 = 64 \equiv 64 - 2 \times 23 = 18 \pmod{23}$.
$17^{10} = 17^8 \cdot 17^2 \equiv 18 \times 13 = 234 \equiv 234 - 10 \times 23 = 234 - 230 = 4 \pmod{23}$.

For $10^{15} \pmod{23}$:
$10^2 = 100 \equiv 100 - 4 \times 23 = 8 \pmod{23}$.
$10^4 \equiv 8^2 = 64 \equiv 18 \pmod{23}$.
$10^8 \equiv 18^2 = 324 \equiv 324 - 14 \times 23 = 324 - 322 = 2 \pmod{23}$.
$10^{15} = 10^8 \cdot 10^4 \cdot 10^2 \cdot 10^1 \equiv 2 \times 18 \times 8 \times 10 = 2880 \pmod{23}$.
$2880 \div 23 = 125$ remainder $5$. So $10^{15} \equiv 5 \pmod{23}$.

Therefore:

$$v_2 = 4 \times 5 = 20 \pmod{23}$$

Since $v_1 = v_2 = 20$, the signature is **valid**. $\blacksquare$

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Security vs. Performance

The following table provides a normalized comparison across multiple engineering dimensions:

| **Dimension** | **RSA** | **DSA** | **ECDSA** | **ElGamal** | **Schnorr** | **Rabin** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Security assumption | IF | DLP | ECDLP | DLP | DLP | SQRT mod n |
| Quantum vulnerability | **Yes (Shor's)** | **Yes (Shor's)** | **Yes (Shor's)** | **Yes (Shor's)** | **Yes (Shor's)** | **Yes (Shor's)** |
| Signature malleability | No | Possible | Possible | Possible | No | **Yes** (forgery by $\pm S$) |
| Determinism | Deterministic | **Non-deterministic** (uses $k$) | **Non-deterministic** (uses $k$) | **Non-deterministic** | **Non-deterministic** | Deterministic |
| Replay attack resistance | Weak (needs timestamps) | Weak (needs timestamps) | Weak (needs timestamps) | Weak (needs timestamps) | Weak (needs timestamps) | Weak (needs timestamps) |
| Implementation complexity | Low | Moderate | **High** (curve math) | Moderate | Low | Low |
| Patent freedom | **Free** | **Free** | **Free** | **Free** | Expired 2008 | **Free** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note on Signature Malleability:** ElGamal, DSA, and ECDSA are all theoretically malleable — an attacker who sees a valid signature $(r, s)$ can produce a second valid signature $(r, -s \pmod n)$ for the same message without knowing the private key. This does not break security but causes issues in protocols like Bitcoin (which had to adopt SegWit to fix ECDSA malleability).

### 3.4 Key-Size Equivalence Table (NIST SP 800-57)

| **Security Level (bits)** | **RSA / DSA** | **ECC (ECDSA)** | **AES equivalent** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **80** (legacy) | 1024 | 160 | AES-128 |
| **112** | 2048 | 224 | AES-128 |
| **128** (standard) | 3072 | 256 | AES-128 |
| **192** | 7680 | 384 | AES-192 |
| **256** (high-grade) | 15360 | 512 | AES-256 |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Generic Digital Signature Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Sender Alice"] --> B["Message M"]
    B --> C["Hash Function SHA-256"]
    C --> D["Hash Digest H(M)"]
    D --> E["Sign Algorithm with Private Key Kpr"]
    F["Alice's Private Key Kpr"] --> E
    E --> G["Digital Signature S"]
    G --> H["Transmit M plus S over Insecure Channel"]
    H --> I["Receiver Bob"]
    I --> J["Hash Function SHA-256"]
    J --> K["Recomputed Hash H prime M"]
    B --> J
    G --> L["Verify Algorithm with Public Key Kpu"]
    K --> L
    M["Alice's Public Key Kpu"] --> L
    L --> N{"Compare H prime M equals H prime from signature"}
    N --> O["Signature Valid Accept Message"]
    N --> P["Signature Invalid Reject Message"]
```

### 4.2 Comparative Architecture — How Each Scheme Generates Signatures

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph RSA["RSA Scheme"]
        R1["Choose p, q"] --> R2["Compute n = pq"]
        R2 --> R3["Compute phi n = p-1 times q-1"]
        R3 --> R4["Choose e, compute d"]
        R4 --> R5["Signature S = M to the power d mod n"]
    end

    subgraph DSA["DSA Scheme"]
        D1["Choose prime p, prime q divides p-1"] --> D2["Choose generator g"]
        D2 --> D3["Private key x, Public key y = g to the x mod p"]
        D3 --> D4["Random k, r = g to the k mod p mod q"]
        D4 --> D5["s = k inverse times H M plus x times r mod q"]
    end

    subgraph ECDSA["ECDSA Scheme"]
        E1["Choose elliptic curve E, base point G"] --> E2["Private key d, Public key Q = dG"]
        E2 --> E3["Random k, compute point kG = x1, y1"]
        E3 --> E4["r = x1 mod n, s = k inverse times H M plus d times r mod n"]
    end

    subgraph ElGamal["ElGamal Scheme"]
        EL1["Prime p, generator g"] --> EL2["Private x, Public y = g to the x mod p"]
        EL2 --> EL3["Random k, r = g to the k mod p"]
        EL3 --> EL4["s = k inverse times H M minus x times r mod p-1"]
    end
```

### 4.3 Decision Flowchart — Choosing the Right Digital Signature Scheme

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["Need Digital Signature"] --> Q1{"Is the application resource constrained?"}
    Q1 -->|"Yes IoT Mobile"| ECDSA["Use ECDSA"]
    Q1 -->|"No Server Desktop"| Q2{"Is backward compatibility critical?"}
    Q2 -->|"Yes TLS Legacy"| RSA["Use RSA"]
    Q2 -->|"No Modern system"| Q3{"Is the application a US Federal system?"}
    Q3 -->|"Yes FIPS Compliance"| DSS["Use DSA or ECDSA per FIPS 186-4"]
    Q3 -->|"No General Use"| Q4{"Is short signature size critical?"}
    Q4 -->|"Yes Blockchain"| ECDSA2["Use ECDSA secp256k1"]
    Q4 -->|"No General"| Schnorr["Use Schnorr or ECDSA"]
```

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    AttackTypes --> A4["Selective Forgery"]
    AttackTypes --> A5["Total Break"]

    A1 --> RSA1["RSA Vulnerable without proper padding"]
    A1 --> ECDSA1["ECDSA Resistant with good randomness"]

    A2 --> RSA2["RSA Needs PSS padding"]

```
