## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*List any three differences between RSA and DSA digital signature schemes.*

**Model Answer:**

| **Parameter** | **RSA** | **DSA** |
|---|---|---|
| Underlying hard problem | Integer Factorization | Discrete Logarithm |
| Signature form | Single integer $S = M^d \bmod n$ | Pair $(r, s)$ where $r$ and $s$ are $\bmod q$ |
| Key generation speed | Slow (large primes $p, q$) | Fast (uses standard domain parameters) |
| Verification speed | Fast (small public exponent) | Slower (multiple modular exponentiations) |
| Patent | Expired (public domain) | Unpatented (NIST standard) |

Any three of the above points fetch full 3 marks. (1 mark per valid difference.)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**
*Why is ECDSA preferred over RSA in resource-constrained environments like IoT devices?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

ECDSA is preferred over RSA in IoT environments because:

1. **Smaller key size:** For equivalent 128-bit security, ECDSA needs only a 256-bit key, while RSA needs 3072 bits — a **12× reduction** in key storage and transmission overhead. *(1 mark)*

2. **Lower computational cost:** Elliptic curve scalar multiplication is far cheaper than RSA's modular exponentiation with large moduli, reducing CPU cycles, battery drain, and signing latency on microcontrollers. *(1 mark)*

3. **Compact signatures:** ECDSA signatures are ~512 bits vs. RSA's ~3072 bits, making them ideal for bandwidth-limited protocols like LoRaWAN, Zigbee, and BLE. *(1 mark)*

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

**Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Describe the RSA digital signature scheme in detail with the key generation, signing, and verification algorithms. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Compare RSA and ECDSA digital signature schemes on the basis of (i) key size for equivalent security, (ii) signature size, (iii) mathematical basis, (iv) performance, and (v) standardization. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution:

**(a) RSA Digital Signature Scheme (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Key Generation (2 marks):**
- Alice selects two large distinct primes $p$ and $q$. *(0.5 mark)*
- Computes modulus $n = p \cdot q$ and Euler totient $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$. *(0.5 mark)*
- Selects public exponent $e$ with $1 < e < \phi(n)$ and $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$. *(0.5 mark)*
- Computes private exponent $d \equiv e^{-1} \pmod{\phi(n)}$. *(0.5 mark)*
- Public key: $(e, n)$. Private key: $d$.

**Step 2 — Signing (2.5 marks):**
- Alice computes hash $h = H(M)$ of the message using a secure hash function (SHA-256). *(0.5 mark)*
- Applies padding scheme PKCS#1 v1.5 or PSS to $h$ producing $\text{PaddedHash}$. *(0.5 mark)*
- Computes signature $S = (\text{PaddedHash})^d \pmod n$. *(1 mark)*
- Sends $(M, S)$ to Bob. *(0.5 mark)*

**Step 3 — Verification (2.5 marks):**
- Bob obtains Alice's public key $(e, n)$. *(0.5 mark)*
- Computes $M' = S^e \pmod n$. *(0.5 mark)*
- Removes padding to recover hash $h'$. *(0.5 mark)*
- Independently hashes received message: $h = H(M)$. *(0.5 mark)*
- Accepts signature if $h = h'$; rejects otherwise. *(0.5 mark)*

**(b) Comparison of RSA and ECDSA (7 marks — 1.4 marks per parameter)**

| **Parameter** | **RSA** | **ECDSA** |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Key size (128-bit security) | 3072 bits | 256 bits |
| (ii) Signature size | 3072 bits | 512 bits |
| (iii) Mathematical basis | Integer factorization of $n = pq$ | ECDLP on elliptic curve group |
| (iv) Performance | Slow key gen, fast verify | Fast key gen, fast sign+verify |
| (v) Standardization | PKCS#1, RFC 8017 | FIPS 186-4, ANSI X9.62, SEC1 |

---

**Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Alternative]**

**(a)** Explain the ElGamal digital signature scheme with all three algorithms. Demonstrate signing the message $M = 5$ with $p = 23$, $g = 5$, $x = 7$, $k = 3$. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Compare ElGamal, DSA, and Schnorr signature schemes. Highlight the key innovation of Schnorr over DSA. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution:

**(a) ElGamal Digital Signature Scheme (7 marks)**

**Key Generation (1.5 marks):**
- Choose large prime $p$ and primitive root $g$. *(0.5)*
- Choose private key $x \in [1, p-2]$. *(0.5)*
- Compute public key $y = g^x \bmod p$. *(0.5)*

**Signing Algorithm (3 marks — including numerical example):**
- Choose per-message random $k$ with $\gcd(k, p-1) = 1$. *(0.5)*
- Compute $r = g^k \bmod p$. *(0.5)*
- Compute $s = k^{-1}(H(M) - x \cdot r) \bmod (p-1)$. *(0.5)*
- For $p = 23$, $g = 5$, $x = 7$, $k = 3$, $M = 5$, $H(M) = 5$:
  - $y = 5^7 \bmod 23 = 17$. *(0.25)*
  - $r = 5^3 \bmod 23 = 125 \bmod 23 = 10$. *(0.25)*
  - $k^{-1} \bmod 22 = 15$ (since $3 \cdot 15 = 45 = 2 \cdot 22 + 1$). *(0.25)*
  - $s = 15 \cdot (5 - 7 \cdot 10) \bmod 22 = 15 \cdot (-65) \bmod 22 = 15 \cdot 1 = 15$. *(0.5)*

**Verification (2.5 marks):**
- Check $0 < r < p$. *(0.5)*
- Compute $v_1 = g^{H(M)} \bmod p$. *(0.5)*
- Compute $v_2 = y^r \cdot r^s \bmod p$. *(1)*
- Accept if $v_1 \equiv v_2 \pmod p$. *(0.5)*

**(b) Comparison of ElGamal, DSA, Schnorr (7 marks)**

| **Aspect** | **ElGamal** | **DSA** | **Schnorr** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Hard problem | DLP in $\mathbb{Z}_p^*$ | DLP in subgroup of order $q$ | DLP in subgroup of order $q$ |
| Signature length | ~2048 bits | ~320–512 bits | ~320 bits |
| Modular operations in verify | Two exponentiations | Two exponentiations | One exponentiation |
| Verification speed | Slow | Moderate | **Fast** |
| Provable security | Not proven | Proven in random oracle | Proven in random oracle |
| Length extension | Vulnerable | Resistant (uses $H(M)$) | Resistant (uses $H(M \vert r)$) |

**Key Innovation of Schnorr over DSA (2 marks, included in 7):**

Schnorr's signature pairs a **hash output $e$** with a scalar $s$, allowing verification to use only **one** modular exponentiation $g^s y^e \bmod p$ (vs. DSA's two). Additionally, Schnorr's proof of security is **simpler and tighter** than DSA's, and the signature $e$ being the hash binds $r$ to the message — preventing the length-extension weaknesses that affect naive ElGamal variants.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Modulus vs. Order Confusion (2–3 mark loss):** Students often write ElGamal verification modulo $p$ when the exponent arithmetic $s = k^{-1}(H(M) - xr)$ must be modulo $(p-1)$. Mixing these is the #1 cause of partial-mark deductions.
>
> 2. **DSA's $r$ is computed twice:** Remember that $r = (g^k \bmod p) \bmod q$ — the outer $\bmod q$ is mandatory. Forgetting it is a 1-mark deduction.
>
> 3. **ECDSA requires $r \neq 0$:** If $r = 0$, the signer must choose a new $k$. Skipping this check is a 1-mark deduction.
>
> 4. **Hash function is mandatory** for DSA, ECDSA, ElGamal, and Schnorr — they are not signature schemes in the strict sense without a hash.
>
> 5. **Rabin signature malleability:** For every valid signature $S$, both $S$ and $-S \bmod n$ are valid. Examiners often test this with a 1-mark trick question.
>
> 6. **Confusing RSA encryption with RSA signing:** Encryption uses $C = M^e \bmod n$; signing uses $S = M^d \bmod n$. Reversing the exponents is a critical error.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Digital signatures provide three security services:** authenticity, integrity, and non-repudiation — this is the **distinguishing factor** from MACs (which lack non-repudiation).
- **RSA** is the most widely deployed scheme; uses **integer factorization** as the hard problem; signature is a single integer.
- **DSA** is the NIST/FIPS standard; uses **discrete logarithm** in a prime-order subgroup; produces 320–512 bit pair signatures; non-deterministic (uses random $k$).
- **ECDSA** offers **equivalent security with 12× smaller keys**; ideal for IoT, mobile, and blockchain; requires careful random $k$ generation (Sony PS3 hack of 2010 was due to static $k$).
- **ElGamal** is the academic ancestor of DSA; signature size is **double** that of DSA; malleable.
- **Schnorr** improves DSA with **one-exponentiation verification** and tighter security proof; basis for EdDSA (Ed25519) variants.
- **Rabin** is the only scheme whose security is provably as hard as integer factorization; signatures are **malleable** (both $\pm S$ valid).
- **Quantum vulnerability:** ALL classical signature schemes (RSA, DSA, ECDSA, ElGamal, Schnorr, Rabin) are broken by **Shor's algorithm**. Post-quantum alternatives include **CRYSTALS-Dilithium**, **FALCON**, and **SPHINCS+**.
- **Key-size equivalence at 128-bit security:** RSA-3072 ≡ DSA-3072 ≡ ECDSA-256.
- **Signature size at 128-bit security:** RSA-3072 bits > ECDSA-512 bits > Schnorr-320 bits.
- **Standardization bodies:** NIST (FIPS 186-4 for DSA/ECDSA), ANSI (X9.62 for ECDSA), IEEE (P1363 for Schnorr/ECDSA), ISO (9796 for Rabin).
- **Practical applications:** TLS certificates (RSA/ECDSA), Bitcoin (ECDSA-secp256k1), US passports (ECDSA), PGP email (RSA/DSA legacy).
- **The $k$ reuse attack:** Reusing the random $k$ in DSA/ECDSA immediately leaks the private key $x$ — a critical implementation rule examiners love to test.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT523 - DATA PRIVACY AND SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of **Data Privacy and Security**, a **Process** (more precisely, **Data Processing**) refers to *any operation or set of operations performed on personal data or sets of personal data*, whether or not by automated means. This encompasses the entire lifecycle of data manipulation within an organization's information systems.

According to the **General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR), Article 4(2)**, processing includes operations such as **collection, recording, organization, structuring, storage, adaptation or retrieval, consultation, use, disclosure by transmission, dissemination, or otherwise making available, alignment or combination, restriction, erasure, or destruction** of personal data.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Definition of Process**
> "Process" in PECDT523 Module 3 refers to the **operational handling of data** — from collection to destruction — governed by legal bases, controller/processor roles, and the principles of **Privacy by Design (PbD)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The Post Office**
>
> Think of a **Post Office** handling registered mail. The **postal customer** is the *Data Subject* (the person whose information is at stake). The **Postmaster General** is the *Data Controller* (decides *what* to do with the mail and *why*). The **clerk who sorts and delivers** is the *Data Processor* (executes operations on behalf of the Postmaster). Every time the clerk **stamps, sorts, records, forwards, or destroys** a letter, that is a **process**. The law (the **Privacy Act**) requires the Postmaster to keep a register of all processes, ensure lawful purpose, and protect the customer's data — exactly as a privacy-aware organization must do.

## 1.3 Why "Process" is Central to Data Privacy

The word "Process" is the **conceptual anchor** of privacy law because privacy is not just about *data at rest* — it is about what organizations **do** with that data. Every action that touches personal data is a process and must have:

1. A **Lawful Basis** (Consent, Contract, Legal Obligation, Vital Interest, Public Task, Legitimate Interest)
2. A **Documented Purpose**
3. A **Defined Role** (Controller, Processor, Joint-Controller, Sub-Processor)
4. **Engineered Safeguards** (Technical, Organizational, Legal)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard KTU Metric to Remember**
> Under GDPR and the **Digital Personal Data Protection Act (DPDPA), 2023 (India)**, *every single processing activity* must be recorded in a **Record of Processing Activities (RoPA)**. Failing to do so is one of the most cited compliance gaps in privacy audits worldwide.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Building Blocks of a Privacy Process

Every data process, regardless of industry, can be decomposed into **six operational blocks**:

1. **Trigger** — What event initiates the process? (User signup, transaction, sensor reading)
2. **Actor** — Who is performing the operation? (Controller, Processor, Employee, Third-Party)
3. **Operation** — What is being done? (Collect, Store, Share, Analyze, Delete)
4. **Data Asset** — What categories of data are involved? (Identifiers, Sensitive, Behavioral)
5. **Purpose Limitation** — Why is this being done? (Service delivery, Marketing, Compliance)
6. **Safeguard** — What protects the data? (Encryption, Access Control, Anonymization)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these six?**
> The **OECD Privacy Principles (1980)**, the **GDPR (2018)**, the **APEC Privacy Framework**, and the **DPDPA (2023)** all converge on these six dimensions. A well-engineered privacy process is one where all six are explicit, documented, and auditable.

## 2.2 The Three Legal Roles in a Process

| Role | Definition | Key Obligation | KTU Keyword |
|------|------------|----------------|-------------|
| **Data Controller** | Natural or legal person that determines the *purposes* and *means* of processing | Demonstrates accountability, defines lawful basis, oversees all downstream processors | *"Decides why and how"* |
| **Data Processor** | Processes data *on behalf of* the controller under a contract | Implements controller's instructions, maintains security, assists with DSRs | *"Executes the work"* |
| **Data Subject** | Identified or identifiable natural person to whom personal data relates | Exercises rights (access, erasure, portability, objection) | *"Whose data it is"* |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates the **key analytical expressions** used in Module 3. These appear routinely in numerical and descriptive KTU questions.

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Description | Typical Use in KTU |
|---------|---------------------|-------------|---------------------|
| **Lawful Basis Decision** | $L_{base} = \bigcup_{i=1}^{6} \{l_i\}$ | Set of six lawful bases, exactly one must apply per process | Justification question (7 marks) |
| **Processing Necessity Test** | $N_{test} = \frac{P_{clarity}}{R_{risk}}$ | Clarity of purpose divided by risk of the data category | PbD justification |
| **k-Anonymity Compliance** | $k \ge k_{min}$ where $k = \frac{n_{records}}{n_{unique\_quasi\_id}}$ | Every record must be indistinguishable from at least $k-1$ others | Anonymization design |
| **Information Loss (IL)** | $IL = 1 - \frac{H_{anon}}{H_{orig}}$ | Fraction of entropy lost due to anonymization | Privacy-Utility trade-off |
| **Shannon Entropy** | $H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p(x_i) \log_{2} p(x_i)$ | Information content of a dataset in bits | Risk quantification |
| **Privacy Risk Score** | $R_{priv} = P_{breach} \times I_{impact} \times L_{likelihood}$ | Composite score; thresholds decide control levels | PIA / DPIA |
| **Re-identification Risk** | $R_{reid} = 1 - \prod_{j=1}^{m} (1 - p_j)$ | Probability that at least one quasi-identifier matches | De-anonymization analysis |
| **Storage Minimization** | $t_{retain} \le t_{purpose}$ | Retention time bounded by purpose fulfilment | Retention policy |
| **Data Subject Rights Coverage** | $C_{DSR} = \frac{\vert \text{Rights Implemented} \vert}{\vert \text{Rights Required} \vert}$ | Ratio from 0 to 1; KTU expects $\ge 0.95$ | Compliance audit |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility**
> The formulas above are not just academic. The **Shannon Entropy** equation is used by tools like *ARX* and *sdcMicro* to measure dataset utility. The **Privacy Risk Score** is the foundation of any **DPIA (Data Protection Impact Assessment)** as mandated under GDPR Article 35 and DPDPA Section 33.

## 2.4 The Eight Data Subject Rights Anchored in Processing

Under GDPR Articles 12–22, every process must be engineered to support these rights:

1. **Right to be Informed**
2. **Right of Access**
3. **Right to Rectification**
4. **Right to Erasure** (Right to be Forgotten)
5. **Right to Restrict Processing**
6. **Right to Data Portability**
7. **Right to Object**
8. **Right not to be subject to Automated Decision-Making**

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake**
> Students often list *confidentiality, integrity, availability* (the CIA triad of security) instead of the *eight data subject rights*. These are **different domains**. The CIA triad belongs to **information security**; the eight rights belong to **data privacy process engineering**. KTU examiners mark them as distinct.

## 2.5 Privacy by Design (PbD) — The 7 Foundational Principles (Cavoukian / GDPR Recital 78)

1. **Proactive not Reactive; Preventative not Remedial**
2. **Privacy as the Default Setting**
3. **Privacy Embedded into Design**
4. **Full Functionality — Positive-Sum, not Zero-Sum**
5. **End-to-End Security — Full Lifecycle Protection**
6. **Visibility and Transparency**
7. **Respect for User Privacy**

Every **process** must be evaluated against all seven principles during the design phase.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Lawful Basis Selection Algorithm

When a controller initiates a new process, the **lawful basis selection** follows a deterministic decision flow. Below is the exhaustive step-by-step logic that KTU expects in 7-mark "explain" answers.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked "How does an organization select the lawful basis for a process?", walk the examiner through the following six steps. Marks are awarded per step, not per final answer.

**Step 1 — Purpose Identification**
The controller first articulates the *exact* purpose of the proposed process. Under GDPR Article 5(1)(b), purposes must be **specific, explicit, and legitimate**.

$$P = \{p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_n\}$$

Where $P$ is the set of purposes and each $p_i$ is documented in the privacy notice.

**Step 2 — Data Classification**
Each data element is classified into one of three tiers:

$$D = \{d_{regular}, d_{sensitive}, d_{criminal}\}$$

- $d_{regular}$: name, email, phone
- $d_{sensitive}$: health, biometrics, religion, sexual orientation
- $d_{criminal}$: criminal records (requires additional safeguards under Article 10)

**Step 3 — Necessity Test**
The controller must demonstrate the data is *adequate*, *relevant*, and *limited to what is necessary* (Data Minimization, Article 5(1)(c)):

$$D_{used} \subseteq D_{required}, \quad \text{where } \vert D_{used} \vert \le \vert D_{required} \vert$$

If $\vert D_{used} \vert > \vert D_{required} \vert$, the process fails the necessity test.

**Step 4 — Lawful Basis Mapping**
The controller maps the purpose to one (and only one, except for special cases) of the six lawful bases in GDPR Article 6:

$$f: P \to L_{base} = \{Consent, Contract, LegalObligation, VitalInterest, PublicTask, LegitimateInterest\}$$

The mapping is governed by the **purpose-binding rule**:

$$L_{base} = \arg\max_{l \in L_{base}} \; \text{Fit}(p, l)$$

**Step 5 — Legitimate Interest Assessment (LIA)**
If Legitimate Interest is selected, the controller must perform a three-part LIA test:

$$\text{LIA}_{score} = \text{Purpose} + \text{Necessity} + \text{Balancing}$$

Each dimension is scored 0–5. Total $\ge 9$ with no dimension $<2$ is required for the process to pass.

**Step 6 — Documentation and Notification**
The chosen basis, the LIA result, and the data classification are recorded in the **RoPA** and disclosed in the privacy notice:

$$\text{RoPA}_{entry} = \langle P, D, L_{base}, \text{LIA}_{score}, t_{retain}, \text{Safeguards} \rangle$$

If any of the six steps fails, the process must be redesigned or abandoned.

## 3.2 Derivation: k-Anonymity Guarantee for a Process Output

Suppose a process outputs a dataset $T$ with quasi-identifiers $Q = \{q_1, q_2, \ldots, q_m\}$. The dataset is **k-anonymous** if every unique combination of $Q$ values appears at least $k$ times.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **k-Anonymity Threshold:** KTU often uses $k \ge 5$ as a baseline classroom example. Industry (HIPAA Safe Harbor) generally requires $k \ge 5$ as well.

The compliance condition is expressed as:

$$k = \min_{(q_1,\ldots,q_m) \in \pi_Q(T)} \; \text{count}(q_1,\ldots,q_m) \;\ge\; k_{min}$$

Where $\pi_Q(T)$ is the projection of $T$ onto the quasi-identifier columns.

**Generalization and Suppression Cost** — the *information loss* incurred by anonymization is:

$$IL = 1 - \frac{H_{anon}}{H_{orig}} = 1 - \frac{-\sum_{i} p_i^{anon} \log_2 p_i^{anon}}{-\sum_{i} p_i^{orig} \log_2 p_i^{orig}}$$

A **lower $IL$** means higher data utility but potentially higher re-identification risk; KTU expects students to discuss this trade-off explicitly.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Process Design Checklist

| # | Design Question | Governing Principle | KTU Reference |
|---|-----------------|---------------------|---------------|
| 1 | Is the purpose documented and lawful? | Purpose Limitation | GDPR Art. 5(1)(b), DPDPA §4 |
| 2 | Is the data minimized to what is necessary? | Data Minimization | GDPR Art. 5(1)(c) |
| 3 | Is there a valid lawful basis? | Lawfulness | GDPR Art. 6 |
| 4 | Is accuracy maintained with update mechanisms? | Accuracy | GDPR Art. 5(1)(d) |
| 5 | Is retention time bounded and enforced? | Storage Limitation | GDPR Art. 5(1)(e) |
| 6 | Are integrity and confidentiality ensured? | Integrity & Confidentiality | GDPR Art. 5(1)(f) |
| 7 | Is accountability demonstrable? | Accountability | GDPR Art. 5(2) |
| 8 | Is a DPIA required for high-risk processes? | Risk-based approach | GDPR Art. 35, DPDPA §33 |
| 9 | Are data subject rights supported technically? | Rights Engineering | GDPR Art. 12–22 |
| 10 | Are international transfer rules respected? | Cross-border governance | GDPR Chapter V |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram A — The Data Processing Lifecycle (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

The diagram below maps the **end-to-end functional flow** of a data processing activity, from initial trigger to final destruction, including the role interactions at each stage.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Stage 1 Trigger Event] --> B[Stage 2 Data Collection with Lawful Basis]
    B --> C[Stage 3 Storage with Encryption and Access Control]
    C --> D[Stage 4 Processing Analysis and Transformation]
    D --> E[Stage 5 Sharing Internal and External with Contracts]
    E --> F[Stage 6 Retention Monitoring with Auto Deletion Timer]
    F --> G[Stage 7 Destruction Cryptographic Erasure or Physical Shred]
    G --> H[Stage 8 Audit Logging for Accountability]
    H --> I[End of Process Archive and Compliance Report]
    A --> J[Data Subject Rights Channel Parallel]
    J --> D
    J --> F
```

**Interpretation:**
- The **horizontal axis** represents the chronological lifecycle of a process.
- The **vertical branch (J)** represents the parallel *Data Subject Rights* channel that can intercept the main flow (e.g., a right-to-erasure request can force an early transition from Stage 6 to Stage 7).
- The **H → I terminal** captures the **accountability loop** required by GDPR Article 5(2).

## 4.2 Diagram B — Controller, Processor, and Sub-Processor Relationship

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Data Subject] -->|Provides Data and Exercises Rights| B[Data Controller]
    B -->|Instruction and Contract DPA| C[Data Processor]
    C -->|Sub Contract and Audit| D[Sub Processor]
    B --> E[Joint Controller with Shared Purpose]
    E --> C
    B --> F[Supervisory Authority for Compliance Reporting]
    F -.->|Oversight and Penalties| B
```

**Interpretation:**
- The **Controller** is the only entity that decides the purpose and means.
- The **Processor** acts strictly on the controller's documented instructions.
- A **Sub-Processor** is engaged by the processor *only with prior written authorization* of the controller.
- **Joint Controllers** share the purpose determination, but must transparently allocate responsibilities.
- The **Supervisory Authority** (e.g., the *Data Protection Board of India* under DPDPA) oversees the entire ecosystem.

## 4.3 Diagram C — Privacy by Design (PbD) Layered Model Applied to a Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L0[Process Initiation] --> L1[Layer 1 Proactive Risk Assessment]
    L1 --> L2[Layer 2 Privacy Defaults Configured]
    L2 --> L3[Layer 3 Embedded Controls in Architecture]
    L3 --> L4[Layer 4 Full Functionality Utility Preservation]
    L4 --> L5[Layer 5 End to End Lifecycle Security]
    L5 --> L6[Layer 6 Transparent Audit and Logging]
    L6 --> L7[Layer 7 User Centric Rights Interface]
    L7 --> L8[Process Operational and Continuously Monitored]
```

**Interpretation:**
- Each **layer** maps to one of the **seven PbD principles** (Cavoukian, 2011).
- KTU questions often ask: *"Which PbD principle does encryption in transit satisfy?"* — Answer: **Layer 5 — End-to-End Security**.
- A process that skips **Layer 2 (Defaults)** is non-compliant with *Privacy as the Default*.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define "Data Processing" as per GDPR. List any four operations that constitute processing.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** According to **GDPR Article 4(2)**, *processing* means *any operation or set of operations performed on personal data, whether or not by automated means*, such as collection, recording, organization, structuring, storage, adaptation, retrieval, consultation, use, disclosure, dissemination, alignment, combination, restriction, erasure, or destruction.
- **Four Operations (1.5 marks — 0.375 each):**
  1. **Collection** of personal data during user signup.
  2. **Storage** of the data in an encrypted database.
  3. **Sharing** with a third-party payment processor under a DPA.
  4. **Erasure** upon receipt of a right-to-be-forgotten request.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**Differentiate between a Data Controller and a Data Processor with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Data Controller | Data Processor |
|--------|----------------|----------------|
| Role | Determines *purposes* and *means* of processing | Processes data *on behalf of* the controller |
| Decision Power | Full | None — follows documented instructions |
| Example | A hospital deciding to store patient records for treatment | A cloud vendor (AWS, Azure) hosting the hospital's database |
| Liability | Primary | Secondary (for following unlawful instructions) |

The hospital is the **controller** because it decides *why* patient data is stored and *how long*; the cloud vendor is the **processor** because it merely provides infrastructure on the hospital's instructions under a Data Processing Agreement (**DPA**). **(3 marks — 1.5 for distinction, 1.5 for example)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the **seven principles of Privacy by Design (PbD)** as articulated by Ann Cavoukian. How do they apply to the design of a customer onboarding process for a mobile banking app? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **six lawful bases for processing** personal data under GDPR Article 6. For each basis, give one real-world use case. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks:**

The seven principles of **Privacy by Design (PbD)**, formulated by Dr. Ann Cavoukian (Information and Privacy Commissioner of Ontario, 2011), are:

1. **Proactive not Reactive; Preventative not Remedial** (1 mark) — Privacy risks are anticipated *before* they materialize, not patched after a breach. In the banking app, this means performing a **DPIA during the design phase**, not after launch.

2. **Privacy as the Default Setting** (1 mark) — The strongest privacy settings must be active without user intervention. Concretely, the app should default to **opt-in for data sharing**, **biometric login disabled by default for low-risk actions**, and **marketing consent set to OFF**.

3. **Privacy Embedded into Design** (1 mark) — Privacy is treated as a *core functional requirement*, not an add-on. The database schema should include **column-level encryption for PII** and **pseudonymization of customer IDs**.

4. **Full Functionality — Positive-Sum, not Zero-Sum** (1 mark) — Privacy and usability are not traded off. The app can offer **frictionless biometric login** *and* **on-device processing of sensitive data** simultaneously.

5. **End-to-End Security — Full Lifecycle Protection** (1 mark) — Data is secured from collection through deletion. Implement **TLS 1.3 in transit**, **AES-256 at rest**, and **cryptographic erasure** upon account closure.

6. **Visibility and Transparency** (1 mark) — All processes are auditable. The app provides a **"Privacy Dashboard"** showing what data is collected, why, and with whom it is shared.

7. **Respect for User Privacy** (1 mark) — The user is empowered. The dashboard includes one-tap controls for **access, rectification, erasure, portability, and restriction**.

**Synthesis (bonus 0 marks; ensure all 7 are mapped to the banking context for full credit).**

---

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks:**

The **six lawful bases for processing** under **GDPR Article 6(1)** are:

| # | Lawful Basis | When It Applies | Real-World Use Case |
|---|--------------|-----------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Consent** (1 mark) | The data subject has given *freely given, specific, informed, and unambiguous* consent via a clear affirmative act. | A newsletter subscription checkbox on an e-commerce site. |
| 2 | **Contract** (1 mark) | Processing is necessary to perform a contract to which the data subject is a party. | Processing shipping address to deliver an Amazon order. |
| 3 | **Legal Obligation** (1 mark) | Processing is required to comply with a legal obligation. | A bank reporting suspicious transactions to the Financial Intelligence Unit (FIU-IND). |
| 4 | **Vital Interests** (1 mark) | Processing is necessary to protect someone's life. | A hospital sharing a patient's emergency medical record with a paramedic. |
| 5 | **Public Task** (1 mark) | Processing is necessary for a task carried out in the public interest. | A municipal corporation maintaining a property tax database. |
| 6 | **Legitimate Interests** (1 mark) | Processing is necessary for the controller's legitimate interests, balanced against the data subject's rights. Requires a documented **LIA**. | A fraud-detection system analyzing transaction patterns in a payments app. |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Organizations must identify and document *at least one* lawful basis *before* initiating any process. The basis cannot be retroactively applied. Special categories of data (Article 9) require an *additional* condition, typically **explicit consent**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** What is a **Record of Processing Activities (RoPA)**? Explain the **mandatory fields** in a RoPA entry as required by GDPR Article 30. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA)** process. When is a DPIA mandatory? Outline the four-step methodology. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks:**

A **Record of Processing Activities (RoPA)** is a comprehensive, documented register maintained by an organization that records *every* data processing activity it undertakes (1 mark). Under **GDPR Article 30**, RoPA is mandatory for organizations with **250 or more employees**, *or* for any organization engaged in **high-risk processing**, regular processing of special categories, or cross-border data flows (1 mark).

**Mandatory Fields in a RoPA Entry (5 marks — 1 each):**

1. **Name and Contact Details** of the controller, the controller's representative, and the joint/controller's Data Protection Officer (DPO).
2. **Purposes of Processing** — the specific, explicit, and legitimate reason for which data is processed.
3. **Description of Categories of Data Subjects and Personal Data** — e.g., *customers* and *email, phone, payment info*.
4. **Categories of Recipients** — internal departments and third parties to whom data is disclosed.
5. **Transfers to Third Countries** — destination, mechanism (SCCs, BCRs, adequacy decision).
6. **Time Limits for Erasure (Retention Schedule)** — the legally defined storage period.
7. **Description of Technical and Organizational Security Measures (TOMs)** — encryption, pseudonymization, access control, logging.
8. **Legal Basis** mapped to the activity.

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU board generally expects **at least 6–8 fields** to be named and briefly described for full credit.

---

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks:**

A **Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA)** is a systematic process designed to evaluate the **impact of a proposed data processing activity on the privacy rights of data subjects** and to identify mitigation measures *before* the processing begins (1 mark).

**When is a DPIA Mandatory?** (2 marks)
- Under **GDPR Article 35(1)**, a DPIA is mandatory when processing is **likely to result in a high risk** to the rights and freedoms of natural persons.
- **Specific triggers (Article 35(3)):**
  1. Systematic and extensive evaluation of personal aspects (profiling, scoring).
  2. Large-scale processing of special categories of data (Art. 9) or criminal data (Art. 10).
  3. Systematic monitoring of a publicly accessible area on a large scale (e.g., CCTV with AI analytics).

**Four-Step DPIA Methodology** (4 marks — 1 each):

1. **Step 1 — Describe the Processing:** Define the nature, scope, context, and purposes; identify the data, recipients, retention, and processors involved.
2. **Step 2 — Assess Necessity and Proportionality:** Verify the lawful basis, evaluate data minimization, purpose limitation, and whether less intrusive alternatives exist.
3. **Step 3 — Identify and Assess Risks:** Catalog risks to data subjects (unauthorized access, re-identification, function creep), score them by likelihood and severity.
4. **Step 4 — Identify Mitigations and Residual Risk:** Apply safeguards (encryption, pseudonymization, access control); calculate residual risk; if residual risk remains *high*, consult the supervisory authority (Article 36 — *Prior Consultation*).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> - Conflating **RoPA (Article 30)** with **DPIA (Article 35)**. RoPA is a *register of all activities*; DPIA is a *risk assessment of one specific high-risk activity*. Examiners **deduct 2–3 marks** for mixing these up.
> - Listing *confidentiality, integrity, availability* under lawful bases. These are *security* principles, not *lawful bases*.
> - Saying "consent is always required for processing". This is **wrong** — consent is only *one* of six bases and cannot be used when another basis is more appropriate (e.g., legal obligation).
> - Omitting the **Article 35(3)** triggers when asked "When is a DPIA mandatory?" A vague answer loses 2 marks.
> - Failing to mention **Privacy by Default (PbD Principle 2)** when discussing process design — this is a **3-mark penalty** in many model answers.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3: Process**

- ✅ **Definition:** Process = *any operation on personal data* — collection, storage, use, sharing, deletion.
- ✅ **Six Lawful Bases (GDPR Art. 6):** Consent, Contract, Legal Obligation, Vital Interest, Public Task, Legitimate Interest.
- ✅ **Three Roles:** Data Subject, Data Controller, Data Processor (+ Sub-Processor, Joint Controller).
- ✅ **Seven PbD Principles:** Proactive, Default, Embedded, Full Functionality, End-to-End Security, Transparency, User Respect.
- ✅ **Eight Data Subject Rights:** Information, Access, Rectification, Erasure, Restriction, Portability, Object, No Automated Decisions.
- ✅ **RoPA (Art. 30):** Mandatory register — purposes, categories, recipients, retention, security measures, legal basis, cross-border transfers.
- ✅ **DPIA (Art. 35):** Mandatory for high-risk processing — 4 steps (Describe, Necessity, Risk, Mitigate).
- ✅ **DPDPA 2023 (India):** Aligns closely with GDPR; introduces the **Data Protection Board of India** as the supervisory authority.
- ✅ **Key Formulas:** $k$-anonymity inequality, Shannon Entropy $H(X) = -\sum p_i \log_2 p_i$, Information Loss $IL = 1 - H_{anon}/H_{orig}$.
- ✅ **Trade-off:** Anonymization vs. Utility — lower $IL$ means higher utility but higher re-identification risk.
- ✅ **Pitfall to Avoid:** *CIA triad ≠ Data Subject Rights*; these belong to security and privacy respectively and are marked separately.
- ✅ **Mnemonic for PbD:** *"P**D**E**F** **E**TS **U**"* → **P**roactive, **D**efault, **E**mbedded, **F**ull Functionality, **E**nd-to-End, **T**ransparency, **U**ser Respect.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Services
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT523 - DATA PRIVACY AND SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Services

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Security Services

In the context of **Data Privacy and Security**, a **Security Service** is a processing or communication service that enhances the security of the data processing systems and the information transfers within an organization or between organizations. The term is formally standardized by the **ITU-T Recommendation X.800 (Security Architecture for Open Systems Interconnection)** and **ISO 7498-2**, which collectively define the foundational taxonomy of security services used globally in network and information security engineering.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A security service is a capability that supports the prevention, detection, or recovery from a security attack. Security services implement security policies and are realized through security mechanisms such as encryption, digital signatures, and access controls.

The **International Telecommunication Union (ITU-T)** in **X.800** classifies security services into **five primary categories** and **one specific service category**, while the more modern interpretation extends to **seven core services** used in cloud, web, and data-centric environments.

## 1.2 The Seven Canonical Security Services

| # | Service | One-Line Meaning |
|---|---------|------------------|
| 1 | **Confidentiality** | Keep data secret from unauthorized parties |
| 2 | **Integrity** | Ensure data is not tampered with |
| 3 | **Authentication** | Prove the identity of a peer |
| 4 | **Authorization (Access Control)** | Enforce who can do what |
| 5 | **Non-Repudiation** | Prevent denial of actions |
| 6 | **Availability** | Ensure systems are accessible when needed |
| 7 | **Privacy** | Control collection, use, and disclosure of personal data |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Bank Vault System**
>
> Imagine a high-security bank vault with the following protections:
> - The vault door is locked with a key only the manager has → **Confidentiality** (only authorized can see the contents).
> - The vault has a tamper-proof seal that breaks if touched → **Integrity** (any modification is detectable).
> - The bank verifies the customer's signature and ID before entry → **Authentication** (proving identity).
> - Customers can only access safe deposit boxes assigned to them → **Authorization** (role-based access).
> - Each transaction is logged with a timestamped signature → **Non-Repudiation** (cannot deny the action).
> - The vault has backup power and fire suppression → **Availability** (operational even under stress).
> - The customer's personal details are never shared with third parties → **Privacy** (regulated data handling).
>
> **Just like a bank uses multiple overlapping protections, a secure system layers several services to defend against diverse threats.**

## 1.4 Distinction Between Service, Mechanism, and Attack

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Pillars of Security Vocabulary (X.800)**
>
> - **Security Attack**: Any action that compromises the security of information owned by an organization (e.g., eavesdropping, masquerading).
> - **Security Mechanism**: A process (or device) designed to detect, prevent, or recover from an attack (e.g., encryption, digital signature, access matrix).
> - **Security Service**: A service that enhances the security of data processing systems and information transfers, *using one or more mechanisms* to counter attacks.

The relationship can be summarized as:

$$\text{Service} = f(\text{Mechanism}, \text{Policy}) \quad \text{where it counters an } \text{Attack}$$

## 1.5 Why Services Matter in Data Privacy

> [!NOTE]
> **Modern Significance:** In the era of GDPR, DPDP Act 2023 (India), and CCPA, the line between "security" and "privacy" is drawn by **services**:
> - **Security services** protect data from external and internal threats.
> - **Privacy services** govern how, why, and with whom data is shared — often by enforcing purpose limitation, consent management, and data minimization.
>
> KTU emphasizes that **privacy cannot exist without security**, but security alone is not privacy.

---

## 1.6 Threats Addressed by Security Services

> [!CAUTION]
> **Common Threats and the Service that Defeats Them**
>
> | Threat | Service that Mitigates |
> |--------|------------------------|
> | Eavesdropping / Snooping | Confidentiality |
> | Tampering / Modification | Integrity |
> | Masquerading / Spoofing | Authentication |
> | Unauthorized Access | Authorization |
> | Repudiation of Origin/Receipt | Non-Repudiation |
> | Denial of Service (DoS) | Availability |
> | Data Exfiltration / Profiling | Privacy |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Layered Theoretical Model

The **X.800 Security Architecture** organizes services into a layered model aligned with the **OSI Reference Model**. The services are not tied to a single layer — a service can be provided at one or more OSI layers.

> [!NOTE]
> **OSI Layers and their typical security services:**
>
> - **Application Layer (L7):** Privacy, Authentication, Non-Repudiation, Access Control
> - **Presentation Layer (L6):** Confidentiality (via encryption)
> - **Session Layer (L5):** Authentication, Session integrity
> - **Transport Layer (L4):** Confidentiality, Integrity (TLS)
> - **Network Layer (L3):** Authentication, Confidentiality (IPSec)
> - **Data Link Layer (L2):** Confidentiality, Integrity (MAC, WPA2)
> - **Physical Layer (L1):** Confidentiality (via shielded cabling)

## 2.2 Detailed Service Definitions

### 2.2.1 Confidentiality

**Definition:** The property that information is not made available or disclosed to unauthorized individuals, entities, or processes.

**Sub-categories:**
- **Data confidentiality:** Protects the content of data at rest and in transit.
- **Privacy:** Protects personally identifiable information (PII) from being linked to an individual.
- **Traffic-flow confidentiality:** Hides the source, destination, frequency, and length of communications.

**Mechanisms that provide it:** Encryption (symmetric and asymmetric), steganography, access control lists, physical security.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Memory Hook:** *Confidentiality = "Only the right eyes see the message."*

---

### 2.2.2 Integrity

**Definition:** The property whereby data has not been altered or destroyed in an unauthorized manner.

**Sub-categories:**
- **Data integrity:** Content of data is unchanged.
- **Origin integrity:** The source of data is verified (the data originated from where it claims).
- **System integrity:** The system performs its intended function without unauthorized manipulation.

**Mechanisms that provide it:** Hash functions (SHA-256, SHA-3), Message Authentication Codes (MAC), Digital Signatures, checksums.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Memory Hook:** *Integrity = "The message arrives exactly as sent, bit for bit."*

---

### 2.2.3 Authentication

**Definition:** The process of verifying the identity of a user, process, or device.

**Sub-categories:**
- **Peer-entity authentication:** Confirms the identity of a peer in a real-time connection.
- **Data-origin authentication:** Confirms that the source of a received message is as claimed (no replay protection).

**Mechanisms that provide it:** Passwords, biometrics, smart cards, certificates, challenge-response protocols, Kerberos, OAuth tokens.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Memory Hook:** *Authentication = "Are you really who you say you are?"*

---

### 2.2.4 Authorization / Access Control

**Definition:** The prevention of unauthorized use of a resource, including the prevention of use of a resource in an unauthorized manner.

**Models:**
- **DAC (Discretionary Access Control):** Owner decides access.
- **MAC (Mandatory Access Control):** System policy enforces access via labels.
- **RBAC (Role-Based Access Control):** Access is based on roles.
- **ABAC (Attribute-Based Access Control):** Access is based on attributes of user, resource, and environment.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Memory Hook:** *Authorization = "You are who you say, but are you *allowed* to do this?"*

---

### 2.2.5 Non-Repudiation

**Definition:** Provides protection against false denial by one of the parties involved in a communication of having participated in all or part of the communication.

**Sub-categories:**
- **Non-repudiation of origin:** Protects against the sender's false denial of sending.
- **Non-repudiation of receipt:** Protects against the receiver's false denial of receiving.
- **Non-repudiation of submission:** Protects against denial of submitting a message to a delivery system.
- **Non-repudiation of delivery:** Protects against denial of delivery by the delivery system.

**Mechanisms that provide it:** Digital signatures, timestamps, trusted third-party notarization, audit logs.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Memory Hook:** *Non-Repudiation = "You cannot take back what you digitally signed."*

---

### 2.2.6 Availability

**Definition:** The property of being accessible and usable on demand by an authorized entity.

**Mechanisms that provide it:** Redundancy, fault tolerance, backups, RAID, UPS, DDoS mitigation, disaster recovery plans.

**Threats that reduce availability:** DoS, DDoS, ransomware, hardware failure, natural disasters.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Memory Hook:** *Availability = "The system is up when you need it, no matter what."*

---

### 2.2.7 Privacy (Data Privacy Service)

**Definition:** The right of individuals to control how their personal data is collected, processed, stored, shared, and deleted.

**Principles (OECD, GDPR-aligned):**
- **Lawfulness, fairness, transparency**
- **Purpose limitation**
- **Data minimization**
- **Accuracy**
- **Storage limitation**
- **Integrity and confidentiality**
- **Accountability**

**Mechanisms that provide it:** Consent management platforms, data anonymization, pseudonymization, differential privacy, privacy by design, DPIA (Data Protection Impact Assessment).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Memory Hook:** *Privacy = "It's not just about hiding the data; it's about controlling its life cycle."*

---

## 2.3 Mapping Services to OSI Layers — KTU Formula Sheet

| OSI Layer | Services Typically Provided | Typical Mechanisms |
|-----------|----------------------------|---------------------|
| Application (7) | Authentication, Authorization, Non-Repudiation, Privacy | Kerberos, OAuth, SAML, PGP, S/MIME |
| Presentation (6) | Confidentiality, Integrity | TLS record protocol |
| Session (5) | Authentication, Session Integrity | Session tokens |
| Transport (4) | Confidentiality, Integrity | TLS, DTLS, SSH |
| Network (3) | Authentication, Confidentiality, Integrity | IPSec (AH, ESP) |
| Data Link (2) | Confidentiality, Integrity | WPA2/3, MAC, LTE encryption |
| Physical (1) | Confidentiality | Shielded cables, Faraday cages |

## 2.4 Information-Theoretic Foundation

Security services, particularly confidentiality and integrity, can be modeled using **Claude Shannon's information theory**.

### 2.4.1 Shannon's Definition of Perfect Secrecy

A cryptosystem achieves **perfect secrecy** if for every plaintext $M$ and ciphertext $C$:

$$P(M \mid C) = P(M)$$

That is, observing the ciphertext provides **zero information** about the plaintext.

### 2.4.2 Entropy and Uncertainty

The **uncertainty** (entropy) of a message source is measured in bits:

$$H(M) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p(m_i) \log_2 p(m_i)$$

Where $p(m_i)$ is the probability of message $m_i$.

### 2.4.3 Key Entropy

For a uniformly random $k$-bit key:

$$H(K) = k \text{ bits}$$

### 2.4.4 Authentication Information

The **authentication information** required to verify a message is the minimum number of bits that an impostor must guess correctly:

$$I_{\text{auth}} = \log_2 \frac{1}{P(\text{success of forgery})}$$

### 2.4.5 Availability as a Reliability Metric

Availability is often quantified as the ratio of **uptime** to **total time**, typically expressed as a percentage:

$$A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} \times 100\%$$

Where:
- **MTBF** = Mean Time Between Failures
- **MTTR** = Mean Time To Repair

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Quick Reference Table — Key Equations**

| Symbol / Term | Meaning | Unit |
|---------------|---------|------|
| $H(M)$ | Entropy of message | bits |
| $H(K)$ | Entropy of key | bits |
| $A$ | Availability | % (often written as "nines") |
| MTBF | Mean Time Between Failures | hours |
| MTTR | Mean Time To Repair | hours |
| $P(M \vert C)$ | Posterior probability of $M$ given $C$ | dimensionless |
| $I_{\text{auth}}$ | Authentication information | bits |

---

## 2.5 Service-to-Threat Matrix (Defensive Engineering View)

| Service | Primary Threat | Defensive Mechanism |
|---------|----------------|---------------------|
| Confidentiality | Eavesdropping, Traffic analysis | Encryption, Padding |
| Integrity | Tampering, Replay, Substitution | Hash, MAC, Digital Signature |
| Authentication | Masquerading, Replay | Password, Biometric, Challenge-Response |
| Authorization | Privilege escalation | RBAC, ABAC, ACL |
| Non-Repudiation | False denial | Digital Signature, Timestamping |
| Availability | DoS, DDoS, Ransomware | Redundancy, Backup, Rate limiting |
| Privacy | Surveillance, Profiling, Data leakage | Anonymization, Consent, DPIA |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Shannon's Perfect Secrecy Condition

**Objective:** Show that for a perfectly secret system, the ciphertext and plaintext are statistically independent.

### Step 1 — Start with Bayes' Theorem

$$P(M \mid C) = \frac{P(C \mid M) \cdot P(M)}{P(C)}$$

### Step 2 — Impose the Perfect Secrecy Condition

Perfect secrecy requires that observing $C$ does not change our belief about $M$:

$$P(M \mid C) = P(M)$$

### Step 3 — Substitute and Rearrange

Substituting Step 1 into Step 2:

$$\frac{P(C \mid M) \cdot P(M)}{P(C)} = P(M)$$

Multiplying both sides by $P(C)$:

$$P(C \mid M) \cdot P(M) = P(M) \cdot P(C)$$

### Step 4 — Conclude the Independence Condition

Since $P(M) > 0$ for any meaningful message:

$$P(C \mid M) = P(C)$$

This means: **the probability of a ciphertext appearing is independent of the plaintext that produced it.** Therefore, an attacker learns nothing from the ciphertext.

### Step 5 — Corollary: Shannon's Theorem

Shannon proved that for perfect secrecy:

$$H(K) \geq H(M)$$

The key space must be at least as large as the message space. The **One-Time Pad (OTP)** achieves this with $H(K) = H(M)$ and a truly random key used exactly once.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Availability Calculation for Redundant Systems

**Objective:** Compute the availability of a system with one primary server and one hot-standby backup.

### Step 1 — Define Component Availabilities

Let:
- $A_1$ = availability of primary server
- $A_2$ = availability of backup server

The **unavailability** of each is:

$$U_1 = 1 - A_1, \quad U_2 = 1 - A_2$$

### Step 2 — System Fails Only if Both Components Fail

For a parallel redundant system, the system fails only when both components are down:

$$U_{\text{sys}} = U_1 \times U_2$$

### Step 3 — Compute System Availability

$$A_{\text{sys}} = 1 - U_{\text{sys}} = 1 - (1 - A_1)(1 - A_2)$$

### Step 4 — Numerical Example

If $A_1 = 0.99$ and $A_2 = 0.99$:

$$A_{\text{sys}} = 1 - (0.01)(0.01) = 1 - 0.0001 = 0.9999$$

Expressed as "nines": **99.99% ("four nines")** — corresponding to roughly **52.6 minutes of downtime per year**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Downtime per year = $(1 - A) \times 365 \times 24 \times 60$ minutes**

For $A = 0.9999$:

$$\text{Downtime} = 0.0001 \times 525600 \approx 52.56 \text{ minutes/year}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Probability of Forgery in a MAC Scheme

**Objective:** Derive the maximum probability that an attacker can produce a valid MAC tag on a message without knowing the secret key.

### Step 1 — Setup

A MAC scheme uses a $k$-bit secret key. An attacker can submit up to $q$ chosen-message queries and obtain valid tags.

### Step 2 — Brute-Force Guessing

If the attacker tries $t$ random tags on a target message, the probability of guessing a valid tag is:

$$P_{\text{guess}} = \frac{t}{2^k}$$

### Step 3 — Adversarial Advantage with $q$ Queries

With $q$ observed valid tags, the attacker can try to find an internal collision. By the **birthday bound**, a collision is found after:

$$q \approx \sqrt{2^k} = 2^{k/2}$$

So the probability of a successful forgery is:

$$P_{\text{forgery}} = \frac{q^2}{2^{k+1}}$$

### Step 4 — Example

For **HMAC-SHA-256** with $k = 256$ bits and $q = 2^{32}$ observed tags:

$$P_{\text{forgery}} = \frac{(2^{32})^2}{2^{257}} = \frac{2^{64}}{2^{257}} = 2^{-193}$$

This is astronomically small — providing **strong authentication guarantees**.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Entropy of a Password

**Objective:** Calculate the entropy of a password for strength analysis.

### Step 1 — Definition

If a password is drawn uniformly from a character set of size $C$ with length $L$:

$$H_{\text{password}} = L \times \log_2 C \text{ bits}$$

### Step 2 — Example 1: 8-character ASCII password

With $C = 95$ (printable ASCII) and $L = 8$:

$$H = 8 \times \log_2 95 \approx 8 \times 6.57 \approx 52.6 \text{ bits}$$

### Step 3 — Example 2: 12-character with all symbols

With $C = 95$ and $L = 12$:

$$H = 12 \times \log_2 95 \approx 12 \times 6.57 \approx 78.8 \text{ bits}$$

### Step 4 — Effective Entropy Reduction (Human Patterns)

Real passwords are not uniformly random. A typical **NIST 800-63B** rule reduces effective entropy based on dictionary attacks. If a password appears in a top-1M dictionary:

$$H_{\text{effective}} = \log_2(10^6) \approx 19.93 \text{ bits}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Always distinguish between *theoretical* entropy and *effective* entropy. Boards expect you to mention dictionary attacks and patterns in answers.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Services vs. Cryptographic Primitives

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mapping of Services to Underlying Cryptographic Mechanisms**

| Security Service | Symmetric Encryption | Asymmetric Encryption | Hash | MAC | Digital Signature | PKI / Cert |
|-----------------|----------------------|------------------------|------|-----|--------------------|-------------|
| Confidentiality | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ |
| Integrity | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ |
| Authentication | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
| Non-Repudiation | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ |
| Privacy | ✅ (with key mgmt) | ✅ (with key mgmt) | ✅ (for fingerprints) | ❌ | ✅ (for consent) | ✅ (for identity) |

Legend: ✅ = primary use, ❌ = not applicable

---

## 3.6 Worked-Out Numerical Example: Service Stack Design

**Scenario:** A bank wants to deploy an online transaction system. Identify the **security services** needed and the **mechanisms** that provide them.

| Step | Service Required | Threat Modeled | Mechanism Used |
|------|------------------|----------------|----------------|
| 1 | **Authentication** | Imposter login | Username + password + OTP (TOTP) |
| 2 | **Authorization** | Customer accesses another's account | RBAC with account-level scopes |
| 3 | **Confidentiality** | Eavesdropping on network | TLS 1.3 with AES-256-GCM |
| 4 | **Integrity** | Transaction amount tampering | HMAC-SHA-256 over transaction payload |
| 5 | **Non-Repudiation** | Customer denies transaction | Digital signature with customer's private key |
| 6 | **Availability** | DDoS attack on bank portal | CDN + rate limiting + Anycast |
| 7 | **Privacy** | Customer's personal data leaked | Data minimization + tokenization + DPIA |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1: Taxonomy of Security Services (X.800)

<!-- This diagram presents the canonical classification of security services as per ITU-T X.800 and ISO 7498-2, extended with the modern Privacy service. -->

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[Security Services X.800] --> nodeB[Confidentiality]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Integrity]
    nodeA --> nodeD[Authentication]
    nodeA --> nodeE[Access Control Authorization]
    nodeA --> nodeF[Non Repudiation]

    nodeA --> nodeG[Availability]
    nodeA --> nodeH[Privacy Modern]

    nodeB --> nodeB1[Data Confidentiality]
    nodeB --> nodeB2[Traffic Flow Confidentiality]
    nodeB --> nodeB3[PII Protection]

    nodeC --> nodeC1[Data Integrity]
    nodeC --> nodeC2[System Integrity]
    nodeC --> nodeC3[Origin Integrity]

    nodeD --> nodeD1[Peer Entity Auth]
    nodeD --> nodeD2[Data Origin Auth]

    nodeE --> nodeE1[DAC]
    nodeE --> nodeE2[MAC]
    nodeE --> nodeE3[RBAC]
    nodeE --> nodeE4[ABAC]

    nodeF --> nodeF1[Non Repudiation of Origin]
    nodeF --> nodeF2[Non Repudiation of Receipt]
    nodeF --> nodeF3[Non Repudiation of Delivery]

    nodeG --> nodeG1[Redundancy]
    nodeG --> nodeG2[Backups]
    nodeG --> nodeG3[DDoS Mitigation]

    nodeH --> nodeH1[Consent Management]
    nodeH --> nodeH2[Anonymization]
    nodeH --> nodeH3[DPIA]
    nodeH --> nodeH4[Privacy by Design]
```

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2: Service-Mechanism-Attack Triangle (X.800)

<!-- This diagram illustrates the interrelationship between attacks, mechanisms, and services as defined in the X.800 standard. -->

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeAttack[Security Attacks] --> nodeMech[Security Mechanisms]
    nodeMech --> nodeService[Security Services]
    nodeService --> nodeGoal[Security Goal Protected Information]

    nodeAttack --> a1[Eavesdropping]
    nodeAttack --> a2[Tampering]
    nodeAttack --> a3[Masquerading]
    nodeAttack --> a4[Replay]
    nodeAttack --> a5[Repudiation]
    nodeAttack --> a6[DoS]
    nodeAttack --> a7[Data Leakage]

    nodeMech --> m1[Encryption]
    nodeMech --> m2[Digital Signature]
    nodeMech --> m3[Access Control]
    nodeMech --> m4[Data Integrity]
    nodeMech --> m5[Traffic Padding]
    nodeMech --> m6[Routing Control]
    nodeMech --> m7[Notarization]

    nodeService --> s1[Confidentiality]
    nodeService --> s2[Integrity]
    nodeService --> s3[Authentication]
    nodeService --> s4[Authorization]
    nodeService --> s5[Non Repudiation]
    nodeService --> s6[Availability]
    nodeService --> s7[Privacy]
```

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3: Service-to-OSI Layer Mapping

<!-- This diagram visualizes which security services are typically enforced at each OSI layer, helping students understand the layered defense approach. -->

```mermaid
graph TB
    nodeL7[Application Layer] --> nodeS1[Privacy]
    nodeL7 --> nodeS2[Authentication]
    nodeL7 --> nodeS3[Non Repudiation]

    nodeL6[Presentation Layer] --> nodeS4[Confidentiality]
    nodeL6 --> nodeS5[Integrity]

    nodeL5[Session Layer] --> nodeS6[Authentication]
    nodeL5 --> nodeS7[Session Integrity]

    nodeL4[Transport Layer] --> nodeS8[Confidentiality TLS]
    nodeL4 --> nodeS9[Integrity TLS]

    nodeL3[Network Layer] --> nodeS10[Authentication IPSec]
    nodeL3 --> nodeS11[Confidentiality IPSec ESP]
    nodeL3 --> nodeS12[Integrity IPSec AH]

    nodeL2[Data Link Layer] --> nodeS13[Confidentiality WPA2]
    nodeL2 --> nodeS14[Integrity MAC]

    nodeL1[Physical Layer] --> nodeS15[Confidentiality Shielding]
```

---

## 4.4 Diagram 4: Service Stack for a Modern Web Transaction

<!-- A block-level functional architecture showing how multiple security services combine in a real-world e-commerce or banking transaction. -->

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeUser[User Device Browser] --> nodeTL1[TLS 1.3 Handshake]
    nodeTL1 --> nodeAuth[Authentication Service]
    nodeAuth --> nodeAuthz[Authorization Service RBAC]
    nodeAuthz --> nodeConf[Confidentiality AES 256 GCM]
    nodeConf --> nodeIntg[Integrity HMAC SHA 256]
    nodeIntg --> nodeNR[Non Repudiation Digital Signature]
    nodeNR --> nodePriv[Privacy Service Tokenization]
    nodePriv --> nodeAvail[Availability CDN Anycast]
    nodeAvail --> nodeLog[Audit and Monitoring]
    nodeLog --> nodeDB[(Encrypted Database)]
    nodeDB --> nodeUser

    nodeAtt[Attacker] -.->|Tries| nodeTL1
    nodeAtt -.->|Tries| nodeAuth
    nodeAtt -.->|Tries DoS| nodeAvail
```

---

## 4.5 Diagram 5: Lifecycle of a Privacy Service (GDPR-Aligned)

<!-- A privacy service is more than a mechanism — it is a lifecycle. This diagram walks through the data lifecycle stages where a privacy service must be applied. -->

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> nodeCollect: 1. Data Collection with Consent
    nodeCollect --> nodeStore: 2. Encrypted Storage with Purpose Tag
    nodeStore --> nodeProcess: 3. Processing under Purpose Limitation
    nodeProcess --> nodeShare: 4. Sharing with Third Party Contract
    nodeShare --> nodeRetain: 5. Retention with Auto Deletion
    nodeRetain --> nodeErase: 6. Right to be Forgotten
    nodeErase --> [*]

    nodeCollect: Collection
    nodeStore: Storage
    nodeProcess: Processing
    nodeShare: Sharing
    nodeRetain: Retention
    nodeErase: Erasure
```

---

## 4.6 Diagram 6: Defense-in-Depth Across Services

<!-- Defense-in-depth means multiple services overlap so that the failure of one does not compromise the system. -->

```mermaid
graph TB
    nodePerim[Perimeter Firewall] --> nodeAuth[Authentication Service]
    nodeAuth --> nodeAuthz[Authorization Service]
    nodeAuthz --> nodeCrypto[Crypto Layer TLS IPSec]
    nodeCrypto --> nodeIntg[Integrity Check HMAC]
    nodeIntg --> nodeAudit[Audit Log Non Repudiation]
    nodeAudit --> nodeBackup[Backup and Availability]
    nodeBackup --> nodePrivacy[Privacy Layer Anonymization]

    nodeAtt1[Attacker Layer 7] -.->|Blocked| nodePerim
    nodeAtt2[Attacker Internal] -.->|Blocked| nodeAuthz
    nodeAtt3[Attacker Network] -.->|Blocked| nodeCrypto
    nodeAtt4[Attacker Data] -.->|Blocked| nodePrivacy
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **KTU Pattern:** Answer in 2–3 sentences maximum. Direct, factual responses. No sub-parts.

---

### **Question 1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define security services as per ITU-T X.800. List the five classical categories of security services.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> A **security service**, as defined in **ITU-T Recommendation X.800 (Security Architecture for OSI)**, is a service provided by a system to give a specific kind of protection to system resources; security services implement security policies and are implemented by security mechanisms.
>
> The **five classical security services** specified by X.800 are:
> 1. **Confidentiality** — protection of data from unauthorized disclosure.
> 2. **Integrity** — assurance that data has not been modified.
> 3. **Authentication** — assurance that the communicating entity is the one claimed.
> 4. **Access Control (Authorization)** — prevention of unauthorized use of resources.
> 5. **Non-Repudiation** — protection against false denial of having participated in a communication.

**Mark Split (for self-evaluation):**
- *1 mark* — Correct definition
- *1 mark* — Listing the five services
- *1 mark* — One-line description of each

---

### **Question 2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Differentiate between authentication and authorization with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Authentication** is the process of **verifying the identity** of a user, system, or entity, ensuring that the principal is who it claims to be. Example: A user logging in with a username and password, or providing a fingerprint to unlock a phone.
>
> **Authorization** is the process of **granting or denying access rights** to an authenticated entity, based on policies. Example: Once authenticated, a bank customer can view their own account balance but not another customer's account.
>
> **Key Distinction:** Authentication answers *"Who are you?"*; Authorization answers *"What are you allowed to do?"* Authentication must occur **before** authorization.

**Mark Split:**
- *1 mark* — Authentication definition + example
- *1 mark* — Authorization definition + example
- *1 mark* — Clear distinction

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question has sub-parts (a) and (b), each carrying 7 marks. Internal choice is mandatory.

---

### **Question A. [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **five classical security services** defined by ITU-T X.800 in detail. For each service, mention **one cryptographic mechanism** that provides it.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the **mapping of security services to the OSI 7-layer model**. Provide an example of a protocol at each layer that implements confidentiality and authentication.

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

The **ITU-T Recommendation X.800**, in conjunction with **ISO 7498-2**, defines five classical security services that form the foundation of information security.

**1. Confidentiality (Confidentiality Service):**
This service protects data from **unauthorized disclosure**. It has three aspects:
- **Data confidentiality:** Protects the content of messages.
- **Privacy:** Prevents traffic analysis that could reveal who is communicating with whom.
- **Traffic-flow confidentiality:** Hides source, destination, frequency, and length of communication.
- *Cryptographic mechanism:* **AES-256** (symmetric encryption).

**2. Integrity (Integrity Service):**
This service ensures that data is received **exactly as sent**, with no modification, insertion, deletion, or replay. It has two aspects:
- **Data integrity:** Content-level integrity.
- **System integrity:** The system operates as intended without unauthorized manipulation.
- *Cryptographic mechanism:* **SHA-256 hash** combined with **HMAC**.

**3. Authentication (Authentication Service):**
Verifies the **identity** of a peer in a connection or the source of a message. Two forms:
- **Peer-entity authentication:** Used in connection-oriented communications.
- **Data-origin authentication:** Used in connectionless communications (does not protect against replay).
- *Cryptographic mechanism:* **Digital certificates (X.509)** issued by a Certificate Authority (CA).

**4. Access Control (Authorization Service):**
Protects against **unauthorized use** of resources. The access matrix specifies for each subject (row) the access rights (read, write, execute) on each object (column). Implementations include **ACLs (Access Control Lists)** and **capability lists**.
- *Cryptographic mechanism:* **Attribute-Based Encryption (ABE)** in modern systems.

**5. Non-Repudiation:**
Provides **proof of integrity and origin** of data, such that neither the sender nor the receiver can later deny the action. Two forms:
- **Non-repudiation of origin** (proof of sending).
- **Non-repudiation of receipt** (proof of receiving).
- *Cryptographic mechanism:* **RSA Digital Signature** or **ECDSA** with a trusted timestamp authority.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mark Split for (a):** 1 mark per service definition + 0.4 mark per mechanism ≈ 7 marks.

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

Security services are not bound to a single layer of the OSI model. The **X.800** architecture maps services to the most appropriate layer, and a single service may be implemented at multiple layers.

| OSI Layer | Typical Services | Example Protocol |
|-----------|------------------|------------------|
| 7 — Application | Authentication, Non-Repudiation, Privacy | **PGP**, **S/MIME**, **Kerberos** |
| 6 — Presentation | Confidentiality, Integrity | **TLS Record Protocol** |
| 5 — Session | Authentication, Session Integrity | **SSH Session Layer** |
| 4 — Transport | Confidentiality, Integrity | **TLS (over TCP)**, **DTLS (over UDP)** |
| 3 — Network | Authentication, Confidentiality, Integrity | **IPSec (AH + ESP)** |
| 2 — Data Link | Confidentiality, Integrity | **WPA2/WPA3** (Wi-Fi), **MACsec** (Ethernet) |
| 1 — Physical | Confidentiality (limited) | **Shielded cabling**, **Faraday cages** |

**Example — TLS (Transport Layer 4):**
TLS provides **confidentiality** using symmetric encryption (e.g., AES-GCM) and **authentication** using X.509 certificates. During the TLS handshake, the server (and optionally the client) authenticates with a digital certificate, and a session key is negotiated using asymmetric cryptography (e.g., ECDHE).

**Example — IPSec (Network Layer 3):**
IPSec uses the **Authentication Header (AH)** for integrity and authentication, and the **Encapsulating Security Payload (ESP)** for confidentiality (and optionally integrity). It can operate in **transport mode** (host-to-host) or **tunnel mode** (gateway-to-gateway, used in VPNs).

**Defense-in-Depth Insight:** Modern secure systems layer services across multiple OSI levels. For example, a banking application uses **HTTPS (Layer 7)** for application-level authentication, **TLS (Layer 4)** for transport confidentiality, and **IPSec (Layer 3)** for network-level integrity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mark Split for (b):** 3 marks for the table + 2 marks for TLS example + 2 marks for IPSec example.

---

### **Question B. [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **importance of Non-Repudiation and Availability services** in modern e-commerce. Explain the mechanisms used to provide each.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a suitable diagram, describe **Defense-in-Depth** as a layered approach to security services. How does it differ from relying on a single service?

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Non-Repudiation in E-Commerce:**
In e-commerce, customers and merchants exchange digital messages that may have legal and financial consequences. **Non-repudiation** ensures that **neither party can later deny** having sent, received, or acted upon a message.

- *Non-repudiation of origin* prevents a customer from falsely denying that they placed an order.
- *Non-repudiation of receipt* prevents a merchant from falsely denying that they received a payment.
- *Non-repudiation of delivery* prevents a logistics company from denying that the goods were delivered.

**Mechanisms:**
1. **Digital Signatures** (e.g., RSA, ECDSA): The sender signs the hash of the message with their private key. The receiver verifies the signature with the sender's public key. This binds the message to the sender cryptographically.
2. **Trusted Timestamps**: A Time-Stamping Authority (TSA) attaches a verifiable timestamp to the digital signature, proving that the message existed at a particular time.
3. **Third-Party Notarization**: A trusted intermediary (e.g., a Notary Public or Trusted Third Party) records the transaction in an audit log.
4. **Immutable Audit Logs**: Logs are written to write-once storage (e.g., blockchain, WORM storage) so they cannot be retroactively modified.

**Availability in E-Commerce:**
An e-commerce site that is **unavailable** loses revenue and customer trust. **Availability** ensures the system is operational when needed.

- For a site like Amazon, even **1 minute of downtime** during a sale can mean losses of millions of dollars.
- **SLA (Service Level Agreement):** Most e-commerce platforms promise "three nines" (99.9%) to "four nines" (99.99%) availability.

**Mechanisms:**
1. **Redundancy:** Multiple servers in different geographic regions; if one fails, others take over.
2. **Load Balancing:** Distributes traffic across servers to prevent overload.
3. **DDoS Mitigation:** Services like **Cloudflare**, **AWS Shield**, and **Akamai** filter malicious traffic.
4. **Disaster Recovery:** Off-site backups and hot-standby sites.
5. **UPS and Power Backup:** Protects against power failures.

**Numerical Example:** A system has MTBF = 5000 hours and MTTR = 1 hour. Compute availability:

$$A = \frac{5000}{5000 + 1} \times 100\% = 99.98\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Split for (a):** 3 marks for Non-Repudiation explanation + 2 marks for mechanisms + 1 mark for numerical example + 1 mark for conclusion.

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Defense-in-Depth:**
Defense-in-Depth is a **layered security strategy** in which multiple security services and mechanisms are deployed in overlapping layers, so that the failure or bypass of one layer does not result in a complete compromise.

**Conceptual Diagram:**

```mermaid
graph TB
    L1[Layer 1 Perimeter Security Firewall IDS] --> L2[Layer 2 Network Security IPSec VPN]
    L2 --> L3[Layer 3 Host Security Antivirus Hardening]
    L3 --> L4[Layer 4 Application Security Authentication Input Validation]
    L4 --> L5[Layer 5 Data Security Encryption Tokenization]
    L5 --> L6[Layer 6 Monitoring and Audit SIEM]
    L6 --> L7[Layer 7 Privacy Layer DPIA Consent]

    Att[Attacker] -.->|Tries| L1
    Att -.->|Bypassed| L2
    Att -.->|Bypassed| L3
    Att -.->|Bypassed| L4
    Att -.->|Bypassed| L5
    Att -.->|Detected| L6
```

**The Seven Layers (mapped to security services):**

| Layer | Security Service Provided | Example Mechanism |
|-------|---------------------------|-------------------|
| Perimeter | Confidentiality, Availability | Firewall, IDS/IPS |
| Network | Confidentiality, Integrity | IPSec, MACsec |
| Host | Integrity, Authentication | OS hardening, EDR |
| Application | Authentication, Authorization, Non-Repudiation | OAuth, RBAC, Logging |
| Data | Confidentiality, Integrity, Privacy | AES, hashing, anonymization |
| Monitoring | Non-Repudiation, Availability | SIEM, audit logs |
| Privacy | Privacy, Accountability | DPIA, consent management |

**Differences from Single-Service Reliance:**

| Aspect | Single Service | Defense-in-Depth |
|--------|----------------|------------------|
| Failure tolerance | Single point of failure | Multiple overlapping layers |
| Cost | Lower initial cost | Higher upfront cost, lower breach cost |
| Complexity | Simple to deploy | Complex to manage |
| Coverage | Limited threat model | Comprehensive |
| Example | "We only encrypt" | Encryption + ACL + Auth + Audit + Backup |

**Real-World Example:** A bank uses TLS (network), MFA (application), AES (data), audit logs (non-repudiation), and disaster recovery (availability) — all at the same time.

> [!TIP]
> **Mark Split for (b):** 2 marks for concept + 2 marks for diagram + 2 marks for layer table + 1 mark for comparison.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Students Make (and How to Lose Marks):**
>
> 1. **Confusing Authentication with Authorization:** A frequent blunder. Authentication = *who you are*; Authorization = *what you can do*. Examiners specifically look for this distinction.
>
> 2. **Forgetting OSI Layer Mapping:** KTU examiners expect a table or diagram showing which security service maps to which OSI layer. Skipping this loses **2–3 marks** in 14-mark questions.
>
> 3. **Listing Services Without Mechanisms:** Saying "we use integrity" without mentioning *which* mechanism (e.g., SHA-256, HMAC, digital signature) is incomplete. Each service must be paired with at least one mechanism.
>
> 4. **Ignoring Availability and Privacy:** Many students focus only on CIA (Confidentiality, Integrity, Authentication) and forget **Availability** and the modern **Privacy** service. KTU's 2024 syllabus explicitly includes them.
>
> 5. **Numerical Errors in Availability:** Mixing up MTBF and MTTR, or misplacing the decimal, can cost a full mark. Always write the formula before substituting values.
>
> 6. **Missing Internal Choice in Part B:** KTU mandates that the answer script have *only one* Part B question attempted. Writing both Question A and Question B forfeits marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Services**
>
> ✅ **Definition (X.800):** A security service enhances security of data processing and information transfers; it is implemented via security mechanisms to counter security attacks.
>
> ✅ **Five Classical Services:** Confidentiality, Integrity, Authentication, Authorization (Access Control), Non-Repudiation.
>
> ✅ **Two Modern Additions:** Availability, Privacy.
>
> ✅ **Confidentiality Mechanisms:** Symmetric encryption (AES, 3DES), Asymmetric encryption (RSA, ECC), Access Control.
>
> ✅ **Integrity Mechanisms:** Hash functions (SHA-256, SHA-3), Message Authentication Codes (HMAC), Digital Signatures.
>
> ✅ **Authentication Mechanisms:** Passwords, Biometrics, Smart Cards, Certificates (X.509), Kerberos, OAuth 2.0, SAML.
>
> ✅ **Authorization Models:** DAC, MAC, RBAC, ABAC — *know the difference*.
>
> ✅ **Non-Repudiation Mechanisms:** Digital Signatures, Trusted Timestamps, Audit Logs, Notarization.
>
> ✅ **Availability Mechanisms:** Redundancy, Load Balancing, Backups, DDoS Mitigation, Disaster Recovery.
>
> ✅ **Availability Formula:** $A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ — *"nines"* notation.
>
> ✅ **Privacy Principles:** Lawfulness, Purpose Limitation, Data Minimization, Accuracy, Storage Limitation, Integrity & Confidentiality, Accountability (GDPR Article 5).
>
> ✅ **OSI Mapping:** Confidentiality often at L1–L4; Authentication at L4–L7; Non-Repudiation at L7; Privacy at L7.
>
> ✅ **Perfect Secrecy:** $P(M \vert C) = P(M)$; requires $H(K) \geq H(M)$; achieved by **One-Time Pad**.
>
> ✅ **Defense-in-Depth:** Multiple overlapping services; failure of one layer does not compromise the system.
>
> ✅ **Service-Mechanism-Attack Triangle:** Every service counters one or more attacks via one or more mechanisms.
>
> ✅ **Key Distinction:** *Authentication* answers "Who are you?"; *Authorization* answers "What can you do?"; *Non-Repudiation* answers "Can you deny it?"
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Attacks on Digital Signature
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECDT523 - DATA PRIVACY AND SECURITY > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Attacks on Digital Signature # 1.
