Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition A **digital signature attack** is any cryptanalytic effort, mathematical manipulation, or protocol abuse aimed at subverting the security guarantees of a digital signature scheme — namely **authenticity, integrity, and non-repudiation**. The attacker exploits weaknesses in the cryptographic primitives (hash function, modular arithmetic, elliptic curve group), the padding scheme, the random number generator, or the protocol logic itself. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** > An attack on a digital signature is a procedure executed by an adversary $\mathcal{A}$ that, given access to certain resources (public key, signed messages, or even an oracle), tries to break one of the four security goals of the scheme: *total break, universal forgery, selective forgery*, or *existential forgery*. The attacker model in signature security is formally captured by a 2-tuple: $$\text{Attack} = (\mathcal{A}, \text{Goal})$$ where the adversary $\mathcal{A}$ is constrained by an **attack model** (what she knows / can request) and the **goal** (what forgery level she targets). ## 1.2 The Four Attack Models (What the Adversary Knows) | # | Attack Model | Adversary's Capability | |---|---|---| | 1 | **Key-Only Attack (KOA)** | Knows only the public verification key $V$ | | 2 | **Known Message Attack (KMA)** | Knows a list of valid (message, signature) pairs | | 3 | **Chosen Message Attack (CMA)** | Can obtain signatures on messages of her choice before forging | | 4 | **Adaptive CMA (ACMA)** | Can adaptively query the signing oracle based on previous replies | ## 1.3 The Four Forgery Goals (What the Adversary Wants) | # | Forgery Type | Strength | Description | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Total Break** | Strongest | Recover the private signing key $S$ | | 2 | **Universal Forgery** | Very Strong | Forge a valid signature for **any** message | | 3 | **Selective Forgery** | Strong | Forge a valid signature for a **chosen target** message | | 4 | **Existential Forgery** | Weakest | Produce **at least one** valid (message, signature) pair — message can be random | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Golden Rule for KTU:** A scheme is considered *secure* only if it resists **existential forgery under adaptive chosen-message attack (EUF-CMA)**. Anything weaker is unacceptable for modern standards like RSA-PSS, ECDSA, EdDSA. ## 1.4 Intuitive Analogy — "The Signature Stamp Forgery" Imagine a CEO's official rubber-stamp on company cheques. The stamp mark is **public** — everyone has seen it on legitimate cheques. - **Key-Only Attack:** You walk past the CEO's office, glance at the stamp design, and try to carve a replica out of rubber. - **Known Message Attack:** You collect a pile of legitimately signed cheques from the trash. You study exactly how the ink flows, the pressure, the tilt. - **Chosen Message Attack:** You sneak a fake cheque into the secretary's in-tray and watch the CEO sign it — *now you have a perfect template*. - **Existential Forgery:** You don't care *what* message you forge — you just need **one** cheque that passes the bank's visual verification. - **Universal Forgery:** You can forge the CEO's signature on **any** cheque, any amount, anytime. The bank's verifier (the *verification algorithm*) is your cryptographic hash + public key. The CEO's stamp is the *private key*. Attacks on digital signatures are precisely these categories, formalised in the **Goldwasser–Micali–Rivest (GMR) security model (1988)**. ## 1.5 The "Why" Behind Digital Signature Attacks A successful attack breaks one (or more) of three pillars: 1. **Authenticity** — message truly came from claimed sender. 2. **Integrity** — message was not altered in transit. 3. **Non-Repudiation** — sender cannot later deny signing. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse *symmetric-key forgery* (replaying a shared MAC) with *asymmetric-key forgery* (breaking the public-key primitive). Digital signature attacks almost always revolve around **breaking the asymmetric primitive** or its *randomness source*, not the hash function alone. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Anatomy of a Signature Scheme (Recap) A digital signature scheme $\Sigma$ is a triple of probabilistic polynomial-time (PPT) algorithms: $$\Sigma = (\text{KeyGen}, \text{Sign}, \text{Verify})$$ - **KeyGen** $\to (pk, sk)$ — outputs a public verification key and a private signing key. - **Sign**$(sk, m) \to \sigma$ — produces signature on message $m$. - **Verify**$(pk, m, \sigma) \to \{0, 1\}$ — accepts or rejects. **Security experiment (EUF-CMA):** $$\begin{aligned} \text{Exp}^{\text{EUF-CMA}}_{\Sigma, \mathcal{A}}(\lambda): \quad & (pk, sk) \leftarrow \text{KeyGen}(1^\lambda) \\ & (m^*, \sigma^*) \leftarrow \mathcal{A}^{\text{Sign}(sk, \cdot)}(pk) \\ & \text{Return } 1 \iff \text{Verify}(pk, m^*, \sigma^*) = 1 \;\land\; m^* \notin Q \end{aligned}$$ where $Q$ is the set of messages queried to the signing oracle. If this probability is **negligible in $\lambda$**, the scheme is EUF-CMA secure. ## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of a Signature Attack 1. **Threat Identification:** Attacker identifies the signature scheme in use (e.g., *textbook RSA*, *RSA-PSS*, *plain DSA*, *Schnorr*, *ECDSA*). 2. **Resource Enumeration:** Determines what is exposed — public key $pk$, signed messages, oracle access, side channels. 3. **Weakness Mapping:** Locates a flaw — e.g., deterministic padding, biased nonce, multiplicative homomorphism, small hash size. 4. **Exploitation:** Crafts inputs that exploit the flaw. 5. **Forgery Production:** Outputs $(m^*, \sigma^*)$ that passes $\text{Verify}$. 6. **Goal Evaluation:** Checks whether the forgery is *existential, selective,* or *universal*. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > Use `\vert` instead of the vertical pipe `|` in table cells to preserve markdown syntax. | Topic | Formula / Property | Use in Attack | |---|---|---| | RSA Verification | $\sigma^e \equiv m \pmod{n}$ | For existential forgery on textbook RSA | | RSA Blinding | $m' = m \cdot r^e \bmod n$ | Trick signer into signing blinded message | | DSA Signature | $s = k^{-1}(H(m) + x \cdot r) \bmod q$ | Nonce-reuse recovers private key $x$ | | Nonce Recovery (DSA) | $k = (H(m_1) - H(m_2)) \cdot (s_1 - s_2)^{-1} \bmod q$ | When same $k$ used twice | | Private Key Recovery | $x = (s \cdot k - H(m)) \cdot r^{-1} \bmod q$ | After nonce $k$ is recovered | | Schnorr Verification | $s = H(M \;\vert\; g^r y^s)$ | Existential forgery if no random $r$ | | Birthday Bound | $O(2^{n/2})$ for $n$-bit hash | Collision attack on weak hash | | Hash Collision | $H(m_1) = H(m_2)$ | Forge two contracts with same hash | | Pollard–Rho DLP | $O(\sqrt{q})$ group operations | Break DSA / ElGamal if subgroup small | | ElGamal Verification | $\text{verify}(r,s) = g^M \stackrel{?}{=} y^r \cdot r^s \bmod p$ | Existential forgery possible | | RSA Modulus | $n = p \cdot q$ | Factor $n$ to break key | | Totient | $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$ | Recover $d$ from $e$ | | Modular Inverse | $a \cdot a^{-1} \equiv 1 \pmod{n}$ | Used in blinding, unblinding | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Banking & Payments:** UPI, SWIFT, and credit card EMV chips rely on **RSA-PSS** and **ECDSA**. A single nonce-reuse bug in EMV was historically exploited to clone cards. - **Software Updates:** Code-signing (Apple, Microsoft, Linux distros) uses RSA / ECDSA. A successful attack enables **supply-chain compromise** (e.g., Stuxnet, SolarWinds-style). - **Blockchain:** Bitcoin and Ethereum use **ECDSA over secp256k1**. Repeated $k$ in Sony PS3's ECDSA allowed full key recovery — a textbook KTU example. - **TLS / HTTPS:** Server certificates signed via RSA or ECDSA; CA's private key compromise = total Internet break. - **Smart Cards & Passports:** e-Passports use **Active Authentication** with RSA/ECDSA. Nonce bias in older passports was a published attack vector. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Attack 1 — Existential Forgery on Textbook RSA **The flaw:** Textbook RSA signature verification is $\sigma^e \equiv m \pmod n$, and signing is $m^d \bmod n$. There is no padding, no hash, no structure. **Attack walkthrough:** Step 1 — Adversary picks an arbitrary integer $s \in \mathbb{Z}_n^*$. Step 2 — Adversary computes the message: $$m = s^e \bmod n$$ Step 3 — Adversary outputs the pair $(m, s)$. Step 4 — Verifier checks: $$\sigma^e = s^e \equiv m \pmod n \;\;\checkmark$$ Step 5 — Verification passes. **Existential forgery successful.** > [!NOTE] > The forged message $m$ is *random garbage* — semantically meaningless — but the pair $(m, s)$ is a valid signature. This is precisely why *existential forgery* is the weakest goal: it does not require control over message content. **Why this works algebraically:** $$\begin{aligned} \text{Sign}(d, m) &= m^d \bmod n \\ \text{Verify}(e, m, \sigma) &= \sigma^e \equiv m \pmod n \\ \text{If } \sigma = m^d \bmod n &\Rightarrow (m^d)^e = m^{de} = m \pmod n \end{aligned}$$ The structure of RSA forms a cyclic group homomorphism. The attacker exploits the *surjectivity* of $x \mapsto x^e \bmod n$ — every $m$ has a pre-image, namely $m^d$. --- ## 3.2 Attack 2 — RSA Blinding Attack (Chosen Message) **Scenario:** A signing oracle refuses to sign suspicious messages (e.g., contracts with $m$ = "Pay $1,000,000"). The attacker wants signature on a specific $m^*$. **Step 1:** Choose a random blinding factor $r \in \mathbb{Z}_n^*$. **Step 2:** Compute the blinded message: $$m' = m^* \cdot r^e \bmod n$$ Step 3 — Because $m'$ is statistically uniform, the oracle cannot distinguish it from a random message and signs it: $$\sigma' = (m')^d = (m^*)^d \cdot r^{ed} = (m^*)^d \cdot r \bmod n$$ Step 4 — Attacker unblinds: $$\sigma = \sigma' \cdot r^{-1} \bmod n = (m^*)^d \cdot r \cdot r^{-1} = (m^*)^d \bmod n$$ Step 5 — Output $(m^*, \sigma)$. The oracle was fooled into signing the target message. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Defence:** Use randomised padding (RSA-PSS, RSA-OAEP) so that the same logical message yields different $m'$ each time. PSS effectively randomises the input, denying attackers the structural relation $m' = m \cdot r^e$. --- ## 3.3 Attack 3 — RSA Multiplicative Property Attack **Mathematical property:** $$\text{Sign}(m_1) \cdot \text{Sign}(m_2) = m_1^d \cdot m_2^d = (m_1 \cdot m_2)^d = \text{Sign}(m_1 \cdot m_2)$$ **Attack:** Step 1 — Attacker obtains two valid signatures $\sigma_1 = m_1^d \bmod n$ and $\sigma_2 = m_2^d \bmod n$ (KMA scenario). Step 2 — Forges signature on $m_3 = m_1 \cdot m_2 \bmod n$ *without* calling the signing oracle: $$\sigma_3 = \sigma_1 \cdot \sigma_2 \bmod n$$ Step 3 — Verifier checks: $$\sigma_3^e = (\sigma_1 \sigma_2)^e = \sigma_1^e \sigma_2^e = m_1 \cdot m_2 = m_3 \pmod n \;\;\checkmark$$ > [!WARNING] > This is why textbook RSA is **multiplicatively homomorphic** — a structural weakness. Real schemes hash the message first, destroying the algebraic structure. --- ## 3.4 Attack 4 — DSA / ECDSA Nonce Reuse (Sony PS3, 2010) **DSA signing equation:** $$s = k^{-1} \bigl( H(m) + x \cdot r \bigr) \bmod q$$ where $r = (g^k \bmod p) \bmod q$. **Scenario:** Same nonce $k$ used for two messages $m_1, m_2$. **Step 1 — Get two signatures $(r_1, s_1)$ on $m_1$ and $(r_2, s_2)$ on $m_2$:** $$\begin{aligned} s_1 &= k^{-1} \bigl( H(m_1) + x \cdot r \bigr) \bmod q \\ s_2 &= k^{-1} \bigl( H(m_2) + x \cdot r \bigr) \bmod q \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2 — Subtract:** $$s_1 - s_2 = k^{-1} \bigl( H(m_1) - H(m_2) \bigr) \bmod q$$ **Step 3 — Solve for $k$:** $$k = (H(m_1) - H(m_2)) \cdot (s_1 - s_2)^{-1} \bmod q$$ **Step 4 — Recover private key $x$ from either signature:** $$x = (s_1 \cdot k - H(m_1)) \cdot r^{-1} \bmod q$$ > [!NOTE] > **Real-world impact:** Sony's PS3 firmware signing key was hardcoded with a *static* $k$ for years. In 2010, "fail0verflow" recovered the ECDSA private key using exactly this attack. The PS3 could then sign arbitrary homebrew code — a *universal forgery* / total break. --- ## 3.5 Attack 5 — Existential Forgery on Plain ElGamal **Signing:** pick random $k$, compute $r = g^k \bmod p$, then $s = k^{-1}(H(m) - x \cdot r) \bmod (p-1)$. **Verification:** $g^{H(m)} \equiv y^r \cdot r^s \pmod p$ where $y = g^x$. **Attack (chosen-message):** Step 1 — Choose arbitrary integers $i, j$ with $\gcd(j, p-1) = 1$. Step 2 — Set: $$r = g^i \cdot y^j \bmod p$$ $$s = -r \cdot j^{-1} \bmod (p-1)$$ $$m = -s \cdot i \cdot j^{-1} \bmod (p-1) \;\;\text{(or rather, } m = s \cdot i \bmod (p-1)\text{)}$$ Step 3 — Check verification: $$y^r \cdot r^s = g^{xr} \cdot (g^i y^j)^s = g^{xr} \cdot g^{is} \cdot g^{xjs} = g^{xr + is + xjs}$$ We need this to equal $g^{H(m)}$, i.e.: $$xr + is + xjs \equiv H(m) \pmod{p-1}$$ $$x(r + js) + is \equiv H(m) \pmod{p-1}$$ Substituting the chosen $i, j, r$ values, the equation is satisfied. **Existential forgery achieved.** > [!IMPORTANT] > This is a standard KTU question: *"Show how plain ElGamal signature is existentially forgeable."* The structure is again due to *no hash on the message* and *multiplicative homomorphism* in the exponent. --- ## 3.6 Attack 6 — Birthday / Collision Attack on Hash If the underlying hash function $H$ has $n$-bit output, finding a collision requires: $$O(2^{n/2}) \text{ operations}$$ by the **Birthday Paradox**. **Signature forgery via collision:** Step 1 — Generate $2^{n/2}$ variants of a *benign* contract $C_1$ (changes in whitespace, synonyms, font encoding — all producing same semantic meaning). Step 2 — Generate $2^{n/2}$ variants of a *malicious* contract $C_2$ (e.g., "Pay $0" vs "Pay $1,000,000"). Step 3 — By the Birthday Bound, with high probability there exist $C_1'$ and $C_2'$ with $H(C_1') = H(C_2')$. Step 4 — Get the signer to sign $C_1'$ (benign). Transferred signature is also valid for $C_2'$ (malicious). > [!WARNING] > **MD5 is broken** in 2004 (Wang et al.) — collisions generated in seconds. SHA-1 broken in 2017 (Google SHAttered). This is why TLS 1.3 mandates **SHA-256 or stronger**. --- ## 3.7 Attack 7 — Bleichenbacher's Low-Exponent RSA Signature Forgery (2006) **Setup:** RSA signature with $e = 3$ and PKCS#1 v1.5 padding (or weak PSS implementations). **Attack:** Attacker constructs a cube root of a message padded with a chosen block: $$\sigma = \sqrt[3]{0\text{x}0001\text{FF}00 \cdots \text{H}(M) \cdots 00}$$ For a sufficiently long modulus (e.g., 2048 bits) and short hash, the cube root is an integer, and the padding appears valid. The verifier accepts. > [!IMPORTANT] > This attack is the *primary reason* modern schemes use **RSA-PSS** with random salt, not PKCS#1 v1.5. --- ## 3.8 Analytical Matrix — Attack vs Scheme | Attack \ Scheme | Textbook RSA | Plain ElGamal | Plain DSA | RSA-PSS | ECDSA (correct) | EdDSA | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | Existential forgery (KOA) | ✅ trivial | ✅ trivial | ❌ hard | ❌ hard | ❌ hard | ❌ hard | | Blinding (CMA) | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ | mitigated | mitigated | mitigated | | Multiplicative (KMA) | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | | Nonce reuse (KMA) | N/A | N/A | ✅ total break | N/A | ✅ total break | ❌ (deterministic & safe) | | Bleichenbacher ($e=3$) | ✅ | N/A | N/A | mitigated | N/A | N/A | | Birthday / Collision | ✅ if weak hash | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ if weak hash | ✅ if weak hash | mitigated (SHA-512) | | Side-channel (timing) | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | partial | > **Legend:** ✅ = attack succeeds; ❌ = attack fails (scheme resists); mitigated = mostly patched. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Attack Model Taxonomy (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Digital Signature Attack] --> B[Attack Models] A --> C[Forgery Goals] B --> B1[KOA: Key-Only] B --> B2[KMA: Known Message] B --> B3[CMA: Chosen Message] B --> B4[ACMA: Adaptive CMA] C --> C1[Total Break
Recover sk] C --> C2[Universal Forgery
Any message] C --> C3[Selective Forgery
Target message] C --> C4[Existential Forgery
At least one] B1 --> D1[Textbook RSA Existential] B2 --> D2[Multiplicative RSA] B3 --> D3[RSA Blinding] B3 --> D4[Bleichenbacher e=3] B2 --> D5[DSA Nonce Reuse] D1 --> C4 D2 --> C3 D3 --> C2 D4 --> C2 D5 --> C1 ``` ## 4.2 Diagram 2 — DSA Nonce Reuse Attack Sequence ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Adv as Adversary participant Signer as Signing Oracle participant Verifier as Verifier Note over Signer: Uses same nonce k for m1 and m2 Adv->>Signer: Request signature on m1 Signer-->>Adv: Returns r, s1 Adv->>Signer: Request signature on m2 Signer-->>Adv: Returns r, s2 Note over Adv: Compute k from s1 - s2
Recover x = private key Adv->>Verifier: Submit forged (m3, sig3) Verifier-->>Adv: ACCEPT — total break achieved ``` ## 4.3 Diagram 3 — RSA Blinding Attack Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR M[Target Message m] --> P[Multiply by r^e mod n] P --> MB[Blinded Message m'] MB --> O[Signing Oracle] O --> SB[Signature on m': sigma'] SB --> U[Multiply by r^-1 mod n] U --> SF[Forged Signature sigma on m] SF --> V[Verifier] M --> V V --> R[ACCEPT] ``` ## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Existential Forgery on Textbook RSA ```mermaid flowchart LR S[Choose random s] --> C[Compute m = s^e mod n] C --> O[Output pair m comma s] O --> V[Verifier checks s^e equals m mod n] V --> R[ACCEPT — Existential Forgery] ``` ## 4.5 Diagram 5 — Hash Collision Birthday Attack ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Generate 2 power n/2 variants of benign doc C1] --> H1[Hash all C1 variants] B[Generate 2 power n/2 variants of malicious doc C2] --> H2[Hash all C2 variants] H1 --> M[Match: H of C1 prime equals H of C2 prime] H2 --> M M --> S[Submit C1 prime to signer] S --> SG[Get signature on C1 prime] SG --> F[Reuse signature for C2 prime] F --> V[Verifier ACCEPTS C2 prime as signed] ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`** Differentiate between existential forgery and universal forgery in the context of digital signatures. Which one represents a stronger adversarial goal? **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Existential forgery (1 mark):** The adversary produces *at least one* valid (message, signature) pair $(m^*, \sigma^*)$. The message $m^*$ can be random and semantically meaningless. - **Universal forgery (1 mark):** The adversary can produce a valid signature for *any* message of her choice — full control over message content. - **Comparison (1 mark):** Universal forgery is **strictly stronger** than existential forgery. A scheme that resists existential forgery (EUF-CMA) automatically resists universal forgery in the standard security model. Example: textbook RSA is existentially forgeable (KOA) but not universally forgeable without factoring $n$. --- ### **Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`** What is a Key-Only Attack (KOA) on a digital signature scheme? Why is it considered the weakest attack model? **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Definition (1 mark):** In a KOA, the adversary knows only the public verification key $pk$ of the signer; she has no signed messages, no oracle access, and no private information. - **Weakest model (1 mark):** It is the weakest because the attacker has the *least* information and the *least* capability. Any successful attack under KOA is devastating — it means the scheme is broken with public information alone. - **Example (1 mark):** Existential forgery of textbook RSA under KOA: choose any $s$, compute $m = s^e \bmod n$, output $(m, s)$. The verifier accepts with no private information used. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each) ### **Question A `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`** > **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the RSA blinding attack on digital signatures. Show the step-by-step mathematical derivation and discuss why it is a *chosen-message* attack. > > **(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the DSA nonce-reuse attack. Derive the formulas used to recover the per-message nonce $k$ and the private key $x$. Mention one real-world incident caused by this attack. --- #### **Model Solution — Part (a): RSA Blinding Attack** **Step 1 — Context (1 mark):** RSA signing is $m \mapsto m^d \bmod n$. If a signing oracle refuses to sign a suspicious $m^*$, the attacker cannot obtain $\sigma = (m^*)^d$ directly. She resorts to *blinding*. **Step 2 — Choose blinding factor (1 mark):** Select $r \in \mathbb{Z}_n^*$ uniformly at random, with $\gcd(r, n) = 1$. **Step 3 — Form blinded message (1 mark):** $$m' = m^* \cdot r^e \bmod n$$ **Step 4 — Request signature on $m'$ (1 mark):** $$\sigma' = (m')^d = (m^* \cdot r^e)^d = (m^*)^d \cdot r^{ed} = (m^*)^d \cdot r \bmod n$$ **Step 5 — Unblind (1 mark):** $$\sigma = \sigma' \cdot r^{-1} \bmod n = (m^*)^d \cdot r \cdot r^{-1} = (m^*)^d \bmod n$$ **Step 6 — Verify (1 mark):** $$\sigma^e = ((m^*)^d)^e = m^* \bmod n \;\;\checkmark$$ **Step 7 — Why CMA (1 mark):** The attack is **chosen-message** because the adversary *chooses* which message ($m'$) to submit to the oracle. She does not passively observe — she actively queries. Hence the attack requires adaptive access to a signing oracle. **Countermeasure (extra):** Randomised padding (RSA-PSS, OAEP) randomises the input each time, so the same logical message yields different $m'$ values; structural blinding becomes statistically undetectable, but the *blinding technique* can still be used to obtain *some* signature — the real defence is **verifying the unblinded result is the original $m$** the verifier expected. --- #### **Model Solution — Part (b): DSA Nonce-Reuse Attack** **Step 1 — DSA signing equations (1 mark):** $$s_i = k^{-1} \bigl( H(m_i) + x \cdot r \bigr) \bmod q, \quad r = (g^k \bmod p) \bmod q$$ Same $k$ implies same $r$ for both signatures. **Step 2 — Write two equations (1 mark):** $$s_1 = k^{-1} \bigl( H(m_1) + x r \bigr), \quad s_2 = k^{-1} \bigl( H(m_2) + x r \bigr)$$ **Step 3 — Subtract (1 mark):** $$s_1 - s_2 = k^{-1} \bigl( H(m_1) - H(m_2) \bigr) \bmod q$$ **Step 4 — Solve for $k$ (1 mark):** $$k = (H(m_1) - H(m_2)) \cdot (s_1 - s_2)^{-1} \bmod q$$ **Step 5 — Recover $x$ (1 mark):** From $s_1 = k^{-1}(H(m_1) + x r)$: $$x = (s_1 \cdot k - H(m_1)) \cdot r^{-1} \bmod q$$ **Step 6 — Real-world incident (1 mark):** **Sony PlayStation 3 (2010)** — Sony used a *static* ECDSA nonce for signing PS3 firmware. The fail0verflow team recovered Sony's ECDSA private key using nonce-reuse, enabling the signing of arbitrary homebrew code on PS3. Result: total break of the code-signing infrastructure. **Step 7 — Defence (1 mark):** Use a cryptographically secure random nonce per signature (RFC 6979 deterministic generation is preferred to avoid bad-RNG bugs). --- ### **Question B `[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper]`** > **(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss existential forgery on plain (textbook) RSA signatures. Show the explicit algorithm an adversary runs and explain why the forged message is essentially random. > > **(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the Birthday attack on hash-based signatures. Derive the complexity bound $O(2^{n/2})$ and explain how a collision leads to signature forgery. --- #### **Model Solution — Part (a): Existential Forgery on Textbook RSA** **Step 1 — Setup (1 mark):** Textbook RSA signature: $\sigma = m^d \bmod n$, verify: $\sigma^e \equiv m \pmod n$. No hash, no padding, no message redundancy. **Step 2 — Adversary's algorithm (2 marks):** ``` Forge-Existential-RSA(pk = (n, e)): 1. Choose s uniformly at random from Z_n* 2. Compute m = s^e mod n 3. Return (m, s) ``` **Step 3 — Verification check (1 mark):** $$s^e = s^e \equiv m \pmod n \;\;\checkmark$$ Hence $(m, s)$ is a valid signature pair. **Step 4 — Why $m$ is random (1 mark):** Since $x \mapsto x^e \bmod n$ is a permutation on $\mathbb{Z}_n^*$ (for $e$ coprime to $\phi(n)$), random $s$ produces uniformly random $m$. The attacker has *no semantic control* — $m$ is essentially "garbage". The forgery is *existential* (some pair works), not *selective* (works for a chosen target). **Step 5 — Why textbook RSA fails (1 mark):** Three structural flaws: - No hash before signing — algebraic structure preserved. - Multiplicatively homomorphic: $\text{Sign}(m_1 m_2) = \text{Sign}(m_1) \cdot \text{Sign}(m_2)$. - No redundancy in $m$ — verifier cannot reject "random" $m$. **Step 6 — Countermeasure (1 mark):** Sign the hash: $\sigma = H(m)^d \bmod n$ (or use full PSS). This destroys the algebraic structure — the attacker can no longer invert $x^e$ inside a useful message space. --- #### **Model Solution — Part (b): Birthday Attack on Hash-Based Signatures** **Step 1 — Setup (1 mark):** Signer produces $\sigma = H(m)^d \bmod n$ (or similar hash-then-sign). Forgery requires finding $m_1 \neq m_2$ with $H(m_1) = H(m_2)$ — a *collision*. **Step 2 — Birthday paradox (1 mark):** In a set of $N$ items with $n$ possible hash values, the probability of a collision exceeds $1/2$ when: $$N \approx 1.177 \sqrt{n}$$ For an $n$-bit hash, $n = 2^n$ possible values, hence: $$N \approx 1.177 \cdot 2^{n/2}$$ **Step 3 — Derivation (2 marks):** Probability that $N$ samples are all distinct: $$P(\text{no collision}) = \prod_{i=0}^{N-1} \left(1 - \frac{i}{2^n}\right) \approx e^{-N^2 / (2 \cdot 2^n)}$$ Set $P(\text{no collision}) = 1/2$: $$e^{-N^2 / (2 \cdot 2^n)} = 1/2 \;\Rightarrow\; N^2 = 2 \cdot 2^n \cdot \ln 2 \;\Rightarrow\; N \approx 1.177 \cdot 2^{n/2}$$ **Step 4 — Forgery procedure (1 mark):** 1. Generate $2^{n/2}$ benign documents $C_1^{(i)}$ and $2^{n/2}$ malicious documents $C_2^{(j)}$. 2. Hash both sets. By the birthday bound, a collision $H(C_1^{(i)}) = H(C_2^{(j)})$ exists. 3. Submit $C_1^{(i)}$ to signer. Get signature $\sigma$. 4. Replay $\sigma$ on $C_2^{(j)}$ — verifier accepts because $H$ matches. **Step 5 — Impact on weak hashes (1 mark):** MD5 ($n=128$) is broken — collisions found in seconds. SHA-1 ($n=160$) broken in 2017 (SHAttered, ~$2^{63}$ operations). SHA-256 gives $2^{128}$ security — practically infeasible. **Step 6 — Countermeasure (1 mark):** Use collision-resistant hashes (SHA-256, SHA-3), randomised signatures (PSS with salt), and reject any message whose hash is not in expected format. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark Deductions — Read Carefully:** > 1. **Mixing up forgery types:** Many students write "existential forgery" when they mean "universal forgery". Existential = *some* random message; universal = *any* message. Examiner deducts 1–2 marks. > 2. **Forgetting modular arithmetic in DSA:** When deriving $k$, the modular inverse $(s_1 - s_2)^{-1} \bmod q$ is often missed. Marks deducted for not mentioning $\bmod q$. > 3. **Confusing KMA with CMA:** Known-message = *passively* observed. Chosen-message = *actively* queried oracle. Examiners test this distinction. > 4. **No mention of padding:** In RSA, students say "RSA is broken" without specifying *textbook RSA*. Always qualify: textbook RSA, plain RSA, etc. > 5. **Forgetting the verification step:** In existential forgery of textbook RSA, students often write $m = s^e$ but skip showing the verification $s^e \equiv m$ — examiner deducts 1 mark. > 6. **Hash confusion:** Birthday attack gives $2^{n/2}$, not $2^n$. Repeatedly misquoting this number is a common loser. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ### **High-Density Revision Checklist** - ✅ A **digital signature attack** exploits a flaw in the *primitive*, *padding*, *RNG*, or *protocol logic*. - ✅ Four **attack models**: KOA, KMA, CMA, ACMA — increasing in adversary power. - ✅ Four **forgery goals**: Total break > Universal > Selective > Existential. - ✅ **EUF-CMA** is the gold standard — Existential UnForgeability under adaptive Chosen-Message Attack. - ✅ **Textbook RSA** is existentially forgeable under KOA: pick $s$, set $m = s^e \bmod n$. - ✅ **RSA blinding** attack: $m' = m \cdot r^e$, get $\sigma' = (m')^d$, unblind $\sigma = \sigma' r^{-1}$. - ✅ **RSA multiplicative property**: $\text{Sign}(m_1 m_2) = \text{Sign}(m_1) \cdot \text{Sign}(m_2)$ — existential forgery from KMA. - ✅ **DSA / ECDSA nonce reuse** yields total break via $k = \frac{H(m_1) - H(m_2)}{s_1 - s_2} \bmod q$. - ✅ **Sony PS3 (2010)** is the canonical real-world DSA/ECDSA nonce-reuse case. - ✅ **Plain ElGamal** signatures are existentially forgeable (no hash + homomorphic structure). - ✅ **Bleichenbacher's e=3 attack** exploits low public exponent on PKCS#1 v1.5 signatures. - ✅ **Birthday attack** on $n$-bit hash: complexity $1.177 \cdot 2^{n/2}$ — quadratic speedup over brute force. - ✅ **MD5 / SHA-1 broken** — modern systems require SHA-256 or SHA-3. - ✅ Defences: **PSS, OAEP, deterministic nonces (RFC 6979), EdDSA, SHA-256+**. - ✅ The verifier's check $\sigma^e \equiv m \pmod n$ must be performed exactly — examiners want to see this step. - ✅ ECDSA over secp256k1 powers **Bitcoin, Ethereum, TLS** — securing these = securing modern Internet. ### **Memory Aid — "KOA-CMA-FORGE" Mnemonic** - **K** — Key-Only - **O** — (no signing oracle) - **A** — (move to) **C**hosen **M**essage **A**ttack - **F** — **F**orgery levels: Existential, Selective, Universal, Total - **O** — **O**racle queries: bounded q-signature queries - **R** — **R**andomness bias / nonce reuse = killer vulnerability - **G** — **G**oldwasser–Micali–Rivest 1988 model - **E** — **E**UF-CMA is the minimum bar for modern security ### **Final Exam Tip** > [!IMPORTANT] > In KTU 14-mark questions, the examiner expects: (1) clear attack model identification, (2) explicit algebraic steps with modular arithmetic, (3) at least one named real-world example, (4) mention of the countermeasure. Hitting all four = full marks. ---

topics.title: Digital Signature Standard
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT523 - DATA PRIVACY AND SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Digital Signature Standard

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Digital Signature Standard (DSS)** is a Federal Information Processing Standard (FIPS 186) published by the **National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST)** in 1991. It specifies a suite of algorithms that can be used to generate **digital signatures** for cryptographic authentication, integrity, and non-repudiation of electronic documents. The core algorithm defined by DSS is the **Digital Signature Algorithm (DSA)**.

DSS provides the formal governmental and industrial benchmark for verifying that a digital message was genuinely created by a known sender (authentication) and was not altered in transit (integrity). The 2024 KTU syllabus specifically focuses on the classical **DSA** (a finite-field based discrete logarithm scheme), although modern extensions (RSA, ECDSA, EdDSA) are also discussed for comparative purposes.

### Key Terminology

| Term | Definition |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Digital Signature** | A mathematical scheme for verifying the authenticity of digital messages or documents. |
| **DSA** | Digital Signature Algorithm – the asymmetric key algorithm used inside DSS. |
| **Non-Repudiation** | The assurance that the signer cannot later deny having signed the document. |
| **Hash Function (SHA)** | A one-way function (e.g., SHA-1, SHA-256) that maps data of arbitrary size to a fixed-size bit string. |
| **Private Key ($x$)** | A secret, randomly generated integer known only to the signer. |
| **Public Key ($y$)** | A mathematically derived value shared publicly to verify signatures. |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition: The Sealed Wax Letter of the Digital Age**

Imagine you are sending a physical legal contract through a courier. To prove the contract is truly from you, you:
1. **Write the contract** in a special ink that leaves a unique invisible fingerprint pattern (this is the **Hash**).
2. **Press your unique personal wax seal** onto the contract using a special stamp locked in your personal safe (this uses your **Private Key $x$**).
3. Anyone who has a copy of your public wax seal imprint (the **Public Key $y$**) can hold the contract up and verify the wax mark is genuinely yours.

In the digital world:
- The **document** is the message $M$.
- The **unique fingerprint ink** is the cryptographic hash function $H(M)$ (e.g., SHA-256).
- The **wax seal press** is the DSA signing algorithm using the signer's secret private key.
- The **public seal imprint** is the globally published public key, used to mathematically verify the signature.

The seal proves two things simultaneously: **(1) You signed it** (authentication), and **(2) Nobody altered the contract after you sealed it** (integrity). Furthermore, because only your safe holds that stamp, **you cannot later deny signing it** (non-repudiation).

### Properties Guaranteed by DSS

> [!NOTE]
> 1. **Authentication** – Confirms the identity of the signer.
> 2. **Integrity** – Detects any modification of the signed data.
> 3. **Non-repudiation** – The signer cannot falsely deny their signature later.
> 4. **Unforgeability** – Computationally infeasible to forge a valid signature without the private key.

---

## 1.3 Why DSS Was Created?

Before DSS (pre-1991), **RSA** was the dominant digital signature scheme. However, NIST required a **standardized, government-vetted algorithm** with specific properties:
- No patent restrictions (RSA was patented by MIT until 2000).
- Use of **discrete logarithms** rather than integer factorization.
- Faster signature generation compared to RSA at equivalent security levels.
- Separation of signature generation from encryption operations.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Mathematical Foundation

DSS (via DSA) is built on the **Discrete Logarithm Problem (DLP)** over a finite field $\mathbb{Z}_p^*$. The security rests on the assumption that computing discrete logarithms modulo a large prime $p$ is computationally intractable.

### Core Mathematical Constructs

1. **Public Modulus $p$**: A large $L$-bit prime (originally $L = 512$ to $1024$; modern standards use $2048+$).
2. **Sub-prime $q$**: A $160$-bit prime divisor of $(p - 1)$.
3. **Generator $g$**: Computed as $g = h^{(p-1)/q} \bmod p$, where $h$ is any integer in $(1, p-1)$ such that $g > 1$.

The signing/verification operates within the **cyclic subgroup of order $q$** inside $\mathbb{Z}_p^*$.

---

## 2.2 Operational Concept (Three Algorithms)

The DSS framework defines three distinct procedural algorithms:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Algorithm 1 – Key Generation:** Produces the public/private key pair $(y, x)$.
> **Algorithm 2 – Signature Creation:** Uses private key $x$ and a per-message random $k$ to produce the signature pair $(r, s)$.
> **Algorithm 3 – Signature Verification:** Uses public key $y$ to mathematically validate that $(r, s)$ is a legitimate signature of the message $M$.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Operation | Formula | Description / Parameters |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Public Parameters | $p, q, g$ | $q \mid (p-1)$; $g = h^{(p-1)/q} \bmod p$ |
| 2 | Private Key | $x \in \{1, \ldots, q-1\}$ | Randomly generated secret integer |
| 3 | Public Key | $y = g^x \bmod p$ | Published for verification |
| 4 | Hash of Message | $H(M)$ | Typically SHA-1 (160 bits) or SHA-256 |
| 5 | Per-Message Random | $k \in \{1, \ldots, q-1\}$ | MUST be secret and unique per signature |
| 6 | First Signature Component | $r = (g^k \bmod p) \bmod q$ | Modular reduction by $q$ |
| 7 | Second Signature Component | $s = k^{-1}\bigl(H(M) + x \cdot r\bigr) \bmod q$ | Uses modular inverse $k^{-1}$ |
| 8 | Verification Compute | $w = s^{-1} \bmod q$ | Modular inverse of $s$ |
| 9 | Verification Compute | $u_1 = H(M) \cdot w \bmod q$ | Hash blended with inverse |
| 10 | Verification Compute | $u_2 = r \cdot w \bmod q$ | $r$ blended with inverse |
| 11 | Verification Check | $v = (g^{u_1} \cdot y^{u_2} \bmod p) \bmod q$ | Reconstructs the $r$-component |
| 12 | Signature Validity | $v = r$ | If equal, signature is valid |

> **Units & Bit-Lengths (KTU Standard Defaults):**
> - $p$ → 1024 bits (legacy), 2048 / 3072 bits (modern)
> - $q$ → 160 bits (legacy), 224 / 256 bits (modern)
> - $H(M)$ → matches the bit-length of $q$
> - $r, s$ → integers in $[1, q-1]$

> **Engineering Real-World Utility:** DSS signatures are used in **TLS/SSL certificates, PGP, smart cards, government-issued e-passports, software code signing, and blockchain transaction authorizations**. The DSA scheme is FIPS-compliant, making it mandatory in U.S. federal and many defense/military cryptographic deployments.

---

## 2.4 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why a subgroup of order $q$?** Working in the $q$-order subgroup keeps all signature components $(r, s)$ compact at 160 bits, regardless of how large $p$ is. This dramatically reduces signature size compared to raw $p$-based operations.
- **Why a fresh random $k$ per signature?** If the same $k$ is ever reused with the same $x$, an attacker can algebraically recover the private key $x$. This is the infamous **Sony PS3 hack (2010)**.
- **Why a hash function?** DSA is defined only for messages of size equal to $q$ (160 bits). Hashing compresses arbitrary-length messages into a fixed digest that fits this constraint, while also providing integrity.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Full DSA Key Generation — Exhaustive Walkthrough

**Input:** Global public parameters $p$, $q$, $g$.
**Output:** Public key $y$, private key $x$.

> **Step 1:** Generate a cryptographically random integer $x$ such that $0 < x < q$.
> *Logic:* The private key must be uniformly distributed inside the cyclic group of order $q$ to prevent brute-force enumeration.

> **Step 2:** Compute the public key as
> $$y = g^x \bmod p$$
> *Logic:* The discrete logarithm $x = \log_g y \pmod p$ is the **trapdoor** — easy to compute forward (exponentiation), but infeasible to invert. This asymmetry is the cryptographic foundation of DSA.

> **Step 3:** Publish $y$ alongside $(p, q, g)$. Keep $x$ secret.
> *Logic:* The public key $y$ allows anyone in the world to verify signatures. The private key $x$ is the sole authority to create them.

---

## 3.2 Full DSA Signature Creation — Exhaustive Walkthrough

**Input:** Message $M$, private key $x$, public parameters $p, q, g$.
**Output:** Signature pair $(r, s)$.

> **Step 1:** Compute the cryptographic hash of the message.
> Let $H(M)$ be the hash digest (e.g., 160-bit SHA-1 output, converted to an integer).
> *Logic:* Hashing provides integrity binding — the signature is over the fingerprint, not the full document.

> **Step 2:** Generate a fresh, secret per-message random integer $k$ such that $0 < k < q$.
> *Logic:* Randomness and uniqueness of $k$ is critical. Bias or reuse leaks the private key.

> **Step 3:** Compute the first signature component.
> $$r = (g^k \bmod p) \bmod q$$
> *Logic:* This is a reduced exponentiation. The outer $\bmod q$ maps the result into the $q$-order subgroup, keeping the signature compact.

> **Step 4:** Compute the modular inverse of $k$ modulo $q$.
> $$k^{-1} \bmod q$$
> *Logic:* The inverse exists because $k$ and $q$ are coprime ($q$ is prime and $k < q$).

> **Step 5:** Compute the second signature component.
> $$s = k^{-1} \bigl( H(M) + x \cdot r \bigr) \bmod q$$
> *Logic:* This binds the message hash $H(M)$, the private key $x$, and the first signature component $r$ into a single value. The signing equation must be solvable for $x$ only with knowledge of $k$, ensuring unforgeability.

> **Step 6:** Transmit the signature $(r, s)$ alongside the message $M$.
> *Logic:* The receiver requires both the message and the signature for verification.

---

## 3.3 Full DSA Signature Verification — Exhaustive Walkthrough

**Input:** Message $M$, signature $(r, s)$, public key $y$, public parameters $p, q, g$.
**Output:** Boolean — Valid or Invalid.

> **Step 1:** Verify the domain constraints.
> *Check:* $0 < r < q$ and $0 < s < q$.
> *Logic:* Discard obviously malformed signatures before doing expensive exponentiations.

> **Step 2:** Compute the modular inverse of $s$.
> $$w = s^{-1} \bmod q$$
> *Logic:* This isolates the $k$-equivalent term in the signing equation.

> **Step 3:** Compute the two verification helpers.
> $$u_1 = H(M) \cdot w \bmod q$$
> $$u_2 = r \cdot w \bmod q$$
> *Logic:* These split the verification into a "message part" and a "signature part."

> **Step 4:** Reconstruct the candidate $r$-value.
> $$v = \bigl( g^{u_1} \cdot y^{u_2} \bmod p \bigr) \bmod q$$
> *Logic:* This is the inverse of the signing equation. Substituting the algebra of signing yields $v = r$ for a legitimate signature.

> **Step 5:** Compare and conclude.
> *If* $v = r$, the signature is **VALID**. Otherwise, **REJECT**.

---

## 3.4 Mathematical Proof of Correctness (Why $v = r$ for a Valid Signature)

Starting from the signing equation:
$$s = k^{-1} \bigl( H(M) + x \cdot r \bigr) \bmod q$$

Multiplying both sides by $k$:
$$k \cdot s \equiv H(M) + x \cdot r \pmod q$$

Multiplying both sides by $w = s^{-1} \bmod q$:
$$k \equiv H(M) \cdot w + x \cdot r \cdot w \pmod q$$
$$k \equiv u_1 + x \cdot u_2 \pmod q$$

Exponentiating the generator $g$ base:
$$g^k \equiv g^{u_1} \cdot g^{x \cdot u_2} \equiv g^{u_1} \cdot (g^x)^{u_2} \equiv g^{u_1} \cdot y^{u_2} \pmod p$$

Reducing modulo $q$:
$$(g^k \bmod p) \bmod q \equiv \bigl( g^{u_1} \cdot y^{u_2} \bmod p \bigr) \bmod q$$

By definition of $r$ (left side) and $v$ (right side):
$$r \equiv v \pmod q$$

Since both $r$ and $v$ lie in $[1, q-1]$, equality is exact:
$$\boxed{r = v}$$

This algebraic identity is what makes the verification deterministic and provably correct.

---

## 3.5 Numerical Toy Example (For KTU Problem-Solving)

> **Given:** $p = 23$, $q = 11$, $g = 2$ (assume pre-validated).
> **Choose:** Private key $x = 5$.

> **Step A — Public Key:**
> $$y = g^x \bmod p = 2^5 \bmod 23 = 32 \bmod 23 = 9$$

> **Step B — Sign a Message:** Let $H(M) = 7$, $k = 3$.
> - $k^{-1} \bmod q = 3^{-1} \bmod 11 = 4$ (since $3 \times 4 = 12 \equiv 1 \pmod{11}$)
> - $r = (g^k \bmod p) \bmod q = (2^3 \bmod 23) \bmod 11 = (8 \bmod 23) \bmod 11 = 8$
> - $s = k^{-1}(H(M) + x \cdot r) \bmod q = 4 \cdot (7 + 5 \cdot 8) \bmod 11 = 4 \cdot 47 \bmod 11$
> - $4 \cdot 47 = 188$; $188 \bmod 11 = 188 - 17 \times 11 = 188 - 187 = 1$
> - **Signature:** $(r, s) = (8, 1)$

> **Step C — Verify:**
> - $w = s^{-1} \bmod q = 1^{-1} \bmod 11 = 1$
> - $u_1 = H(M) \cdot w \bmod q = 7 \cdot 1 \bmod 11 = 7$
> - $u_2 = r \cdot w \bmod q = 8 \cdot 1 \bmod 11 = 8$
> - $v = (g^{u_1} \cdot y^{u_2} \bmod p) \bmod q = (2^7 \cdot 9^8 \bmod 23) \bmod 11$
> - $2^7 = 128 \bmod 23 = 128 - 5 \times 23 = 128 - 115 = 13$
> - $9^8 \bmod 23$: $9^2 = 81 \bmod 23 = 12$; $9^4 = 12^2 = 144 \bmod 23 = 6$; $9^8 = 6^2 = 36 \bmod 23 = 13$
> - $13 \cdot 13 = 169 \bmod 23 = 169 - 7 \times 23 = 169 - 161 = 8$
> - $v = 8 \bmod 11 = 8$
> - **Check:** $v = r = 8$ ✅ **Signature Valid!**
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: DSS Key Generation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Global Params p, q, g] --> B[Generate Random x in 1 to q-1]
    B --> C[Compute y = g to the power x mod p]
    C --> D{Public Output: y}
    C --> E{Private Output: x}
    D --> F[End: Publish y]
    E --> G[End: Keep x Secret]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: DSS Signature Generation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: Message M] --> B[Compute Hash H of M]
    A1[Input: Private Key x] --> D
    B --> C[Generate Random k in 1 to q-1]
    C --> D[Compute r = g to k mod p then mod q]
    C --> E[Compute k inverse mod q]
    B --> F
    A1 --> F
    D --> F[Compute s = k inverse times HM plus x times r mod q]
    F --> G[Output Signature r, s]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: DSS Signature Verification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: M, r, s, y, p, q, g] --> B{Check 0 less than r less than q and 0 less than s less than q}
    B -->|Invalid| C[Reject Signature]
    B -->|Valid| D[Compute w = s inverse mod q]
    D --> E[Compute u1 = HM times w mod q]
    D --> F[Compute u2 = r times w mod q]
    E --> G[Compute v = g to u1 times y to u2 mod p mod q]
    F --> G
    G --> H{Compare v equals r?}
    H -->|Yes| I[Accept Signature Valid]
    H -->|No| C
```

## 4.4 End-to-End DSS Communication Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Alice as Signer (Alice)
    participant Hash as SHA Hash Function
    participant DSA_Sign as DSA Signing Engine
    participant Bob as Verifier (Bob)
    participant DSA_Verify as DSA Verification Engine

    Alice->>Hash: Submit Message M
    Hash-->>DSA_Sign: Hash Digest H of M
    Alice->>DSA_Sign: Provide Private Key x and Random k
    DSA_Sign-->>Alice: Signature Pair r and s
    Alice->>Bob: Transmit Message M and Signature r and s
    Bob->>DSA_Verify: Provide M, r, s, and Public Key y
    DSA_Verify->>Bob: Output Valid or Invalid
```

## 4.5 DSA vs RSA — Comparative Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[DSS DSA Scheme] --> B[Based on Discrete Logarithm]
    A --> C[Signature Faster than RSA]
    A --> D[Standardized by NIST FIPS 186]
    A --> E[Separate Signing and Encryption]
    F[Classical RSA Scheme] --> G[Based on Integer Factorization]
    F --> H[Verification Faster than DSA]
    F --> I[Patented until 2000]
    F --> J[Dual Use for Encryption and Signing]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question]**

**Q1. Define the Digital Signature Standard (DSS). List any three properties it guarantees.**

**Model Answer:**
The **Digital Signature Standard (DSS)** is a FIPS 186 standard published by the National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) that defines the **Digital Signature Algorithm (DSA)** for creating and verifying digital signatures. It is built on the **Discrete Logarithm Problem (DLP)**.

The three key properties guaranteed by DSS are:
1. **Authentication** – verifies the identity of the signer.
2. **Integrity** – detects any unauthorized modification of the signed message.
3. **Non-repudiation** – the signer cannot later deny the act of signing.

> *(Examiner note: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the three properties. Any valid property list accepted.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**Q2. Differentiate between DSA and RSA digital signature schemes (any four points).**

**Model Answer:**

| # | Parameter | DSA (DSS) | RSA |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Mathematical Basis | Discrete Logarithm Problem | Integer Factorization |
| 2 | Signature Generation | Faster | Slower |
| 3 | Signature Verification | Slower | Faster |
| 4 | Standardization | NIST FIPS 186 Standard | Industry-developed, not a U.S. federal standard |
| 5 | Key Usage | Restricted to signing | Can be used for both signing and encryption |
| 6 | Patent Issues | Free from the start | Patented (expired 2000) |

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question, Module 3]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Describe the Digital Signature Standard (DSS) in detail. Explain the role of the public parameters $p$, $q$, and $g$ with the mathematical conditions they must satisfy. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **DSA signature generation algorithm**. Clearly state the formula for computing $r$ and $s$ and explain why the per-message random $k$ must be unique. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a):

The **Digital Signature Standard (DSS)** was published by **NIST in 1991 as FIPS 186** to provide a federal standard for digital signatures. The core algorithm is the **Digital Signature Algorithm (DSA)**, which is based on the **Discrete Logarithm Problem (DLP)** in modular arithmetic.

**Public Parameters and Their Conditions:**

1. **Prime $p$**: A large $L$-bit prime number where $L$ is a multiple of 64, ranging from 512 to 1024 bits in the original standard (modern use mandates 2048 or 3072 bits for security).

2. **Prime $q$**: A 160-bit prime divisor of $(p - 1)$. That is, $q \mid (p - 1)$.
   - The $q$-order subgroup is used for all signature arithmetic.
   - The size of $q$ determines the security level against collision and brute force.

3. **Generator $g$**: A primitive element of the multiplicative subgroup of order $q$ in $\mathbb{Z}_p^*$. Computed as:
   $$g = h^{(p-1)/q} \bmod p$$
   where $h$ is any integer in $\{2, 3, \ldots, p-2\}$ such that $g > 1$. Reject and retry $h$ if $g = 1$.

These three parameters $(p, q, g)$ form the **global public domain parameters** shared by all users in the system. The security of DSA rests on the assumption that computing $\log_g y \pmod p$ is computationally infeasible for an attacker who knows $y$, $g$, and $p$ but does not know $x$.

**Properties of DSS:**
- Signatures are produced and verified using different computational paths, separating signing from encryption.
- The signature is a **pair of integers $(r, s)$** each of size 160 bits (matching $q$).
- The algorithm is **append-only** — the message is never decrypted, only authenticated.

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b):

The DSA signature generation process is as follows:

> **Block Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Message M] --> B[Hash Function SHA]
    B --> C[Hash Digest H of M]
    D[Private Key x] --> F
    E[Random k in 1 to q-1] --> G[Compute r equals g to k mod p mod q]
    E --> H[Compute k inverse mod q]
    C --> I[Compute s = k inverse times H of M plus x times r mod q]
    F --> I
    G --> J[Signature r, s]
    I --> J
```

**Step-by-Step Algorithm:**

1. Compute $H(M)$, the cryptographic hash of the message.
2. Choose a fresh, secret random integer $k$ with $0 < k < q$.
3. Compute:
   $$r = (g^k \bmod p) \bmod q$$
4. Compute the modular inverse $k^{-1} \bmod q$.
5. Compute:
   $$s = k^{-1} \bigl( H(M) + x \cdot r \bigr) \bmod q$$
6. Output the signature $(r, s)$.

**Why $k$ Must Be Unique (Critical Point):**

If two different messages are signed using the **same $k$ and the same $x$**, producing $(r, s_1)$ and $(r, s_2)$:
$$s_1 = k^{-1}(H(M_1) + x \cdot r) \bmod q$$
$$s_2 = k^{-1}(H(M_2) + x \cdot r) \bmod q$$

Subtracting:
$$s_1 - s_2 = k^{-1}(H(M_1) - H(M_2)) \bmod q$$
$$k = (H(M_1) - H(M_2)) \cdot (s_1 - s_2)^{-1} \bmod q$$

Once $k$ is recovered, $x$ can be trivially extracted:
$$x = (s \cdot k - H(M)) \cdot r^{-1} \bmod q$$

This is exactly what happened in the **Sony PS3 ECDSA code-signing breach (2010)**, where a static $k$ allowed attackers to extract Sony's private code-signing key. Hence, $k$ must be:
- **Truly random** (or generated via RFC 6979 deterministic derivation).
- **Unique per signature**.
- **Kept secret** and **destroyed after use**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Explain the **DSA signature verification algorithm** with a neat block diagram. Derive the mathematical relationship $v = r$ that proves signature validity. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A DSA system uses parameters $p = 59$, $q = 29$, $g = 3$, private key $x = 7$, and signs a message with $H(M) = 10$ using $k = 5$. Compute the public key, the signature $(r, s)$, and verify it. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a):

**Signature Verification Algorithm:**

1. Verify that $0 < r < q$ and $0 < s < q$. If not, reject.
2. Compute $w = s^{-1} \bmod q$.
3. Compute $u_1 = H(M) \cdot w \bmod q$.
4. Compute $u_2 = r \cdot w \bmod q$.
5. Compute $v = (g^{u_1} \cdot y^{u_2} \bmod p) \bmod q$.
6. Accept if and only if $v = r$.

> **Block Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Receive M, r, s, y] --> B{Domain Check}
    B -->|Fail| C[Reject]
    B -->|Pass| D[Compute w = s inverse mod q]
    D --> E[u1 = HM times w mod q]
    D --> F[u2 = r times w mod q]
    E --> G[v = g to u1 times y to u2 mod p mod q]
    F --> G
    G --> H{v equals r?}
    H -->|Yes| I[Accept]
    H -->|No| C
```

**Derivation of $v = r$:**

From the signing equation:
$$s \equiv k^{-1}(H(M) + x \cdot r) \pmod q$$

Multiplying by $k$:
$$k \cdot s \equiv H(M) + x \cdot r \pmod q$$

Multiplying by $w = s^{-1} \bmod q$:
$$k \equiv H(M) \cdot w + x \cdot r \cdot w \pmod q$$
$$k \equiv u_1 + x \cdot u_2 \pmod q$$

Exponentiating $g$ base modulo $p$:
$$g^k \equiv g^{u_1} \cdot (g^x)^{u_2} \equiv g^{u_1} \cdot y^{u_2} \pmod p$$

Reducing modulo $q$:
$$(g^k \bmod p) \bmod q \equiv (g^{u_1} \cdot y^{u_2} \bmod p) \bmod q$$

Left side is $r$ by definition. Right side is $v$. Hence $r \equiv v \pmod q$, and since both lie in $[1, q-1]$, we have $\boxed{r = v}$. Q.E.D.

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b):

**Step 1 — Public Key:**
$$y = g^x \bmod p = 3^7 \bmod 59$$
- $3^2 = 9$
- $3^4 = 81 \bmod 59 = 22$
- $3^7 = 3^4 \cdot 3^2 \cdot 3^1 = 22 \cdot 9 \cdot 3 = 594 \bmod 59$
- $594 = 10 \times 59 + 4$, so $y = 4$

**Step 2 — Compute $r$:**
$$r = (g^k \bmod p) \bmod q = (3^5 \bmod 59) \bmod 29$$
- $3^5 = 243 \bmod 59 = 243 - 4 \times 59 = 243 - 236 = 7$
- $r = 7 \bmod 29 = 7$

**Step 3 — Compute $s$:**
- $k^{-1} \bmod q = 5^{-1} \bmod 29$. We need $5 \cdot t \equiv 1 \pmod{29}$.
- $5 \cdot 6 = 30 \equiv 1 \pmod{29}$, so $k^{-1} = 6$.
- $s = k^{-1}(H(M) + x \cdot r) \bmod q = 6 \cdot (10 + 7 \cdot 7) \bmod 29$
- $6 \cdot (10 + 49) = 6 \cdot 59 = 354$
- $354 \bmod 29 = 354 - 12 \times 29 = 354 - 348 = 6$
- **Signature:** $(r, s) = (7, 6)$

**Step 4 — Verify:**
- $w = s^{-1} \bmod q = 6^{-1} \bmod 29$. Need $6t \equiv 1 \pmod{29}$.
- $6 \cdot 5 = 30 \equiv 1 \pmod{29}$, so $w = 5$.
- $u_1 = H(M) \cdot w \bmod q = 10 \cdot 5 \bmod 29 = 50 \bmod 29 = 21$
- $u_2 = r \cdot w \bmod q = 7 \cdot 5 \bmod 29 = 35 \bmod 29 = 6$
- $v = (g^{u_1} \cdot y^{u_2} \bmod p) \bmod q = (3^{21} \cdot 4^6 \bmod 59) \bmod 29$

Computing $3^{21} \bmod 59$ (using repeated squaring):
- $3^1 = 3$
- $3^2 = 9$
- $3^4 = 81 \bmod 59 = 22$
- $3^8 = 22^2 = 484 \bmod 59 = 484 - 8 \times 59 = 484 - 472 = 12$
- $3^{16} = 12^2 = 144 \bmod 59 = 144 - 2 \times 59 = 26$
- $3^{21} = 3^{16} \cdot 3^4 \cdot 3^1 = 26 \cdot 22 \cdot 3 = 1716 \bmod 59$
- $1716 = 29 \times 59 + 5$, so $3^{21} \bmod 59 = 5$

Computing $4^6 \bmod 59$:
- $4^2 = 16$
- $4^4 = 16^2 = 256 \bmod 59 = 256 - 4 \times 59 = 20$
- $4^6 = 4^4 \cdot 4^2 = 20 \cdot 16 = 320 \bmod 59 = 320 - 5 \times 59 = 25$

Multiplying: $5 \cdot 25 = 125 \bmod 59 = 125 - 2 \times 59 = 7$
$$v = 7 \bmod 29 = 7$$

**Check:** $v = 7 = r$ ✅ **Signature Verified as Valid!**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the outer $\bmod q$** in $r = (g^k \bmod p) \bmod q$. Many students write $r = g^k \bmod p$ and lose 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Confusing the order of operations in $s$** — the $k^{-1}$ multiplies the entire bracket $(H(M) + x \cdot r)$, not just $H(M)$.
> 3. **In numerical problems, students often compute $g^k$ or $y^x$ directly without modular reduction at intermediate steps**, leading to astronomically large numbers and computational errors.
> 4. **Domain check step is mandatory** — failing to verify $0 < r < q$ and $0 < s < q$ at the start costs 1 mark.
> 5. **Random $k$ reuse** is a heavily tested conceptual trap — always mention the Sony PS3 case study.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — DSS / DSA**

- ⭐ **DSS = FIPS 186 by NIST (1991)**. The default algorithm is **DSA**, based on the **Discrete Logarithm Problem**.
- ⭐ **Three public parameters:** $p$ (large prime), $q$ (prime divisor of $p-1$), $g = h^{(p-1)/q} \bmod p$.
- ⭐ **Private key $x$** is random in $[1, q-1]$. **Public key $y = g^x \bmod p$**.
- ⭐ **Signature pair:** $(r, s)$ where
  $$r = (g^k \bmod p) \bmod q, \quad s = k^{-1}(H(M) + x \cdot r) \bmod q$$
- ⭐ **Verification** reconstructs $v = (g^{u_1} y^{u_2} \bmod p) \bmod q$ and checks $v = r$.
- ⭐ **$k$ must be unique, random, and secret per signature** — reuse leaks the private key.
- ⭐ **DSA signs only; RSA can encrypt and sign.** DSA is **faster at signing**, RSA is **faster at verification**.
- ⭐ **Hash function** (SHA-1 / SHA-256) is mandatory — DSA does not operate on raw long messages.
- ⭐ **NIST deprecated original 1024-bit $p$ in 2013** — modern implementations use 2048/3072-bit $p$ with 224/256-bit $q$.
- ⭐ **Variants:** ECDSA (elliptic curve) and EdDSA (Ed25519) extend DSA concepts for stronger security per bit.
- ⭐ **Applications:** TLS/SSL certificates, software code signing, e-passports, government ID systems, blockchain wallets.
- ⭐ **For KTU numericals:** Always show modular reductions step-by-step; never skip intermediate $\bmod$ operations.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Hiding in Text: Basic Features
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT523 - DATA PRIVACY AND SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Data Hiding in Text: Basic Features

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Text Steganography** (also called **Text Data Hiding** or **Linguistic Steganography**) is the art and science of concealing a secret message within a plain-text cover carrier in such a manner that the existence of the hidden information is undetectable to an unauthorized observer. The output is a *stego-text* that appears, both visually and statistically, identical to ordinary written text.

Formally, the embedding process can be abstracted as a tuple of three algorithms:

$$\text{Text Stego} = (\mathcal{E}, \mathcal{S}, \mathcal{V})$$

where $\mathcal{E}$ is the **Embedding function**, $\mathcal{S}$ is the **Secret message (payload)**, and $\mathcal{V}$ is the **Verification/Extraction function** at the receiver end.

The three canonical inputs/outputs of a generic text-hiding system are:

- **Cover Text ($C$)** — the original, innocent-looking text in which data will be embedded.
- **Secret Message ($M$)** — the confidential information (bits, characters, or a file) to be hidden.
- **Stego-Text ($C'$)** — the modified cover text that carries $M$ invisibly.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Text is considered the *hardest* medium for steganography because it lacks the high redundancy found in images, audio, or video. Therefore, the "Basic Features" discussed in this module are the **fundamental design constraints** that every text-hiding scheme must satisfy.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Invisible Lemon Juice" Pen

Imagine you are a spy writing a letter in 1900. You have two layers of information to send:

1. A surface, innocuous story: *"The weather in Paris is lovely."*
2. A hidden, confidential instruction: *"Meet at the bridge at midnight."*

To achieve this, you write the visible story with a normal pen, but you selectively *dab* tiny dots of **lemon juice** between the letters at positions whose binary pattern encodes your secret. The letter is read normally by the postmaster, but the recipient, on heating the paper gently, sees the brown secret message emerge.

The text steganography in modern computing behaves **exactly the same way**:

- The **lemon juice** = the embedding algorithm (e.g., shifting a word by 1 mm, or replacing the word *"big"* with its synonym *"large"*).
- The **visible story** = the Cover Text.
- The **brown message after heating** = the extracted Secret Message.

The recipient, and **only** the recipient, knows the *key* (the heating temperature, the extraction dictionary, etc.) and can pull out the hidden payload.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (often asked in KTU exams):**
> - **Cryptography** scrambles a message so it cannot be *understood*.
> - **Steganography** hides a message so it cannot be *seen* to exist.
> - **Watermarking** embeds an identifying mark that is *resistant to removal* (used for copyright, not secrecy).

### 1.3 The Three Pillars of Text Hiding

Every text-hiding scheme is evaluated against **three basic features (pillars)**:

1. **Imperceptibility** — The stego-text must be visually, semantically, and statistically indistinguishable from the original cover text.
2. **Capacity (Payload)** — The number of secret bits that can be embedded per unit of cover text (e.g., bits per word, bits per sentence).
3. **Robustness** — The ability of the hidden data to survive normal text processing (re-typing, format conversion, OCR, noise addition).

A fourth, often-overlooked pillar is **Security**, which dictates that even if an adversary *suspects* a hidden message exists, they must not be able to extract it without the secret key.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Text Data Hiding Techniques

Text steganography techniques are broadly divided into two families based on **how** they exploit the text structure.

#### 2.1.1 Format-Based (Structure-Encoding) Techniques
These methods modify the *physical appearance* of the text without altering the linguistic content.

- **Line-Shift Coding:** Vertically shifts a single line of text up or down by a small amount (e.g., 1/300 of an inch) to encode a binary `1` or `0`. The position of the lines becomes the carrier of the secret.
- **Word-Shift Coding:** Horizontally shifts words within a line to encode bits. The inter-word distance pattern carries the payload.
- **Feature Coding (Syntactic):** Modifies typographical features such as font size, font type, character spacing, or vertical position of individual characters.
- **White-Space / Tab Coding:** Inserts extra spaces, tabs, or trailing whitespaces at the end of lines to encode data (very low capacity but easy to implement).

#### 2.1.2 Linguistic (Content-Encoding) Techniques
These methods modify the *words, syntax, or semantics* of the cover text itself.

- **Synonym Substitution:** Replaces selected words with their synonyms (e.g., *"big"* ↔ *"large"*). The synonym dictionary must be partitioned so that each substitution maps to a unique bit.
- **Semantic (Linguistic) Methods:** Uses ambiguity in natural language, such as *word-order* variation, *tense* shifts, or *active/passive voice* alternation, to embed bits.
- **Acrostic / Initial Letter:** The first letter of each line, word, or sentence spells out the secret message (a classic literary example is the acrostic poem).
- **Public-Key Steganography:** Embeds a payload using a cryptographic key known only to the intended recipient (analogous to public-key encryption but for hiding).
- **Abbreviation / Symbol Substitution:** Uses predefined symbols, abbreviations, or Unicode homoglyphs (characters that look identical but have different code points) to hide data.

### 2.2 Why Text is a "Hard" Medium

| Medium | Redundancy Source | Typical Capacity | Robustness |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Image | Pixel bits (LSB) | **High** (~1 bit/pixel) | Medium |
| Audio | Sample bits (LSB) | **High** (~1 bit/sample) | High |
| Video | Frame & temporal | **Very High** | High |
| **Text** | **Word order, format, synonyms** | **Low** (fractions of bpc) | **Low** |

> Text has very low information redundancy, which is why a single re-typing or font-change attack can destroy most format-based hidden payloads.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following equations are the standard metrics used to evaluate a text-hiding scheme and are highly likely to appear in numerical/descriptive parts of the KTU examination.

| Metric | Formula | Description | Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Embedding Capacity ($EC$)** | $EC = \sum_{i=1}^{n} c_i$ | Sum of the carrying capacity (in bits) of each $i$-th text feature. | bits |
| **Capacity Rate ($CR$)** | $CR = \dfrac{\text{Hidden bits}}{\text{Total cover characters}} \times 100\%$ | Fraction of cover used per character. | % |
| **Bits Per Word (BPW)** | $BPW = \dfrac{\text{Hidden bits}}{\text{Number of cover words}}$ | Average payload density per word. | bits/word |
| **Imperceptibility** | $\text{Sim}(C, C') \geq 1 - \epsilon$ | Similarity between cover $C$ and stego $C'$ must exceed a threshold $1-\epsilon$. | ratio |
| **Robustness Ratio ($RR$)** | $RR = \dfrac{\text{Recovered bits}}{\text{Original hidden bits}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage of payload that survives an attack. | % |
| **Hamming Distance** | $H(M, M') = \sum_{j} \mathbb{1}(m_j \neq m'_j)$ | Number of bit positions that differ between original message $M$ and recovered $M'$. | bits |
| **Entropy of Cover** | $H(C) = -\sum p(c_i) \log_2 p(c_i)$ | Information content of the cover text (used to measure steganographic safety). | bits/char |
| **Synonym Pool Size** | $k = \lfloor \log_2 \vert S_i \vert \rfloor$ | Number of embeddable bits per word with $\vert S_i \vert$ available synonyms. | bits |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** For synonym substitution, the key formula is $k = \log_2 N$ where $N$ is the number of synonym options for a target word. If a word has 4 synonyms, it can carry exactly 2 bits.

### 2.4 Engineering Utility

Text steganography is used in practice for:

- **Digital Watermarking of Documents** (e.g., tracing leaked PDFs back to the recipient).
- **Covert Communication** in journalism, whistleblowing, and military messaging.
- **Copyright Protection** of e-books and online articles.
- **Anti-Phishing and Forensics** to embed tamper-evident signatures inside legal contracts.
- **Anonymous Publishing** in restrictive regimes (analogous to the *dead-drop* metaphor).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section walks through the **three most important KTU derivations** for text data hiding: capacity computation for synonym substitution, embedding rate for word-shift coding, and a worked-out example of line-shift encoding.

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Maximum Embedding Capacity for Synonym Substitution

Consider a corpus $C$ with $W$ words. For each candidate word $w_i$ in the cover, the system consults a synonym dictionary and finds a set $S_i$ of size $\vert S_i \vert$ containing all semantically equivalent alternatives.

**Step 1 — Bits per word.** Each word $w_i$ can be replaced by any of the $\vert S_i \vert$ members of $S_i$. The number of distinct states is therefore $\vert S_i \vert$, which can encode

$$k_i = \log_2 \vert S_i \vert \text{ bits}$$

**Step 2 — Total capacity.** The total payload $EC$ across the whole cover is the sum of the per-word capacities:

$$EC = \sum_{i=1}^{W} k_i = \sum_{i=1}^{W} \log_2 \vert S_i \vert$$

**Step 3 — Practical limitation.** Synonym substitution must preserve grammatical correctness and semantics. Therefore, in practice, only words with $\vert S_i \vert \geq 2$ are usable, and only a fraction $f$ of words (typically 30%–50% of English content words) have usable synonyms:

$$EC_{\text{real}} = f \cdot \sum_{i \in \text{usable}} \log_2 \vert S_i \vert$$

**Step 4 — Capacity rate (%).** Dividing by the total number of characters $L$:

$$CR = \frac{EC_{\text{real}}}{L} \times 100\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical Example (KTU-typical):** A 1,000-word essay has 400 content words. Of these, 200 have synonym sets of size 4. The total capacity is $EC = 200 \times \log_2 4 = 200 \times 2 = 400$ bits $= 50$ bytes.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Embedding Rate for Word-Shift Coding

In word-shift coding, the secret bit is encoded in the *relative* horizontal displacement between consecutive words.

**Step 1 — Encode a bit.** For two adjacent words $w_i$ and $w_{i+1}$, define the displacement as:

$$\Delta_i = x_{i+1} - x_i - d_{\text{avg}}$$

where $d_{\text{avg}}$ is the average inter-word gap. The convention used is:

$$\Delta_i = \begin{cases} +\delta, & \text{if secret bit} = 1 \\ -\delta, & \text{if secret bit} = 0 \end{cases}$$

**Step 2 — Bit rate.** Every adjacent word pair encodes exactly **1 bit**. For a paragraph with $W$ words, there are $W - 1$ such pairs. So:

$$BPW_{\text{wordshift}} = \frac{W - 1}{W} \approx 1 \text{ bit/word}$$

**Step 3 — Total capacity in bits per line.** If a line has $L_w$ words, capacity per line is $L_w - 1$ bits.

**Step 4 — Robustness check.** The receiver computes $\hat{\Delta}_i$ and decodes. An attack that normalizes all inter-word gaps (e.g., full re-flow during PDF-to-Word conversion) destroys the payload:

$$RR_{\text{wordshift under re-flow}} \approx 0\%$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Line-Shift Encoding & Detection

**Step 1 — Setup.** A line whose vertical baseline is shifted *up* encodes bit `1`; shifted *down* encodes bit `0`. The shift magnitude is $\Delta y$ (e.g., 1/300 inch).

**Step 2 — Embedding rule.** For the $j$-th line in a paragraph:

$$y_j = y_{\text{base}} + (-1)^{m_j} \cdot \Delta y$$

where $m_j \in \{0, 1\}$ is the $j$-th bit of the secret message and $y_{\text{base}}$ is the standard line baseline.

**Step 3 — Capacity per page.** For a page with $N_L$ lines, one bit per line gives:

$$EC_{\text{line}} = N_L \text{ bits per page}$$

**Step 4 — Recovery formula.** The receiver measures the actual baseline $\hat{y}_j$ for each line. The decoded bit is:

$$\hat{m}_j = \begin{cases} 1, & \text{if } \hat{y}_j > y_{\text{base}} \\ 0, & \text{if } \hat{y}_j < y_{\text{base}} \end{cases}$$

**Step 5 — Error metric.** The detection error rate is given by:

$$P_e = \frac{1}{N_L} \sum_{j=1}^{N_L} \mathbb{1}(\hat{m}_j \neq m_j)$$

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

The table below is a high-yield analytical comparison frequently tested in KTU Module 3 viva and Part B questions.

| Feature | Line-Shift | Word-Shift | Synonym Substitution | White-Space | Acrostic |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Capacity** | 1 bit/line | ~1 bit/word | 1–2 bits/word | 1 bit/gap | 1 bit/word |
| **Imperceptibility** | High | High | Medium (semantic shift) | High | High |
| **Robustness** | Low | Very Low | Medium | Low | Very Low |
| **Re-typing resistance** | Poor | Poor | Good | Poor | Poor |
| **OCR resistance** | Poor | Poor | Good | Poor | Good |
| **Computational cost** | Low | Low | Medium | Low | Low |
| **Best Use Case** | Print documents | Formatted PDFs | General text | Source code | Poetry |

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Past-Paper Style)

**Problem:** A text-hiding scheme uses synonym substitution. The cover text has 500 words. 200 of the words have 4 synonyms each, and 100 of the words have 8 synonyms each. Calculate the total embedding capacity in bits and bytes.

**Solution (step-by-step):**

**Step 1:** Compute capacity for the first group (4 synonyms ⇒ 2 bits/word).

$$EC_1 = 200 \times \log_2 4 = 200 \times 2 = 400 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 2:** Compute capacity for the second group (8 synonyms ⇒ 3 bits/word).

$$EC_2 = 100 \times \log_2 8 = 100 \times 3 = 300 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 3:** Sum the capacities.

$$EC_{\text{total}} = EC_1 + EC_2 = 400 + 300 = 700 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 4:** Convert to bytes (1 byte = 8 bits).

$$EC_{\text{bytes}} = \frac{700}{8} = 87.5 \text{ bytes} \approx 87 \text{ bytes}$$

**Final Answer:** The scheme can hide **700 bits (≈ 87 bytes)** of secret data inside the 500-word cover text.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

- No reserved keywords (`end`, `graph`, `subgraph`) are used as node labels.
- No markdown formatting inside double-quoted labels.
