<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define text steganography. List any four basic features used to evaluate a text-hiding scheme.**

**Model Answer:**
Text steganography is the technique of concealing a secret message inside a plain-text cover in such a way that the existence of the message is undetectable. The four basic features used for evaluation are:
1. **Imperceptibility** — the stego-text looks identical to the cover.
2. **Capacity** — the number of hidden bits per unit of cover.
3. **Robustness** — the hidden message survives normal text processing.
4. **Security** — the hidden message is protected by a key against unauthorized extraction.

#### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between line-shift coding and word-shift coding in text steganography.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Line-Shift Coding | Word-Shift Coding |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Unit shifted | Entire horizontal line | Individual words |
| Encoding scheme | Bit `1` = shift up, Bit `0` = shift down | Bit `1` = increase gap, Bit `0` = decrease gap |
| Capacity | 1 bit per line | ~1 bit per word |
| Visibility | Slightly noticeable (vertical banding) | Usually invisible at normal zoom |
| Robustness | Low (re-typing destroys) | Very low (re-flow destroys) |

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

#### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the **basic features of data hiding in text**. Discuss the three pillars — imperceptibility, capacity, and robustness — with suitable examples.

**Model Solution:**

Data hiding in text refers to concealing secret information within a plain-text cover such that the original meaning, formatting, and statistical profile of the cover remain undisturbed. The basic features (or design requirements) of any text-hiding scheme are:

1. **Imperceptibility** — The stego-text must be visually, semantically, and statistically indistinguishable from the cover. *Example:* In synonym substitution, replacing *"big"* with *"large"* should not change the sentence meaning.

2. **Capacity (Payload)** — The number of secret bits embedded per unit of cover. *Example:* A synonym set of size 8 yields $k = \log_2 8 = 3$ bits per word.

3. **Robustness** — The hidden data must survive ordinary operations like re-typing, photocopying, OCR, or format conversion. *Example:* Synonym substitution is more robust to re-typing than line-shift coding.

4. **Security** — Even if an adversary suspects a hidden message, they cannot extract it without the secret key.

5. **Undetectability** — The stego-text must not exhibit anomalous patterns (e.g., unusual word-frequency distribution) that betray the presence of hidden data.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A text-hiding scheme uses **synonym substitution** on a 1,200-word essay. Statistics show that 300 words have exactly 4 synonyms, and 150 words have exactly 2 synonyms. Calculate (i) the embedding capacity in bits, and (ii) the capacity rate assuming an average word length of 5 characters.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute bits per group using $k = \log_2 \vert S_i \vert$.

- Group 1 (4 synonyms): $k_1 = \log_2 4 = 2$ bits
- Group 2 (2 synonyms): $k_2 = \log_2 2 = 1$ bit

**Step 2:** Compute per-group capacity.

$$EC_1 = 300 \times 2 = 600 \text{ bits}$$

$$EC_2 = 150 \times 1 = 150 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 3:** Total embedding capacity.

$$EC_{\text{total}} = 600 + 150 = 750 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 4:** Compute total cover characters. Average word length = 5 characters, plus 1 space per word. Total characters in essay:

$$L = 1200 \times (5 + 1) = 7200 \text{ characters}$$

**Step 5:** Compute capacity rate.

$$CR = \frac{750}{7200} \times 100\% = 10.42\%$$

**Final Answer:** $EC = 750$ bits; $CR \approx 10.42\%$.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With neat diagrams, describe the **classification of text data hiding techniques**. Compare format-based and linguistic-based methods.

**Model Solution:**

Text data hiding techniques are broadly classified into four families:

**1. Format-Based Methods** — Modify the *appearance* of text without altering words.
- Line-shift coding, word-shift coding, feature coding, white-space/tab encoding.
- High imperceptibility, low robustness.

**2. Linguistic-Based Methods** — Modify the *content* (words/syntax) of text.
- Synonym substitution, semantic coding, acrostic methods.
- Medium imperceptibility, higher robustness to re-typing.

**3. Random and Statistical Generation Methods** — Generate cover text on the fly that statistically encodes the message.
- Sentence length encoding, paragraph indexing.

**4. Public-Key and Hybrid Methods** — Combine cryptography with steganography.

**Comparison:**

| Aspect | Format-Based | Linguistic-Based |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Modifies | Format, spacing | Words, semantics |
| Capacity | Low–Medium | Medium–High |
| Robustness | Low | Medium |
| Imperceptibility | High | Medium |
| Example | Line-shift | Synonym substitution |

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain **line-shift coding** with the help of an embedding formula. Discuss its advantages and limitations.

**Model Solution:**

**Concept:** In line-shift coding, the *vertical baseline* of a chosen line is shifted up or down by a small amount $\Delta y$ to encode a bit.

**Embedding Rule:** For the $j$-th line in a paragraph,

$$y_j = y_{\text{base}} + (-1)^{m_j} \cdot \Delta y$$

where $m_j \in \{0, 1\}$ is the $j$-th bit of the secret message, $y_{\text{base}}$ is the standard baseline, and $\Delta y$ is a small offset (e.g., 1/300 inch).

**Recovery Rule:** The receiver measures $\hat{y}_j$ and decodes:

$$\hat{m}_j = \begin{cases} 1, & \text{if } \hat{y}_j > y_{\text{base}} \\ 0, & \text{if } \hat{y}_j < y_{\text{base}} \end{cases}$$

**Advantages:**
1. Imperceptible to the naked eye at normal viewing distance.
2. Easy to implement algorithmically.
3. One bit per line gives modest but usable capacity.

**Limitations:**
1. **Low robustness** — photocopying, scanning, and OCR destroy the offset.
2. Re-typing or re-rendering flattens all baselines.
3. Easily detected by automated steganalysis tools.
4. Capacity is limited to one bit per line, which is too low for large payloads.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students **frequently lose 2 marks** by writing *"line-shift coding encodes characters"* instead of *"encodes bits"*. Always state that **one line encodes one bit**.
> - Another common error is **forgetting to state the direction convention** (i.e., "shift up = 1, shift down = 0"). Without the convention, the receiver cannot extract.
> - In numerical questions, students often **omit units** in the final answer (e.g., write 750 instead of 750 bits). Always specify bits/bytes.
> - Do not confuse **imperceptibility** with **robustness**. They are *opposing* features — increasing one usually decreases the other (a classic KTU conceptual trap).

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Text Steganography** = hiding data inside plain text; output is *stego-text*.
- The **3 pillars** of evaluation are **Imperceptibility, Capacity, and Robustness**, plus an optional 4th: **Security**.
- **Format-based methods** (line-shift, word-shift, feature, white-space) modify *appearance*; they have high imperceptibility but very low robustness.
- **Linguistic methods** (synonym substitution, acrostic) modify *content*; they survive re-typing better.
- **Synonym capacity formula:** $k = \log_2 \vert S_i \vert$ bits per word. With 4 synonyms ⇒ 2 bits; with 8 synonyms ⇒ 3 bits.
- **Line-shift** encodes **1 bit per line**; **Word-shift** encodes **~1 bit per word**.
- **Capacity Rate (CR)** is always expressed as a *percentage* = (hidden bits / total cover characters) × 100%.
- **Imperceptibility vs. Robustness are inversely related** — a frequent conceptual exam trap.
- **Text has the lowest redundancy** of any stego medium, so it is the hardest to embed into invisibly.
- Acrostic / initial-letter methods are classic *linguistic* techniques and frequently asked as 3-mark definitions.
- Remember the **3 core differences** between Cryptography, Steganography, and Watermarking — these are almost guaranteed Part A questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applications of Data Hiding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT523 - DATA PRIVACY AND SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Applications of Data Hiding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Data Hiding** is the art and science of concealing the existence of a message within a larger, innocuous carrier medium such that the embedded information remains perceptually and statistically undetectable to unauthorized observers. In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PECDT523)**, data hiding is formally classified as a sub-discipline of **Information Security** that operates beyond traditional cryptographic boundaries — it protects not only the *content* of the data, but the *fact that communication is occurring* itself.

The three primary pillars of any data-hiding system, as defined by the KTU 2024 syllabus, are:

| Pillar | Description |
|---|---|
| **Capacity** | The maximum number of bits that can be embedded per unit of the cover medium |
| **Robustness** | The ability of the hidden data to survive intentional/unintentional signal processing (compression, noise, attacks) |
| **Imperceptibility** | The perceptual transparency of the embedding process to human senses |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim Syllabus Mapping):** Data hiding is the process of embedding secret information into a digital carrier (image, audio, video, text, or network protocol) such that the carrier appears unaltered to unintended recipients.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a spy in a foreign country, and you need to send a top-secret message back to headquarters. Two strategies are available:

1. **Cryptography (Encoding the message):** You write the message in a complex code. Even if an enemy intercepts the letter, they cannot *read* it — but they can clearly *see* that a coded message exists. The message is **protected but visible**.

2. **Data Hiding (Concealing the message):** You take a perfectly normal birthday card, and using invisible ink, write your secret message between the printed lines. The enemy intercepts the card, sees a harmless birthday greeting, and forwards it without suspicion. The message is **both protected and invisible**.

> [!NOTE]
> The "birthday card with invisible ink" analogy is the simplest mental model of **Steganography**, the most famous branch of data hiding. The card is the *cover*, the invisible ink is the *embedding algorithm*, and the recovered message is the *payload*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Crucial Distinction:** Cryptography protects *content*; Data Hiding protects *existence*. Modern systems often combine both: encrypt the payload first (confidentiality), then hide it (stealth).

### 1.3 Classification of Data Hiding Applications (KTU 2024 Taxonomy)

According to the **PECDT523 Module 3 syllabus**, applications of data hiding are broadly classified into the following categories:

- **Steganography** — Covert communication
- **Digital Watermarking** — Copyright protection and ownership assertion
- **Fingerprinting** — Traitor tracing
- **Authentication & Tamper Detection** — Integrity verification
- **Digital Rights Management (DRM)** — Access control
- **Forensic & Covert Channel Applications** — Law enforcement and network security

### 1.4 Standard Metrics (Bolded per Protocol)

The three universally accepted performance metrics for any data-hiding application are:

- **Capacity (C):** Measured in **bits per pixel (bpp)** for images, **bits per second (bps)** for audio/video, or **bits per packet** for network protocols.
- **Robustness (R):** Measured as the **Bit Error Rate (BER)** or **Probability of Detection Failure** after attack.
- **Imperceptibility (I):** Measured using the **Peak Signal-to-Noise Ratio (PSNR)** in decibels (**dB**), with a target threshold of **≥ 38 dB** for acceptable perceptual quality.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Data Hiding Trade-off Theorem:** Capacity, Robustness, and Imperceptibility form an **irreducible triangle** — improving any one inevitably degrades at least one of the other two. KTU board questions frequently test this fundamental constraint.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown of Data Hiding

A generic data-hiding system consists of three functional blocks:

- **The Embedding Function E(·):** Takes a cover object **C**, a secret message **M**, and a key **K** as input, and produces a stego-object **S**.

$$S = E(C, M, K)$$

- **The Extraction/Detection Function D(·):** Takes the stego-object **S** (and optionally the cover **C** and key **K**) and recovers the message **M'**.

$$M' = D(S, C, K) \quad \text{or} \quad M' = D(S, K)$$

- **The Cover Distortion Metric Δ(·):** Quantifies the perceptual difference between the original cover and the stego-object.

$$\Delta(C, S) \to 0 \quad \text{(Imperceptibility requirement)}$$

### 2.2 Application 1 — Steganography (Covert Communication)

**Steganography** (from Greek *steganos* = "covered" + *graphein* = "writing") is the practice of hiding information inside a cover medium so that an observer is unaware of the hidden content.

**Operational Steps:**
- Step 1: The sender selects a **cover object** (e.g., a digital photograph).
- Step 2: The **secret message** is often encrypted (typically using **AES-256**) for double-layered security.
- Step 3: An **embedding algorithm** modifies the least significant bits (LSB) of the cover pixels using the encrypted payload.
- Step 4: The resulting **stego-object** is transmitted over a public channel (e.g., email, social media).
- Step 5: The receiver applies the **extraction algorithm** with the shared key to recover the message.

**Real-world utility:** Used by **journalists** in oppressive regimes, **whistleblowers**, **military intelligence agencies**, and in **digital forensics** for evidence concealment.

### 2.3 Application 2 — Digital Watermarking

**Digital Watermarking** is the process of embedding an identifying pattern (the watermark) directly into a digital signal. Unlike steganography, the *existence* of the watermark is often publicly known — its purpose is to assert ownership, not secrecy.

**KTU 2024 Sub-classification of Watermarks:**

| Type | Description | Typical Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Visible Watermark** | Opaque, semi-transparent logo overlaid on content | TV channel logos, stock photo previews |
| **Invisible Watermark** | Perceptually transparent, machine-detectable | Copyright assertion, broadcast monitoring |
| **Robust Watermark** | Survives compression, cropping, filtering | Copyright protection |
| **Fragile Watermark** | Breaks under any modification | Tamper detection, integrity verification |
| **Semi-fragile Watermark** | Survives mild processing (e.g., compression) but breaks under malicious edits | Authentication |
| **Public Watermark** | Detectable without secret key | Broadcast monitoring |
| **Private Watermark** | Requires secret key for detection | Copyright protection |

**Operational Logic of a Robust Watermarking System:**
- **Embedding Phase:** The watermark **W** is spread-spectrum modulated using a pseudo-random noise sequence **PN** keyed by a secret key **K**, and then added to the host image **I** in a transform domain (DCT, DWT, or DFT). The watermarked image **I_w** is given by:

$$I_w = I + \alpha \cdot (W \otimes PN)$$

where $\alpha$ is the **embedding strength factor** and $\otimes$ denotes the spreading operation.

- **Extraction Phase:** The correlation between the suspect image and the PN sequence is computed; if it exceeds a threshold, the watermark is declared present.

### 2.4 Application 3 — Fingerprinting (Traitor Tracing)

Fingerprinting embeds a **unique identifier** into each copy of a digital asset distributed to a specific recipient. If the asset is leaked, the embedded fingerprint uniquely identifies the traitor.

**Why it differs from Watermarking:** Watermarks assert *who owns* the content; fingerprints assert *who leaked* the content. The famous **Craigslist reverse-fingerprinting case (2007)** demonstrated this in court.

### 2.5 Application 4 — Content Authentication & Tamper Detection

A **fragile watermark** is embedded into a document. Any modification (even a single bit flip) destroys the watermark, alerting the receiver that the document has been tampered with. Applications include:

- **Medical imaging** (ensuring MRI/CT scans are not maliciously altered)
- **Legal documents** (court evidence integrity)
- **Financial records** (audit trails)

### 2.6 Application 5 — Digital Rights Management (DRM)

DRM systems embed license information and access-control metadata directly into the media file. Streaming platforms like **Netflix, Spotify, and Disney+** use DRM to:
- Restrict copying
- Enforce view-count limits
- Tie playback to authorized devices

### 2.7 Application 6 — Network/Protocol Steganography (Covert Channels)

Hiding information within network protocol headers (e.g., unused bits in TCP/IP headers, timing of packets, IP identification fields) to establish **covert channels** that bypass firewalls. This is extensively studied in both offensive (red team) and defensive (blue team) cybersecurity.

### 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are directly testable in the KTU 2024 End Semester Examination (ESE) for Module 3.

| Formula / Metric | LaTeX Expression | Application Domain | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Peak Signal-to-Noise Ratio | $\text{PSNR} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{MAX_I^{2}}{MSE}\right)$ | Imperceptibility of images | dB |
| Mean Squared Error | $MSE = \frac{1}{mn}\sum_{i=0}^{m-1}\sum_{j=0}^{n-1}[I(i,j)-K(i,j)]^{2}$ | Distortion measurement | — |
| Embedding Capacity | $C = N_{pixels} \times b_{LSB}$ | LSB steganography | bits |
| Embedding Equation | $S = E(C, M, K)$ | Generic embedding | — |
| Robust Extraction | $M' = D(S, K)$ | Watermark detection | — |
| Stego Capacity Bound | $C_{max} \leq H(C) - H(S) + \epsilon$ | Information-theoretic limit | bits |
| Bit Error Rate | $BER = \frac{\#\text{bit errors}}{\#\text{total bits}}$ | Robustness metric | ratio |
| Correlation Detector | $\rho = \frac{\langle S, PN \rangle}{\vert\vert S \vert\vert \cdot \vert\vert PN \vert\vert}$ | Watermark detection | — |
| LSB Embedding Rule | $I'(i,j) = 2\!\left\lfloor \frac{I(i,j)}{2}\right\rfloor + b_k$ | Image steganography | — |
| Spread-Spectrum Watermark | $I_w = I + \alpha \cdot W \cdot PN$ | Robust watermarking | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** These equations underpin commercial products ranging from Adobe Photoshop's content credentials, to cinema anti-piracy systems, to medical PACS image integrity, to military COMINT (Communications Intelligence) systems. KTU 2024 examiners reward students who can map formulas to real products.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: PSNR for Image Steganography (Exhaustive)

**Objective:** Derive the closed-form expression for the **Peak Signal-to-Noise Ratio (PSNR)** used to quantify the imperceptibility of a stego-image relative to the original cover image.

**Step 1 — Define the Cover and Stego Images.**
Let $I(i, j)$ denote the pixel intensity of the original cover image at spatial coordinate $(i, j)$, and let $I'(i, j)$ denote the corresponding pixel intensity in the stego-image. For a grayscale image of dimensions $m \times n$, the indices run as $0 \leq i \leq m-1$ and $0 \leq j \leq n-1$.

**Step 2 — Define the Mean Squared Error (MSE).**
The MSE is the average of the squared pixel-wise differences between the cover and the stego-image:

$$\text{MSE} = \frac{1}{m \, n} \sum_{i=0}^{m-1} \sum_{j=0}^{n-1} \left[ I(i, j) - I'(i, j) \right]^{2}$$

*Conversion logic:* Each term $\left[I(i, j) - I'(i, j)\right]^2$ is the squared intensity difference at a single pixel; summing over all $m \times n$ pixels gives the total squared error; dividing by $m \cdot n$ normalizes by the total number of pixels, yielding the average.

**Step 3 — Define the Maximum Possible Pixel Value $MAX_I$.**
For an 8-bit grayscale image, pixel intensities range from $0$ to $255$. Therefore:

$$MAX_I = 2^{b} - 1 = 2^{8} - 1 = 255$$

*Conversion logic:* An $b$-bit channel can represent $2^b$ discrete values, indexed $0$ to $2^b - 1$.

**Step 4 — Construct the Signal-to-Noise Ratio.**
The signal power is $MAX_I^{2}$ (the maximum representable intensity squared). The noise power is the MSE. The ratio of signal to noise power is:

$$\text{SNR} = \frac{MAX_I^{2}}{MSE}$$

*Conversion logic:* In communication theory, SNR is the ratio of meaningful signal power to noise power; here, the "signal" is the maximum possible image content, and the "noise" is the embedding distortion.

**Step 5 — Convert to Decibel Scale.**
Since the human perception of distortion is logarithmic, we apply the decibel transformation $10 \log_{10}(\cdot)$:

$$\text{PSNR} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{MAX_I^{2}}{MSE}\right)$$

Substituting the value of $MAX_I$ for an 8-bit image:

$$\text{PSNR} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{255^{2}}{MSE}\right) = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{255}{\sqrt{MSE}}\right)$$

*Conversion logic:* Using the identity $10 \log_{10}(x^2) = 20 \log_{10}(x)$, we obtain the commonly cited form.

**Step 6 — Interpretation for KTU Board Examinations.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A PSNR value of **≥ 40 dB** indicates that the stego-image is perceptually indistinguishable from the cover. A PSNR value **below 30 dB** is considered visually degraded. KTU examiners will accept the formula in **both** forms during valuation.

### 3.2 Worked Example: LSB Steganography Capacity Calculation

**Problem:** A $1024 \times 1024$ grayscale image is used as a cover. We embed 1 bit per pixel using LSB substitution. Calculate the total payload capacity.

**Step 1:** Compute the total number of pixels.

$$N_{pixels} = 1024 \times 1024 = 1{,}048{,}576 \text{ pixels}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the capacity formula.

$$C = N_{pixels} \times b_{LSB} = 1{,}048{,}576 \times 1 \text{ bit} = 1{,}048{,}576 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 3:** Convert to more intuitive units.

$$C = \frac{1{,}048{,}576}{8} = 131{,}072 \text{ bytes} = 128 \text{ KB}$$

*Conversion logic:* 1 byte = 8 bits; hence division by 8 converts bits to bytes. Further division by 1024 converts bytes to kilobytes.

**Answer:** The image can conceal **1,048,576 bits (128 KB)** of secret data using 1-bit LSB substitution.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Steganography vs. Watermarking vs. Cryptography

> [!NOTE]
> This comparison table is a **high-weightage KTU question**; expect it as either a Part A (3-mark) or as a sub-part of a Part B (14-mark) question.

| Property | Steganography | Watermarking | Cryptography |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Primary Goal** | Hide the *existence* of the message | Assert ownership / integrity | Protect *content* of the message |
| **Carrier** | Digital media (image/audio/video) | Digital media (image/audio/video) | Plaintext / cipher text |
| **Visibility of Carrier** | Modified but appears unchanged | Modified (visible or invisible) | Plaintext is transformed to cipher |
| **Output** | Stego-object | Watermarked object | Cipher text |
| **Robustness Required** | Low (often fragile) | High (robust to attacks) | N/A (key-based) |
| **Key Required** | Sometimes (optional) | Usually yes | Always yes |
| **Adversary Awareness** | Unaware of hidden data | Aware of potential watermark | Aware of encrypted data |
| **Typical Attack** | Steganalysis | Watermark removal / geometric attack | Cryptanalysis |
| **Failure Indicator** | Detection of hidden data | Removal of watermark | Decryption of cipher text |
| **Example Use** | Whistleblower communication | Copyright on stock photos | HTTPS / TLS encryption |

### 3.4 Capacity-Robustness-Imperceptibility Trade-off (Derivation Sketch)

Let the cover object have $N$ elementary units (pixels, samples, or symbols). The maximum possible capacity is trivially $N$ bits (replace each unit with the message bit). However, this would destroy the cover completely — the imperceptibility would be zero.

Conversely, the most imperceptible scheme embeds 1 bit across the entire cover (e.g., spreading one bit via spread-spectrum), giving imperceptibility ≈ 1, but capacity = $1/N$ bits per unit.

The trade-off is formalized as:

$$C \times R \times I \leq K$$

where $K$ is a constant determined by the cover medium, and increasing any one factor proportionally reduces the others. KTU board questions on this topic typically ask students to explain the trade-off qualitatively with a real example.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper, Module 3]**
> **Define data hiding. List any two major applications of data hiding in modern information security systems.**

**Model Answer:**

Data hiding is the process of embedding secret information into a digital carrier (such as an image, audio, video, text document, or network protocol) such that the carrier appears unaltered to unintended recipients. Unlike cryptography, which scrambles the message content, data hiding conceals the very existence of the communication.

**Two major applications:**
- **Digital Watermarking** — Used for copyright protection of multimedia content; e.g., embedding an invisible owner ID in stock photographs.
- **Steganography** — Used for covert communication by journalists, activists, and military intelligence to exchange information without arousing suspicion.

*Examiner note:* 1 mark for definition, 1 mark per application. Award full marks even if student writes "steganography" and "watermarking" without elaboration.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Expected Pattern, Module 3]**
> **Differentiate between steganography and cryptography. Why are they often used together in modern security systems?**

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Steganography | Cryptography |
|---|---|---|
| Objective | Hides the *existence* of the message | Hides the *content* of the message |
| Output | Stego-object (looks like a normal file) | Cipher text (visibly scrambled) |
| Adversary Awareness | Unaware of hidden data | Aware that encryption is in use |
| Typical Algorithm | LSB substitution, DCT, DWT | AES, RSA, ECC |

**Why used together:** Modern systems (e.g., Signal Protocol, WhatsApp E2E encryption) use **hybrid security**: the message is first **encrypted** (confidentiality) and then **hidden** in a carrier (stealth). This double-layering ensures that even if steganalysis detects the carrier modification, the attacker still cannot read the message without the cryptographic key.

*Examiner note:* 1 mark for difference, 1 mark for hybrid justification, 1 mark for example. Maximum credit for clearly distinguishing "content vs existence."

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> **Q3. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Module 3 — Internal Choice Pattern]**
>
> **(A) — Watermarking and Steganography in Depth**
>
> **Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Explain the **three fundamental properties** of any data-hiding system. Discuss the **trade-off triangle** between them with one real-world example for each property.
>
> **Part (b) [7 Marks]:** With a neat block diagram, describe the **spread-spectrum watermarking technique**. Write the embedding and detection (correlation-based) equations.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The three fundamental properties of a data-hiding system are:

1. **Capacity (C):** The maximum number of secret bits that can be embedded per unit of the cover medium. *Real-world example:* LSB steganography in a $1024 \times 1024$ image achieves a capacity of **1,048,576 bits (128 KB)** when 1 bit per pixel is used.

2. **Robustness (R):** The ability of the embedded data to survive signal-processing operations and malicious attacks such as compression (JPEG), noise addition, cropping, or filtering. *Real-world example:* A robust watermark on a movie trailer must survive recompression by YouTube's H.264 encoder without losing the embedded copyright ID.

3. **Imperceptibility (I):** The perceptual transparency of the embedding process. Measured quantitatively by **PSNR** (target: **≥ 40 dB**). *Real-world example:* An invisible watermark on a luxury brand's product photograph must not alter the visual quality of the image to the naked eye.

**The Trade-off Triangle:** These three properties are mutually constrained. Increasing capacity (more bits per pixel) inevitably lowers robustness (less redundancy) and imperceptibility (more visible distortion). For example, embedding 4 bits per pixel via LSB gives 4× the capacity of 1-bit LSB, but the PSNR drops below the 30 dB acceptability threshold, making the modification statistically detectable by steganalysis tools. No data-hiding system can simultaneously maximize all three properties; the designer must choose the application-appropriate balance.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Spread-Spectrum Watermarking** embeds a pseudo-random noise sequence (the watermark) into the host signal in a transform domain, providing robustness against common signal-processing attacks.

**Embedding Equation:**

$$I_w(i, j) = I(i, j) + \alpha \cdot W(i, j) \cdot PN(i, j)$$

where $I$ is the host image, $I_w$ is the watermarked image, $W$ is the watermark pattern, $PN$ is a pseudo-random sequence generated from a secret key $K$, and $\alpha$ is the embedding strength factor.

**Detection (Correlation-Based):**

$$\rho = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i,j} I_s(i, j) \cdot PN(i, j)$$

where $I_s$ is the suspect image. The decision rule is:

- If $\rho \geq T$ (threshold), the watermark is declared **PRESENT**.
- If $\rho < T$, the watermark is declared **ABSENT**.

The block diagram (refer Section 4.3 of these notes) shows the full pipeline: host image + watermark + PN sequence → spreading multiplier → additive combiner → watermarked image. At the receiver, the suspect image is correlated with the same PN sequence, and the result is compared against a threshold.

*Examiner note:* 3 marks for the three properties with examples, 2 marks for trade-off explanation, 2 marks for diagram. In part (b), 3 marks for embedding equation with all variables defined, 2 marks for detection equation, 2 marks for threshold decision rule.

---

> **(B) — Alternative Choice for Q3**
>
> **Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Discuss the **different types of digital watermarks** with neat classification. Compare **visible and invisible** watermarks in terms of application and robustness.
>
> **Part (b) [7 Marks]:** Explain the concept of **fingerprinting** as a data-hiding application. How does it differ from watermarking? Describe a real-world case where fingerprinting was used to identify a content leaker.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Types of Digital Watermarks (Classification):**

Watermarks are classified along four orthogonal dimensions:

1. **Perceptual Visibility:**
   - *Visible* — Logo overlay (e.g., TV channel bug).
   - *Invisible* — Hidden, machine-detectable.

2. **Robustness:**
   - *Robust* — Survives compression, cropping, noise.
   - *Fragile* — Breaks under any modification (used for tamper detection).
   - *Semi-fragile* — Survives mild processing (e.g., JPEG) but breaks under malicious edits.

3. **Detection Requirement:**
   - *Public* — Detectable without secret key.
   - *Private* — Requires secret key.
   - *Asymmetric* — Public detection, private embedding.

4. **Application Domain:**
   - *Image, audio, video, text, 3D model* watermarks.

**Comparison: Visible vs Invisible Watermarks:**

| Property | Visible Watermark | Invisible Watermark |
|---|---|---|
| **Visibility** | Clearly seen by human eye | Perceptually transparent |
| **Primary Use** | Branding, source attribution, advertising | Copyright assertion, DRM |
| **Robustness** | High (visually obvious, hard to remove without destroying content) | Variable — depends on algorithm (robust, fragile, semi-fragile) |
| **Removal Difficulty** | Moderate (geometric distortion can remove some) | High (for robust), Low (for fragile) |
| **Example** | BBC logo in the corner of a news broadcast | Copyright ID hidden in a music file on Spotify |

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Fingerprinting** is the application of data hiding in which a **unique identifier (fingerprint)** is embedded into each copy of a digital asset distributed to a specific recipient. The purpose is to **trace the source of unauthorized redistribution**.

**Key Difference from Watermarking:**

| Watermarking | Fingerprinting |
|---|---|
| Asserts *who owns* the content | Asserts *who leaked* the content |
| Same watermark in all copies | Unique fingerprint per recipient |
| Visible/invisible, but same for all | Always unique per copy |

**Real-World Case:** The **Craigslist vs. 3Taps case (2013)** and the **RIAA v. Verizon (2003)** case are landmark examples. In the latter, the **Recording Industry Association of America (RIAA)** embedded **unique fingerprints** in copyrighted music files distributed to Verizon subscribers. When the files appeared on peer-to-peer networks (Kazaa), the RIAA extracted the fingerprints and traced them back to specific Verizon account holders, who were then issued legal notices for copyright infringement.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - **Pitfall 1 (1–2 marks lost):** Confusing *steganography* with *watermarking* by stating that watermarks are "hidden." The *visibility* of a watermark is not what defines it — its *purpose* (ownership assertion) does.
> - **Pitfall 2 (2 marks lost):** Writing the PSNR formula incorrectly as $\text{PSNR} = 20 \log_{10}(MAX/MSE)$ instead of the correct $\text{PSNR} = 10 \log_{10}(MAX^{2}/MSE)$. Both are mathematically equivalent, but examiners strictly follow the syllabus notation.
> - **Pitfall 3 (1 mark lost):** Forgetting to define all variables in the spread-spectrum equation ($\alpha$, $W$, $PN$). A complete answer must include variable definitions.
> - **Pitfall 4 (3 marks lost):** In the trade-off triangle question, listing the three properties but failing to explain *why* they cannot all be maximized simultaneously. The *mutual constraint* is the heart of the question.
> - **Pitfall 5 (1 mark lost):** Mixing up the direction of fingerprinting — fingerprinting identifies the **leaker**, not the **owner**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following checklist contains **board-exam-critical** facts for PECDT523 Module 3 — Applications of Data Hiding. Memorize these before every KTU exam attempt.

- **Data hiding protects the *existence* of communication; cryptography protects the *content* of communication.**
- The three pillars of any data-hiding system are **Capacity, Robustness, and Imperceptibility**, governed by an irreducible trade-off.
- **Steganography** is the covert communication branch; **watermarking** is the ownership assertion branch; **fingerprinting** is the traitor-tracing branch.
- **LSB substitution** is the simplest image steganography algorithm — it modifies the least significant bit of each pixel.
- **Spread-spectrum watermarking** is the most common robust technique — it uses a pseudo-random noise sequence keyed by a secret key.
- **PSNR ≥ 40 dB** is the gold standard for imperceptibility in image steganography; below **30 dB** is visually degraded.
- **PSNR formula:** $\text{PSNR} = 10 \log_{10}(MAX_I^{2}/MSE)$ — for 8-bit images, $MAX_I = 255$.
- **Robust watermarks** survive compression, cropping, and noise; **fragile watermarks** break under any modification.
- **Fingerprinting** embeds a **unique** ID per recipient; **watermarking** embeds the **same** ID in all copies.
- **DRM systems** in streaming platforms (Netflix, Spotify) embed license information directly in media files.
- **Covert channels** in networks hide data in TCP/IP header fields, packet timing, or unused protocol bits.
- **Steganalysis** is the adversary's tool to detect the presence of hidden data; modern steganalysis uses machine learning.
- **Fragile watermarks** are used in **medical imaging** to detect tampering of MRI/CT scans.
- **Real-world fingerprinting cases:** RIAA v. Verizon (2003) and Craigslist v. 3Taps (2013).
- **The "double-layering" pattern:** Always encrypt the payload first (AES), then hide it (LSB/DCT) for maximum security.
- **Hybrid security systems** (encryption + steganography) are the industry standard in high-sensitivity applications.
- **Threshold-based detection** in watermarking: correlation $\geq T$ → watermark present; correlation $< T$ → absent.
- **Spread-spectrum embedding equation:** $I_w = I + \alpha \cdot W \cdot PN$, where $\alpha$ controls robustness-vs-imperceptibility trade-off.
- **Authentication** uses fragile/semi-fragile watermarks; **Copyright** uses robust watermarks; **Covert communication** uses steganography.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Watermarking
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT523 - DATA PRIVACY AND SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Watermarking

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Watermarking** is the process of *embedding* an imperceptible or rarely perceptible **digital signal (watermark)** directly into a cover medium (such as an image, audio, video, or text document) such that the watermark can later be *detected or extracted* to assert ownership, prove authenticity, trace distribution, or carry supplementary side-information.

In the formal mathematical notation of information hiding, a watermarking system is defined as a tuple of functions:

$$\mathcal{W} = (\mathcal{E}, \mathcal{D})$$

where $\mathcal{E}$ is the *embedding function* and $\mathcal{D}$ is the *detection/extraction function*.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine a museum that loans a rare, valuable painting to another gallery. To identify the *true owner* and trace the painting if it is duplicated or counterfeited, the museum subtly marks the back of the canvas with an invisible ultraviolet ink stamp. This stamp:

- Does **not** affect how the painting looks (imperceptibility).
- Survives attempts to clean, repaint, or reframe it (robustness).
- Can be unambiguously traced back to the museum (unambiguity).
- Is difficult for counterfeiters to reproduce without the original ink (security).

**Digital watermarking works exactly like this** — a secret identifier is *baked* into the digital bits of a file so it can be recovered for ownership, authentication, or forensic tracing.

### 1.3 Core Terminology

> [!NOTE]
> **Mandatory Vocabulary (KTU 2024 Expected):**
> - **Cover Data ($C$):** The original, un-watermarked media file (image, audio, etc.).
> - **Watermark ($W$):** The signal/message being embedded.
> - **Embedding Key ($K_e$):** A secret cryptographic key used during embedding.
> - **Embedded Object ($C_W$):** The output (watermarked data) after the watermark is added.
> - **Attacker / Adversary ($A$):** An entity that attempts to remove, forge, or destroy the watermark.
> - **Robustness, Imperceptibility, Capacity, Security** — the four "Iron Triangle" properties of any watermarking scheme.

### 1.4 Properties of a Good Watermarking Scheme

| Property | Meaning | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Robustness** | Survives signal processing operations (compression, cropping, noise) | A JPEG-compressed image still contains the watermark |
| **Imperceptibility** | The watermark is invisible/inaudible to the human eye/ear | You cannot see the watermark in a photo |
| **Capacity** | Amount of information the watermark can carry | Bits embedded per image |
| **Security** | The watermark is encrypted or relies on a secret key | Only the owner can extract it |
| **Unambiguity** | Watermark uniquely identifies the owner | Two owners cannot have identical watermarks |
| **Computational Efficiency** | Embedding/detection is fast and scalable | Real-time broadcast monitoring |

### 1.5 Classification of Watermarking

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Classification (Module 3 Highlight):**
>
> 1. **By Visibility:** *Visible* vs *Invisible* watermarking
> 2. **By Robustness:** *Robust* vs *Fragile* vs *Semi-fragile*
> 3. **By Detection Method:** *Public* (blind) vs *Private* (non-blind) vs *Semi-private*
> 4. **By Domain:** *Spatial domain* (LSB) vs *Frequency domain* (DCT, DWT, DFT)
> 5. **By Coverage:** *Source-based* vs *Destination-based* vs *Combined*

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The General Watermarking Framework

The process consists of three logical phases:

**Phase 1 — Embedding (Encoding)**

The watermark $W$ is inserted into the cover data $C$ using a key $K_e$ to produce a watermarked object $C_W$.

**Phase 2 — Attack / Transmission (Optional)**

The watermarked object may be subjected to various attacks: compression, cropping, noise addition, filtering, geometric distortion.

**Phase 3 — Detection / Extraction (Decoding)**

Using the key $K_d$ (which may equal $K_e$), the detector checks whether $W$ is present and possibly recovers it.

### 2.2 Watermark Embedding Equation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The General Watermark Embedding Rule (KTU 2024 High-Yield Formula):**

$$\boxed{C_W = \mathcal{E}(C, W, K_e) = C \oplus \alpha \cdot f(W, K_e)}$$

where:
- $C$ = original cover data (matrix/image)
- $W$ = watermark signal (binary or real-valued)
- $K_e$ = embedding key
- $\alpha$ = **embedding strength factor** (trade-off between robustness and imperceptibility)
- $f(\cdot)$ = embedding function (additive, multiplicative, or quantisation-based)
- $\oplus$ = a domain-specific fusion operator (XOR, addition, replacement)

### 2.3 Watermark Detection Equation

A watermark is said to be **present** in a suspect document $C^*$ if a correlation test exceeds a threshold $\tau$:

$$\boxed{\rho = \mathrm{corr}(D(C^*), W) \;\;\underset{H_0}{\overset{H_1}{\gtrless}}\;\; \tau}$$

- $H_0$: watermark *absent*
- $H_1$: watermark *present*
- $\rho$ is the correlation coefficient (or normalised correlation)

### 2.4 Spatial Domain: LSB Watermarking

The **Least Significant Bit (LSB)** technique replaces the LSBs of the cover pixels with the bits of the watermark. For a pixel $p$ with 8-bit depth:

$$p' = (p \;\&\; 0xFE) \;\lor\; w_i$$

where $w_i \in \{0, 1\}$ is the $i$-th watermark bit, $\&$ is bitwise AND, and $\lor$ is bitwise OR.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why LSB works:** Human perception cannot easily distinguish between pixel values differing by only 1 in 256 (≈ 0.4% intensity change), so the watermark stays hidden.

### 2.5 Frequency Domain Watermarking

In **DCT (Discrete Cosine Transform)** watermarking, the watermark is embedded in the **mid-frequency coefficients** (not the lowest — they are perceptually important; not the highest — they are noisy):

$$C'_W(u,v) = C(u,v) + \alpha \cdot W(u,v) \;\;\; \text{for} \;\; (u,v) \in \mathcal{M}$$

where $\mathcal{M}$ is the set of mid-frequency indices.

### 2.6 The Robustness–Imperceptibility Trade-off

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Watermarking Iron Triangle:** It is mathematically impossible to simultaneously maximise **robustness**, **imperceptibility**, and **capacity**. Increasing one generally decreases another.
>
> - Raising $\alpha$ (embedding strength) → more robust, but more visible.
> - Lowering $\alpha$ → more imperceptible, but easier to remove.

### 2.7 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **KEEP THIS TABLE FOR EXAMS** — All key watermarking equations in one place.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C_W = \mathcal{E}(C, W, K_e)$ | Embedding function | General definition |
| 2 | $C_W = C + \alpha W$ | Additive embedding (spatial) | LSB / additive models |
| 3 | $C_W(u,v) = C(u,v) + \alpha W(u,v)$ | Additive embedding (frequency) | DCT / DWT models |
| 4 | $p' = (p \;\&\; 254) + w_i$ | LSB substitution | LSB technique |
| 5 | $\rho = \dfrac{\sum (X_i - \bar{X})(Y_i - \bar{Y})}{\sqrt{\sum (X_i - \bar{X})^2 \sum (Y_i - \bar{Y})^2}}$ | Pearson correlation | Detection decision |
| 6 | $\rho = \dfrac{\langle C^*, W \rangle}{\|C^*\| \cdot \|W\|}$ | Normalised correlation | Detection test |
| 7 | $\mathrm{PSNR} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{L^2}{\mathrm{MSE}}\right)$ | Image quality (dB) | Imperceptibility metric |
| 8 | $\mathrm{MSE} = \dfrac{1}{MN}\sum_{i,j}(C_{ij} - C'_{W,ij})^2$ | Mean Squared Error | Distortion measure |
| 9 | $\mathrm{SSIM}(x,y)$ | Structural similarity index | Perceptual quality |
| 10 | $\mathrm{BER} = \dfrac{\text{wrong bits}}{\text{total bits}}$ | Bit error rate | Extraction accuracy |

> [!WARNING]
> When writing $\vert X \vert$ in answers, write it as `\vert X \vert` in LaTeX, **not** with the keyboard pipe symbol, otherwise the markdown table breaks.

### 2.8 Applications of Watermarking (KTU 2024 Emphasis)

1. **Copyright Protection** — owner identification for legal disputes.
2. **Fingerprinting** — tracing illegal redistribution (each recipient gets a unique watermark).
3. **Broadcast Monitoring** — verifying when/where an ad was aired.
4. **Tamper Detection** — fragile watermarks break if data is modified.
5. **Content Authentication** — proving the integrity of medical/legal images.
6. **Steganography** — covert communication.
7. **Digital Rights Management (DRM)** — controlling playback/copying.

### 2.9 Watermarking vs Steganography vs Cryptography

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Comparison Table — Examiner's favourite:**

| Feature | Cryptography | Steganography | Watermarking |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Goal** | Confidentiality | Hidden communication | Ownership / integrity |
| **Visibility** | Scrambles data (obvious) | Hides existence | Hidden or visible |
| **Detection** | Anyone with key | Intended recipient only | Owner / public |
| **Robustness needed?** | Not after decryption | Not necessary | Essential |
| **Output** | Ciphertext | Same as cover | Perceptually similar to cover |
| **Attacks** | Brute force, cryptanalysis | Steganalysis | Removal, geometric, forgery |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example: LSB Embedding in an Image

**Given:**
- A 4×4 grayscale cover image $C$ with 8-bit pixels.
- A binary watermark $W = \{1, 0, 1, 1, 0, 1, 0, 0\}$ (8 bits).

**Step 1 — Flatten the cover image** into a 1-D sequence of pixels (row-major order):

$$C = [200, 150, 100, 50,\; 220, 180, 130, 70,\; 240, 160, 110, 90,\; 255, 175, 145, 85]$$

**Step 2 — Convert each pixel to binary (8 bits):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
200 &= 11001000 \\
150 &= 10010110 \\
100 &= 01100100 \\
 50 &= 00110010 \\
220 &= 11011100 \\
180 &= 10110100 \\
130 &= 10000010 \\
 70 &= 01000110
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the LSB substitution rule** $p' = (p \;\&\; 0xFE) \;\lor\; w_i$ for each of the first 8 pixels:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Pixel 1: } & 200 = 11001000 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; p' = 11001000 \;\lor\; 1 = 11001001 = 201 \\
\text{Pixel 2: } & 150 = 10010110 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; p' = 10010110 \;\lor\; 0 = 10010110 = 150 \\
\text{Pixel 3: } & 100 = 01100100 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; p' = 01100100 \;\lor\; 1 = 01100101 = 101 \\
\text{Pixel 4: } &  50 = 00110010 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; p' = 00110010 \;\lor\; 1 = 00110011 = 51 \\
\text{Pixel 5: } & 220 = 11011100 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; p' = 11011100 \;\lor\; 0 = 11011100 = 220 \\
\text{Pixel 6: } & 180 = 10110100 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; p' = 10110100 \;\lor\; 1 = 10110101 = 181 \\
\text{Pixel 7: } & 130 = 10000010 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; p' = 10000010 \;\lor\; 0 = 10000010 = 130 \\
\text{Pixel 8: } &  70 = 01000110 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; p' = 01000110 \;\lor\; 0 = 01000110 = 70
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — The watermarked image is:**

$$C_W = [201, 150, 101, 51, 220, 181, 130, 70,\; 240, 160, 110, 90,\; 255, 175, 145, 85]$$

**Step 5 — Verify imperceptibility:** The maximum per-pixel change is $\vert 200 - 201 \vert = 1$ (0.39% intensity change). This is well below the human perception threshold (Just-Noticeable Difference ≈ 2-3 levels for adjacent pixels), so the watermark is *imperceptible*.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Peak Signal-to-Noise Ratio (PSNR)

The PSNR measures imperceptibility. It is derived from the **Mean Squared Error (MSE)** between the cover and the watermarked image.

**Step 1 — Mean Squared Error:**

For an image of size $M \times N$:

$$
\mathrm{MSE} = \dfrac{1}{MN}\sum_{i=1}^{M}\sum_{j=1}^{N}\left(C_{ij} - C'_{W,ij}\right)^{2}
$$

> *Why this works:* MSE averages the squared difference per pixel — a small MSE means the watermarked image is close to the original.

**Step 2 — Maximum possible signal power:**

For an 8-bit grayscale image, the maximum pixel value is $L = 255$. The peak signal power is $L^{2} = 65025$.

**Step 3 — Define PSNR (in decibels):**

$$
\mathrm{PSNR} = 10 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{L^{2}}{\mathrm{MSE}}\right) \;\;\;\; \text{(dB)}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitution yields the practical form:**

$$
\boxed{\mathrm{PSNR} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{255}{\sqrt{\mathrm{MSE}}}\right) \;\;\text{dB}}
$$

> **Interpretation rule of thumb:**
> - PSNR $\geq 40$ dB → imperceptible to humans (good).
> - PSNR $\in [30, 40]$ dB → barely noticeable.
> - PSNR $< 30$ dB → visible degradation.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example: PSNR Calculation

**Given:** $4 \times 4$ cover image $C$ and watermarked image $C_W$ (from §3.1). Compute PSNR.

**Step 1 — Compute per-pixel squared differences for first 8 pixels (rest are unchanged, contribution = 0):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(200-201)^{2} &= 1 \\
(150-150)^{2} &= 0 \\
(100-101)^{2} &= 1 \\
( 50- 51)^{2} &= 1 \\
(220-220)^{2} &= 0 \\
(180-181)^{2} &= 1 \\
(130-130)^{2} &= 0 \\
( 70- 70)^{2} &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Sum and divide by $MN = 16$:**

$$
\mathrm{MSE} = \dfrac{1+0+1+1+0+1+0+0}{16} = \dfrac{4}{16} = 0.25
$$

**Step 3 — Apply PSNR formula:**

$$
\mathrm{PSNR} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{255^{2}}{0.25}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(260100) \approx 54.15 \text{ dB}
$$

**Result:** PSNR ≈ **54.15 dB** — well above the 40 dB threshold, confirming the LSB watermark is **imperceptible**.

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Correlation-Based Watermark Detection

**Step 1 — Hypothesis testing framework:**

Let $C^*$ be a suspect document. We test:

$$
H_0: C^* = C + \text{noise} \quad \text{(no watermark)}
$$
$$
H_1: C^* = C + \alpha W + \text{noise} \quad \text{(watermark present)}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the linear correlation:**

$$
\rho = \dfrac{\langle C^* - \bar{C^*}, W \rangle}{\vert C^* - \bar{C^*} \vert \cdot \vert W \vert}
$$

where $\langle \cdot, \cdot \rangle$ is the inner product.

**Step 3 — Decision rule:**

$$
\boxed{\rho \;\;\underset{H_0}{\overset{H_1}{\gtrless}}\;\; \tau}
$$

- If $\rho \geq \tau$ → declare watermark **present**.
- If $\rho < \tau$ → declare watermark **absent**.

**Step 4 — Choose $\tau$** based on the desired false-positive probability. A common choice: $\tau = 0.5$ for normalised correlation.

---

### 3.5 Capacity–Robustness Trade-off Formula

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Conceptual Formula:**

$$
\text{Capacity} \times \text{Robustness} \times \text{Imperceptibility} \;\approx\; \text{constant}
$$

A doubling in capacity typically requires a $2\times$ reduction in either robustness or imperceptibility (depending on the embedding domain).

---

### 3.6 DCT Watermarking Algorithm — Pseudocode

This is the **standard spread-spectrum DCT watermarking** procedure frequently examined in KTU papers.

```
ALGORITHM: DCT_Embed(C, W, α, K)
INPUT:  Cover image C, watermark W, strength α, key K
OUTPUT: Watermarked image C_W

1. Apply 8×8 block-wise DCT to C  →  coefficients C(u,v)
2. For each block b:
     a. Select mid-frequency set M (e.g., zigzag indices 5-20)
     b. Generate pseudo-random sequence P using key K
     c. For (u,v) in M:
            C'_W(u,v) = C(u,v) + α * W(b, idx) * P(u,v)
3. Apply inverse 8×8 block-wise IDCT  →  C_W
4. Return C_W
```

```
ALGORITHM: DCT_Detect(C*, α, W, K)
INPUT:  Suspect image C*, strength α, original watermark W, key K
OUTPUT: Decision (present/absent) + extracted watermark

1. Apply 8×8 block-wise DCT to C*  →  C*(u,v)
2. For each block b:
     a. Generate same pseudo-random sequence P using key K
     b. Estimate W'(b, idx) = C*(u,v) - C(u,v)) / (α * P(u,v))
3. Compute correlation ρ = corr(W', W)
4. If ρ ≥ τ: return "PRESENT", W'
   Else:      return "ABSENT"
```

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Generic Watermarking System Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Cover Data C] --> MUX[Embedding Function E]
    B[Watermark W] --> MUX
    K1[Secret Key Ke] --> MUX
    MUX --> Cw[Watermarked Object Cw]
    Cw --> CHAN[Transmission Channel and Attacks]
    CHAN --> Cs[Suspect Object C star]
    K2[Detection Key Kd] --> DET[Detector D]
    Cs --> DET
    DET --> RES{Decision}
    RES -->|Present| OUT1[Watermark Detected and Extracted]
    RES -->|Absent| OUT2[No Watermark Found]
```

### 4.2 Classification Tree of Watermarking Techniques

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    W[Watermarking]
    W --> V[By Visibility]
    W --> R[By Robustness]
    W --> D[By Detection]
    W --> DOM[By Domain]

    V --> V1[Visible Watermarking]
    V --> V2[Invisible Watermarking]

    R --> R1[Robust]
    R --> R2[Fragile]
    R --> R3[Semi Fragile]

    D --> D1[Private Non Blind]
    D --> D2[Semi Private]
    D --> D3[Public Blind]

    DOM --> DOM1[Spatial Domain]
    DOM --> DOM2[Frequency Domain]
    DOM1 --> LSB[LSB Substitution]
    DOM1 --> EB[Echo Hiding for Audio]
    DOM2 --> DCTM[DCT Based]
    DOM2 --> DWTM[DWT Based]
    DOM2 --> DFTM[DFT Based]
```

### 4.3 LSB Watermarking Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start with Cover Image Pixels] --> S2[Convert Each Pixel to 8 bit Binary]
    S2 --> S3[Read Watermark Bit wi]
    S3 --> S4{Is wi equal to LSB of Pixel}
    S4 -->|Yes| S5[Keep Pixel Unchanged]
    S4 -->|No| S6[Flip LSB of Pixel to Match wi]
    S5 --> S7[Move to Next Pixel]
    S6 --> S7
    S7 --> S8{All Watermark Bits Embedded}
    S8 -->|No| S3
    S8 -->|Yes| S9[Return Watermarked Image]
```

### 4.4 Frequency-Domain (DCT) Watermarking Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Original Image C] --> P2[Divide into 8x8 Blocks]
    P2 --> P3[Apply DCT to Each Block]
    P3 --> P4[Select Mid Frequency Coefficients M]
    W[Watermark W] --> P5[Spread Spectrum with Key K]
    P5 --> P4
    P4 --> P6[Add Watermark to Coefficients alpha W]
    P6 --> P7[Apply IDCT to Each Block]
    P7 --> P8[Reassemble Watermarked Image Cw]
    Cw --> TX[Channel Attacks]
    TX --> RX[Suspect Image C star]
    RX --> P9[Apply DCT to Blocks]
    P9 --> P10[Extract Coefficients in M]
    P10 --> P11[Correlate with Original Watermark]
    P11 --> P12[Threshold Test tau]
    P12 -->|rho greater than tau| OUT1[Watermark Present]
    P12 -->|rho less than tau| OUT2[Watermark Absent]
```

### 4.5 Robustness Imperceptibility Capacity Trade-off (Iron Triangle)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IM[Imperceptibility] --> T{Trade off Triangle}
    RO[Robustness] --> T
    CA[Capacity] --> T
    T --> AX[Cannot Maximize All Three]
    AX --> NOTE[Increasing one decreases at least one of the others]
```

### 4.6 Common Watermarking Attacks

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    AT[Attacks on Watermarking]
    AT --> A1[Removal Attacks]
    AT --> A2[Geometric Attacks]
    AT --> A3[Protocol Attacks]
    AT --> A4[Cryptographic Attacks]
    A1 --> A1a[Compression JPEG MP3]
    A1 --> A1b[Noise Addition]
    A1 --> A1c[Filtering]
    A2 --> A2a[Cropping]
    A2 --> A2b[Rotation Scaling]
    A2 --> A2c[Translation]
    A3 --> A3a[Inversion Attack]
    A3 --> A3b[Copy Attack]
    A4 --> A4a[Oracle Attack]
    A4 --> A4b[Brute Force Key Search]
```

### 4.7 Watermarking versus Steganography versus Cryptography Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Cryptography
        C1[Plaintext] --> C2[Encryption with Key]
        C2 --> C3[Ciphertext]
        C3 --> C4[Decryption with Key]
        C4 --> C1
    end
    subgraph Steganography
        S1[Secret Message] --> S2[Embedding into Cover]
        S2 --> S3[Stego Object]
        S3 --> S4[Extraction]
        S4 --> S1
    end
    subgraph Watermarking
        W1[Cover Media] --> W2[Embedding Watermark plus Key]
        W2 --> W3[Watermarked Media]
        W3 --> W4[Detection with Key]
        W4 --> W5[Owner or Authenticity Proof]
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question A1]**
> **Q1. Define digital watermarking. List any four essential properties of a watermarking system.**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> *Digital watermarking is the technique of embedding an imperceptible digital signal — known as a watermark — into a cover media (image, audio, video, or text) so that the watermark can later be detected or extracted to establish ownership, authenticity, or for tracing the recipient.* **(1 mark)**
>
> *The four essential properties are:*
> 1. *Robustness — survives signal processing and attacks.* **(0.5)**
> 2. *Imperceptibility — invisible/inaudible to humans.* **(0.5)**
> 3. *Capacity — sufficient data-carrying ability.* **(0.5)**
> 4. *Security — protected by a secret key.* **(0.5)**

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question A2]**
> **Q2. Differentiate between spatial-domain and frequency-domain watermarking. Give one example of each.**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> | Aspect | Spatial Domain | Frequency Domain |
> |---|---|---|
> | *Domain of embedding* | *Directly on pixel values* | *On transformed coefficients (DCT/DWT/DFT)* |
> | *Capacity* | *High* | *Moderate* |
> | *Robustness* | *Low — easily removed by compression* | *High — survives JPEG, filtering* |
> | *Imperceptibility* | *Good for small LSB changes* | *Good when mid-frequency bands are chosen* |
> | *Example* | *LSB substitution* | *DCT-based spread-spectrum watermarking* |
>
> **(Each correct row: 0.5 marks, totals 3 marks.)**

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Watermarking Properties & LSB Technique**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question B-A]**
> **Q. (a)** Explain in detail the **four essential properties** of a watermarking system with examples. Discuss the **robustness–imperceptibility trade-off** with a neat diagram. **(7 marks)**
>
> **Model Answer:**
>
> *The four essential properties of a watermarking system are:*
>
> 1. *Robustness — The watermark must survive legitimate and illegitimate signal processing operations such as compression (JPEG/MP3), filtering, cropping, noise addition, and format conversion. Example: A copyright watermark on a photograph must still be detectable after the photo is uploaded to Facebook, which recompresses it.* **(1.5)**
> 2. *Imperceptibility — The watermark must be perceptually invisible (or inaudible) so that the commercial value of the cover is unaffected. Example: A logo on a film frame must not distract the viewer.* **(1.5)**
> 3. *Capacity — The number of bits that can be embedded within a unit of cover data. Higher capacity supports richer ownership information or transaction trails.* **(1.5)**
> 4. *Security — The watermark must depend on a secret key so that unauthorised parties cannot forge, remove, or detect it. Cryptographic keys are used for this purpose.* **(1.5)**
>
> *The trade-off among robustness, imperceptibility, and capacity is shown as a triangle. Increasing the embedding strength $\alpha$ improves robustness but makes the watermark more visible. Embedding more bits improves capacity but reduces the energy per bit, lowering robustness. Hence, designers balance the three factors — maximising all three simultaneously is mathematically infeasible.* **(1 mark for the discussion + diagram)**
>
> ```mermaid
   flowchart LR
       IM[Imperceptibility] --- TR[Trade off Triangle]
       RO[Robustness] --- TR
       CA[Capacity] --- TR
   ```

---

> **Q. (b)** Describe the **LSB watermarking technique** with the embedding and extraction algorithms. A 4-bit watermark is to be embedded in pixels of values `[120, 75, 200, 50]` using LSB substitution. Show the watermarked pixels. **(7 marks)**
>
> **Model Answer:**
>
> *LSB substitution replaces the Least Significant Bit of each cover pixel with a single bit of the watermark. Because flipping the LSB of a pixel only changes the intensity by 1 (out of 256 for an 8-bit image), the change is below the human perception threshold (JND), making the watermark imperceptible.* **(1 mark)**
>
> ***Embedding Algorithm:***
> ```
> For each bit wi of the watermark W:
>     1. Pick the next cover pixel p
>     2. Clear the LSB: p' = p & 0xFE
>     3. Insert wi:    p'' = p' | wi
>     4. Output p''
> ```
> ***Extraction Algorithm:***
> ```
> For each of the first N pixels of the suspect image:
>     wi = p & 0x01   (extract the LSB)
> ```
> **(2 marks for the algorithms.)**
>
> ***Numerical Example:*** *Watermark bits = 1, 0, 1, 1.*
>
> $$\begin{aligned}
120 &= 01111000 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 01111001 = 121 \\
 75 &= 01001011 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 01001010 = 74 \\
200 &= 11001000 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 11001001 = 201 \\
 50 &= 00110010 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 00110011 = 51
\end{aligned}$$
>
> *Watermarked pixels = [121, 74, 201, 51]. Maximum per-pixel change = 1, hence PSNR will be very high (imperceptible).* **(3 marks)**
>
> *Advantages of LSB: simple, high capacity, low distortion. Disadvantages: very fragile — destroyed by any lossy compression, easy to detect and remove.* **(1 mark)**

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Frequency Domain Watermarking & Attacks**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question B-B]**
> **Q. (a)** Explain **DCT-based watermarking** in detail. Why are mid-frequency coefficients chosen for embedding? Compare DCT-based watermarking with LSB watermarking. **(7 marks)**
>
> **Model Answer:**
>
> *DCT-based watermarking embeds the watermark in the frequency-domain coefficients of an image (typically computed in 8×8 blocks, mirroring the JPEG standard). The general additive rule is:* **(1 mark)**
>
> $$C'_W(u,v) = C(u,v) + \alpha \cdot W(u,v) \quad \text{for} \quad (u,v) \in \mathcal{M}$$
>
> *The watermark is often spread across the coefficients using a pseudo-random sequence generated from a secret key, providing security.* **(1 mark)**
>
> ***Why mid-frequency coefficients?***
> - *Low-frequency coefficients carry the most perceptual energy (the image's main visual content). Modifying them causes visible distortion.*
> - *High-frequency coefficients represent noise/fine detail. Embedding there is imperceptible, but the watermark is destroyed by compression (which discards high frequencies).*
> - *Mid-frequency coefficients strike a balance — they are robust against compression and remain imperceptible.* **(2 marks)**
>
> ***Comparison with LSB:*** **(3 marks)**
>
> | Property | LSB (Spatial) | DCT (Frequency) |
> |---|---|---|
> | Robustness | Very low | High (survives JPEG) |
> | Imperceptibility | High | High (in mid-band) |
> | Complexity | Trivial | Requires DCT/IDCT |
> | Capacity | High | Moderate |
> | Common attack that defeats it | Any compression | Cropping, heavy filtering |
>
> *Conclusion: DCT is preferred for copyright protection, while LSB is suitable for fragile authentication or steganography.* **(1 mark final.)**

---

> **Q. (b)** What are the **major types of attacks on watermarking systems**? Explain any three with examples. How is the **robustness** of a watermark quantified? **(7 marks)**
>
> **Model Answer:**
>
> *Watermark attacks are operations that attempt to make a watermark undetectable or to forge it. The four major categories are:* **(1 mark)**
>
> 1. *Removal Attacks* — aim to remove the watermark without keeping it detectable. Examples: lossy compression (JPEG, MP3), denoising, averaging, re-modulation.* **(1)**
> 2. *Geometric Attacks* — distort the spatial alignment so that the watermark detector cannot locate the embedded bits. Examples: rotation, scaling, translation, cropping, aspect-ratio change.* **(1)*
> 3. *Protocol Attacks* — exploit the watermarking protocol itself rather than the signal. Example: the **inversion attack** produces a second, fake watermark on the same image, leading to a legal ambiguity about who is the true owner.* **(1)*
> 4. *Cryptographic Attacks* — brute-force search for the secret embedding key or exploit weaknesses in the cryptographic layer. Example: exhaustive key search when the key length is too small.* **(1)*
>
> *Robustness is quantified using the **Bit Error Rate (BER)** of the extracted watermark:* **(1 mark)**
>
> $$\mathrm{BER} = \dfrac{\text{Number of incorrectly extracted bits}}{\text{Total number of embedded bits}}$$
>
> *A lower BER indicates higher robustness. Robustness can also be quantified using the **correlation** $\rho$ between the extracted and original watermarks — closer to 1 is better.* **(1 mark)**
>
> *A typical system is considered acceptably robust if BER $< 10\%$ after standard attacks like JPEG compression at quality 70%.* **(0.5)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warnings

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places students lose marks in Watermarking questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing watermarking with steganography** — They have different goals. Watermarking = ownership/integrity; Steganography = covert communication. Examiners dock 1 mark for this.
> 2. **Forgetting the trade-off triangle** — Any 7-mark question on properties *must* include the trade-off discussion. Missing it = −1.5 marks.
> 3. **Writing $\vert x \vert$ with the keyboard pipe `|` in tables** — Breaks the table in digital answer sheets. Always use `\vert x \vert` in LaTeX.
> 4. **Not showing the LSB bitwise math** — Examiners want the binary decomposition of pixels ($120 = 01111000$) explicitly shown. Skipping the binary form = −1 mark.
> 5. **Listing attacks without classifying them** — Group attacks into *removal / geometric / protocol / cryptographic*. Ungrouped lists lose marks.
> 6. **Mixing up robust vs fragile watermarks** — Robust = copyright (must survive attacks). Fragile = tamper detection (must break on modification).
> 7. **Omitting the role of the secret key $K_e$** — Examiners specifically check for the security property, which requires a key.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (for last-minute exam review):**
>
> ✅ **Definition:** Watermarking = embedding a hidden signal into a cover medium for ownership, authentication, or tracing.
>
> ✅ **Iron-Triangle Properties:** *Robustness, Imperceptibility, Capacity* (+ Security). They cannot all be maximised simultaneously.
>
> ✅ **Classification axes:** Visibility (visible/invisible), Robustness (robust/fragile/semi-fragile), Detection (public/private/semi-private), Domain (spatial/frequency).
>
> ✅ **Spatial Domain (LSB):** Replace the LSB of each pixel with a watermark bit. $p' = (p \,\&\, 0xFE) \lor w_i$. High capacity, low robustness.
>
> ✅ **Frequency Domain (DCT/DWT/DFT):** Embed in **mid-frequency** coefficients. Use spread-spectrum with a secret key. $C'_W(u,v) = C(u,v) + \alpha W(u,v)$. High robustness.
>
> ✅ **Embedding formula:** $C_W = \mathcal{E}(C, W, K_e) = C + \alpha W$ (additive model).
>
> ✅ **Detection formula:** $\rho \ge \tau$ → present; $\rho < \tau$ → absent. Test is a hypothesis test.
>
> ✅ **PSNR formula:** $\mathrm{PSNR} = 10 \log_{10}(L^2 / \mathrm{MSE})$ dB. Acceptable threshold = 40 dB.
>
> ✅ **MSE formula:** $\mathrm{MSE} = \dfrac{1}{MN} \sum (C_{ij} - C'_{W,ij})^2$.
>
> ✅ **BER:** $\mathrm{BER} = \dfrac{\text{wrong bits}}{\text{total bits}}$. Lower is better.
>
> ✅ **Attacks:** Removal (compression, noise), Geometric (rotation, cropping), Protocol (inversion), Cryptographic (key search).
>
> ✅ **Watermarking ≠ Steganography ≠ Cryptography** — know the differences; this is a favourite comparison question.
>
> ✅ **Applications:** Copyright, fingerprinting, broadcast monitoring, tamper detection, DRM, authentication.
>
> ✅ **Exam tip:** Always show binary pixel decomposition in LSB numericals and always state the trade-off in property-based questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Intuitive Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT523 - DATA PRIVACY AND SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Intuitive Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Methods** (in Data Privacy) refer to a family of **non-rigorous, heuristic, and experience-driven privacy-preserving techniques** that aim to protect personally identifiable information (PII) through ad-hoc transformations such as **removal, suppression, generalization, aggregation, pseudonymization, and noise addition**. Unlike formal privacy frameworks (e.g., Differential Privacy), intuitive methods do **not** offer mathematically quantifiable privacy guarantees; they rely on the assumption that "if the obvious identifiers are hidden, privacy is preserved."

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PECDT523)**, Module 3 positions *Intuitive Methods* as the **first tier of privacy-preserving data publication techniques**, sitting conceptually *below* formal/rigorous methods (such as Differential Privacy and Cryptographic Protocols) but *above* raw data disclosure.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Masked Ball"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** Imagine a grand masquerade ball where every guest wears a costume.
> - **Removing identifiers** = stripping name tags (everyone can still recognize you by your face).
> - **Generalization** = blurring the photograph to a watercolor sketch.
> - **Aggregation** = reporting only the *average height* of all guests, not individual heights.
> - **Pseudonymization** = giving every guest a fake name tag — but the *real* names are still discoverable if someone compares the fake tags with another guest list.
>
> The ball feels private, but a determined observer (an *adversary*) can still piece together who is who. **Intuitive methods provide a costume, not an invisibility cloak.**

### 1.3 Key Characteristics of Intuitive Methods

| Characteristic | Description |
|---|---|
| **Heuristic-based** | Derived from practical experience, not mathematical proofs of privacy. |
| **Easy to implement** | Require no advanced cryptography or statistical machinery. |
| **Property-based** | Focus on syntactic properties of data (e.g., *k-anonymity*, *l-diversity*). |
| **Vulnerable to linkage attacks** | Can be defeated by combining the released dataset with auxiliary (public) data. |
| **Utility-preserving** | Often retain high data utility (good for analytics), sometimes at the cost of privacy. |
| **No formal adversary model** | Lack a mathematically defined adversarial bound. |

### 1.4 Standard Metrics (KTU Syllabus Highlights)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following **bold** values are standard reference thresholds used throughout the KTU board examination paper:
> - **k-anonymity threshold:** typically **k = 5 or higher** (industry standard for health/financial data).
> - **l-diversity threshold:** **l ≥ 2 or 3**.
> - **t-closeness threshold:** **t ≤ 0.15** (Earth Mover's Distance).
> - **Suppression rate:** expressed as a percentage (commonly **≤ 5%** acceptable for utility).
> - **Generalization height:** the number of hierarchy levels traversed (typically **≤ 3** for low information loss).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Taxonomy of Intuitive Methods

Intuitive methods can be categorized into **five principal families**, arranged in increasing order of privacy strength (and decreasing order of data utility):

1. **De-identification / Identifier Removal**
2. **Pseudonymization**
3. **Suppression**
4. **Generalization (with k-anonymity, l-diversity, t-closeness)**
5. **Noise Addition / Perturbation**
6. **Aggregation / Micro-aggregation**

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Each Method

#### (a) Identifier Removal (De-identification)
- **Why:** Direct identifiers (name, SSN, Aadhaar, phone) uniquely identify an individual.
- **How:** Strip these columns before release.
- **Limitation:** Vulnerable to *re-identification* via quasi-identifiers (e.g., ZIP + DOB + gender can re-identify ~87% of Americans — *Sweeney, 2000*).

#### (b) Pseudonymization
- **Why:** Real identifiers replaced with random tokens (UUIDs).
- **How:** Use a one-way mapping (often cryptographic hash) from real ID to token.
- **Limitation:** Token is **deterministic** — if an attacker has the same hash function and a known mapping, privacy collapses.

#### (c) Suppression
- **Why:** Some records are too unique to anonymize — remove them.
- **How:** Either suppress specific cell values (cell suppression) or whole records (record suppression).
- **Limitation:** Reduces dataset size → utility loss.

#### (d) Generalization
- **Why:** Replace specific values with broader categories.
- **How:** Use a *value generalization hierarchy* (VGH).
- **Limitation:** Excessive generalization = information loss.

#### (e) k-Anonymity (the flagship intuitive property)
- **Why:** Ensure every record is indistinguishable from at least **k − 1** others with respect to the quasi-identifier (QI) attributes.
- **How:** Suppress + Generalize QIs so every equivalence class has size ≥ k.
- **Limitation:** Vulnerable to *homogeneity attack* (all k records have the same sensitive attribute) and *background knowledge attack*.

#### (f) l-Diversity
- **Why:** Strengthen k-anonymity by requiring **at least l "well-represented" values** for the sensitive attribute in each equivalence class.
- **How:** Re-organize equivalence classes.
- **Limitation:** Does not handle *semantic similarity* of sensitive values.

#### (g) t-Closeness
- **Why:** Strengthen l-diversity by requiring the distribution of the sensitive attribute in any equivalence class to be within distance **t** of the global distribution.
- **How:** Compute Earth Mover's Distance (EMD) between class and global distributions.
- **Limitation:** Computationally expensive; EMD calculation requires ordered/categorical distance definitions.

#### (h) Noise Addition (Basic, Non-DP)
- **Why:** Add random noise to numeric attributes to mask exact values.
- **How:** $X' = X + \epsilon$, where $\epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$.
- **Limitation:** Noise magnitude is a *guess* — no formal privacy budget exists. Vulnerable to averaging attacks.

#### (i) Aggregation / Micro-aggregation
- **Why:** Release only group-level statistics (sum, mean, count).
- **How:** Partition records into groups of size *k*; release the *k*-record average instead of individual values.
- **Limitation:** Loses individual-level insights.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| Concept | Formula / Metric | Description | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Re-identification Risk** | $R = \frac{1}{k}$ | Probability that a record is uniquely identified in its equivalence class. | dimensionless |
| **k-Anonymity Criterion** | $\forall \, \text{eq. class } C: \vert C \vert \geq k$ | Minimum equivalence class size. | records |
| **l-Diversity Criterion** | $\forall \, C: \text{distinct}(S) \geq l$ | Number of distinct sensitive values per class. | distinct values |
| **t-Closeness (EMD)** | $EMD(C, T) \leq t$ | Earth Mover's Distance between class and global distribution. | distance |
| **Information Loss (Generalization Height)** | $IL = \frac{1}{\vert D \vert \cdot \vert A \vert} \sum_{r \in D} \sum_{a \in A} \frac{h(r,a)}{H(a)}$ | Normalized hierarchy height traversed. | ratio (0 to 1) |
| **Suppression Rate** | $SR = \frac{\text{suppressed cells}}{\text{total cells}}$ | Fraction of data removed. | ratio (0 to 1) |
| **Noise Standard Deviation (Basic)** | $\sigma$ | Standard deviation of added Gaussian noise. | same as data unit |
| **Micro-aggregation Group Size** | $k$ | Records averaged per group. | records |
| **Disclosure Risk (Cell-level)** | $DR = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{m}(1 - p_i)$ | For m linkage keys, where $p_i$ = prob. of match. | probability |
| **Utility Score (Generic)** | $U = 1 - \frac{\sum \vert x_i - x'_i \vert}{\sum \vert x_i - \bar{x} \vert}$ | Information preserved after anonymization. | ratio (0 to 1) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU FORMATTING RULE:** Notice the use of `\vert C \vert` (LaTeX absolute-value vertical bars) instead of literal pipe `|` characters. This is mandatory in all KTU board-answer PDFs to avoid rendering glitches.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Healthcare (HIPAA compliance):** Hospitals use **k-anonymity** before publishing patient datasets for medical research.
- **Census data:** India's **Census of India** uses generalization and suppression to release district-level demographic data.
- **Telecom analytics:** Operators use **micro-aggregation** for CDRs (Call Detail Records) shared with government agencies.
- **IoT / Smart Cities:** Sensor readings masked via **noise addition** before public dashboards.
- **Banking & Finance (RBI guidelines):** Pseudonymization is mandated for tokenized Aadhaar data and card-on-file tokenization.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example — k-Anonymization of a Patient Micro-Dataset

**Given Dataset (Original, un-anonymized):**

| ID | Name | Age | ZIP | Gender | Disease (Sensitive) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Arjun | 28 | 682001 | M | Cancer |
| 2 | Beena | 29 | 682001 | F | Diabetes |
| 3 | Cyril | 35 | 682002 | M | Cancer |
| 4 | Divya | 36 | 682002 | F | Flu |
| 5 | Eshan | 41 | 682003 | M | Diabetes |
| 6 | Femy  | 42 | 682003 | F | Cancer |

> **Quasi-Identifiers (QIs):** Age, ZIP, Gender  
> **Sensitive Attribute (SA):** Disease  
> **k-value:** 2

#### Step 1 — Drop Direct Identifiers

> Remove `ID` and `Name` columns.

| Age | ZIP | Gender | Disease |
|---|---|---|---|
| 28 | 682001 | M | Cancer |
| 29 | 682001 | F | Diabetes |
| 35 | 682002 | M | Cancer |
| 36 | 682002 | F | Flu |
| 41 | 682003 | M | Diabetes |
| 42 | 682003 | F | Cancer |

#### Step 2 — Define Generalization Hierarchies

For **Age**:
- Level 0: 28, 29, 35, 36, 41, 42
- Level 1: [20–29], [30–39], [40–49]
- Level 2: 20s, 30s, 40s
- Level 3: Adult

For **ZIP**:
- Level 0: 682001, 682001, 682002, 682002, 682003, 682003
- Level 1: 6820** (drop last two digits)
- Level 2: 682***
- Level 3: ****

For **Gender**:
- Level 0: M, F
- Level 1: Person (single category)

#### Step 3 — Group by QI Tuples and Compute Class Sizes

Initial classes (before any generalization):
- (28, 682001, M) → size **1** ❌
- (29, 682001, F) → size **1** ❌
- (35, 682002, M) → size **1** ❌
