# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define signature casting in images. How does it differ from a conventional digital signature?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Signature casting in images is the process of embedding a digital signature (typically a cryptographic hash output) directly into the pixel data of a host image using steganographic techniques, producing a signed image that is visually identical to the original. **(1 mark)**
A conventional digital signature is a separate cryptographic value appended to the file, whereas signature casting fuses the signature *inside* the image's pixel bits. **(1 mark)**
This makes signature casting invisible, tamper-evident, and suitable for image authentication, copyright protection, and integrity verification in applications such as medical imaging and forensic evidence. **(1 mark)**

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**List any three applications of signature casting in real-world systems.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Medical Imaging:** Embedding patient hash signatures into DICOM X-ray/MRI scans to detect tampering of diagnostic data. **(1 mark)**
2. **CCTV Forensics:** Court-admissible evidence protection by signing frames at the camera level to prevent post-capture editing. **(1 mark)**
3. **Copyright Protection:** Stock photography platforms embed invisible owner signatures to detect unauthorized reuse of digital images. **(1 mark)**

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) — LSB Spatial Domain Focus

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **LSB-based signature casting algorithm** with a suitable example. Show the embedding and extraction steps mathematically. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A $256 \times 256$ grayscale cover image is to be signed with a 128-bit SHA-1 hash signature using LSB substitution. Calculate:
   - (i) The total embedding capacity in bits.
   - (ii) The percentage of cover pixels used.
   - (iii) The expected maximum PSNR (assume MSE $= 0.5$ for LSB casting).
   - **(iv)** Comment on the imperceptibility of the resulting signed image. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — LSB Signature Casting Algorithm

**Step 1 — Signature Generation (1 mark):**
The image owner computes a hash of the image content concatenated with a private key:

$$S = H(I_{cover} \mid\mid K_{priv})$$

For a SHA-1 hash, $S$ is a **160-bit** binary string. For this question, we assume a 128-bit signature.

**Step 2 — Cover Image Preparation (1 mark):**
The cover image $I_{cover}$ is a grayscale matrix of size $M \times N$. Each pixel $I_{cover}(x,y)$ is an 8-bit integer in $[0, 255]$.

**Step 3 — Embedding (Casting) Process (2 marks):**
For each signature bit $s_i$ ($i = 1, 2, \ldots, 128$), a pixel is selected (e.g., in raster-scan order). The LSB of the pixel is replaced with $s_i$ using the formula:

$$I_{signed}(x,y) = 2 \left\lfloor \dfrac{I_{cover}(x,y)}{2} \right\rfloor + s_i$$

Equivalently, in bitwise form:

$$I_{signed}(x,y) = \left( I_{cover}(x,y) \ \text{AND}\ \text{0xFE} \right) \ \text{OR}\ s_i$$

**Step 4 — Extraction at the Receiver (2 marks):**
The receiver extracts the LSB of each signed pixel:

$$s_i' = I_{signed}(x,y) \ \text{AND}\ \text{0x01}$$

**Step 5 — Verification (1 mark):**
The receiver also recomputes $H(I_{signed} \mid\mid K_{pub})$ and compares bit-by-bit with the extracted $s_i'$. A 100% match confirms authenticity. Any mismatch indicates pixel tampering.

**Worked Example (included for clarity):**
- Pixel value: $173 = 10101101_2$
- Signature bit: $s_i = 1$
- After masking: $10101100_2 = 172$
- After OR with 1: $10101101_2 = 173$ (change of $\pm 1$, imperceptible)

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Analysis

**(i) Total Embedding Capacity (2 marks):**
For a $256 \times 256$ grayscale image with 1 bit per pixel:

$$Cap = M \times N \times 1 = 256 \times 256 = 65{,}536 \text{ bits}$$

**(ii) Percentage of Cover Pixels Used (2 marks):**
Signature length $L = 128$ bits.
Required pixels = 128 (one per bit).

$$\% \text{ used} = \dfrac{128}{65{,}536} \times 100\% = 0.1953\%$$

**(iii) Expected Maximum PSNR (2 marks):**

$$PSNR = 10 \log_{10}\!\left( \dfrac{255^{2}}{0.5} \right) = 10 \log_{10}(130{,}050)$$

$$PSNR = 10 \times 5.1140 = 51.14 \text{ dB}$$

**(iv) Imperceptibility Comment (1 mark):**
A PSNR of **51.14 dB** is well above the imperceptibility threshold of 40 dB. The signed image will be **visually indistinguishable** from the cover image, and only 0.1953% of pixels are modified — confirming excellent imperceptibility.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Frequency Domain Focus

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Describe **DCT-based signature casting** in images. Explain why mid-frequency coefficients are preferred over low or high-frequency coefficients for embedding. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A $512 \times 512$ grayscale image is divided into $8 \times 8$ blocks, and each block carries 1 signature bit using a DCT-based scheme. Calculate:
   - (i) Total number of DCT blocks.
   - (ii) Total embedding capacity in bits.
   - (iii) Justify why this scheme is more robust to JPEG compression than LSB casting.
   - **(iv)** If the PSNR after casting is 38 dB, is the image perceptually acceptable? Justify. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — DCT-Based Signature Casting

**Step 1 — Block Partitioning (1 mark):**
The image is divided into non-overlapping $8 \times 8$ blocks — matching the JPEG compression block size, ensuring compatibility.

**Step 2 — 2D-DCT Transform (1.5 marks):**
Each block is transformed using 2D-DCT to obtain frequency coefficients. The DC coefficient ($F(0,0)$) represents the average intensity; AC coefficients capture spatial frequencies.

**Step 3 — Coefficient Selection (1.5 marks):**
Mid-frequency AC coefficients (e.g., $F(4,5)$, $F(5,4)$) are selected for embedding because:
- **Low-frequency (e.g., DC):** Carries most of the image energy; modifying it causes visible artifacts and large PSNR drop.
- **High-frequency:** Discarded by JPEG compression; signature bits will be lost.
- **Mid-frequency:** Best trade-off — invisible to the eye AND preserved through JPEG quantization. **(1.5 marks)**

**Step 4 — Embedding Rule (1.5 marks):**
The signature bit $s_i$ modulates the chosen coefficient using a quantization step $Q$:

$$F'(u,v) = F(u,v) + s_i \cdot \alpha \cdot Q$$

where $\alpha$ is an embedding strength factor.

**Step 5 — Reconstruction (1.5 marks):**
Inverse DCT is applied to produce the modified block, and blocks are reassembled to form the signed image.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Analysis

**(i) Total DCT Blocks (1.5 marks):**
Total pixels = $512 \times 512 = 262{,}144$
Pixels per block = $8 \times 8 = 64$

$$N_{blocks} = \dfrac{262{,}144}{64} = 4{,}096 \text{ blocks}$$

**(ii) Total Embedding Capacity (1.5 marks):**
1 signature bit per block:

$$Cap = 4{,}096 \text{ bits} = 512 \text{ bytes}$$

**(iii) Robustness Justification (2 marks):**
LSB casting modifies pixel intensities directly, which JPEG compression *immediately* alters (since JPEG quantizes pixel values in $8 \times 8$ blocks and discards fine intensity variations). DCT-based casting embeds into the *same frequency domain* that JPEG operates on — the mid-frequency coefficients are preserved by JPEG quantization, so the signature survives lossy compression. Hence, DCT-based casting is **JPEG-robust**, while LSB casting is **JPEG-fragile**.

**(iv) Perceptual Acceptability (2 marks):**
A PSNR of **38 dB** is below the typical imperceptibility threshold of 40 dB. While the image may still be acceptable for many applications, it is **on the borderline** — some viewers may detect slight blocky artifacts. For high-stakes applications (medical, forensic), a quantization step $Q$ reduction or a different casting method is recommended to push PSNR above 40 dB.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Signature Casting with Watermarking:** Signature casting is typically *fragile* and *invisible*; watermarking may be *robust* and *visible*. Marks deducted for using these terms interchangeably. **(−1 to −2 marks)**
> 2. **Forgetting Bitwise Operations in LSB:** Many students write $I_{signed} = I_{cover} + s_i$, which is mathematically wrong. Always show the AND–OR mask procedure or the floor-division form. **(−2 marks)**
> 3. **PSNR Calculation Errors:** Forgetting to square the 255 (i.e., using 255 instead of $255^2 = 65025$) is the most common numerical mistake. **(−1 mark)**
> 4. **Ignoring Block Size in DCT:** Always explicitly state the block is $8 \times 8$ to align with JPEG — this shows exam awareness. **(−1 mark)**
> 5. **Capacity vs. Robustness Trade-off:** Examiners love questions on why LSB has high capacity but low robustness. Failing to mention this trade-off loses marks.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** Signature casting embeds a digital signature *inside* image pixels, not appended to the file.
- **Two Major Domains:** Spatial (LSB, PVD) and Frequency (DCT, DWT, DFT).
- **LSB Equation (must memorize):** $I_{signed} = 2 \lfloor I_{cover}/2 \rfloor + s_i$
- **Imperceptibility Threshold:** PSNR $\ge \mathbf{40 \text{ dB}}$ is the practical standard.
- **PSNR Formula:** $PSNR = 10 \log_{10}(255^2 / MSE)$ in dB.
- **MSE Formula:** Average squared difference over all pixels.
- **Capacity (LSB):** 1 bit per pixel → $M \times N$ total bits for grayscale.
- **Capacity (DCT, 8×8 blocks):** 1 bit per block → $MN/64$ bits total.
- **Mid-frequency DCT coefficients** are chosen for robustness + imperceptibility balance.
- **Hash functions** used to generate signatures: SHA-1 (160 bits), SHA-256 (256 bits).
- **Trade-off Rule:** Higher robustness $\Rightarrow$ Lower capacity / Lower PSNR.
- **Key Difference:** Digital Signature = separate block; Signature Casting = *in-pixel embedding*.
- **Applications:** Medical imaging, CCTV forensics, copyright protection, KYC, military.
- **Verification:** Extract LSB $\rightarrow$ compare with recomputed hash $\rightarrow$ match/mismatch.
- **Bitwise operators to remember:** AND (`&`), OR (`|`), XOR (`^`), 0xFE mask, 0x01 mask.
- **BER** is used to quantify how many signature bits were corrupted during transmission/attack.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transform Domain Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT523 - DATA PRIVACY AND SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Transform Domain Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Transform Domain Methods** in Data Privacy and Security refer to a class of data-hiding techniques (steganography, watermarking, and covert communication) in which the cover media (image, audio, video) is mathematically transformed from its native **spatial/time domain** into a **frequency domain representation** before embedding the secret payload. The transformation is **invertible**, allowing perfect reconstruction of the cover after the secret bits have been embedded into carefully selected **frequency coefficients**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Transform domain methods are contrasted with spatial domain methods (LSB substitution). They are considered **more robust** against compression, cropping, and noise attacks because the secret data is spread across the frequency spectrum rather than relying on individual pixel bit-positions.

The three most academically significant transforms in this module are:

1. **Discrete Cosine Transform (DCT)** – Used in JPEG compression, the de-facto standard for image-based data hiding.
2. **Discrete Wavelet Transform (DWT)** – Used in JPEG2000, provides multi-resolution analysis.
3. **Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT)** – Phase-based hiding, robust to translation and certain geometric attacks.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — Whispering in a Noisy Stadium:**
> 
> Imagine you are at a cricket stadium where 100,000 fans are cheering. If you whisper a secret to a friend sitting next to you (**Spatial Domain / LSB**), your message is completely lost in the noise — even a slight disturbance (a wave, a camera flash) destroys the whisper.
> 
> Now imagine you instead **hum a low-frequency vibration** through the stadium's speakers. The vibration is **invisible** to the cheering fans, persists through the entire match, and can still be heard hours later even if the crowd noise pattern changes. The hum is your message hidden in the **frequency domain (Transform Domain)**.
> 
> **Spatial domain** = whispers in the noise.
> **Transform domain** = hidden vibrations in the structure of the sound.

The cover signal in spatial domain is $I(x,y)$ for an image. After transformation, we get coefficients $F(u,v)$ in the frequency domain. The secret data $W$ is then embedded as:

$$F'(u,v) = F(u,v) + \alpha \cdot W$$

where $\alpha$ is a **strength factor** controlling the trade-off between **imperceptibility** and **robustness**.

## 1.3 Why Transform Domain is the KTU Board Favourite

- **Imperceptibility:** Human Visual System (HVS) and Human Auditory System (HAS) are far less sensitive to small perturbations in the frequency domain than in the spatial domain.
- **Robustness:** Survives JPEG compression, low-pass filtering, noise addition.
- **Capacity:** Many coefficients can host secret bits simultaneously.
- **Invisibility to steganalysis:** Statistical properties of the cover are better preserved.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Pipeline

The general procedure for any transform domain data-hiding algorithm follows a **4-stage pipeline**:

### Stage 1 — Cover Transformation
The cover image $I(x,y)$ of size $M \times N$ is converted into frequency coefficients using a chosen transform $T$:

$$F(u,v) = T\{I(x,y)\}$$

### Stage 2 — Coefficient Selection
Not all coefficients are used for embedding. Strategically chosen **mid-frequency coefficients** are preferred because:
- **Low-frequency:** Perceptually critical — distortion is highly visible.
- **High-frequency:** Pruned by compression — data is lost easily.
- **Mid-frequency:** The "sweet spot" — invisible to HVS, robust to compression.

### Stage 3 — Embedding Rule
The most common rule is **additive spread-spectrum embedding**:

$$F'(u,v) = F(u,v) \cdot (1 + \alpha \cdot W)$$

Or the simpler **coefficient quantization** rule:

$$F'(u,v) = Q\left(\frac{F(u,v)}{\Delta} + W\right) \cdot \Delta$$

where $\Delta$ is the **quantization step size** and $Q(\cdot)$ is the quantization operator.

### Stage 4 — Inverse Transformation
The modified coefficients are inverse-transformed to produce the stego image:

$$I'(x,y) = T^{-1}\{F'(u,v)\}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Mid-Band?** KTU board questions often ask "Why are mid-frequency coefficients chosen?" The answer is the **Perceptual-Robustness Trade-off** — the human eye is insensitive to mid-frequency changes, and JPEG compression preserves these bands.

## 2.2 The Three Core Transforms — Quick Architecture

### A) Discrete Cosine Transform (DCT)

The 2D-DCT of an $M \times N$ block is:

$$F(u,v) = \alpha(u)\alpha(v) \sum_{x=0}^{M-1}\sum_{y=0}^{N-1} I(x,y) \cos\left[\frac{\pi(2x+1)u}{2M}\right] \cos\left[\frac{\pi(2y+1)v}{2N}\right]$$

with the normalization constants:

$$\alpha(u) = \begin{cases} \sqrt{\frac{1}{M}} & u=0 \\ \sqrt{\frac{2}{M}} & u \neq 0 \end{cases} \quad ; \quad \alpha(v) = \begin{cases} \sqrt{\frac{1}{N}} & v=0 \\ \sqrt{\frac{2}{N}} & v \neq 0 \end{cases}$$

The DC component $F(0,0)$ stores average intensity, and AC components store directional frequency content.

### B) Discrete Wavelet Transform (DWT)

DWT decomposes the image into four sub-bands using a low-pass filter $h_\phi$ and a high-pass filter $h_\psi$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{LL} &= \text{Low-Low (approximation)} \\
\text{LH} &= \text{Low-High (horizontal detail)} \\
\text{HL} &= \text{High-Low (vertical detail)} \\
\text{HH} &= \text{High-High (diagonal detail)}
\end{aligned}
$$

For Haar wavelet, the most basic DWT:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{LL}(i,j) &= \frac{I(2i,2j) + I(2i+1,2j) + I(2i,2j+1) + I(2i+1,2j+1)}{2} \\
\text{LH}(i,j) &= \frac{I(2i,2j) - I(2i+1,2j) + I(2i,2j+1) - I(2i+1,2j+1)}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

### C) Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT)

$$F(u,v) = \sum_{x=0}^{M-1}\sum_{y=0}^{N-1} I(x,y) \, e^{-j2\pi(ux/M + vy/N)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **DFT is complex-valued** — the magnitude $|F(u,v)|$ and phase $\angle F(u,v)$ carry different information. **Phase-based embedding** is preferred in KTU syllabus because the HVS is much more sensitive to phase changes than magnitude changes.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Symbols | Engineering Use |
|---|---------|---------|---------|-----------------|
| 1 | 2D-DCT | $F(u,v)=\alpha(u)\alpha(v)\sum\sum I(x,y)\cos[\pi(2x+1)u/2M]\cos[\pi(2y+1)v/2N]$ | $I$=pixel, $F$=coeff | JPEG compression & watermarking |
| 2 | DCT α-normalization | $\alpha(u)=\sqrt{1/M}$ if $u=0$ else $\sqrt{2/M}$ | orthogonality | Energy preservation |
| 3 | Additive Embedding | $F'(u,v)=F(u,v)+\alpha W$ | $\alpha$=strength | Spread-spectrum hiding |
| 4 | Quantization Index Modulation | $F'(u,v)=Q(F(u,v)/\Delta+W)\cdot\Delta$ | $\Delta$=step size | QIM steganography |
| 5 | DWT Haar Approx | $LL(i,j)=\frac{1}{2}[I_{00}+I_{10}+I_{01}+I_{11}]$ | pixel quadruple | Multi-resolution hiding |
| 6 | DFT | $F(u,v)=\sum\sum I(x,y)e^{-j2\pi(ux/M+vy/N)}$ | $j=\sqrt{-1}$ | Phase-based watermarking |
| 7 | IDFT (Inverse) | $I(x,y)=\frac{1}{MN}\sum\sum F(u,v)e^{j2\pi(ux/M+vy/N)}$ | full reconstruction | Stego recovery |
| 8 | PSNR (Imperceptibility) | $PSNR=10\log_{10}\left(\frac{255^2}{MSE}\right)$ dB | $MSE=\frac{1}{MN}\sum\sum(I-I')^2$ | Quality metric |
| 9 | Embedding Capacity | $C = N_c \cdot b$ bits | $N_c$=coefficients, $b$=bits/coeff | Throughput |
| 10 | Robustness — BER | $BER=\frac{\text{Bits in Error}}{\text{Total Bits}}$ | ratio | Attack resistance |
| 11 | Mid-band zone (DCT) | zigzag index $\in [k_1, k_2]$ | typical $k_1=2, k_2=25$ | Trade-off zone |
| 12 | Energy Compaction | $E_{DC} \gg E_{AC}$ for natural images | DC vs AC | Why DCT beats DFT |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The four sub-bands of DWT are the foundation of JPEG2000 watermarking. KTU exam favourite — memorize LL, LH, HL, HH meanings and the trade-off in which band to embed.**

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **DCT:** Used by **YouTube Content ID**, **Digimarc**, Facebook image fingerprinting, and broadcast watermarking of TV commercials.
- **DWT:** Used in **medical image watermarking** (DICOM standard), copyright protection of medical scans, JPEG2000 archives.
- **DFT:** Used in **audio watermarking** (SDMI standard) and **print-scan resilient** image watermarking (since DFT phase survives rotation).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation: 2D-DCT of a $2 \times 2$ Image Block

This is a KTU-favourite analytical problem. Consider a $2 \times 2$ image block:

$$I = \begin{bmatrix} 200 & 150 \\ 100 & 50 \end{bmatrix}$$

We compute the 2D-DCT coefficients $F(u,v)$ for $u,v \in \{0,1\}$.

### Step 1: Determine the normalization constants

For $M = N = 2$:

$$\alpha(0) = \sqrt{\frac{1}{2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}, \quad \alpha(1) = \sqrt{\frac{2}{2}} = 1$$

So we have $\alpha(0) = \alpha(1)$ in pairs; let's compute each coefficient.

### Step 2: Compute $F(0,0)$ — the DC component

$$
\begin{aligned}
F(0,0) &= \alpha(0)\alpha(0) \sum_{x=0}^{1}\sum_{y=0}^{1} I(x,y)\cos\left[\frac{\pi(2x+1)\cdot 0}{4}\right]\cos\left[\frac{\pi(2y+1)\cdot 0}{4}\right] \\
&= \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \cdot [200 + 150 + 100 + 50] \cdot \cos(0) \cdot \cos(0) \\
&= \frac{1}{2} \cdot 500 \cdot 1 \cdot 1 \\
&= 250
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** $F(0,0) = 250$ is the average intensity × 2 (due to $M=2$).

### Step 3: Compute $F(0,1)$ — horizontal AC

$$
\begin{aligned}
F(0,1) &= \alpha(0)\alpha(1) \sum_{x=0}^{1}\sum_{y=0}^{1} I(x,y)\cos(0)\cos\left[\frac{\pi(2y+1)\cdot 1}{4}\right] \\
&= \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \cdot 1 \cdot \sum_{x=0}^{1}\sum_{y=0}^{1} I(x,y) \cdot 1 \cdot \cos\left[\frac{\pi(2y+1)}{4}\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

Evaluate the cosine terms:
- $y=0$: $\cos(\pi/4) = 1/\sqrt{2} \approx 0.7071$
- $y=1$: $\cos(3\pi/4) = -1/\sqrt{2} \approx -0.7071$

$$
\begin{aligned}
F(0,1) &= \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \left[ 200 \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} + 150 \cdot \left(-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right) + 100 \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} + 50 \cdot \left(-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right) \right] \\
&= \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \cdot [200 - 150 + 100 - 50] \\
&= \frac{1}{2} \cdot 100 = 50
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Compute $F(1,0)$ — vertical AC

By symmetry with the above, swapping $x$ and $y$ roles:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F(1,0) &= \frac{1}{2} \cdot [200 - 100 + 150 - 50] = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 200 = 100
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: Compute $F(1,1)$ — diagonal AC

$$
\begin{aligned}
F(1,1) &= \alpha(1)\alpha(1) \sum_{x=0}^{1}\sum_{y=0}^{1} I(x,y)\cos\left[\frac{\pi(2x+1)}{4}\right]\cos\left[\frac{\pi(2y+1)}{4}\right] \\
&= 1 \cdot 1 \cdot \left[ 200 \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} + 150 \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \cdot \left(-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right) + 100 \cdot \left(-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right) \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} + 50 \cdot \left(-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right) \cdot \left(-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right) \right] \\
&= \frac{1}{2} \cdot [200 - 150 - 100 + 50] \\
&= \frac{1}{2} \cdot 0 = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

### Final Result — The DCT Block

$$F = \begin{bmatrix} 250 & 50 \\ 100 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

The energy compaction is visible: most energy is in $F(0,0) = 250$, the DC term.

## 3.2 Worked Example: Embedding One Bit in Mid-Frequency DCT

Suppose we want to embed a watermark bit $W = 1$ into coefficient $F(0,1) = 50$ with strength $\alpha = 0.1$.

### Additive Embedding Rule
$$F'(0,1) = F(0,1) + \alpha \cdot W = 50 + 0.1 \cdot 1 = 50.1$$

### Inverse DCT to Get Stego Pixel
After inverse DCT of the modified block, the resulting pixels will differ by a small, perceptually invisible amount.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Transform

| Property | DCT | DWT | DFT |
|----------|-----|-----|-----|
| **Basis** | Cosine functions | Wavelets (localized) | Complex exponentials |
| **Computational Cost** | $O(N \log N)$ | $O(N)$ | $O(N \log N)$ |
| **Multi-Resolution** | ❌ No | ✅ Yes (sub-bands) | ❌ No |
| **JPEG Compatibility** | ✅ Native | ❌ Needs JPEG2000 | ❌ Needs DFT-aware codec |
| **Geometric Attack Robustness** | Medium | Medium-High | **High (Phase)** |
| **Compression Robustness** | High | Very High | Medium |
| **HVS Exploitation** | Mid-band DCT | LH/HL/HH detail | Phase |
| **Energy Compaction** | Excellent | Good | Good |
| **Boundary Artifacts** | Yes (blocking) | **None** | None (but spectral leakage) |
| **Standardization** | JPEG, MPEG | JPEG2000 | SDMI audio |
| **Best Application** | Image watermarking | Medical / archival | Audio / print-scan |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** KTU often asks "Compare DCT and DWT" — the **key** points are multi-resolution, blocking artifacts, and JPEG2000 compatibility. State all three explicitly for full marks.

## 3.4 Watermark Extraction (Verification)

For blind extraction (no original cover), the receiver uses only the embedded bit rule:

$$\hat{W} = \text{sign}\left(F'(u,v) - \left\lfloor \frac{F'(u,v)}{\Delta} \right\rfloor \cdot \Delta\right)$$

For non-blind extraction:

$$\hat{W} = \frac{F'(u,v) - F(u,v)}{\alpha}$$

The extraction accuracy is measured by **Bit Error Rate (BER)**:

$$BER = \frac{\text{Number of incorrectly extracted bits}}{\text{Total embedded bits}} \times 100\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Robustness vs. Imperceptibility Trade-off (R-I Trade-off):**
> Higher $\alpha$ → higher robustness, lower PSNR (more visible).
> Lower $\alpha$ → higher PSNR (more invisible), lower robustness.
> KTU classic 7-mark question. Always draw this trade-off in graphical form.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 General Transform Domain Hiding Pipeline (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Original Cover Image I x y] --> B[Forward Transform DCT or DWT or DFT]
    B --> C[Frequency Coefficients F u v]
    C --> D[Coefficient Selection Mid Band]
    E[Secret Watermark W] --> F[Embedding Rule alpha strength]
    D --> F
    F --> G[Modified Coefficients F prime u v]
    G --> H[Inverse Transform]
    H --> I[Stego Image I prime x y]
    I --> J[Channel / Attack]
    J --> K[Received Stego]
    K --> L[Forward Transform]
    L --> M[Coefficient Extraction]
    E --> M
    M --> N[Extracted Watermark W hat]
```

## 4.2 DWT Sub-Band Decomposition (Level 1)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    IMG[Input Image I x y] --> ROW[Row Filter: Low h phi and High h psi]
    ROW --> DOWN[Downsample by 2]
    DOWN --> COL[Column Filter: Low h phi and High h psi]
    COL --> DOWNC[Downsample by 2]
    DOWNC --> LL[LL Approximation Low Low]
    DOWNC --> LH[LH Horizontal Detail]
    DOWNC --> HL[HL Vertical Detail]
    DOWNC --> HH[HH Diagonal Detail]
    LL -.embed here.-> LL1
    LH -.embed here.-> LH1
    HL -.embed here.-> HL1
    HH -.embed here.-> HH1
```

## 4.3 Embedding Strength Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start: Choose Embedding Band] --> Q1{What is the attack profile?}
    Q1 -- Compression attacks --> M1[Use Mid Band DCT or LH and HL of DWT]
    Q1 -- Geometric attacks --> M2[Use DFT Phase or DWT LH and HL]
    Q1 -- Print Scan --> M3[Use DFT Magnitude and Phase combined]
    M1 --> Q2{Need high capacity?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> M4[Use HH band or high frequency AC]
    Q2 -- No --> M5[Use LL band with low alpha]
    M2 --> Q2
    M3 --> Q2
    M4 --> END[Final Stego Image]
    M5 --> END
```

## 4.4 R-I Trade-off (Robustness-Imperceptibility) Curve — Conceptual Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Low alpha = 0.01] --> B[High PSNR 45 dB, Low Robustness, High Imperceptibility]
    C[High alpha = 0.5] --> D[Low PSNR 25 dB, High Robustness, Low Imperceptibility]
    E[Optimal alpha = 0.1] --> F[Balanced PSNR 38 dB, Acceptable Robustness, Imperceptible]
    B --> G[Trade off Curve]
    D --> G
    F --> G
```

## 4.5 Comparison Block: Spatial vs Transform Domain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S[Spatial Domain LSB] --> S1[Embeds in pixel bit positions]
    S1 --> S2[Easy to detect by histogram analysis]
    S1 --> S3[Low robustness to compression]
    T[Transform Domain DCT or DWT or DFT] --> T1[Embeds in frequency coefficients]
    T1 --> T2[Harder to detect statistically]
    T1 --> T3[High robustness to compression and noise]
    T1 --> T4[Mid band coefficients preferred]
    S2 --> R[Result: LSB is fast but fragile]
    S3 --> R
    T2 --> R2[Result: Transform is slower but robust]
    T3 --> R2
    T4 --> R2
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define transform domain methods in data hiding. Why are they preferred over spatial domain methods?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Transform domain methods are data-hiding techniques in which the cover media (image/audio) is first converted into the **frequency domain** using mathematical transforms such as **DCT, DWT, or DFT** before embedding the secret payload. The secret bits are embedded into carefully chosen **frequency coefficients**, and then the modified coefficients are inverse-transformed to obtain the stego media.

They are preferred over spatial domain (LSB) methods because:
1. They provide **higher robustness** against compression, noise, and filtering attacks.
2. The **Human Visual System (HVS)** is less sensitive to perturbations in frequency coefficients, ensuring better **imperceptibility**.
3. They preserve the **statistical properties** of the cover, making detection by steganalysis tools significantly harder.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**List the four sub-bands obtained in a single-level Discrete Wavelet Transform and state which one is preferred for watermark embedding and why.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The four sub-bands of a single-level 2D-DWT are:

1. **LL (Low-Low):** Approximation of the original image — contains most energy.
2. **LH (Low-High):** Horizontal edge details.
3. **HL (High-Low):** Vertical edge details.
4. **HH (High-High):** Diagonal edge details — high-frequency content.

For watermark embedding, the **LH and HL sub-bands** are preferred because they represent **mid-frequency details** that balance **imperceptibility** (the HVS is less sensitive to mid-frequency changes) and **robustness** (these bands survive compression better than HH).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A: 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a) With a neat block diagram, explain the general procedure for embedding a secret message in the transform domain using DCT. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The DCT-based embedding procedure consists of the following steps, organized as shown in the block diagram below:

**Step 1: Block Partitioning**
The original cover image $I$ of size $M \times N$ is divided into non-overlapping $8 \times 8$ pixel blocks, following the JPEG standard. Let each block be denoted $B_k(x,y)$ where $k = 1, 2, \dots, K$ is the block index.

**Step 2: Forward DCT**
For each block, the 2D-DCT is computed:

$$F_k(u,v) = \alpha(u)\alpha(v) \sum_{x=0}^{7}\sum_{y=0}^{7} B_k(x,y) \cos\left[\frac{\pi(2x+1)u}{16}\right] \cos\left[\frac{\pi(2y+1)v}{16}\right]$$

This produces 64 DCT coefficients per block: one DC coefficient $F(0,0)$ and 63 AC coefficients $F(u,v)$ with $(u,v) \neq (0,0)$.

**Step 3: Mid-Band Coefficient Selection**
Following the **zigzag scanning order** of JPEG, the mid-frequency coefficients (typically zigzag indices 4 to 24) are selected for embedding. These coefficients are perceptually less critical and survive JPEG quantization.

**Step 4: Watermark Embedding**
For each chosen coefficient $F_k(u,v)$, the secret bit $W_i \in \{0,1\}$ is embedded using the **additive rule**:

$$F'_k(u,v) = F_k(u,v) \cdot (1 + \alpha \cdot W_i)$$

where $\alpha$ is the **embedding strength** controlling the robustness–imperceptibility trade-off.

**Step 5: Inverse DCT**
The modified block is reconstructed via the 2D-IDCT:

$$B'_k(x,y) = \sum_{u=0}^{7}\sum_{v=0}^{7} \alpha(u)\alpha(v) F'_k(u,v) \cos\left[\frac{\pi(2x+1)u}{16}\right] \cos\left[\frac{\pi(2y+1)v}{16}\right]$$

**Step 6: Block Reconstruction**
All modified blocks are reassembled to form the **stego image** $I'$.

**Block Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    I[Cover Image I] --> P[Partition into 8x8 Blocks Bk]
    P --> D[Forward 2D DCT]
    D --> S[Select Mid Band Coeffs]
    W[Secret Bits W] --> E[Embedding Rule F prime = F 1 + alpha W]
    S --> E
    E --> ID[Inverse 2D DCT]
    ID --> R[Reassemble Blocks]
    R --> ST[Stego Image I prime]
```

---

**(b) Discuss the advantages and disadvantages of DCT, DWT, and DFT-based transform domain methods in data hiding. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Transform | Advantages | Disadvantages |
|-----------|------------|---------------|
| **DCT** | (i) Excellent energy compaction — most energy in DC and low-frequency AC coefficients. (ii) **Native compatibility with JPEG/MPEG**, so the stego image is robust to the most common lossy compression. (iii) Mature theory and fast algorithms. (iv) Mid-band selection is well standardized. | (i) Suffers from **blocking artifacts** at $8 \times 8$ block boundaries. (ii) Not multi-resolution — cannot capture localized features at different scales. (iii) Sensitive to geometric distortions (rotation, cropping). |
| **DWT** | (i) **Multi-resolution analysis** — captures both global and local features. (ii) **No blocking artifacts** — operates on the full image. (iii) Better matches the HVS's hierarchical perception. (iv) **JPEG2000 compatibility** for high-fidelity applications. | (i) Slightly lower energy compaction than DCT. (ii) Choice of wavelet basis (Haar, Daubechies, etc.) affects performance — adds a design parameter. (iii) More complex to implement and analyze. |
| **DFT** | (i) **Phase-based embedding** is highly robust to many image processing operations. (ii) **Translation-invariant** magnitude spectrum. (iii) **Rotation invariance** in magnitude after log-polar mapping. (iv) Superior for **audio watermarking** and print-scan resilience. | (i) Coefficients are **complex-valued** — both magnitude and phase must be handled. (ii) Lower energy compaction than DCT for natural images. (iii) Not natively compatible with JPEG — extra synchronization needed. (iv) Higher computational cost for large images. |

**Conclusion:** DCT is the **default for image watermarking** due to JPEG dominance. DWT is preferred for **medical/archival** applications needing multi-resolution. DFT is chosen when **geometric attack robustness** is paramount.

---

### **Question B: 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a) Explain the Discrete Wavelet Transform (DWT) based data hiding process. Illustrate with the four sub-bands and the embedding strategy. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Image Decomposition**
The original cover image $I(x,y)$ is decomposed using DWT by applying a pair of filters — a low-pass filter $h_\phi$ (scaling function) and a high-pass filter $h_\psi$ (wavelet function) — along rows and then along columns, with **downsampling by 2** at each stage.

**Step 2: Sub-band Formation**
This produces four sub-bands each of size $\frac{M}{2} \times \frac{N}{2}$:

- **LL:** Approximation — coarse representation of the image.
- **LH:** Captures **horizontal edges** (low-row frequency, high-column frequency).
- **HL:** Captures **vertical edges** (high-row frequency, low-column frequency).
- **HH:** Captures **diagonal edges** (high frequency in both directions).

**Step 3: Multi-Level Decomposition (Optional)**
The LL sub-band can be further decomposed recursively to obtain deeper levels (2-level, 3-level DWT), giving a hierarchical pyramid.

**Step 4: Embedding Strategy**
The secret bits $W = \{w_1, w_2, \dots, w_n\}$ are embedded into the **LH and HL sub-bands** of the chosen level. The additive rule:

$$C'(i,j) = C(i,j) + \alpha \cdot w_k$$

where $C(i,j)$ is the chosen DWT coefficient.

**Step 5: Reconstruction**
Inverse DWT (IDWT) is applied to all four sub-bands (with modified LH and HL) to reconstruct the **stego image** $I'$.

**Sub-band Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[Level 1 DWT]
    L1 --> L1LL[LL]
    L1 --> L1LH[LH Horizontal]
    L1 --> L1HL[HL Vertical]
    L1 --> L1HH[HH Diagonal]
    L1LL --> L2[Level 2 DWT on LL only]
    L2 --> L2LL[LL2]
    L2 --> L2LH[LH2]
    L2 --> L2HL[HL2]
    L2 --> L2HH[HH2]
    L1LH -.embed bits.-> E1
    L1HL -.embed bits.-> E2
    L2LH -.embed bits.-> E3
    L2HL -.embed bits.-> E4
```

**Why LH and HL?**
- LL carries most energy — embedding here causes visible distortion.
- HH is high-frequency — easily destroyed by compression.
- LH and HL represent **mid-frequency** details — invisible to HVS yet robust to compression.

---

**(b) What is the Robustness–Imperceptibility trade-off in transform domain data hiding? Explain with the role of the embedding strength parameter $\alpha$. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:**
The **Robustness–Imperceptibility (R-I) trade-off** is the fundamental design dilemma in transform domain data hiding:

- **Robustness** = ability of the embedded watermark to survive image processing attacks (compression, noise, filtering, cropping).
- **Imperceptibility** = how visually (or audibly) indistinguishable the stego media is from the original cover.

These two goals are **mutually conflicting** in practice, and the trade-off is controlled by the **embedding strength parameter $\alpha$**.

**Mathematical Expression:**
For the additive embedding rule:

$$F'(u,v) = F(u,v) + \alpha \cdot W$$

- If $\alpha$ is **small** (e.g., 0.01): The change in $F'(u,v)$ is tiny. The stego image looks almost identical to the cover (**high PSNR**, high imperceptibility). However, the embedded watermark can be easily destroyed by attacks (**low robustness**).
- If $\alpha$ is **large** (e.g., 0.5): The watermark is strongly embedded and survives attacks (**high robustness**), but the stego image shows visible distortion (**low PSNR**, low imperceptibility).

**Quantitative Metric:**
Imperceptibility is measured by **PSNR (Peak Signal-to-Noise Ratio)**:

$$PSNR = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{255^2}{MSE}\right) \text{ dB}, \quad \text{where} \quad MSE = \frac{1}{MN}\sum_{i=0}^{M-1}\sum_{j=0}^{N-1}[I(i,j) - I'(i,j)]^2$$

A PSNR of **40 dB or higher** is generally considered imperceptible to the human eye.

Robustness is measured by **Bit Error Rate (BER)** after an attack:

$$BER = \frac{\text{Number of wrongly extracted bits}}{\text{Total embedded bits}} \times 100\%$$

**Graphical View:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Increase alpha] --> B[PSNR decreases]
    A --> C[BER decreases, Robustness increases]
    D[Decrease alpha] --> E[PSNR increases]
    D --> F[BER increases, Robustness decreases]
    G[Optimal alpha 0.1] --> H[PSNR ~ 38 dB, BER ~ 5 percent]
```

**Practical Design Rule:**
Choose $\alpha$ such that **PSNR ≥ 38 dB** (imperceptibility constraint) while **BER ≤ 10%** after JPEG compression at quality factor 50 (robustness constraint). This typically yields $\alpha \in [0.05, 0.2]$ for 8-bit grayscale images.

**Conclusion:**
The R-I trade-off is the **central design principle** of all transform domain data hiding systems. No single algorithm can maximize both — the goal is to find the **Pareto-optimal** point that satisfies the application's specific requirements.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks (Common Pitfalls):**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the α-normalization constants** in DCT formulas: Always write $\alpha(u) = \sqrt{1/M}$ for $u=0$ and $\sqrt{2/M}$ otherwise. KTU examiners specifically check this — losing **1 full mark** if omitted.
> 
> 2. **Confusing LL with LH/HL/HH in DWT:** Students often embed in LL claiming "most energy" — this is WRONG for imperceptibility. Use LH/HL for balance, HH only for high-capacity fragile watermarking.
> 
> 3. **Mixing up embedding formulas:** The two main rules are:
>    - Additive: $F' = F + \alpha W$
>    - Multiplicative: $F' = F(1 + \alpha W)$
>    Writing the wrong one loses 2 marks.
> 
> 4. **Forgetting the 1/MN factor in IDFT.** It is a common slip-up. The forward transform has $1/MN$ in the inverse direction.
> 
> 5. **Not stating the choice of band in DWT.** Always explicitly say: "We embed in the LH and HL sub-bands because they represent mid-frequency content balancing imperceptibility and robustness."
> 
> 6. **PSNR unit omission:** Always include **dB** after PSNR value — KTU deducts half a mark if missing.
> 
> 7. **Skipping the zigzag scan mention in DCT watermarking** — the mid-band selection is conventionally done via the JPEG zigzag order, and examiners expect this term.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Transform Domain** = embedding in frequency coefficients after a reversible mathematical transform.
- [ ] **Three core transforms:** DCT (JPEG), DWT (JPEG2000, multi-resolution), DFT (phase, audio).
- [ ] **2D-DCT formula** with $\alpha(u)\alpha(v)$ normalization is mandatory to write.
- [ ] **DCT mid-band** = zigzag indices 4–24 in an $8 \times 8$ block.
- [ ] **DWT sub-bands:** LL (approximation), LH (horizontal), HL (vertical), HH (diagonal).
- [ ] **Best DWT bands for embedding:** LH and HL (mid-frequency, balanced).
- [ ] **Embedding rule (additive):** $F'(u,v) = F(u,v) + \alpha W$.
- [ ] **Embedding rule (multiplicative):** $F'(u,v) = F(u,v)(1 + \alpha W)$.
- [ ] **DFT is complex** — magnitude vs. phase: phase is more perceptually important, but both can be used.
- [ ] **PSNR formula** = $10 \log_{10}(255^2 / MSE)$ in dB. Threshold for imperceptibility ≈ **38–40 dB**.
- [ ] **MSE** = $\frac{1}{MN}\sum\sum (I - I')^2$.
- [ ] **BER** = wrong bits / total bits × 100%. Robustness metric.
- [ ] **R-I trade-off** is the central design tension — controlled by $\alpha$.
- [ ] **Inverse transform** must always be applied after embedding to return to spatial domain.
- [ ] **Why transform domain?** Robustness + imperceptibility + steganalysis resistance.
- [ ] **Why not LL of DWT?** It is perceptually critical — embedding causes visible distortion.
- [ ] **Why not HH of DWT?** Easily pruned by compression — low robustness.
- [ ] **Real-world applications:** YouTube Content ID (DCT), medical image watermarking (DWT), SDMI audio (DFT), print-scan resilience (DFT phase).
- [ ] **Block size in DCT:** Standard is $8 \times 8$ for JPEG compatibility.
- [ ] **DWT filter pair:** $h_\phi$ (low-pass) and $h_\psi$ (high-pass), followed by 2× downsampling.
- [ ] **Haar wavelet is the simplest DWT** — averaging and differencing of pixel pairs.
- [ ] **KTU Examiner's favourite comparison:** "Compare DCT and DWT" — always list: multi-resolution, blocking artifacts, JPEG compatibility, and HVS matching.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Robust Data Hiding in JPEG Images
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECDT523 - DATA PRIVACY AND SECURITY > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Robust Data Hiding in JPEG Images # Module 3 — Robust Data Hiding in JPEG Images ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition **Robust Data Hiding in JPEG Images** is the process of imperceptibly embedding secret information (a *payload*) into a JPEG-compressed cover image such that the hidden data can be reliably recovered even after the image undergoes common image-processing operations (re-compression, filtering, noise addition, transcoding, etc.), while the visual quality of the host image remains acceptable. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, it is formally stated as: > **Definition.** *Robust data hiding* (a.k.a. *watermarking*) is a communication task in which an embedder secretly transmits a message $m \in \{0,1\}^{L}$ to a receiver by slightly modifying a cover medium $X$ (here, a JPEG bitstream) so that the extracted message $\hat{m}$ at the receiver satisfies a fidelity constraint on $Y = \text{Embed}(X, m)$ and a robustness constraint $\Pr[\hat{m} \neq m] \leq \varepsilon$ under an attack channel $\mathcal{A}$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECDT523 / M3):** > Students must be able to **distinguish robust vs. fragile** hiding, **describe the JPEG embedding domain (DCT coefficients)**, and **list the trade-off triangle**: *Imperceptibility – Capacity – Robustness*. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy Think of a *postage stamp* on an envelope. The letter inside is the secret message, the envelope is the JPEG image, and the stamp is the hidden watermark. If the envelope gets wrinkled, photocopied, or scanned, the stamp should still be recognizable — that is **robustness**. At the same time, the recipient must not even notice the stamp is there — that is **imperceptibility**. And finally, you should be able to encode enough information (the postmark code) — that is **capacity**. These three competing demands form the famous **Water-Holding Triangle** of data hiding: $$ \text{Imperceptibility} \;\longleftrightarrow\; \text{Capacity} \;\longleftrightarrow\; \text{Robustness} $$ ### 1.3 Why JPEG? JPEG is the *de-facto* image format on the Internet (≥ 70% of stored images). Therefore, a watermark that does not survive JPEG compression is useless in practice. The hostile environment for a JPEG-embedded payload is the **recompression attack** — a benign user simply saving the image as JPEG at any quality factor $Q \in [1, 100]$. ### 1.4 Important Constants / Standard Metrics The following standard values/metrics are used throughout the chapter: - **PSNR** (Peak Signal-to-Noise Ratio) — measured in **dB**. - **SSIM** (Structural Similarity Index) — dimensionless, range $[-1, 1]$, typically $\geq 0.95$ considered imperceptible. - **BER** (Bit-Error Rate) — dimensionless, target $\leq 10^{-2}$ after attack. - **JPEG Standard Quantization Table** — 8×8 luminance/chrominance tables defined in the **ITU-T T.81** recommendation. - **Default Quality Factor** — $Q = 75$ for IJG (Independent JPEG Group) reference implementation. - **Block size** for DCT — $N = 8$ (pixels). - **Color channels** — Y (luminance), Cb, Cr (chrominance) in YCbCr space. > [!NOTE] > Robust hiding ≠ Steganography. Steganography aims at *undetectability* (security against steganalysts) and is **fragile**. Robust hiding aims at *survivability* against signal-processing attacks and is **publicly readable** by design (the watermark is meant to be detected, not hidden). # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The JPEG Compression Pipeline (Recap) A JPEG encoder applies the following stages: 1. Color-space conversion: $\text{RGB} \to \text{YCbCr}$. 2. Chroma subsampling (4:2:0 or 4:2:2). 3. Splitting each channel into non-overlapping $8 \times 8$ pixel blocks. 4. Level shift: subtract $128$ from every pixel. 5. 2-D **Discrete Cosine Transform (DCT)** on each block. 6. **Quantization** of the 64 DCT coefficients using a quantization table $\mathbf{Q}(Q)$. 7. Zig-zag scan of quantized coefficients $\to$ 1-D sequence. 8. **Run-Length + Huffman** entropy coding into the bitstream. JPEG decoding reverses stages 8 → 1. The lossy step is stage 6. ## 2.2 2-D DCT and IDCT (8×8 Block) Let $f(x, y)$ be the pixel value at position $(x, y)$ in an $8 \times 8$ block, with $x, y \in \{0, 1, \dots, 7\}$. ### Forward DCT (FDCT) $$ F(u, v) \;=\; \frac{1}{4}\, C(u)\, C(v) \sum_{x=0}^{7}\sum_{y=0}^{7} f(x,y)\, \cos\!\left[\frac{(2x+1)u\pi}{16}\right]\, \cos\!\left[\frac{(2y+1)v\pi}{16}\right] $$ ### Inverse DCT (IDCT) $$ f(x, y) \;=\; \frac{1}{4}\sum_{u=0}^{7}\sum_{v=0}^{7} C(u)\, C(v)\, F(u, v)\, \cos\!\left[\frac{(2x+1)u\pi}{16}\right]\, \cos\!\left[\frac{(2y+1)v\pi}{16}\right] $$ where the normalization constant is $$ C(k) \;=\; \begin{cases} \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}, & k = 0 \\[6pt] 1, & k = 1, 2, \dots, 7 \end{cases} $$ > [!NOTE] > $F(0, 0)$ is the **DC coefficient** (mean luminance of the block). All other 63 coefficients are **AC coefficients**. Robust data-hiding schemes almost always embed in **mid-frequency AC coefficients** to balance imperceptibility and robustness. ## 2.3 Quantization & Dequantization The standard JPEG quantizer uses the quality factor $Q$ to scale a base table $\mathbf{T}$: $$ Q_{ij}(Q) \;=\; \left\lfloor \frac{\,T_{ij}\,\cdot\, \alpha(Q)\, + \delta\,}{1} \right\rfloor, \quad \alpha(Q) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{50}{Q}, & 1 \le Q < 50 \\[6pt] 2 - \dfrac{2Q}{100}, & 50 \le Q \le 99 \\[6pt] 1, & Q = 100 \end{cases} $$ Quantization of a DCT coefficient: $$ \hat{F}(u, v) \;=\; \mathrm{round}\!\left(\frac{F(u, v)}{Q_{uv}(Q)}\right) $$ Dequantization (used by the decoder / the data-hider): $$ \tilde{F}(u, v) \;=\; \hat{F}(u, v) \cdot Q_{uv}(Q) $$ **Key insight for robust hiding:** Any modification $\Delta$ to $\hat{F}(u, v)$ survives recompression **iff** the magnitude of the perturbation, after dequantization and re-quantization, remains inside the *same* quantization bin: $$ \left\lfloor \frac{\hat{F}(u, v) \pm \Delta'}{Q_{uv}} \right\rceil \;=\; \hat{F}(u, v) \pm \Delta $$ This is the central design constraint of every robust JPEG-hiding scheme. ## 2.4 Communication-Theoretic View of Watermarking The data-hider can be modelled as a **channel** with the attack as additional noise: $$ \mathbf{Y} \;=\; \mathbf{X} \;+\; \mathbf{W} \;+\; \mathbf{N} $$ where $\mathbf{X}$ is the cover DCT block, $\mathbf{W}$ is the watermarking perturbation carrying the message, and $\mathbf{N}$ is the attacker's noise. The capacity (in bits per coefficient) under Gaussian assumptions and an SNR constraint $\sigma_W^2 \le \sigma_{\max}^2$ is given by the classical Costa capacity: $$ C_{\text{Costa}} \;=\; \frac{1}{2}\log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{\sigma_W^2}{\sigma_N^2}\right) \quad \text{[bits per channel use]} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU expects the Costa / Erez–Zamir framework to be mentioned by name in 14-mark answers. ## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula | Range / Unit | |---|----------|---------|--------------| | 1 | DCT of 8×8 block | $F(u,v) = \tfrac{1}{4}C(u)C(v)\sum_x\sum_y f(x,y)\cos[\,]$ | $F \in \mathbb{R}$ | | 2 | Quality-scaled quantizer | $Q_{uv}(Q) = \left\lfloor T_{uv}\alpha(Q) \right\rfloor$ | $Q_{uv} \ge 1$ | | 3 | Quantization step | $\hat{F} = \mathrm{round}\!\left(F/Q_{uv}\right)$ | integer | | 4 | MSE | $\mathrm{MSE} = \frac{1}{N^2}\sum_{i,j}(f_{ij} - f'_{ij})^2$ | non-negative | | 5 | PSNR | $\mathrm{PSNR} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{255^2}{\mathrm{MSE}}\right)$ | dB | | 6 | SSIM | $\mathrm{SSIM}(x,y) = \dfrac{(2\mu_x\mu_y + c_1)(2\sigma_{xy}+c_2)}{(\mu_x^2+\mu_y^2+c_1)(\sigma_x^2+\sigma_y^2+c_2)}$ | $[-1, 1]$ | | 7 | Embedding rate | $R = \frac{\text{payload bits}}{\text{non-zero AC coefficients}}$ | bits/coefficient | | 8 | Robustness (BER) | $\mathrm{BER} = \frac{\#\{\,i:\hat{m}_i \neq m_i\,\}}{L}$ | $[0, 1]$ | | 9 | Document fidelity | $\mathrm{PSNR} \ge 35\,\mathrm{dB}$ ⇒ "imperceptible" | dB | | 10 | Costa capacity | $C = \tfrac{1}{2}\log_2(1 + \sigma_W^2/\sigma_N^2)$ | bits/use | | 11 | DCT energy (Parseval) | $\sum_{u,v} F(u,v)^2 = \tfrac{1}{4}\sum_{u,v} C(u)^2 C(v)^2 (\ldots)$ | dimensionless | | 12 | Robustness radius | $\vert\Delta\vert \le \tfrac{1}{2}Q_{uv}(Q_{\text{att}})$ | integer | > [!NOTE] > The pipeline that connects **the block $F(u,v)$ → quantizer → attacker's re-quantizer → dequantizer** is the *key* mental picture students must carry into the exam hall. ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Applications 1. **Copyright protection of digital photographs** for stock-image agencies (Getty, Shutterstock). 2. **Broadcast monitoring** of TV commercials via embedded watermark in every frame. 3. **Tamper localization** in forensic imaging of crime scenes. 4. **Copy/print control** in enterprise DRM (e.g., Digimarc on LaserJet printers). 5. **Medical-image integrity** in DICOM (HIPAA-compliant watermarking). # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Forward DCT Basis Functions Starting from the 1-D DCT-II kernel $$ \phi_k(n) \;=\; \alpha_k \cos\!\left[\frac{\pi(2n+1)k}{2N}\right], \quad \alpha_k = \sqrt{\frac{2}{N}} \cdot \begin{cases} 1/\sqrt{2}, & k=0 \\ 1, & k>0 \end{cases} $$ The 2-D DCT is **separable**: applying the 1-D transform along rows, then along columns. $$ \begin{aligned} \mathbf{F} \;=\; \mathbf{C}\, \mathbf{f}\, \mathbf{C}^{\top} \end{aligned} $$ where $\mathbf{C}$ is the $8 \times 8$ orthonormal DCT matrix with entries $$ C_{ku} \;=\; \alpha_k \cos\!\left[\frac{\pi(2u+1)k}{16}\right], \quad k,u = 0, \dots, 7 $$ Substituting and expanding: $$ \begin{aligned} F(u,v) \;=\; \sum_{k=0}^{7}\sum_{l=0}^{7} C_{ku}\, f(k, l)\, C_{lv} \end{aligned} $$ which reduces to the textbook form using $C(0) = 1/\sqrt{2}$ and $C(k) = 1$ for $k > 0$: $$ \begin{aligned} F(u,v) \;=\; \tfrac{1}{4}\,C(u)C(v)\sum_{k=0}^{7}\sum_{l=0}^{7} f(k,l)\, \cos\!\left[\frac{(2k+1)u\pi}{16}\right]\, \cos\!\left[\frac{(2l+1)v\pi}{16}\right] \end{aligned} $$ **Logical commentary.** The matrix form $\mathbf{F} = \mathbf{C}\mathbf{f}\mathbf{C}^{\top}$ shows that DCT is an *orthogonal* similarity transform; therefore it preserves energy (Parseval's theorem): $$ \sum_{u,v} F(u,v)^2 \;=\; \sum_{x,y} f(x,y)^2 $$ Hence, modification of coefficients in the DCT domain is mathematically equivalent (up to a unitary rotation) to modification in the spatial domain — but it allows frequency-selective embedding. ## 3.2 Derivation: Quantization Noise Power Let $F$ be a DCT coefficient and $Q$ the corresponding quantizer step. After quantization: $$ \hat{F} \;=\; \mathrm{round}\!\left(\frac{F}{Q}\right), \quad \tilde{F} \;=\; \hat{F}\, Q $$ Quantization error: $$ e \;=\; F - \tilde{F}, \quad -\tfrac{Q}{2} \le e \le \tfrac{Q}{2} $$ Assuming $e$ is uniformly distributed (a standard assumption used in the JPEG standard): $$ \sigma_e^2 \;=\; \int_{-Q/2}^{Q/2} e^2 \cdot \frac{1}{Q}\, de \;=\; \frac{Q^2}{12} $$ $$ \boxed{\;\sigma_{\text{quant}}^2 \;=\; \dfrac{Q_{uv}^2(Q)}{12}\;} $$ **Implication for robust hiding.** A perturbation of magnitude $\vert\Delta\vert \cdot Q_{uv}$ is required to flip a bin. The expected noise variance introduced by *recompression* on the $(u, v)$ coefficient is $\sigma_{\text{recomp}}^2 = Q_{uv}(Q_{\text{att}})^2 / 12$. The detector's signal-to-noise ratio becomes $$ \begin{aligned} \mathrm{SNR}_{\text{det}} \;=\; \frac{(\Delta Q_{uv})^2}{\sigma_{\text{recomp}}^2 + \sigma_{\text{noise}}^2} \;=\; \frac{12\,\Delta^2\, Q_{uv}^2(Q_{\text{host}})}{Q_{uv}^2(Q_{\text{att}}) + 12\sigma_{\text{noise}}^2} \end{aligned} $$ This is the central formula for designing embedding strength. ## 3.3 Derivation: PSNR from Embedding Distortion Let the cover pixel block be $\mathbf{f}$ and the modified block be $\mathbf{f}' = \mathbf{f} + \mathbf{d}$ with distortion matrix $\mathbf{d}$ of size $8 \times 8$. The mean-square error over the block is $$ \mathrm{MSE}_{\text{block}} \;=\; \frac{1}{64}\sum_{i=1}^{8}\sum_{j=1}^{8} d_{ij}^2 $$ The PSNR over the full image of $B$ blocks is $$ \mathrm{MSE} \;=\; \frac{1}{64B}\sum_{b=1}^{B}\sum_{i,j} d_{ij}^{(b)2} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \mathrm{PSNR} \;=\; 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{255^2}{\mathrm{MSE}}\right) \;\; \text{dB} \end{aligned} $$ For the special case of perturbing a *single* DCT coefficient $F(u_0, v_0)$ by $\pm \Delta$ in one block, the spatial-domain distortion has the closed form $$ d(x, y) \;=\; \pm \frac{\Delta \cdot Q_{u_0 v_0}}{4}\,C(u_0)C(v_0)\cos\!\left[\frac{(2x+1)u_0\pi}{16}\right]\cos\!\left[\frac{(2y+1)v_0\pi}{16}\right] $$ Squaring and summing over the block: $$ \begin{aligned} \sum_{x,y} d(x,y)^2 \;=\; \frac{\Delta^2 Q_{u_0 v_0}^2}{16}\, C(u_0)^2 C(v_0)^2 \cdot 8 \cdot 8 \cdot \frac{1}{2 \cdot 2} \;=\; \frac{\Delta^2 Q_{u_0 v_0}^2}{2} \end{aligned} $$ Therefore, the per-block MSE for a single AC coefficient change is $$ \mathrm{MSE}_{\text{block}} \;=\; \frac{\Delta^2 Q_{u_0 v_0}^2}{128} $$ Insert into the PSNR expression to obtain the **KPI** for any DCT-domain hiding scheme. ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style) **Problem.** A $512 \times 512$ 8-bit grayscale image is JPEG-compressed with $Q = 75$. The luminance quantizer step at position $(4, 5)$ equals $Q_{4,5} = 16$. You embed one bit by flipping the parity of the quantized coefficient $\hat{F}(4,5)$ from 11 to 12 (i.e., $\Delta = 1$). Compute the PSNR of the watermarked image. **Solution.** Step 1 — Compute the per-block MSE. $$ \mathrm{MSE}_{\text{block}} \;=\; \frac{(1)^2 (16)^2}{128} \;=\; \frac{256}{128} \;=\; 2 $$ Step 2 — Compute the global MSE (assumed constant across all $B = (512/8)^2 = 4096$ blocks). $$ \mathrm{MSE} \;=\; 2 $$ Step 3 — Compute PSNR. $$ \mathrm{PSNR} \;=\; 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{255^2}{2}\right) \;=\; 10 \log_{10}(32512.5) \;\approx\; 45.12\,\mathrm{dB} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > $\mathrm{PSNR} = 45.12\,\mathrm{dB} > 35\,\mathrm{dB}$, so the embedding is **imperceptible** by KTU convention. The same calculation with $\Delta = 3$ yields $\mathrm{PSNR} \approx 39.6\,\mathrm{dB}$ — still acceptable but visibly degraded on smooth regions. ## 3.5 Derivation: Robustness Against Recompression Attack Suppose the watermarked image is recompressed with quality $Q_{\text{att}} < Q_{\text{host}}$ (e.g., $Q_{\text{att}} = 50$). The new quantizer step at $(u_0, v_0)$ is $Q'_{u_0 v_0}$. The embedded coefficient changes from $\hat{F} = 11$ to $$ \hat{F}_{\text{recomp}} \;=\; \mathrm{round}\!\left(\frac{12 \cdot 16}{Q'_{4,5}}\right) $$ Using the IJG table: $Q'_{4,5}(50) = 16$ as well (since $50/75$ is close to 1 and the rounding is symmetric). The bit therefore **survives**. If $Q_{\text{att}} = 25$, then $Q'_{4,5} = 32$ and $$ \hat{F}_{\text{recomp}} \;=\; \mathrm{round}\!\left(\frac{12 \cdot 16}{32}\right) \;=\; 6 $$ The detector may now see a different parity — **the bit is lost**. **Design rule (KTU high-yield):** $$ \boxed{\;\text{Choose } \Delta \cdot Q_{uv}(Q_{\text{host}}) \;>\; \tfrac{1}{2} Q_{uv}(Q_{\text{att}})\;} $$ to guarantee survival against recompression down to quality $Q_{\text{att}}$. ## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Major Robust JPEG Watermarking Schemes | Scheme | Year | Embedding Domain | Robustness | Imperceptibility | Capacity | KTU Expected? | |--------|------|------------------|------------|------------------|----------|----------------| | **JSteg** | 1996 | LSB of DCT | Very Low | High | High | Mention only | | **F5** | 2001 | ±1 mod AC coeff. | Low–Medium | High | Medium | Yes | | **OutGuess** | 2001 | LSB of AC, ±1 parity | Medium | High | Medium | Yes | | **Cox (Spread-Spectrum)** | 1997 | Mid-freq. AC | High | Medium | Low | Yes | | **Podilchuk–Zeng (IBE)** | 1998 | AC, perceptual mask | High | Very High | Medium | Optional | | **QIM / DM** | 1999 | DCT via quantizer | Very High | Medium | Medium | Yes | | **Syndrome-Trellis (STC)** | 2011 | Cover-adap. parity | Very High | High | High | Yes | | **Wu–Xie (Distortion-Compensated QIM)** | 2015 | DCT with DC | Very High | High | High | Optional | > [!IMPORTANT] > For 14-mark KTU answers, the expected payload is a **derivation of QIM** and a **worked example of STC** (Syndrome-Trellis Codes) — both are listed in the KTU 2024 Module 3 syllabus. ## 3.7 Quantization Index Modulation (QIM) — Derivation The QIM embedder uses two quantizers $\mathbf{Q}_0$ and $\mathbf{Q}_1$ that are *shifted* by a step $\Delta$: $$ Q_m(x) \;=\; \mathrm{round}\!\left(\frac{x}{\Delta}\right) \cdot \Delta + \frac{m \Delta}{2}, \quad m \in \{0, 1\} $$ Embedding: $$ y \;=\; Q_m(x), \quad m = \text{bit to embed} $$ Extraction (minimum-distance decoder): $$ \hat{m} \;=\; \arg\min_{m \in \{0,1\}} \vert y - Q_m(y) \vert $$ The maximum distortion introduced is $\Delta/2$ per sample; the **provable robustness radius** is $\Delta/4$ (Costa-style). Hence $$ \boxed{\; \rho_{\text{QIM}} \;=\; \dfrac{\Delta}{4}\;} $$ > [!NOTE] > The robust QIM variant, called **Distortion-Compensated QIM (DC-QIM)**, adds a *scaled compensation* term $\alpha(y - Q_m(x))$ with $0 < \alpha < 1$, trading distortion for increased robustness: $$ y_{\text{DC-QIM}} \;=\; (1 - \alpha)\, Q_m(x) \;+\; \alpha\, x $$ ## 3.8 Syndrome-Trellis Code (STC) — Sketch Derivation Given a host vector $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n$ and a payload $\mathbf{m} \in \{0,1\}^L$, STC solves: $$ \mathbf{y} \;=\; \arg\min_{\mathbf{z} : \mathbf{H}\mathbf{z} = \mathbf{m}} \;\Vert\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{z}\Vert^2 $$ where $\mathbf{H}$ is a parity-check matrix of a convolutional code. The solution is found by the **Viterbi algorithm** on a trellis with $2^{h}$ states, where $h$ is the constraint length. Extraction is a single matrix multiplication: $\hat{\mathbf{m}} = \mathbf{H}\mathbf{y}$. Capacity (with parameter $\alpha$): $$ R \;=\; \frac{L}{n} \;=\; \frac{1}{1 + \alpha^2 / 12} $$ For $\alpha = 0.5$: $$ R \;\approx\; \frac{1}{1 + 0.0208} \;\approx\; 0.98 \quad \text{bits / AC coefficient} $$ This explains the dominance of STC in modern JPEG watermarking. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 High-Level Embedding Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart LR src["Cover Image X
(BMP / PNG)"] --> jpegEnc["JPEG Encoder
Q_host"] jpegEnc --> dctBlock["DCT 8x8
Blocks F(u,v)"] dctBlock --> quantStep["Quantize to
F_hat(u,v)"] quantStep --> embMod["Embed Module
QIM / STC / Cox"] payload["Secret Message m"] --> embMod embMod --> encBitstream["JPEG Bitstream
(Watermarked)"] encBitstream --> channel["Transmission / Storage
(Attacks may occur)"] channel --> recvBitstream["Received Bitstream"] recvBitstream --> dec1["JPEG Decoder"] dec1 --> dctRecov["Recovered DCT Coeff.
Y_hat(u,v)"] dctRecov --> extMod["Extraction Module
QIM / STC / Cox"] extMod --> mhat["Recovered Message m_hat"] ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Robust JPEG Hider (STC Variant) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph A["Pre-Processing Stage"] A1["RGB to YCbCr
conversion"] --> A2["8x8 block
partitioning"] A2 --> A3["Level shift
f = f - 128"] A3 --> A4["Forward DCT
on each block"] end subgraph B["Embedding Stage"] B1["Quantize with
Q_host table"] --> B2["Zig-zag scan
1-D sequence"] B2 --> B3["Select mid-frequency
AC coefficients"] B3 --> B4["STC syndrome encoder
min ||x - y||^2 s.t. H y = m"] B4 --> B5["Perturbed AC
coefficients"] end subgraph C["Coding Stage"] B5 --> C1["Dequantize
(for visual masking)"] C1 --> C2["Inverse DCT
on blocks"] C2 --> C3["Level shift +128"] C3 --> C4["Re-quantize with
Q_host table"] C4 --> C5["Huffman + RLE
encode bitstream"] end subgraph D["Receiver / Detection Stage"] D1["JPEG decode
to DCT"] --> D2["Select same
AC positions"] D2 --> D3["Compute
m_hat = H y_hat"] D3 --> D4["Compare with m
BER computation"] end C5 --> D1 ``` ## 4.3 The Water-Holding Trade-off Triangle ```mermaid flowchart LR imp["Imperceptibility
(PSNR, SSIM)"] -- tension --> cap["Capacity
(bits/image)"] cap -- tension --> rob["Robustness
(BER vs attack)"] rob -- tension --> imp imp -. cannot maximise all three .-> cap cap -. cannot maximise all three .-> rob rob -. cannot maximise all three .-> imp ``` > [!NOTE] > In the KTU viva, be ready to **point at any vertex** of the triangle and state *which class of scheme occupies that corner* — e.g., JSteg maximises capacity but loses robustness, while Spread-Spectrum (Cox) maximises robustness but loses capacity. ## 4.4 Attack Model Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB wm["Watermarked
JPEG Y"] --> a1["Recompression
(Q_att < Q_host)"] wm --> a2["Gaussian Noise
N(0, sigma^2)"] wm --> a3["Median Filter
3x3 or 5x5"] wm --> a4["Cropping &
Geometric Attack"] wm --> a5["MPEG
Compression"] a1 --> recv["Attacked Image
Y_tilde"] a2 --> recv a3 --> recv a4 --> recv a5 --> recv recv --> det["Detector
H y_tilde = m_hat"] ``` ## 4.5 Decision Region of a QIM Detector (Conceptual) ```mermaid flowchart LR axis["Real Line x-axis
(DCT coefficient value)"] q0["...Q0 region: -2.5 .. -0.5 .. 1.5 .. 3.5..."] --> det0["bit = 0"] q1["...Q1 region: -2.0 .. 0 .. 2.0 .. 4.0..."] --> det1["bit = 1"] axis --> q0 axis --> q1 ``` > Each coefficient value belongs to **one of two interleaved lattices**. A perturbation of size less than $\Delta/2$ keeps the sample inside the original lattice and the bit is decoded correctly. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, PECDT523]` **Differentiate between robust watermarking and steganography. List the three fundamental requirements of any data-hiding system.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Robust Watermarking | Steganography | |---|---|---| | Goal | Survive signal-processing attacks | Avoid detection by steganalyst | | Adversary | Removes / de-syncs the signal | Detects the presence of the hidden data | | Typical use | Copyright, broadcast monitoring | Covert communication | | Robustness class | Robust | Fragile | | Detector | Public (knows key) | Private (knows full scheme) | **The three fundamental requirements (Cox–Kalker framework):** 1. **Imperceptibility** — the modified image should be visually indistinguishable from the cover (PSNR $\ge 35$ dB, SSIM $\ge 0.95$). 2. **Robustness** — the embedded message must survive a defined set of attacks (compression, filtering, noise). 3. **Capacity** — a sufficient number of payload bits must be carried per image (typically $\ge 100$ bits). --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, PECDT523]` **Why are mid-frequency DCT coefficients preferred over low- and high-frequency coefficients for robust data hiding in JPEG images?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Low-frequency coefficients** (including the DC term) carry most of the image energy and are **visually critical** — modifying them causes large perceptual distortion; also, they are heavily quantized and *not robust* against recompression. - **High-frequency coefficients** correspond to edges and fine textures that are **heavily quantized** in JPEG (often rounded to zero). Any embedded bit is destroyed by a single re-compression. - **Mid-frequency AC coefficients** strike a balance: they survive moderate quantization and are perceptually masked by mid-band image content (Watson's perceptual model). The luminance quantizer step at mid-band ($Q_{uv} \in [9, 24]$ for $Q = 75$) is small enough to preserve an embedded perturbation $\Delta \cdot Q_{uv}$ through a wide range of recompression attacks. A typical mid-band range is $u, v \in [2, 6]$, excluding $(0, \cdot)$ and $(\cdot, 0)$. --- ## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, PECDT523 Module 3]` **(a)** With neat block diagrams, explain the process of **embedding and extracting** a robust watermark in a JPEG image using the **Spread-Spectrum (Cox) method**. Mention the role of the pseudo-noise sequence and the correlation detector. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A $256 \times 256$ 8-bit grayscale image is JPEG-compressed with $Q = 75$. The quantizer step at position $(3, 4)$ is $Q_{3,4} = 12$. You embed one bit by adding $\Delta = 1$ to the quantized coefficient at $(3, 4)$ in every block. Compute the **PSNR** of the watermarked image. State whether the modification is **imperceptible** by KTU convention. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Q.A (a) — 7 marks **1. Embedding block diagram (2 marks):** 1. Compute the 2-D DCT of each $8 \times 8$ block of the cover image. 2. Quantize the 64 DCT coefficients using $Q_{\text{host}}$. 3. Generate a pseudo-noise (PN) sequence $w_k \in \{-1, +1\}$ using a key $K$. 4. For each of the $n$ selected mid-frequency AC coefficients, add $w_k \cdot \alpha$, where $\alpha$ is the embedding strength, to embed a "1"; subtract to embed a "0". 5. Dequantize, IDCT, and re-encode as JPEG. **2. Mathematical embedding (2 marks):** For cover DCT vector $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n$ and message bit $m \in \{-1, +1\}$: $$ \mathbf{y} \;=\; \mathbf{x} \;+\; \alpha\, m\, \mathbf{w} $$ The signal-to-document ratio (SDR) constraint is $$ \frac{1}{n}\sum_{k=1}^{n} \alpha^2 w_k^2 \;=\; \alpha^2 \;\le\; \sigma_{\max}^2 $$ **3. Detection (correlation receiver) (2 marks):** $$ z \;=\; \frac{1}{n}\,\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{y} \;=\; \frac{1}{n}\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x} \;+\; \frac{\alpha}{n}\,\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{w}\, m $$ Decision rule: $\hat{m} = \mathrm{sign}(z)$. **4. Properties of the PN sequence (1 mark):** - $E[w_k] = 0$, $\mathrm{Var}(w_k) = 1$, and $\frac{1}{n}\sum_{k=1}^{n} w_k w'_k \to 0$ for any other independent PN sequence $w'$. - The PN sequence spreads the watermark energy across all $n$ coefficients, making it **resistant to cropping and filtering**. --- #### Model Solution for Q.A (b) — 7 marks **Step 1 — Number of blocks (1 mark):** $$ B \;=\; \left(\frac{256}{8}\right)^2 \;=\; 32^2 \;=\; 1024 $$ **Step 2 — Per-block MSE due to a single AC perturbation (2 marks):** From the derivation in §3.3: $$ \mathrm{MSE}_{\text{block}} \;=\; \frac{\Delta^2 \, Q_{u_0 v_0}^2}{128} \;=\; \frac{(1)^2 (12)^2}{128} \;=\; \frac{144}{128} \;=\; 1.125 $$ **Step 3 — Global MSE (1 mark):** $$ \mathrm{MSE} \;=\; \frac{1}{B}\sum_{b=1}^{B} \mathrm{MSE}_{\text{block}}^{(b)} \;=\; 1.125 $$ **Step 4 — PSNR (1 mark):** $$ \begin{aligned} \mathrm{PSNR} \;=\; 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{255^2}{1.125}\right) \;=\; 10 \log_{10}(57800) \;\approx\; 47.62\;\mathrm{dB} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Imperceptibility verdict (1 mark):** $$ \mathrm{PSNR} = 47.62\;\mathrm{dB} \;>\; 35\;\mathrm{dB} $$ > **Verdict:** The modification is **imperceptible** by KTU convention. The image retains its visual fidelity. **Step 6 — Robustness check (bonus, 1 mark):** Against recompression with $Q_{\text{att}} = 50$, the new step is $Q'_{3,4} \approx 14$, so the perturbation $\Delta \cdot Q_{3,4} = 12$ survives the re-bin (the bit remains in its quantization bin). The scheme is **robust** to moderate recompression. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, PECDT523 Module 3]` **(a)** Derive the **embedding and detection equations of QIM (Quantization Index Modulation)** for embedding a single bit in a DCT coefficient. Compute the **maximum tolerable attack magnitude** that preserves a correct bit decision. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Discuss the **Syndrome-Trellis Code (STC)** framework for JPEG watermarking. Show how the **Viterbi algorithm** is used to find the minimum-distortion embedding and compute the capacity for embedding strength parameter $\alpha = 0.4$. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Q.B (a) — 7 marks **Step 1 — Two quantizers (2 marks):** $$ Q_0(x) \;=\; 2\Delta \cdot \mathrm{round}\!\left(\frac{x}{2\Delta}\right), \quad Q_1(x) \;=\; \Delta \;+\; 2\Delta \cdot \mathrm{round}\!\left(\frac{x - \Delta}{2\Delta}\right) $$ These are two **even and odd** lattices separated by $\Delta$. **Step 2 — Embedding rule (2 marks):** $$ y \;=\; \begin{cases} Q_0(x), & m = 0 \\ Q_1(x), & m = 1 \end{cases} $$ The maximum distortion incurred is $$ \max_{x}\vert x - y \vert \;=\; \frac{\Delta}{2} $$ **Step 3 — Detection rule (1 mark):** $$ \hat{m} \;=\; \arg\min_{m \in \{0,1\}} \vert y - Q_m(y) \vert $$ **Step 4 — Attack tolerance (2 marks):** If an attacker adds a perturbation $n$ to $y$, the decoder will be correct as long as $y + n$ stays closer to the original quantizer: $$ \boxed{\; \vert n \vert \;<\; \frac{\Delta}{4} \;} $$ This is the *provable* robustness radius of QIM, often quoted as the half of the half-bin width. --- #### Model Solution for Q.B (b) — 7 marks **Step 1 — STC formulation (2 marks):** For a host vector $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n$ and message $\mathbf{m} \in \{0,1\}^L$, STC solves: $$ \mathbf{y} \;=\; \arg\min_{\mathbf{z} : \mathbf{H}\mathbf{z} = \mathbf{m}} \; \Vert\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{z}\Vert^2 $$ where $\mathbf{H}$ is a parity-check matrix of a *convolutional* code of constraint length $h$. **Step 2 — Viterbi solution (2 marks):** The minimization is a shortest-path problem on a trellis with $2^{h-1}$ states. Each edge carries an additive cost: $$ c_{e} \;=\; (x_i - z_i)^2 $$ Viterbi's dynamic programming finds the **minimum-distortion embedding** in $O(n \cdot 2^{h-1})$ time. **Step 3 — Extraction (1 mark):** The decoder is a single matrix multiplication: $$ \hat{\mathbf{m}} \;=\; \mathbf{H}\mathbf{y} \pmod{2} $$ **Step 4 — Capacity for $\alpha = 0.4$ (2 marks):** The empirical capacity formula of STC is $$ R(\alpha) \;=\; \frac{L}{n} \;=\; \frac{1}{1 + \alpha^2 / 12} $$ Substituting $\alpha = 0.4$: $$ \begin{aligned} R(0.4) \;=\; \frac{1}{1 + 0.16 / 12} \;=\; \frac{1}{1 + 0.01333\dots} \;=\; \frac{1}{1.01333\dots} \;\approx\; 0.9868 \end{aligned} $$ > **Conclusion:** Approximately **0.987 bits per AC coefficient** can be embedded with $\alpha = 0.4$ using STC, while keeping the watermark near-imperceptible. This justifies STC's status as the state-of-the-art in robust JPEG watermarking. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls** > > 1. **Confusing DCT with DFT.** The DCT uses $\cos\!\left[\frac{(2x+1)u\pi}{16}\right]$ (no phase); DFT has complex exponentials. Marks are routinely lost for this mistake. > 2. **Forgetting the $1/4 \, C(u) C(v)$ prefactor** in the 2-D DCT. This costs exactly **0.5 marks** in step-marking. > 3. **Treating AC and DC coefficients identically.** DC carries the mean luminance; it must never be modified for imperceptibility. > 4. **Using JPEG-quality quantizer tables without scaling by $\alpha(Q)$.** At $Q = 50$, $\alpha = 1$; at $Q = 75$, $\alpha = 2/3$ — the quantizer step changes by 50% between these two qualities. > 5. **Quoting the SSIM formula with absolute-value bars inside a markdown table** — this breaks the table layout. Use `\vert x \vert` or `\mathrm{abs}(x)` instead. > 6. **Forgetting the $\Delta^2 Q^2 / 128$ per-block MSE formula** in the numerical. Many students derive it from scratch in the exam and run out of time. > 7. **Saying "QIM is robust" without proof.** You must compute the robustness radius $\Delta/4$ to earn full credit. > 8. **Not stating the KTU imperceptibility threshold** ($\mathrm{PSNR} \ge 35\,\mathrm{dB}$) explicitly in numericals — 1 mark is reserved for the final verdict line. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - [x] **JPEG lossy step = quantization**, not the DCT itself. - [x] **DCT formula** has the prefactor $\tfrac{1}{4} C(u) C(v)$ and the kernel $\cos\!\left[\frac{(2x+1)u\pi}{16}\right]$. - [x] **Mid-frequency AC coefficients** are the standard hiding place. - [x] **PSNR formula** = $10 \log_{10}(255^2 / \mathrm{MSE})$; **imperceptible if $\ge 35$ dB**. - [x] **SSIM** captures structural fidelity; **typical target $\ge 0.95$**. - [x] **BER** is the standard robustness metric; **target $\le 10^{-2}$**. - [x] **Cox Spread-Spectrum** uses $\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{x} + \alpha m \mathbf{w}$; correlation detector. - [x] **QIM** uses two shifted quantizers; **robustness radius = $\Delta/4$**. - [x] **STC** solves $\min \Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{y} \Vert^2$ s.t. $\mathbf{H}\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{m}$ via Viterbi; **extraction is $\mathbf{H}\mathbf{y}$**. - [x] **Capacity of STC**: $R(\alpha) = 1 / (1 + \alpha^2 / 12)$. - [x] **Robustness rule**: pick $\Delta Q_{uv}(Q_{\text{host}}) > \tfrac{1}{2} Q_{uv}(Q_{\text{att}})$. - [x] **JSteg** is *not* robust; F5 and OutGuess are *partially* robust; Cox, QIM, STC are *robust*. - [x] **Three-way trade-off**: Imperceptibility ↔ Capacity ↔ Robustness. - [x] **JPEG standard tables** are defined in **ITU-T T.81**. - [x] **Watson perceptual mask** is used by IBE (Podilchuk–Zeng) to set $\Delta$ per coefficient. - [x] **DC coefficient** carries the block mean — never modify it. - [x] **Zig-zag scan** orders coefficients from low to high frequency in the bitstream. - [x] **Huffman stage** is lossless — no embedding here; only the quantization step is attacked. - [x] **PSNR / SSIM** are computed in the *spatial* domain after full decode. - [x] **Costa's capacity** $C = \tfrac{1}{2}\log_2(1 + \sigma_W^2 / \sigma_N^2)$ is the theoretical upper bound. - [x] **Remember the $1/2$-bin rule** for the maximum perturbation that survives quantization.

topics.title: Robust Frequency Domain Watermarking
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT523 - DATA PRIVACY AND SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Robust Frequency Domain Watermarking

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Robust Frequency Domain Watermarking** is a class of digital watermarking techniques in which a secret message (the *watermark*) is embedded into the *transform-domain coefficients* of a host multimedia signal (image, audio, or video) such that the embedded information **survives a wide class of intentional and unintentional signal-processing operations** — including lossy compression (JPEG/JPEG2000/MPEG), filtering, noise addition, cropping, resampling, and moderate geometric distortions — while remaining perceptually invisible in the spatial/temporal domain.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, the term **"Robust"** is the critical qualifier: it implies that the watermark extraction (or blind detection) process should still recover the hidden bit-stream with a Bit Error Rate (BER) below an application-specific threshold (typically **BER ≤ 10⁻² to 10⁻⁴**) after the watermarked asset has been subjected to standard attacks catalogued by benchmarking suites such as **Stirmark** and **Checkmark**.

The three pillars that govern the design of any robust frequency-domain watermarking system are:

1. **Imperceptibility** — measured by **PSNR ≥ 38 dB** (image) or **ODG ≥ −1** (audio, ITU-R BS.1387).
2. **Robustness** — measured by **BER / NC** after attacks.
3. **Capacity** — measured in *bits per pixel (bpp)* or *bits per second (bps)*.

## 1.2 The Fundamental Trade-off Triangle

| Axis | What it Controls | Consequence of Increasing |
|---|---|---|
| **Imperceptibility** ↑ | Visual/auditory transparency | Robustness ↓ and/or Capacity ↓ |
| **Robustness** ↑ | Survival against attacks | Imperceptibility ↓ |
| **Capacity** ↑ | Data payload embedded | Imperceptibility ↓ and Robustness ↓ |

The **frequency domain is the natural battleground** where this trade-off is best managed because the human visual system (HVS) and human auditory system (HAS) have *non-uniform sensitivity* across spatial/temporal frequencies.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Sub-Bass Whisper"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine a music producer hiding a secret spoken message (the *watermark*) inside a pop song (the *host signal*). If the whisper is layered on top of the vocals in the same audible band, it is audible and ruins the song (poor imperceptibility). If the whisper is placed in the very high treble (≥ 16 kHz), it survives only on expensive speakers and is destroyed by any MP3 compression (poor robustness). The *robust* solution is to bury the whisper in the **mid-bass region (60–250 Hz)** — a band where the human ear is most sensitive *per unit energy*, so the whisper can be *quiet* (imperceptible) yet *durable* because lossy compressors preserve low-mid frequencies aggressively. This is exactly the philosophy of **mid-band frequency-domain watermarking**.

## 1.4 Why the Frequency Domain? — The Three Engineering Reasons

1. **Energy Compaction:** Transforms such as the **DCT** and **DWT** pack most signal energy into a *small set of low-frequency coefficients*. Embedding in these concentrated coefficients gives a *high signal-to-watermark ratio* without disturbing the perceptually important structure.
2. **Perceptual Masking Compatibility:** HVS/HAS masking thresholds are naturally expressed in the frequency domain (e.g., **JND — Just Noticeable Difference** profiles). Embedding strengths can be tuned per coefficient to match the masking curve.
3. **Spread-Spectrum Gain:** A single watermark bit can be spread across *thousands of coefficients*, providing redundancy that resists partial coefficient destruction (e.g., JPEG quantization discarding high frequencies).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Pipeline of a Robust Frequency-Domain Watermarking System

A canonical system (Cox, Kilian, Leighton–Talhoom 1997 paradigm) operates in five stages:

| Stage | Operation | Mathematical Form |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Transform** | Convert host $x$ into coefficient domain | $X = \mathcal{T}(x)$ |
| **2. Perceptual Analysis** | Compute JND mask $J$ | $J = \text{Mask}(X)$ |
| **3. Embedding** | Modify selected coefficients | $X'_k = X_k + \alpha_k W_k$ |
| **4. Inverse Transform** | Reconstruct watermarked signal | $x' = \mathcal{T}^{-1}(X')$ |
| **5. Extraction/Detection** | Re-transform and recover $W$ | $\hat{W}_k = (X'^*_k - X_k)/\alpha_k$ |

> [!NOTE]
> $\mathcal{T}$ can be **DCT, DFT, DWT, or a hybrid (e.g., DWT–DCT or DFT–DCT cascade)**. The choice is the *first design decision* and dramatically affects robustness.

## 2.2 Selection of the Embedding Sub-Band — The "Why" of Mid-Band
