> - **Course:** PECDT523 - DATA PRIVACY AND SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Computer Crime

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Computer Crime (Cybercrime):** Any unlawful, unethical, or unauthorized act involving a computer, computer network, or networked device, where the computer is either the **object** of the crime, the **instrument** used to commit the crime, or a mere **incidental** repository of evidence. The U.S. Department of Justice defines it as *“any violation of criminal law that involves knowledge of computer technology for its perpetration, investigation, or prosecution.”*

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PECDT523 – Module 4)**, computer crime is studied under three primary lenses:
1. **Target Crimes** — The computer itself is the victim (e.g., hacking, DDoS, malware injection).
2. **Tool/Instrument Crimes** — The computer is used as a weapon to commit traditional crimes (e.g., phishing, fraud, cyberstalking).
3. **Computer-Incident Crimes** — Computers are incidentally involved (e.g., storing illegal data, digital evidence).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** The module emphasizes the **legal**, **forensic**, and **preventive** dimensions of computer crime, mapping each crime type to the relevant sections of the **Information Technology Act, 2000 (amended 2008)** and the **Indian Penal Code (IPC)**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> **Plain English Analogy — "The Digital Bank Heist":**
> Imagine a bank. A **computer crime** is analogous to a heist that can take three forms:
> - **Computer as the Vault (Target):** The thief directly breaks into the bank's digital vault (server/database) to steal money or data.
> - **Computer as the Getaway Car (Instrument):** The thief uses a fake uniform (malicious email) printed from a printer connected to a computer to fool the guard and walk out with cash.
> - **Computer as the Locker (Incidental):** The thief hides a stolen physical painting inside a digital locker, and the computer is later searched by police to find evidence.
>
> In all three cases, the computer is central — either attacked, weaponized, or searched.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Classification Constants

| Metric / Constant | Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Global cybercrime cost (2025 est.) | **USD 10.5 Trillion/year** | Cybersecurity Ventures projection |
| Average cost of a data breach | **USD 4.88 Million** | IBM Cost of a Data Breach Report 2024 |
| Ransomware detection time | **~277 days** | Median dwell time before detection |
| IT Act 2000 | **Sections 43–74** | Primary Indian cyber-law statute |
| Budapest Convention | **2001** | First international treaty on cybercrime |

---

## 1.4 KTU Learning Outcomes Mapped to This Topic

> [!NOTE]
> - **CO4 (Module 4):** *Identify, classify, and analyze computer crimes and apply appropriate legal, technical, and forensic countermeasures.*
> - **Bloom's Levels Covered:** Remember (L1), Understand (L2), Apply (L3), Analyze (L4), Evaluate (L5).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three-Domain Classification Model

Computer crime can be rigorously classified using the **Parker Model (1989)** — the foundational taxonomy adopted by KTU:

### **Domain A — Computer as TARGET**
The computer system's integrity, confidentiality, or availability is directly violated.

- **Hacking / Unauthorized Access** — Gaining entry without permission.
- **Malware Attacks** — Viruses, worms, trojans, ransomware, spyware.
- **Denial of Service (DoS / DDoS)** — Overwhelming a system to deny service.
- **Data Theft / Data Breach** — Exfiltration of sensitive records.
- **Website Defacement** — Altering the visual content of a website.

### **Domain B — Computer as INSTRUMENT (Tool)**
A computer is used to commit a crime that could theoretically be committed offline, but the digital medium amplifies scale.

- **Phishing & Social Engineering** — Manipulating human psychology.
- **Identity Theft** — Impersonation using stolen credentials.
- **Online Fraud** — Credit-card fraud, auction fraud, advance-fee scams.
- **Cyberstalking & Cyberbullying** — Harassment via digital channels.
- **Child Exploitation (CSAM)** — Distribution of illegal content.
- **Software Piracy & IP Theft** — Unauthorized reproduction.

### **Domain C — Computer INCIDENTAL (Storage/Evidence)**
The computer is neither the target nor the tool, but evidence of a traditional crime is found on it.

- Drug trafficking ledgers stored digitally.
- Stolen intellectual property cached on a hard drive.
- Digital evidence of money laundering in spreadsheets.

---

## 2.2 Operational Logic Steps of a Generic Cyber Attack

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Cyber Kill Chain® (Lockheed Martin)** is the industry-standard KTU-recommended framework for breaking down an attack into 7 stages.

1. **Reconnaissance** — Attacker gathers intel (OSINT, port scanning, dumpster diving).
2. **Weaponization** — Coupling an exploit with a deliverable payload (e.g., a macro-laden PDF).
3. **Delivery** — Transmitting the weapon (email, USB, malicious URL).
4. **Exploitation** — Triggering the vulnerability in the target system.
5. **Installation** — Installing malware (backdoor, rootkit) for persistence.
6. **Command & Control (C2)** — Establishing a covert channel back to attacker.
7. **Actions on Objectives** — Data exfiltration, destruction, or ransom demand.

---

## 2.3 Malware Taxonomy — The KTU Master Table

| Malware Type | Self-Replicating? | Trigger Required? | Primary Motive | Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Virus** | Yes (needs host) | Yes (user action) | Destruction / Spread | ILOVEYOU |
| **Worm** | Yes (standalone) | No | Propagation | Conficker, Blaster |
| **Trojan** | No | Yes (deception) | Backdoor access | Emotet |
| **Ransomware** | No | Often Yes | Extortion | WannaCry, LockBit |
| **Spyware** | No | Yes | Surveillance | Pegasus |
| **Rootkit** | No | No | Stealth persistence | Stuxnet |
| **Adware** | No | No | Revenue (ads) | Fireball |
| **Logic Bomb** | No | Yes (condition) | Time-delayed attack | — |
| **Botnet Agent** | Yes (network) | No | DDoS / Spam | Mirai |

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Computer crime analysis uses several measurable quantities. The KTU 2024 exam frequently tests these formulae.

| Symbol | Formula / Definition | Meaning / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $C_{total}$ | $C_{total} = C_{detect} + C_{contain} + C_{recover} + C_{legal}$ | Total cost of a cybercrime (USD) |
| $T_{dwell}$ | $T_{dwell} = T_{compromise} - T_{detection}$ | Mean-time-to-detect (Days) |
| $R_{ROI}$ | $R_{ROI} = \dfrac{G_{gain} - C_{attack}}{C_{attack}} \times 100\%$ | Attacker's Return on Investment (%) |
| $P_{breach}$ | $P_{breach} = 1 - (1 - p)^{n}$ | Probability of at least one breach in $n$ attempts |
| $Risk$ | $Risk = Threat \times Vulnerability \times Impact$ | Classic NIST risk model |
| $DDoS_{load}$ | $DDoS_{load} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \dfrac{B_i}{T_i}$ | Aggregate bandwidth from $N$ bots (Gbps) |
| $Hash_{match}$ | $Hash_{match} = \dfrac{M_{hits}}{M_{total}}$ | Forensic hash similarity ratio |

> **Engineering Utility:** These formulae are used by **SOC analysts**, **DFIR teams (Digital Forensics and Incident Response)**, and **cyber-insurance underwriters** to quantify and price cyber risk.

---

## 2.5 The Legal Framework — Indian IT Act 2000 (amended 2008)

| Section | Offense | Penalty |
|---|---|---|
| **Sec 43** | Damage to computer system | Civil liability, damages up to ₹1 Crore |
| **Sec 65** | Tampering with source code | Imprisonment up to 3 years + Fine |
| **Sec 66** | Computer-related offenses (hacking) | Imprisonment up to 3 years + Fine |
| **Sec 66A** *(struck down 2015)* | Offensive messages | — |
| **Sec 66B** | Receiving stolen computer resource | Up to 3 years |
| **Sec 66C** | Identity theft | Up to 3 years + ₹1 Lakh fine |
| **Sec 66D** | Cheating by personation (phishing) | Up to 3 years + ₹1 Lakh fine |
| **Sec 66E** | Violation of privacy | Up to 3 years + ₹2 Lakh fine |
| **Sec 66F** | Cyber-terrorism | Life imprisonment |
| **Sec 67** | Publishing obscene material | Up to 5 years + ₹10 Lakh fine |
| **Sec 69** | Government's interception powers | Authorizes monitoring |
| **Sec 72** | Breach of confidentiality | Up to 2 years + Fine |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Cost of a Cyber Attack Incident

We model the **total cost of a computer crime incident** as the sum of all measurable damage components. KTU examiners often present a scenario and ask students to compute $C_{total}$.

### **Step 1 — Identify Cost Components**

A computer crime generates four types of cost. They are:
- **Detection cost** ($C_{detect}$): Tools, SIEM subscriptions, forensic analysts.
- **Containment cost** ($C_{contain}$): Isolating affected systems, revoking credentials.
- **Recovery cost** ($C_{recover}$): Rebuilding servers, restoring backups, lost productivity.
- **Legal cost** ($C_{legal}$): Lawsuits, regulatory fines, GDPR/IT-Act penalties.

### **Step 2 — Write the Additive Model**

$$
C_{total} = C_{detect} + C_{contain} + C_{recover} + C_{legal}
$$

### **Step 3 — Add Indirect (Hidden) Costs**

Hidden costs include brand damage and customer churn. We extend the model:

$$
C_{total} = C_{detect} + C_{contain} + C_{recover} + C_{legal} + C_{reputation}
$$

### **Step 4 — Express as a Time-Series Risk**

For an organization exposed over $n$ years at a constant risk rate $\lambda$:

$$
E[C] = \lambda \times n \times \overline{C_{incident}}
$$

where $\overline{C_{incident}}$ is the **mean cost per incident**.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** A firm suffers a ransomware attack. Detection cost is ₹2,00,000. Containment cost is ₹5,00,000. Recovery cost is ₹8,00,000. Legal fees are ₹3,00,000. Reputation damage is estimated at ₹12,00,000. Compute the **total cost of the crime**.

### **Solution — Step-by-Step**

**Step 1:** List all components.

$$
C_{detect} = ₹2{,}00{,}000 \quad C_{contain} = ₹5{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
C_{recover} = ₹8{,}00{,}000 \quad C_{legal} = ₹3{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
C_{reputation} = ₹12{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the additive cost formula.

$$
C_{total} = C_{detect} + C_{contain} + C_{recover} + C_{legal} + C_{reputation}
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the numerical values.

$$
C_{total} = 2{,}00{,}000 + 5{,}00{,}000 + 8{,}00{,}000 + 3{,}00{,}000 + 12{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 4:** Compute the sum column-wise.

$$
C_{total} = (2 + 5 + 8 + 3 + 12) \times 1{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 5:** Final arithmetic.

$$
C_{total} = 30 \times 1{,}00{,}000 = ₹30{,}00{,}000
$$

**Result:** The total cost of the ransomware crime = **₹30 Lakhs**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Attacker's ROI — Why Crime is Rational

A rational attacker compares **expected gain** vs **expected cost**. We model this exactly as in financial ROI.

### **Step 1 — Define Gain and Cost**

Let $G_{gain}$ be the monetary value extracted (e.g., ransom paid, card data sold).
Let $C_{attack}$ be the cost of mounting the attack (tools, time, infrastructure).
Let $p$ be the probability of being caught and penalized by $F_{fine}$.

### **Step 2 — Net Profit Function**

$$
\Pi_{net} = G_{gain} - C_{attack} - (p \times F_{fine})
$$

### **Step 3 — ROI in Percentage**

$$
R_{ROI} = \frac{\Pi_{net}}{C_{attack}} \times 100\%
$$

### **Step 4 — Worked Example**

An attacker pays $C_{attack} = \$1{,}000$ for a phishing kit, steals credit cards worth $G_{gain} = \$50{,}000$, has a $p = 5\%$ chance of being caught, and faces $F_{fine} = \$200{,}000$.

**Substitute:**

$$
\Pi_{net} = 50{,}000 - 1{,}000 - (0.05 \times 200{,}000)
$$

$$
\Pi_{net} = 50{,}000 - 1{,}000 - 10{,}000 = \$39{,}000
$$

**Compute ROI:**

$$
R_{ROI} = \frac{39{,}000}{1{,}000} \times 100\% = 3{,}900\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Even a 5% chance of a massive fine still yields a 3,900% ROI. This is why cybercrime is **economically rational** for attackers — a fact KTU exams love to test.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Crimes to Legal Sections

| Crime Type | Indian IT Act Section | IPC Section | CO Mapping |
|---|---|---|---|
| Hacking | Sec 66 | Sec 378, 425 | CO4 (L4: Analyze) |
| Phishing | Sec 66D | Sec 419, 420 | CO4 (L3: Apply) |
| Identity Theft | Sec 66C | Sec 463, 465 | CO4 (L4) |
| Obscene Material | Sec 67 | Sec 292 | CO4 (L2) |
| Cyber-terrorism | Sec 66F | Sec 121A | CO4 (L5) |
| Privacy Violation | Sec 66E | — | CO4 (L4) |
| Source-code Tampering | Sec 65 | Sec 477 | CO4 (L4) |
| Stolen Computer Resource | Sec 66B | Sec 411 | CO4 (L3) |

---

## 4.2 The Cyber Kill Chain — Block Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    R[1 RECONNAISSANCE] --> W[2 WEAPONIZATION]
    W --> D[3 DELIVERY]
    D --> E[4 EXPLOITATION]
    E --> I[5 INSTALLATION]
    I --> C[6 COMMAND AND CONTROL]

    R --> R1[OSINT, Port Scan, Shodan]

```

---

## 4.3 Computer Crime Investigation Workflow

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start[Incident Detected] --> ID[1 IDENTIFICATION]
    ID --> Prep[2 PREPARATION - Tools, Warrants]
    Prep --> Coll[3 COLLECTION - Volatile, Non-Volatile]
    Coll --> Exam[4 EXAMINATION - File System, Logs]
    Exam --> Ana[5 ANALYSIS - Timeline, Correlation]
    Ana --> Rep[6 REPORTING - Legal, Technical]
    Rep --> Court[7 TESTIMONY IN COURT]
    Court --> Close[Case Closed]

    Coll --> Vol[Volatile: RAM, Net Connections]
    Coll --> NVol[Non-Volatile: Disk, Logs, Backups]
    Ana --> T1[Timeline Reconstruction]
    Ana --> T2[Hash Verification - SHA256]
    Ana --> T3[Artifact Correlation]
```

---

## 4.4 Defense-in-Depth Layered Model

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[DEFENSE IN DEPTH] --> L1[Layer 1: POLICIES & GOVERNANCE]
    A --> L2[Layer 2: PHYSICAL SECURITY]
    A --> L3[Layer 3: PERIMETER - Firewalls, IDS/IPS]
    A --> L4[Layer 4: NETWORK - Segmentation, VPN]
    A --> L5[Layer 5: HOST - AV, EDR, Patching]
    A --> L6[Layer 6: APPLICATION - Secure Coding, WAF]
    A --> L7[Layer 7: DATA - Encryption, DLP, Backups]
    A --> L8[Layer 8: HUMAN - Training, Awareness]
    A --> L9[Layer 9: INCIDENT RESPONSE - Forensics, IR Plan]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

**Q1. Define computer crime and list any four categories of computer crime under the Parker Model.**
*(3 Marks — CO4, L1/L2)*

**Model Answer:**
Computer crime refers to any illegal act involving a computer, where the computer acts as the **target**, **instrument**, or **incidental** repository of evidence.

Four categories under the Parker Model:
1. **Computer as target** — e.g., hacking, malware.
2. **Computer as instrument** — e.g., phishing, fraud.
3. **Computer incidental** — e.g., storing illegal data.
4. **Computer-assisted crime** — Traditional crimes enhanced by digital tools.

---

**Q2. Differentiate between a virus, worm, and trojan with one example each.**
*(3 Marks — CO4, L2)*

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Virus | Worm | Trojan |
|---|---|---|---|
| Self-replicating | Yes | Yes (auto) | No |
| Needs user trigger | Yes | No | Yes (deception) |
| Spreads via | Infected files | Network | Disguised software |
| Example | ILOVEYOU | Conficker | Emotet |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Compulsory Set**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Cyber Kill Chain** model. Discuss how understanding each stage helps in **incident detection and prevention**. *(7 Marks — CO4, L4)*

**(b)** A company suffers a data breach. The forensic team is asked to estimate the total cost. The following data is given: detection cost = ₹3,00,000, containment = ₹6,00,000, recovery = ₹10,00,000, legal = ₹4,00,000, reputation damage = ₹15,00,000. Compute the **total cost of the crime** and discuss the **legal recourse** available under the **Indian IT Act 2000**. *(7 Marks — CO4, L3/L5)*

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a)**

The **Cyber Kill Chain (Lockheed Martin)** is a 7-stage model that maps the anatomy of a cyber attack. Understanding each stage enables proactive detection and disruption.

| Stage | Description | Defensive Countermeasure |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Reconnaissance | OSINT, port scanning | Threat intel, honeypots |
| 2. Weaponization | Exploit + payload coupling | Email sandboxing |
| 3. Delivery | Email, USB, web | URL filtering, DMARC |
| 4. Exploitation | Triggering vulnerability | Patch management |
| 5. Installation | Backdoor, rootkit | EDR, application whitelisting |
| 6. Command & Control | Beacon to C2 server | Egress filtering, NDR |
| 7. Actions on Objectives | Exfiltration, ransom | DLP, backups, IR plan |

> The earlier the kill chain is broken, the cheaper the defense. Breaking it at Stage 1 is free; at Stage 7, it costs millions.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b)**

**Step 1 — Cost Formula:**

$$
C_{total} = C_{detect} + C_{contain} + C_{recover} + C_{legal} + C_{reputation}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitution:**

$$
C_{total} = 3{,}00{,}000 + 6{,}00{,}000 + 10{,}00{,}000 + 4{,}00{,}000 + 15{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 3 — Computation:**

$$
C_{total} = 38 \times 1{,}00{,}000 = ₹38{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 4 — Legal Recourse:**
- **Section 43 IT Act** — Civil damages for unauthorized access.
- **Section 66** — Criminal prosecution, up to 3 years imprisonment.
- **Section 66C** — If identity theft occurred.
- **Section 72** — Breach of confidentiality by an insider.
- The aggrieved company can file an **FIR** with the **Cyber Crime Cell** and approach the **Adjudicating Officer** under IT Act.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Set**

**(a)** Classify the various **types of computer crimes** using the Parker Model. Provide at least **two real-world examples** for each category. *(7 Marks — CO4, L2/L4)*

**(b)** Explain the **Indian IT Act 2000 (amended 2008)** provisions for:
   (i) Hacking (Sec 66)
   (ii) Phishing (Sec 66D)
   (iii) Identity Theft (Sec 66C)
   (iv) Cyber-terrorism (Sec 66F)
Compare with the **corresponding IPC sections**. *(7 Marks — CO4, L4/L5)*

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a)**

**Parker Model Classification with Examples:**

**1. Computer as TARGET** — Attacks the system itself.
- Example 1: **WannaCry ransomware (2017)** — Encrypted 230,000+ computers globally.
- Example 2: **Yahoo data breach (2013)** — 3 billion accounts compromised.

**2. Computer as INSTRUMENT** — Computer used as weapon.
- Example 1: **Nigerian 419 scam emails** — Advance-fee fraud via email.
- Example 2: **Cambridge Analytica (2018)** — Harvested Facebook data for political profiling.

**3. Computer as INCIDENTAL** — Evidence stored digitally.
- Example 1: **Enron scandal (2001)** — Emails used as evidence of fraud.
- Example 2: **Silk Road takedown (2013)** — Drug marketplace seized; digital evidence was key.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b)**

| Offense | IT Act Section | Penalty | Corresponding IPC Section |
|---|---|---|---|
| Hacking | **Sec 66** | 3 yrs + ₹5 Lakh fine | Sec 378 (Theft), Sec 425 (Mischief) |
| Phishing | **Sec 66D** | 3 yrs + ₹1 Lakh fine | Sec 419 (Personation), Sec 420 (Cheating) |
| Identity Theft | **Sec 66C** | 3 yrs + ₹1 Lakh fine | Sec 463 (Forgery) |
| Cyber-terrorism | **Sec 66F** | Life imprisonment | Sec 121A (Waging war) |

**Discussion:**
- The IT Act 2000 (amended 2008) was the **first dedicated Indian cyber-law** to address digital crimes, since the IPC (1860) was drafted before computers existed.
- Section 66F (cyber-terrorism) is a **non-bailable, cognizable offense** with the **highest penalty** — life imprisonment — reflecting its threat to national sovereignty.
- IT Act works in **tandem with IPC**: IPC provides the foundational criminal definitions, while IT Act provides the digital-specific extensions.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Conflating Virus & Worm** — Many students write "worms need a host file." They don't. Worms are **standalone** and self-propagate over the network.
> 2. **Mislabelling Section 66A** — This section was **struck down by the Supreme Court in Shreya Singhal v. Union of India (2015)**. Do **NOT** cite it in answers; examiners will deduct marks.
> 3. **Forgetting Section 79 Safe Harbour** — Many students over-criminalize platforms. **Section 79** provides **safe-harbour** to intermediaries acting in good faith.
> 4. **Currency Mismatch in Cost Calculations** — Use **₹ (INR)** for Indian cases and **USD** for international reports. Mixing them loses marks.
> 5. **Skipping the "Why"** — KTU awards marks for **reasoning**, not just definitions. Always state *why* a law was enacted, not just *what* it says.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Computer Crime (Module 4)**

- **Definition:** Computer crime = illegal act where computer is **target, instrument, or incidental**.
- **Parker Model (1989):** Three-domain classification — Target, Instrument, Incidental.
- **Cyber Kill Chain (Lockheed Martin):** 7 stages — Recon → Weaponize → Deliver → Exploit → Install → C2 → Act.
- **Malware Distinctions:**
  - **Virus** = Needs host + user trigger.
  - **Worm** = Standalone, self-propagating.
  - **Trojan** = Disguised, no self-replication.
  - **Ransomware** = Encrypts + extorts.
  - **Rootkit** = Stealth persistence.
- **Cost Formula:** $C_{total} = C_{detect} + C_{contain} + C_{recover} + C_{legal} + C_{reputation}$.
- **Attacker's ROI:** $R_{ROI} = \dfrac{G_{gain} - C_{attack} - p \times F_{fine}}{C_{attack}} \times 100\%$ — Crime is rational.
- **IT Act Key Sections (must memorize):** 43, 65, 66, 66B, 66C, 66D, 66E, 66F, 67, 72, 79.
- **Forensic Workflow:** Identification → Preparation → Collection → Examination → Analysis → Reporting → Testimony.
- **Defense-in-Depth:** Policies → Physical → Perimeter → Network → Host → Application → Data → Human → IR.
- **International Law:** Budapest Convention (2001) — India is **not a signatory**; instead relies on IT Act + Mutual Legal Assistance Treaties (MLAT).
- **Highest Penalty:** Section 66F (Cyber-terrorism) = **Life imprisonment**.
- **Real-world cost:** Global cybercrime = **USD 10.5 Trillion/year (2025 est.)**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ethical Issues in Computer Security
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT523 - DATA PRIVACY AND SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Ethical Issues in Computer Security

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Ethical Issues in Computer Security** refers to the systematic study of moral principles, professional codes of conduct, and value-based dilemmas that govern the behaviour of computing professionals, security researchers, system administrators, and end-users when designing, deploying, attacking, defending, or auditing information systems.

In the context of the KTU 2024 syllabus for *Data Privacy and Security*, ethics encompasses four tightly interlinked pillars:

1. **Professional Conduct** — adherence to codified codes (ACM, IEEE, (ISC)²).
2. **Privacy & Civil Liberties** — balancing surveillance, data collection, and individual rights.
3. **Intellectual Property & Ownership** — software licensing, digital rights, and patent ethics.
4. **Responsible Disclosure** — how vulnerabilities are reported, to whom, and within what timeframe.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Snapshot:** Ethics in computer security is the *applied moral philosophy* that determines *what **ought** to be done* (deontological view) and *what produces the greatest good* (consequentialist view) when technical actions have legal, social, and human consequences.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Surgeon vs. Street Quack" Model

Imagine the medical profession:

- A **certified surgeon** (White-Hat Hacker) is trained, licensed, and bound by the **Hippocratic Oath** to "first, do no harm." They may cut into a human body, but only inside a sterile operating theatre, with patient consent, and for a healing purpose.
- A **street quack** (Black-Hat Hacker) performs the same incisions, but without training, consent, or oversight — purely for personal gain or malice.
- A **controversial practitioner** (Grey-Hat Hacker) sometimes heals, sometimes harms, often blurring the line.

Computer security ethics is the *Hippocratic Oath of the digital world*. It answers the question: **"Just because you *can* break into a system, does that mean you *should* — and if yes, under what rules?"**

## 1.3 The Core Triad of Ethical Tension

$$
\text{Ethical Dilemma} \;=\; f\bigl(\text{Law},\; \text{Morality},\; \text{Professional Duty}\bigr)
$$

| Pillar | What It Governs | KTU 2024 Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Law** | Minimum enforceable behaviour | IT Act 2000/2008 (India), GDPR, DPDP Act 2023 |
| **Morality** | Personal / societal "right vs wrong" | Snooping on a colleague's email |
| **Professional Code** | Industry-accepted standards | ACM, IEEE, (ISC)² Code of Ethics |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction for KTU Exams:** *Legal ≠ Ethical.* An action can be **legal but unethical** (e.g., targeted advertising exploiting user psychology) and **illegal but ethical** (e.g., whistleblowing leaking evidence of corruption under encrypted channels).

## 1.4 Key Terminology (Bold-Defined for Rapid Recall)

- **White-Hat Hacker** — *Authorized* security researcher working under a formal scope-of-work (Rules of Engagement).
- **Black-Hat Hacker** — *Unauthorized* intruder with malicious or criminal intent.
- **Grey-Hat Hacker** — Operates without explicit permission but without malicious intent; often publicises flaws.
- **Hacktivism** — Politically or socially motivated hacking (e.g., Anonymous, WikiLeaks).
- **Responsible Disclosure** — Privately informing the vendor first, allowing a fix window (typically **90 days**) before public release.
- **Full Disclosure** — Immediate public release of vulnerability details, forcing rapid vendor response but risking weaponisation.
- **Whistleblowing** — Internal or external reporting of organisational misconduct via legitimate (or covert) channels.
- **Social Engineering** — Manipulating *people* (not systems) to bypass security controls; ethically grey when used as a *pentest* tool.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Pillars of Computer Security Ethics (KTU 2024 Mapping)

### Pillar 1 — **Privacy**

Privacy is the *right of an individual to control the collection, use, storage, and dissemination of information about themselves*.

- **Threats to Privacy in the Digital Age:**
  - Mass surveillance (NSA PRISM, Aadhaar data aggregation)
  - Data brokers selling behavioural profiles
  - Ubiquitous IoT sensors (smart homes, wearables)
  - Workplace monitoring (keystroke logging, screen capture)
- **Ethical Trade-off Equation:**

$$
\text{Security Gain} \;\overset{?}{\geq}\; \text{Privacy Loss}
$$

If the right-hand side exceeds the left, the measure is *technically effective but ethically unjustifiable*.

### Pillar 2 — **Intellectual Property (IP)**

- **Copyright** — protects the *expression* of an idea (code, documentation).
- **Patent** — protects a *process or method* (e.g., RSA algorithm was once patented by MIT; expired 2000).
- **Trade Secret** — protects confidential know-how (e.g., Coca-Cola formula, Google's search algorithm).
- **Ethical Issues:**
  - Software piracy (globally ~**37%** of installed software is unlicensed — BSA 2023 data approximation).
  - Reverse engineering for interoperability vs. cloning.
  - Open-source licensing (GPL "viral" clause, MIT permissive).

### Pillar 3 — **Accuracy, Truth, and Authenticity**

- **The Dilemma:** If a security tool produces *false positives*, innocent users are flagged. If *false negatives*, real attackers slip through.
- **Ethical Duty:** Transparency about confidence intervals and error rates.
- **AI/ML Ethics:** Biased training data → biased threat detection → discriminatory outcomes.

### Pillar 4 — **Accessibility & Digital Divide**

- Security solutions must not be *luxury goods* — a vulnerability scanner that costs ₹5 lakh/month is ethically problematic when small clinics are targets of ransomware.
- **Universal Design Principle:** Security should scale down to low-end devices (e.g., UPI works on ₹5,000 smartphones).

### Pillar 5 — **Accountability & Liability**

- Who is *ethically responsible* when an AI-powered IDS misses an attack?
  - The developer? The operator? The vendor? The end-user?
- **Chain of Custody:** Logs must be tamper-evident for forensic accountability.

## 2.2 The Computer Ethics Institute's "Ten Commandments" (1985, frequently cited in KTU exams)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield for KTU 2024 — Memorise these 10 rules. They appear in nearly every model paper.**

1. Thou shalt not use a computer to harm other people.
2. Thou shalt not interfere with other people's computer work.
3. Thou shalt not snoop around in other people's computer files.
4. Thou shalt not use a computer to steal.
5. Thou shalt not use a computer to bear false witness.
6. Thou shalt not copy or use proprietary software for which you have not paid.
7. Thou shalt not use other people's computer resources without authorization or proper compensation.
8. Thou shalt not appropriate other people's intellectual output.
9. Thou shalt think about the social consequences of the program you are writing or the system you are designing.
10. Thou shalt always use a computer in ways that ensure consideration and respect for your fellow humans.

## 2.3 Professional Codes of Ethics (Comparison Table)

| Code | Issuing Body | Focus | Key Obligation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **ACM Code** | Association for Computing Machinery | 24 principles across 4 areas | *Contribute to society and human well-being* |
| **IEEE Code** | Institute of Electrical and Electronics Engineers | 10 ethical principles | *Accept responsibility in making decisions* |
| **(ISC)² Code** | International Information System Security Certification Consortium | 4 Canons | *Protect society, the common good, necessary public trust* |
| **NSPE Code** (for engineers) | National Society of Professional Engineers | Engineering-first | *Hold paramount the safety, health, welfare of the public* |
| **RAND Report** | U.S. Air Force (Whitfield Diffie legacy) | Computer ethics foundations | *Codes must be self-imposed, not external* |

## 2.4 KTU 2024 Cheat Sheet — Legal vs Ethical vs Professional

| Scenario | Legal? | Ethical? | Professional? |
|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Pentesting with written authorisation | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
| Pentesting *without* authorisation | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ |
| Reporting a critical bug to vendor first | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
| Selling a 0-day exploit on the grey market | ✅ (in many jurisdictions) | ⚠️ | ❌ |
| Reading a colleague's email *out of curiosity* | ⚠️ (depends on policy) | ❌ | ❌ |
| Whistleblowing via encrypted leak | ⚠️ | ✅ | ⚠️ |
| Using company laptop for personal crypto-mining | ❌ (typically) | ❌ | ❌ |
| Disclosing a vulnerability on Twitter *before* patching | ✅ | ⚠️ | ❌ |

✅ = Yes, ❌ = No, ⚠️ = Context-dependent

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Bug Bounty Programmes** (Google, Microsoft, Apple) — formalise the *ethical* path, turning grey-hat activity into *compensated white-hat work*.
- **CERT-In Mandate (India, 2022)** — requires organisations to report cybersecurity incidents within **6 hours**, raising *legal* questions about *ethical* disclosure of customer data to regulators.
- **GDPR Article 33** — combines legal duty (72-hour breach notification) with ethical duty (data minimisation).
- **DPDP Act 2023 (India)** — explicitly introduces *fiduciary duty* and *consent-based* data processing — an ethical principle codified into law.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Frameworks & Decision Matrices

> [!NOTE]
> Since *Ethical Issues in Computer Security* is a non-quantitative theory topic, "derivations" are replaced here by **structured analytical decision frameworks**, **case-study walkthroughs**, and **weighted scoring matrices** — all of which are examinable as 7–14 mark answers.

## 3.1 Framework 1 — The ACES Ethical Decision Model

A canonical 4-step model examiners love:

$$
\text{ACES} \;=\; \bigl\{\text{Audience},\; \text{Consequence},\; \text{Equity},\; \text{Self-check}\bigr\}
$$

**Step 1 — Audience Identification (Who is affected?)**
- Identify *primary* stakeholders (data subjects, organisation, public).
- Identify *secondary* stakeholders (regulators, third parties, future victims).
- *Why this matters:* Ethical decisions are not made in a vacuum; the affected party often defines the boundary of "right."

**Step 2 — Consequence Analysis (What happens if I act/inaction?)**
- Enumerate *short-term* outcomes (immediate breach disclosure, system downtime).
- Enumerate *long-term* outcomes (loss of trust, regulatory fine, life impact if medical device is compromised).
- Apply the **Greater-Good Test**:

$$
\text{Ethical Score} \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot (\text{Benefit}_i - \text{Harm}_i)
$$

where $w_i$ is the *weight* assigned to stakeholder $i$. If the weighted score $\geq 0$, the action is *ethically defensible* under consequentialism.

**Step 3 — Equity Check (Is it fair across stakeholders?)**
- Apply **Rawls' Veil of Ignorance**: would you choose this rule *if you did not know whether you would be the victim or the beneficiary*?
- If the rule disadvantages the *least powerful* stakeholder disproportionately, it fails the equity test.

**Step 4 — Self-Check (Would I be comfortable if this were public?)**
- The *"New York Times Test"* or *"Front Page Test"*: if tomorrow's headline reads "Engineer X did Y to system Z," would you be proud?
- If yes → ethically aligned. If no → re-evaluate.

## 3.2 Framework 2 — The 90-Day Responsible Disclosure Timeline

A standard industry workflow (Google Project Zero, CERT/CC):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Day 0} &: \text{ Researcher discovers vulnerability} \\
\text{Day 0–7} &: \text{ Researcher privately notifies vendor} \\
\text{Day 7} &: \text{ Vendor acknowledges} \\
\text{Day 7–60} &: \text{ Patch development} \\
\text{Day 60} &: \text{ Patch released to affected users} \\
\text{Day 60–90} &: \text{ Co-ordinated public disclosure} \\
\text{Day 90} &: \text{ Public release of technical details}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** If a student is asked *"What is the ethical window for vulnerability disclosure?"* — answer with the **90-day** standard, citing Google Project Zero and CERT/CC. This is a guaranteed 3-mark fetcher.

## 3.3 Case Study Analysis — Step-by-Step (Examiner Favourite)

### **Case: A junior penetration tester discovers that a major bank's ATM software uses default credentials `admin/admin`. Should they post a tweet immediately?**

**Step 1 — Identify the issue.**
- Hard-coded default credentials = a *critical* vulnerability (CVSS ~9.8).
- It is a *known-bad* practice; the bank has a *duty of care* to fix it.

**Step 2 — Apply the legal lens.**
- Posting the credentials publicly could violate the *Computer Fraud and Abuse Act* analogues, the *IT Act 2000 §66* (India), and the *pentest contract's NDA* clause.
- Legal verdict: **high risk of criminal/civil liability**.

**Step 3 — Apply the ethical lens.**
- Public disclosure *could* force an immediate fix (good consequence).
- But it also *arms attackers* who may not have known (bad consequence).
- The bank has *thousands* of customers whose data is now weaponisable.

**Step 4 — Apply the professional code lens.**
- **(ISC)² Code Canon 1:** *Protect society, the common good, and the public trust.*
- **ACM Principle 1.6:** *Respect privacy.*
- Both codes demand *responsible* disclosure, not reckless broadcasting.

**Step 5 — Recommended ethical course of action.**
- Document the finding with screenshots and timestamps.
- Notify the *project sponsor* (usually the CISO) in writing within **24 hours**.
- Set a *mutually agreed remediation window* (typically 30–90 days).
- If the bank fails to act within the window, escalate to *CERT-In* and *regulators*, *not* to Twitter.

**Step 6 — Self-check.**
- Would the *New York Times* publish this as *"Hero or Villain?"* — the answer must be *Hero*, achieved only by following the responsible path.

## 3.4 Ethical Scoring Matrix for Security Decisions

| Criterion | Weight | Action A: Immediate Public Disclosure | Action B: Private Vendor Notification |
|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Public safety impact | 0.30 | 9 (high immediate) | 7 (delayed but controlled) |
| Legal compliance | 0.25 | 2 (likely violation) | 9 (compliant) |
| Professional code adherence | 0.20 | 3 (violates ACM/(ISC)²) | 9 (aligned) |
| Long-term trust in profession | 0.15 | 4 (encourages vigilantism) | 9 (strengthens norms) |
| Personal liability | 0.10 | 1 (high personal risk) | 8 (low personal risk) |
| **Weighted Score (out of 10)** | **1.00** | **4.55** | **8.30** |

> Action B (private disclosure) wins decisively — this is the *examinable conclusion* expected by KTU evaluators.

## 3.5 Comparative Analysis — Three Major Codes of Ethics

**Dimension 1 — Scope of Responsibility:**
- **ACM** = societal + individual + professional.
- **IEEE** = professional + technical decision-making.
- **(ISC)²** = public trust + infrastructure protection.

**Dimension 2 — Enforcement:**
- **ACM** = *advisory*; members can be expelled.
- **IEEE** = *advisory*; can revoke senior member status.
- **(ISC)²** = *binding*; revocation of CISSP/CCSP certification.

**Dimension 3 — Hierarchy of Obligations (when codes conflict):**
- **ACM & (ISC)²** both place *"public welfare"* *above* *"employer interest"*.
- **IEEE** places *"public safety"* *above* *"employer interest"*.
- Convergent principle: *the public interest is the supreme test.*

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> Per the Mermaid Compilation Safeguards, all node IDs are alphanumeric (prefixed with letters), and all node labels contain only raw uppercase alphanumeric text (no markdown bold/italics, no pipes).

## 4.1 Diagram A — Ethical Decision-Making Workflow (ACES Model)

```mermaid
graph TD
    startA[SECURITY PROFESSIONAL FACES AN ACTION] --> q1{IS IT LEGAL UNDER IT ACT 2000 AND DPDP ACT 2023}
    q1 -- YES --> q2{IS IT AUTHORIZED BY WRITTEN SCOPE OF ENGAGEMENT}
    q1 -- NO --> outA[REJECT ACTION IMMEDIATELY]
    q2 -- YES --> q3{WHICH CODE APPLIES ACM IEEE OR ISC SQUARED}
    q2 -- NO --> outB[ESCALATE TO CISO OR CONTRACT OWNER]
    q3 --> q4{STAKEHOLDER ANALYSIS WHO IS AFFECTED}
    q4 --> q5{CONSEQUENCE ANALYSIS SHORT TERM AND LONG TERM}
    q5 --> q6{EQUITY TEST RAWLS VEIL OF IGNORANCE}
    q6 --> q7{NEW YORK TIMES TEST APPLIED}
    q7 -- PASS --> outC[EXECUTE ACTION WITH DOCUMENTATION]
    q7 -- FAIL --> outD[REVISE OR ABANDON ACTION]
```

## 4.2 Diagram B — Categories of Hackers (Ethical Spectrum)

```mermaid
graph LR
    bh[BLACK HAT UNAUTHORIZED MALICIOUS INTENT] --> sp[SEMI PERIMETER LEGITIMATE BUT UNETHICAL]
    sp --> gh[GREY HAT UNAUTHORIZED BUT NON MALICIOUS]
    gh --> wh[WHITE HAT AUTHORIZED PROFESSIONAL]
    wh --> bh2[BLUE HAT VENDOR INVITED BUG TESTER]
    bh2 --> rs[RED TEAM OFFENSIVE ADVERSARY SIMULATION]
    rs --> es[ETHICAL STATE ACTOR LAW ENFORCEMENT]
    es --> ha[HACKTIVIST POLITICAL OR SOCIAL MOTIVATION]
    ha --> wh
```

## 4.3 Diagram C — Responsible Disclosure Lifecycle (90-Day Window)

```mermaid
graph TD
    d0[DAY 0 VULNERABILITY DISCOVERED] --> d7[DAY 0 TO 7 PRIVATE VENDOR NOTIFICATION]
    d7 --> ack[DAY 7 VENDOR ACKNOWLEDGEMENT]
    ack --> patch[DAY 7 TO 60 PATCH DEVELOPMENT]
    patch --> release[DAY 60 PATCH ROLLOUT]
    release --> coord[DAY 60 TO 90 COORDINATED DISCLOSURE]
    coord --> pub[DAY 90 PUBLIC TECHNICAL DETAILS]
    pub --> learn[POST MORTEM AND LESSONS LEARNED]
    learn --> d0
```

## 4.4 Diagram D — Hierarchy of Ethical Obligations (When Codes Conflict)

```mermaid
graph TD
    top[PUBLIC WELFARE AND HUMAN DIGNITY SUPREME]
    top --> mid1[LEGAL OBLIGATIONS IT ACT 2000 DPDP 2023 GDPR]
    mid1 --> mid2[PROFESSIONAL CODE ACM IEEE ISC SQUARED]
    mid2 --> mid3[EMPLOYER POLICY AND NDA]
    mid3 --> base[PERSONAL CONVENIENCE OR PROFIT]
    base -- CONFLICT --> top
```

## 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Ethical Review Board for a Security Operation

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph detectionLayer[AUTOMATED DETECTION LAYER]
        siemNode[SIEM CORRELATION ENGINE]
        idsNode[IDS IPS SIGNATURE AND ANOMALY ENGINE]
        uebaNode[UEBA USER BEHAVIOUR ANALYTICS]
    end
    subgraph triageLayer[HUMAN TRIAGE LAYER]
        analyst1[SOC L1 ANALYST]
        analyst2[SOC L2 INCIDENT RESPONDER]
        analyst3[THREAT INTELLIGENCE LEAD]
    end
    subgraph ethicsLayer[ETHICS REVIEW COMMITTEE]
        cisoNode[CISO FINAL AUTHORITY]
        legalNode[LEGAL AND COMPLIANCE OFFICER]
        privacyNode[DATA PROTECTION OFFICER]
        externalNode[EXTERNAL ETHICS ADVISOR]
    end
    siemNode --> analyst1
    idsNode --> analyst1
    uebaNode --> analyst2
    analyst1 --> analyst2
    analyst2 --> analyst3
    analyst3 --> ethicsLayer
    ethicsLayer --> decision{FINAL DECISION}
    decision -- PROCEED --> actionNode[ACTION WITH FULL DOCUMENTATION]
    decision -- ABORT --> abortNode[ABORTED WITH ETHICAL RATIONALE]
    decision -- DEFER --> deferNode[DEFERRED TO REGULATOR OR COURT]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
> **Differentiate between legal compliance and ethical conduct in computer security. Give one example where they conflict.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Legal compliance** is the minimum enforceable behaviour codified in statutes (e.g., IT Act 2000, GDPR). It answers *"What must I do?"* (1 mark).
- **Ethical conduct** is a higher, value-based standard drawn from professional codes (ACM, IEEE, (ISC)²) and societal morality. It answers *"What ought I to do?"* (1 mark).
- **Conflicting Example:** Monitoring employee keystrokes to detect insider threats is *legal* under most jurisdictions with prior notice, but *unethical* if used to evaluate personal productivity or union activity, eroding trust and dignity. (1 mark)

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]**
> **What is responsible disclosure? Why is a 90-day window considered an industry-standard ethical norm?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Responsible disclosure** is the *ethical practice* of privately notifying a software vendor of a security vulnerability, allowing a remediation window before any public release. (1 mark)
- It balances the *researcher's duty to inform* against the *public's right to safety* and the *vendor's right to fix*. (1 mark)
- The **90-day window** (popularised by Google Project Zero and CERT/CC) is considered ethical because it gives vendors *sufficient time to develop, test, and deploy a patch*, while limiting the period during which users remain exposed. (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> **Note:** Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, students answer *one* full question (14 marks), typically with sub-parts (a) = 7 marks and (b) = 7 marks.

---

### **QUESTION A — 14 MARKS**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted Model]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Computer Ethics Institute's Ten Commandments** in detail. Discuss how each command applies to a contemporary security scenario such as cloud data storage or social media surveillance.

**Model Solution:**

The Ten Commandments (formulated 1985, frequently cited in KTU 2024 syllabus) constitute a foundational ethical charter for computing professionals. (½ mark)

**Application to cloud data storage:**
1. *Do not harm others* — refusing to deploy a cloud migration that leaks PII. (½ mark)
2. *Do not interfere* — respecting multi-tenant isolation in AWS/Azure. (½ mark)
3. *Do not snoop* — cloud admins must not read customer S3 buckets. (½ mark)
4. *Do not steal* — no unauthorised data exfiltration for resale. (½ mark)
5. *Do not bear false witness* — incident reports must be accurate, not minimised to avoid blame. (½ mark)
6. *Pay for proprietary software* — proper licensing of cloud-vendor tools. (½ mark)
7. *No unauthorised use* — credentials must be scoped, not shared. (½ mark)

(For brevity, 7 of 10 elaborated; remaining 3 follow same pattern — students may reference intellectual property, social consequence, and respect for fellow humans.) (1½ marks for connecting to cloud context)

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare and contrast the **ACM Code of Ethics** with the **(ISC)² Code of Professional Ethics**. In a whistleblowing scenario, which code offers stronger protection to the security professional and why?

**Model Solution:**

| Dimension | ACM Code | (ISC)² Code |
|---|---|---|
| Issuance | 1993, revised 2018 | 2010, periodic updates |
| Structure | 24 principles, 4 areas | 4 Canons |
| Focus | Broad societal + professional | Information security + public trust |
| Enforcement | Advisory, expulsion | Binding, certification revocation |
| Whistleblower stance | *Principle 1.5* — respect privacy *but* also *1.2* — avoid harm | *Canon 1* — protect society even against employer |

(4 marks for table + analysis)

**Whistleblowing Verdict (3 marks):**
- The **(ISC)² Code** offers *stronger* protection because **Canon 4** explicitly obligates members to *"report unethical activities"* and provides a *certification-backed* identity that supports the whistleblower professionally. ACM is more *advisory* and less procedurally protective.
- However, in jurisdictions with weak whistleblower laws, **both codes** must be invoked alongside the *Indian Whistleblowers Protection Act 2014* for legal cover.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part B (a):** Students often *list* the Ten Commandments without applying them to the contemporary scenario. Marks are awarded for **mapping each rule to a real-world cloud/social-media example**, not for rote memorisation. Do not exceed 1 line per commandment; spend time on the *application*, not the *listing*.

### **QUESTION B — 14 MARKS (Alternative Choice)**
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Adapted Model]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **ethical responsibilities of a penetration tester**. What are the *legal*, *ethical*, and *professional* boundaries that must be observed during a pentest engagement?

**Model Solution:**

A penetration tester operates in a *privileged, high-risk* role where technical capability must be matched by ethical restraint. (½ mark)

**Legal boundaries (2 marks):**
- Written, signed **Rules of Engagement (RoE)** defining scope, timing, and methods.
- Compliance with **IT Act 2000 §66, §66F, §70**, GDPR Article 32, and the *pentest contract's* NDA.
- **No testing outside scope**, even if vulnerabilities are discovered accidentally (must be reported, not exploited further).

**Ethical boundaries (2 marks):**
- **Do no harm:** a destructive test on a production database is unethical even if legal.
- **Confidentiality of findings:** the client owns the report, not the pentester's personal blog.
- **No personal use** of access gained during testing.
- **Informed consent** of all affected stakeholders (including *third parties* whose systems might be touched).

**Professional boundaries (2 marks):**
- Adherence to **PTES** (Penetration Testing Execution Standard) and **OSSTMM** methodology.
- Maintaining **chain of custody** for any evidence gathered.
- **Continuing disclosure**: reporting critical findings *immediately*, not waiting for the final report.
- **Professional indemnity insurance** as an ethical backstop.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Analyse the **Stuxnet** and **Edward Snowden** cases from an ethical standpoint. Was each actor's action ethically justifiable, defensible, or indefensible? Justify your reasoning using the ACES framework.

**Model Solution:**

**Case 1 — Stuxnet (2010, US-Israeli operation against Iranian nuclear centrifuges):**
- **Audience:** Iranian nuclear programme operators, global critical-infrastructure defenders, US/Israeli governments. (½ mark)
- **Consequence:** Successfully delayed Iranian enrichment by ~1–2 years (pro); set a dangerous *precedent* of weaponised cyber-operations against civilian infrastructure (con); collateral damage to global SCADA systems. (1 mark)
- **Equity:** Failed the Rawls test — covert state action bypassed international law. (½ mark)
- **Self-check:** Publicly indefensible (still classified). (½ mark)
- **Verdict: Ethically indefensible under ACES**, even if strategically *effective*. (½ mark)

**Case 2 — Edward Snowden (2013, NSA PRISM disclosures):**
- **Audience:** Global citizens, US citizens, US government, intelligence community. (½ mark)
- **Consequence:** Exposed mass surveillance → triggered *USA FREEDOM Act 2015*; strengthened GDPR; endangered some intelligence sources (con). (1 mark)
- **Equity:** Passed the Rawls test — disclosed *systemic* harm to the *least powerful* (citizens vs. state). (½ mark)
- **Self-check:** Has spoken publicly, accepted *exile*; passes the NYT test. (½ mark)
- **Verdict: Ethically defensible (or at minimum *excusable*)** under ACES, though *legally* he remains a fugitive under the Espionage Act. (½ mark)

> **Synthesis (1 mark):** Stuxnet represents *state-actor ethics failure*; Snowden represents *individual-actor ethics courage*. Both cases show that legality and ethics diverge sharply when state secrecy is involved.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part B (b):** Do **not** write a political essay. The question demands an *ethical analysis using the named framework*. Marks are deducted for emotional language, unsupported moralising, or failing to apply each ACES step *explicitly*. Use the four-step structure: **A → C → E → S**.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (High-Density Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Fire Revision — Read this section 30 minutes before the KTU exam.**

- ✅ **Ethics ≠ Law.** Legal is the *floor*; ethical is the *ceiling*. Exam questions often hinge on this distinction.
- ✅ **Memorise the Ten Commandments** (Computer Ethics Institute, 1985) — they are *the* most-tested framework.
- ✅ **90-day responsible disclosure** window is the industry-accepted ethical standard (Google Project Zero, CERT/CC).
- ✅ **White-hat** = authorised, **Black-hat** = unauthorised malicious, **Grey-hat** = unauthorised but not malicious, **Hacktivist** = politically motivated.
- ✅ **Whistleblower protection** is stronger under **(ISC)² Canon 4** than under ACM's advisory principles.
- ✅ **Stuxnet** = ethically *indefensible* (state-actor); **Snowden** = ethically *defensible* under ACES (individual-actor).
- ✅ **Bug bounty programmes** are the *ethical institutionalisation* of grey-hat activity.
- ✅ **DPDP Act 2023 (India)** + **IT Act 2000/2008** + **GDPR** = the legal triad; **ACM + IEEE + (ISC)²** = the professional triad.
- ✅ **Greater-Good Test** uses weighted scoring: $\text{Score} = \sum w_i \cdot (\text{Benefit}_i - \text{Harm}_i)$.
- ✅ **Rawls' Veil of Ignorance** is the equity test used by KTU examiners for "fairness" questions.
- ✅ **New York Times / Front Page Test** is the self-check applied to controversial disclosures.
- ✅ **Pentest** rules: written RoE, scope respect, chain of custody, immediate disclosure of criticals, no personal use of access.
- ✅ **Three pillars of ethics in security:** *Privacy*, *Intellectual Property*, *Accuracy/Truth* — these are the universal themes across all KTU 2024 module questions.
- ✅ Always mention **stakeholders** explicitly in your answer — examiners award marks for *identifying affected parties* before applying any framework.

> **Final Exam Mantra:** *"In KTU answers, *structure* beats *volume*. Use tables, named frameworks, and stakeholder mapping to score 14/14."*
